chunks.title: laplace formula sheet
chunks.text:
nabla ^2= partial _x^2+ partial _y^2+ partial _z^2

(TR) QCGreen 2) Core Properties Mean value property:

Value at a point equals the average over any sphere centered there.

No strict interior maxima or minima unless Phi is constant.

Dirichlet boundary data determine a unique solution.

In electrostatic equilibrium, Phi = constant inside a conductor.

Phi (x,y)=X(x)Y(y), qquad Phi (r, theta )=R(r) Theta ( theta )

(BL) QCOrange 3) Canonical Solutions Parallel plates:

Phi (x)=V_0 x d , qquad mathbf E=- V_0 d mathbf x

General spherical source-free radial solution:

Phi (x,y)= sum _ n=1 ^ infty A_n sin left ( n pi x a right ) sinh left ( n pi y a right )

(BR) QCPurple 4) Boundary Data and Checks Dirichlet:

Mixed conditions are possible on different boundary segments.

1. Verify nabla ^2 Phi =0 in the domain.

3. Check uniqueness before trusting an image or series solution.

4. In a bounded region, interior extrema usually signal an error.

or more carefully from the normal field just outside.

1. Image charges must lie outside the physical region.

2. Verify the boundary exactly, not approximately.

3. Use uniqueness before claiming the answer.

4. Grounded and isolated conductors are different problems.

Inversion symmetry often kills odd moments.

Axial symmetry usually leads to Legendre-polynomial angular structure.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: 10 20 Diego Juarez April 17, 2026 Griffiths Problem Index in A&A Format Chapter-by-chapter scaffold for problem statements Diego Juarez1 Department of Physics, The University of Texas at El Paso, El Paso, TX 79968, USA April 17, 2026 ABSTRACT Context. This document is a structured manuscript scaffold for the exercises in Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, 4th edition. Aims. The goal is to organize the textbook exercises by chapter. Methods. Problem numbers were indexed from the local book PDF and arranged chapter by chapter in a searchable LaTeX source. Results. Conclusions. 1.

Find the surface charge density σ(θ) on a sphere of radius R that reproduces, for all exterior points, the same field as a point charge q located on the z-axis at position a <R. 5. Chapter 4: Electric Fields in Matter This chapter contains 43 indexed problems. Problem 4. 1. A hydrogen atom, with Bohr radius about 0. 5 Å, sits between two parallel metal plates separated by 1 mm and connected to a 500 V source. Estimate what fraction of the atomic radius the induced displacement d represents, and then estimate the voltage required to ionize the atom with that same apparatus. Use the polarizability listed in Table 4. 1. 790 Problem 4. 2.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: For a hydrogen atom in its ground state, the elec- tron cloud has charge density ρ(r)= q(πa3)−1e−2r/a, where q is the electron charge and a is the Bohr radius. Compute the atomic polarizability. A suggested route is to first determine the field of the electron cloud and then expand for r ≪a. Problem 4. 3. Equation 4. 1 says that an induced dipole moment is proportional to the applied field. Test that rule for a model atom whose electron-cloud density grows in proportion to dis- tance from the center up to radius R. Determine the power of E that appears in p(E), and find the condition on ρ(r) that guaran- tees a linear weak-field response. 800 Problem 4. 4. A point charge q is placed far from a neutral atom with polarizability α. Find the attractive force between the charge and the atom. Problem 4. 5. Two perfect dipoles, p1 and p2, are separated by distance r in the geometry shown in Fig. 4. 6.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Find the torque on p1 due to p2, and the torque on p2 due to p1, each taken about that dipole’s own center. 810 820 830 840 850 Diego Juarez: Griffiths Problem Index in A&A Format Problem 4. 6. A perfect dipole p is located a height z above an infinite grounded conducting plane. The dipole makes angle θ with the normal to the plane. Find the torque on the dipole and determine the orientation of stable equilibrium if it is free to rotate. cavity parallel to P, and (c) a thin wafer-shaped cavity perpen- dicular to P. Assume the cavity is small enough that P, E0, and D0 are essentially uniform. Problem 4. 7. Show that the potential energy of an ideal dipole in an external electric field is U=−p·E. Problem 4. 17. For the bar electret of Problem 4. 11, with L ≈ 2a, draw careful field-line sketches for P, E, and D. Use the dis- tinction that E lines begin and end on total charge, while D lines begin and end only on free charge. Problem 4. 8.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Derive the interaction energy of two dipoles sep- arated by displacement r, expressing the answer in terms of p1, p2, and ˆ r. Use the dipole-energy formula together with the elec- tric field of a dipole. Problem 4. 18. A parallel-plate capacitor is filled by two dielec- tric slabs, each of thickness a, so the plate separation is 2a. The upper slab has dielectric constant 2, the lower slab has dielectric constant 1. 5, and the free surface charge densities on the plates are ±σ. Find D, E, and P in each slab, the potential difference, the full bound-charge distribution, and then recompute the fields from all charges as a check. Problem 4. 9. A dipole p is located a distance r from a point charge q, and p makes angle θ with the vector from q to the dipole. Find (a) the force on the dipole and (b) the force on the point charge. Problem 4. 10. A sphere of radius R has polarization P(r)= kr, where k is constant.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Compute the bound surface and volume charge densities, and then determine the electric field inside and outside the sphere. Problem 4. 19. You have just enough linear dielectric of di- electric constant ϵr to occupy half of a parallel-plate capacitor. Compare two arrangements: one where the dielectric fills half the plate area, and one where it fills half the plate separation. For each arrangement, determine the fractional change in capac- itance. Then, for a specified plate voltage V, find E, D, P, and all free and bound surface charges in every region. Problem 4. 11. A short cylinder of radius a and length L carries a permanent uniform polarization parallel to its axis. Determine the bound charge distribution and sketch the field lines for the limiting cases L ≫a, L ≪a, and L∼a. Problem 4. 20. A solid sphere of radius R is made of linear dielectric material and contains a uniform free charge density ρ.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Find the electrostatic potential at the center, measured relative to infinity, if the dielectric constant is ϵr. Problem 4. 12. For the uniformly polarized sphere of Example 4. 2, compute the potential directly from Eq. 4. 9 rather than from the bound-charge picture. Problem 4. 21. A coaxial cable has inner conductor radius a and outer conductor inner radius c. The annular region from b to c is filled with dielectric of constant ϵr, while the region from a to b is empty. Find the capacitance per unit length. Problem 4. 13. An infinitely long cylinder of radius a carries a uniform polarization P perpendicular to its axis. Find the electric field inside the cylinder, and show that the exterior field can be written in the form given in the text in terms of a, s, and P. Problem 4. 22.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: A very long cylinder of linear dielectric ma- terial, radius a and susceptibility χe, is placed in an otherwise uniform electric field E0 perpendicular to its axis. Determine the electric field inside the cylinder. Problem 4. 14. Show directly from the formulas for σb and ρb that the total bound charge of any polarized neutral dielectric is zero. Problem 4. 15. A dielectric spherical shell with inner radius a and outer radius b has frozen-in polarization P(r)= (k/r)ˆ r. There is no free charge. Find the electric field in all three re- gions: first by locating the bound charges and using Gauss’s law for E, and then by computing D first and recovering E. Problem 4. 23. Re-derive the internal field of a dielectric sphere placed in a uniform external field by using successive approx- imations. Start with E0, compute the induced polarization P0, then the field it creates, then the next correction to the polar- ization, and so on.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Sum the resulting series and compare with Eq. 4. 49. Problem 4. 16. Inside a large dielectric, the field is E0 and the displacement is D0 = ϵ0E0+P. A small cavity is carved out while the polarization remains frozen. Find the field and displacement at the center of (a) a spherical cavity, (b) a long needle-shaped Problem 4. 24. An uncharged conducting sphere of radius a is coated with an insulating spherical shell of dielectric constant ϵr out to radius b. The coated sphere is placed in a uniform external electric field E0. Find the electric field throughout the insulating layer. Article number, page 11 860 870 880 890 900 910 920 930 940 Griffiths Problem Problem 4. 25. In the dielectric-interface problem of Example 4. 8, suppose the upper half-space is also filled with a linear di- electric, but with susceptibility χ′ e different from that below the plane. Determine the electrostatic potential in all space. Problem 4. 26.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: A conducting sphere of radius a carries charge Q and is surrounded by linear dielectric material of susceptibility χe out to radius b. Use Eq. 4. 58 to compute the energy stored in the configuration. Problem 4. 27. For a sphere of radius R with frozen uniform polarization P, compute the energy once from Eq. 4. 55 and once from Eq. 4. 58. Explain why the two answers differ and discuss which one, if either, should be interpreted as the physical energy of the system. Problem 4. 28. Two long coaxial metal cylinders stand verti- cally in dielectric oil of susceptibility χe and mass density ρ. The inner cylinder is held at potential V, and the outer cylin- der is grounded. Determine how high the oil rises between the cylinders. Problem 4. 29. Return to the two-dipole arrangement of Prob- lem 4. 5. Compute the force on p2 from p1 and the force on p1 from p2, and comment on Newton’s third law.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Then find the to- tal torque on p2 about the center of p1 and compare it with the torque on p1 about that same point. Problem 4. 30. An electric dipole directed along the y-axis is placed midway between two large conducting plates that make equal small angles θ with the x-axis and are held at potentials +V and−V. Without doing a long calculation, determine the di- rection of the net force on the dipole and justify your reasoning qualitatively. Problem 4. 31. A fixed point charge Q sits at the center of a cir- cular frictionless track of radius R. A dipole p rides on the track and is constrained to remain tangent to the circle. Use the dipole- force formula to show that the force is always forward along the track, and then explain why this does not create a perpetual- motion machine. Problem 4. 32. Earnshaw’s theorem forbids electrostatic trap- ping of a charged particle.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Investigate whether a neutral but polarizable atom can be trapped instead. First show that F= 1 2 α∇(E2). Then show that E2 cannot have a local maximum in a charge-free region, so no stable electrostatic trap is possible. Problem 4. 33. A dielectric cube of side a, centered at the ori- gin, has frozen-in polarization P= kr. Find all bound charges and verify that their total is zero. Problem 4. 34. A parallel-plate capacitor is filled with dielectric whose dielectric constant varies linearly from 1 at the lower plate Article number, page 12 x = 0 to 2 at the upper plate x = d. The plates are connected to a battery of voltage V. Determine the complete bound-charge distribution and check that the net bound charge vanishes. Problem 4. 35. A point charge q is embedded at the center of a linear dielectric sphere of radius R and susceptibility χe.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Find the electric field, the polarization, the bound volume charge density, and the bound surface charge density. Also determine the total bound charge on the surface and identify where the compensat- ing negative bound charge is located. Problem 4. 36. At the boundary between two linear dielectrics with no free surface charge, show that the field-line angles satisfy tan θ2/tan θ1 = ϵ2/ϵ1. Then discuss whether a convex dielectric lens would tend to focus or defocus the electric field. Problem 4. 37. A point dipole p is embedded at the center of a dielectric sphere of radius R and dielectric constant ϵr. Find the electrostatic potential both inside and outside the sphere. Problem 4. 38. Prove the uniqueness theorem for a region con- taining a specified free-charge distribution together with linear dielectrics of known susceptibilities.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Show that if the potential is specified on the boundary, then the potential everywhere inside is unique. Use the suggested integral involving ∇·(V3D3). Problem 4. 39. A conducting sphere at potential V0 is half buried in linear dielectric occupying the half-space z < 0. Test the claim that the potential is exactly the same as if the dielectric were absent. Do this by writing the candidate potential, finding the resulting field, polarization, bound charge, and free surface charge, showing that these reproduce the same potential, and then invoking the uniqueness theorem. Finally discuss whether the same idea works for the two modified geometries shown in Fig. 4. 36. Problem 4. 40. Use the force formula F= (P·∇)Eext dτto find the force on a small dielectric sphere of radius R and suscepti- bility χe located a distance s from a thin wire carrying uniform line charge density λ. Problem 4. 41.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: Relate microscopic polarizability α to macro- scopic susceptibility χe. Starting from the naive estimate P= NαE, account for the difference between the macroscopic field E and the local field acting on a given atom, Eelse. Assuming each atom occupies a spherical cell of radius R, derive the Clausius- Mossotti formula connecting α, N, and ϵr. Problem 4. 42. Check the Clausius-Mossotti relation numeri- cally for the gases listed in Table 4. 1, using dielectric constants from Table 4. 2. Comment on why the correction term is hard to distinguish from the naive low-density approximation in gases. 950 960 970 980 990 1000 1010 1020 Diego Juarez: Griffiths Problem Index in A&A Format Problem 4. 43. Derive the Langevin theory for a polar dielec- tric.

chunks.title: Griffiths problem solution
chunks.text: First, using the Boltzmann distribution for dipole orien- tations in an external field, obtain the polarization law P= N p[cosh(pE/kT )/sinh(pE/kT )−kT/(pE)] and sketch P/(N p) as a function of pE/kT . Then take the weak-field, high- temperature limit to extract the susceptibility, and compare the prediction with data for liquid water at 20◦C and for water vapor at 100◦C and 1 atm. Problem 5. 13. chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
abstract This document is a structured manuscript scaffold for the exercises in Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics , 4th edition. The goal is to organize the textbook exercises by chapter inside the Astronomy & Astrophysics class format while keeping solutions out of the document. Problem numbers were indexed from the local book PDF and arranged chapter by chapter in a searchable LaTeX source. The output is a complete exercise index with placeholders for future paraphrased statements. This file is intended as a working template for notes, paraphrases, and later solution writeups.

This document intentionally does not reproduce the full textbook wording of the exercises. Each entry is a placeholder keyed by the original Griffiths problem number so that paraphrased statements or privately licensed transcriptions can be inserted later.

This chapter contains 64 indexed problems.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 1. 1 Using the geometric definitions of the dot product and cross product, prove that both operations are distributive. Do this first for three coplanar vectors and then for the general three-dimensional case. 1. 2 Decide whether the cross product is associative. Either prove that (( mathbf A times mathbf B) times mathbf C = mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C) ) always holds, or give a simple counterexample showing that it does not. 1. 3 Find the angle between two body diagonals of a cube. 1. 4 Use a cross product to determine the Cartesian components of the unit vector normal to the shaded plane in Fig. ~1. 11. 1. 5 Prove the BAC--CAB identity by expanding both sides in Cartesian components. 1. 6 Show that ([ mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C)] + [ mathbf B times ( mathbf C times mathbf A)] + [ mathbf C times ( mathbf A times mathbf B)] = 0 ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Then determine the conditions under which ( mathbf A times ( mathbf B times mathbf C) = ( mathbf A times mathbf B) times mathbf C ). 1. 7 For a source point at ((2,8,7) ) and a field point at ((4,6,8) ), compute the separation vector from source to field, its magnitude, and the corresponding unit vector. 1. 8 (a) Show that the two-dimensional rotation matrix preserves dot products. (b) Determine the conditions the entries of a three-dimensional rotation matrix must satisfy in order to preserve the length of every vector. 1. 9 Find the rotation matrix corresponding to a (120^ circ ) rotation about the axis through the origin and the point ((1,1,1) ), with the rotation taken clockwise when viewed along the axis toward the origin. 1. 10 Study how vector quantities transform under changes of coordinates. (a) Find how vector components change under a translation. (b) Find how they change under inversion ((x,y,z) mapsto (-x,-y,-z) ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: (c) Determine how a cross product transforms under inversion, decide whether the cross product of two pseudovectors is a vector or pseudovector, and name two classical-mechanics pseudovectors. (d) Determine how the scalar triple product transforms under inversion. 1. 11 Compute the gradient of each of the following scalar fields: (a) (x^2+y^3+z^4 ), (b) (x^2y^3z^4 ), and (c) (e^x sin y ln z ). 1. 12 The height of a hill is (h(x,y)=10(2xy-3x^2-4y^2-18x+28y+12) ), where (x ) and (y ) are measured in miles east and north of South Hadley, and (h ) is in feet. Find the hilltop location, the hilltop height, and the slope magnitude and direction at the point one mile east and one mile north of South Hadley. 1. 13 Let ( mathbf r ) be the separation vector from a fixed point ((x',y',z') ) to the point ((x,y,z) ), and let (r=| mathbf r| ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Show that (a) ( nabla (r^2)=2 mathbf r ), (b) ( nabla (1/r)=- mathbf r /r^2 ), and (c) derive the corresponding general formula for ( nabla (r^n) ). 1. 14 Let (f ) depend only on (y ) and (z ). Show that the gradient ( nabla f = ( partial f/ partial y) mathbf y +( partial f/ partial z) mathbf z ) transforms as a vector under a rotation in the (yz )-plane. 1. 15 Compute the divergence of each of the three vector fields ( mathbf v_a = x^2 mathbf x +3xz^2 mathbf y -2xz mathbf z ), ( mathbf v_b = xy mathbf x +2yz mathbf y +3zx mathbf z ), and ( mathbf v_c = y^2 mathbf x +(2xy+z^2) mathbf y +2yz mathbf z ). 1. 16 Sketch the vector field ( mathbf r /r^2 ) and compute its divergence. Explain why the result may seem surprising at first. 1. 17 Show, in two dimensions, that the divergence transforms as a scalar under rotations. 1. 18 Compute the curl of each of the three vector fields introduced in Problem 1. 15. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 19 Draw a clockwise tangential vector field around a circle in the (xy )-plane. By comparing nearby vectors, determine the signs of ( partial v_x/ partial y ) and ( partial v_y/ partial x ), use them to infer the direction of ( nabla times mathbf v ), and explain how this illustrates the geometric meaning of curl. 1. 20 Construct a nonconstant vector field whose divergence and curl both vanish everywhere. 1. 21 Prove product rules (i), (iv), and (v) from the list given in the text. 1. 22 (a) Write out the meaning of (( mathbf A cdot nabla ) mathbf B ) in components. (b) Compute (( mathbf r cdot nabla ) mathbf r ). (c) For the vector fields ( mathbf v_a ) and ( mathbf v_b ) from Problem 1. 15, evaluate (( mathbf v_a cdot nabla ) mathbf v_b ). 1. 23 Prove product rules (ii) and (vi), using the component definition of (( mathbf A cdot nabla ) mathbf B ). 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 24 Derive the three quotient rules corresponding to the vector-calculus product rules. 1. 25 For ( mathbf A = x mathbf x +2y mathbf y +3z mathbf z ) and ( mathbf B = 3y mathbf x -2x mathbf y ), verify product rules (iv), (ii), and (vi) by computing both sides explicitly. 1. 26 Compute the Laplacian of each of the following: (a) (T_a=x^2+2xy+3z+4 ), (b) (T_b= sin x sin y sin z ), (c) (T_c=e^ -5x sin 4y cos 3z ), and (d) the vector field ( mathbf v = x^2 mathbf x +3xz^2 mathbf y -2xz mathbf z ), interpreted componentwise. 1. 27 Prove that the divergence of any curl is identically zero, and check the result explicitly for the vector field ( mathbf v_a ) from Problem 1. 15. 1. 28 Prove that the curl of any gradient is identically zero, and verify the result for the function in part (b) of Problem 1. 11. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 29 Evaluate the line integral of ( mathbf v=x^2 mathbf x +2yz mathbf y +y^2 mathbf z ) from the origin to ((1,1,1) ) along three different paths, and then compute the closed-loop integral obtained by going out along one path and back along another. 1. 30 For the vector field used in Example 1. 7, compute the flux through the bottom face of the box, taking upward as positive. Decide whether the surface integral depends only on the boundary curve for this field, and then find the total outward flux through the full closed box. 1. 31 Compute the volume integral of (T=z^2 ) over the tetrahedron with vertices ((0,0,0) ), ((1,0,0) ), ((0,1,0) ), and ((0,0,1) ). 1. 32 Check the gradient theorem for (T=x^2+4xy+2yz^3 ) between ((0,0,0) ) and ((1,1,1) ) along the three paths shown in Fig. ~1. 28. 1. 33 Test the divergence theorem for ( mathbf v=(xy) mathbf x +(2yz) mathbf y +(3zx) mathbf z ) on the cube of side (2 ) shown in Fig. ~1. 30. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 34 Test Stokes' theorem for ( mathbf v=(xy) mathbf x +(2yz) mathbf y +(3zx) mathbf z ) on the triangular surface shown in Fig. ~1. 34. 1. 35 Using the same vector field and boundary line as in Example 1. 11, check Corollary 1 by integrating over the five faces of the open cube shown in Fig. ~1. 35. 1. 36 Prove the two integration-by-parts identities given in Eqs. ~(1. 60) and (1. 61). 1. 37 Invert the spherical-coordinate formulas and write (r ), ( theta ), and ( phi ) explicitly in terms of (x ), (y ), and (z ). 1. 38 Express the spherical unit vectors ( mathbf r ), ( theta ), and ( phi ) in terms of ( mathbf x ), ( mathbf y ), and ( mathbf z ), and then invert those relations to express the Cartesian unit vectors in terms of the spherical ones. Check the results using dot and cross products. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 39 Check the divergence theorem on a sphere of radius (R ) centered at the origin for (a) ( mathbf v_1=r^2 mathbf r ) and (b) ( mathbf v_2= mathbf r /r^2 ). Compare the two cases and explain any apparent puzzle. 1. 40 For ( mathbf v=(r cos theta ) mathbf r +(r sin theta ) theta +(r sin theta cos phi ) phi ), compute the divergence and check the divergence theorem on the inverted hemispherical bowl of radius (R ) resting on the (xy )-plane. 1. 41 For (T=r( cos theta + sin theta cos phi ) ), compute the gradient and Laplacian, check the Laplacian by converting to Cartesian coordinates, and verify the gradient theorem along the path shown in Fig. ~1. 41 from ((0,0,0) ) to ((0,0,2) ). 1. 42 Express the cylindrical unit vectors ( mathbf s ), ( phi ), and ( mathbf z ) in terms of the Cartesian basis, and invert those formulas to write ( mathbf x ), ( mathbf y ), and ( mathbf z ) in terms of the cylindrical basis. 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 43 For ( mathbf v=s(2+ sin ^2 phi ) mathbf s +s sin phi cos phi , phi +3z mathbf z ): (a) compute the divergence, (b) verify the divergence theorem on the quarter-cylinder shown in Fig. ~1. 43, and (c) compute the curl. 1. 44 Evaluate the following delta-function integrals: (a) ( int _2^6 (3x^2-2x-1) delta (x-3) ,dx ), (b) ( int _0^5 cos x , delta (x- pi ) ,dx ), (c) ( int _0^3 x^3 delta (x+1) ,dx ), and (d) ( int _ - infty ^ infty ln (x+3) delta (x+2) ,dx ). 1. 45 Evaluate the following one-dimensional delta-function integrals: (a) ( int _ -2 ^ 2 (2x+3) delta (3x) ,dx ), (b) ( int _ 0 ^ 2 (x^3+3x+2) delta (1-x) ,dx ), (c) ( int _ -1 ^ 1 9x^2 delta (3x+1) ,dx ), and (d) ( int _ - infty ^ a delta (x-b) ,dx ). 1. 46 (a) Show that (x ,d delta (x)/dx=- delta (x) ). (b) Let ( theta (x) ) be the step function. Show that (d theta /dx= delta (x) ). 1.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

chunks.text: 2. 1 Place twelve equal charges (q ) at the vertices of a regular 12-gon and a test charge (Q ) at the center. (a) Find the net force on (Q ). For each of the three arrangements shown in Fig. ~3. 35, compute the monopole moment, the dipole moment, and the large- (r ) potential including both the monopole and dipole terms. 3. 33 An ideal dipole ( p =p z ) is fixed at the origin. Find the force on a point charge (q ) placed at (a) ((a,0,0) ) and (b) ((0,0,a) ). Then determine the work required to move the charge from ((a,0,0) ) to ((0,0,a) ). 3. 34 Three point charges lie at distance (a ) from the origin in the arrangement of Fig. ~3. 38. Find the far-field electric field in spherical coordinates, keeping the two lowest nonvanishing orders in the multipole expansion. 3. 35 A solid sphere of radius (R ) is centered at the origin. The northern hemisphere carries uniform volume charge density (+ rho _0 ), and the southern hemisphere carries (- rho _0 ). Find the approximate field ( E (r, theta ) ) for (r gg R ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 3. 36 Show that the electric field of an ideal dipole can be written in coordinate-free form as ( E _ dip ( r ) = (1/4 pi epsilon _0 r^3) ,[3( p cdot r ) r - p ] ). 3. 37 Give an alternative proof of the spherical averaging theorem using only Laplace's equation. Let (V_ ave (R) ) be the average of the potential over a sphere of radius (R ) centered at a point (P ), derive (dV_ ave /dR ) in terms of a flux integral, and use the divergence theorem to show that (V_ ave (R)=V(P) ) in a charge-free region. 3. 38 Re-derive the induced surface charge density on a grounded conducting plane due to a point charge (q ) at height (d ), but do so without the method of images. Write the normal field just below the conductor as the sum of the field from (q ) and the field from the unknown surface charge density ( sigma (x,y) ), set the total to zero, and solve for ( sigma ). 3.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 39 Two infinite grounded conducting planes are parallel and separated by distance (a ). A point charge (q ) lies between them, at distance (x ) from one plate. Find the force on the charge and check the limits (a to infty ) and (x=a/2 ). 3. 40 Two infinitely long straight wires carrying line charges (+ lambda ) and (- lambda ) lie on opposite sides of a long conducting cylinder of radius (R ). The wires are each at distance (a ) from the cylinder axis, and the cylinder carries no net charge. Find the potential everywhere. 3. 41 Approximate the ( C _ 60 ^ - ) ion as a conducting spherical shell of radius (R=3. 5 , AA ) carrying one extra electron smeared uniformly over its surface. A second electron experiences attraction at short distance but repulsion far away. (a) Find numerically the radius (r ) where the net force on the second electron vanishes. (b) Find the energy required to bring an electron from infinity to that radius. 3.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 42 Use superposition of separated solutions to find the potential in a rectangular pipe for which the two faces (x= pm b ) are held at (V_0 ), the face (y=a ) is held at (V_1 ), and the face (y=0 ) is grounded. 3. 43 A conducting sphere of radius (a ), held at potential (V_0 ), is surrounded by a thin concentric spherical shell of radius (b ) carrying surface charge density ( sigma ( theta )=k cos theta ). (a) Find the potential in the regions (r>b ) and (a<r<b ). (b) Determine the induced surface charge density on the inner conductor. (c) Find the total charge of the system and check that it matches the large- (r ) behavior of the potential. 3. 44 A total charge (+Q ) is distributed uniformly along the segment of the (z )-axis from (-a ) to (+a ). Derive the multipole expansion of the potential for (r>a ), showing that only even Legendre polynomials appear and obtaining the first few terms explicitly. 3.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 45 A long cylindrical shell of radius (R ) carries uniform surface charge (+ sigma _0 ) on its upper half and (- sigma _0 ) on its lower half. In each of the cases ( lambda (z)=k cos ( pi z/2a) ), ( lambda (z)=k sin ( pi z/a) ), and ( lambda (z)=k cos ( pi z/a) ), determine the leading nonvanishing term in the multipole expansion of the potential. 3. 47 Show that the average electric field over the volume of a sphere of radius (R ), due to the charge contained inside that sphere, is ( E _ ave =- p /(4 pi epsilon _0 R^3) ), where ( p ) is the total dipole moment of the enclosed charge distribution.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Follow the suggested route: first analyze a single interior point charge by comparison with a uniformly charged sphere, then generalize by superposition, and also show that the average field from exterior charges equals the field at the center. 3. 48 (a) Compute the volume average of the dipole field over a sphere centered at the dipole and compare it with the general averaging theorem. (b) Use that comparison to infer the singular field contribution at the origin, thereby recovering the delta-function term in the exact field of an ideal dipole. 3. 49 For the spherical shell with ( sigma ( theta )=k cos theta ), take the limit (R to 0 ) and show that the result reproduces the delta-function term in the exact dipole field. 3. 50 (a) Prove Green's reciprocity theorem: if ( rho _1 ) produces (V_1 ) and ( rho _2 ) produces (V_2 ), then ( int rho _1 V_2 ,d tau = int rho _2 V_1 ,d tau ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Use two evaluations of ( int E _1 cdot E _2 ,d tau ) and integration by parts. (b) Apply reciprocity to two separated conductors to show that if charge (Q ) on conductor (a ) produces potential (V_ ab ) on conductor (b ), then the same charge on (b ) produces the same potential (V_ ba =V_ ab ) on (a ). 3. 51 Use Green's reciprocity theorem to solve two induced-charge problems. (a) A point charge (q ) lies between the grounded plates of a parallel-plate capacitor; find the total induced charge on each plate in terms of the plate spacing (d ) and the charge position (x ). (b) A point charge (q ) lies between two grounded concentric conducting spheres of radii (a ) and (b ); find the total induced charge on each sphere. 3. 52 (a) Rewrite the quadrupole contribution to the multipole expansion in tensor form and define the quadrupole moment tensor (Q_ ij ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: (b) For the square of side (a ) in the (xy )-plane centered at the origin, with corner charges (-q,+q,+q,-q ) arranged as in Fig. ~3. 30, compute all nine components of (Q_ ij ). (c) Show that the quadrupole moment is independent of the choice of origin when both the monopole and dipole moments vanish. (d) Define the octopole moment and write the corresponding octopole term in the multipole expansion. 3. 53 Re-solve the problem of a conducting sphere in a uniform external electric field by the method of images, and verify that the result agrees with the solution previously obtained by separation of variables. 3. 54 In the infinite rectangular pipe, let the bottom (y=0 ) and the side walls (x= pm b ) be grounded, while the top (y=a ) is held at constant potential (V_0 ). Find the potential inside. Set up the separation in the style of Example 3. 4, include the (k=0 ) mode, and work out the full solution. 3.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 55 (a) A long square metal pipe of side (a ) is grounded on three sides, while the fourth side is insulated and kept at constant potential (V_0 ). Find the net charge per unit length induced on the opposite side. (b) Repeat the question for a circular pipe of radius (R ) split into four equal longitudinal conducting sections, three grounded and one held at (V_0 ); find the charge per unit length on the opposite quarter. 3. 56 An ideal electric dipole is fixed at the origin, pointing along the (z )-axis. A point charge is released from rest somewhere in the (xy )-plane. Show that its subsequent trajectory is a semicircle, like the motion of a pendulum whose support point is at the origin. 3. 57 A positive point charge (q ) of mass (m ) moves in a circular orbit of radius (s ) in the field of a fixed dipole ( p =p z ) at the origin.

chunks.text:
Determine the orientation of the orbital plane and compute the speed, angular momentum, and total energy of the orbiting charge. 3. 58 Find the surface charge density ( sigma ( theta ) ) on a sphere of radius (R ) that reproduces, for all exterior points, the same field as a point charge (q ) located on the (z )-axis at position (a<R ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 4. 1 A hydrogen atom, with Bohr radius about 0. 5~ AA , sits between two parallel metal plates separated by (1 , mm ) and connected to a (500 , V ) source. Estimate what fraction of the atomic radius the induced displacement (d ) represents, and then estimate the voltage required to ionize the atom with that same apparatus. Use the polarizability listed in Table 4. 1. 4. 2 For a hydrogen atom in its ground state, the electron cloud has charge density ( rho (r)=q( pi a^3)^ -1 e^ -2r/a ), where (q ) is the electron charge and (a ) is the Bohr radius. Compute the atomic polarizability. A suggested route is to first determine the field of the electron cloud and then expand for (r ll a ). 4. 3 Equation 4. 1 says that an induced dipole moment is proportional to the applied field. Test that rule for a model atom whose electron-cloud density grows in proportion to distance from the center up to radius (R ).

Find the attractive force between the charge and the atom. 4. 5 Two perfect dipoles, ( mathbf p_1 ) and ( mathbf p_2 ), are separated by distance (r ) in the geometry shown in Fig. ~4. 6. Find the torque on ( mathbf p_1 ) due to ( mathbf p_2 ), and the torque on ( mathbf p_2 ) due to ( mathbf p_1 ), each taken about that dipole's own center. 4. 6 A perfect dipole ( mathbf p ) is located a height (z ) above an infinite grounded conducting plane. The dipole makes angle ( theta ) with the normal to the plane. Find the torque on the dipole and determine the orientation of stable equilibrium if it is free to rotate. 4.

Use the dipole-energy formula together with the electric field of a dipole. 4. 9 A dipole ( mathbf p ) is located a distance (r ) from a point charge (q ), and ( mathbf p ) makes angle ( theta ) with the vector from (q ) to the dipole. Find the electric field inside the cylinder, and show that the exterior field can be written in the form given in the text in terms of (a ), (s ), and ( mathbf P ). 4. 14 Show directly from the formulas for ( sigma _b ) and ( rho _b ) that the total bound charge of any polarized neutral dielectric is zero. 4. 15 A dielectric spherical shell with inner radius (a ) and outer radius (b ) has frozen-in polarization ( mathbf P( mathbf r)=(k/r) mathbf r ). There is no free charge.

A small cavity is carved out while the polarization remains frozen. chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa

Explain why the two answers differ and discuss which one, if either, should be interpreted as the physical energy of the system. 4. 28 Two long coaxial metal cylinders stand vertically in dielectric oil of susceptibility ( chi _e ) and mass density ( rho ). The inner cylinder is held at potential (V ), and the outer cylinder is grounded. Determine how high the oil rises between the cylinders. 4. 29 Return to the two-dipole arrangement of Problem 4. 5.

Then find the total torque on ( mathbf p_2 ) about the center of ( mathbf p_1 ) and compare it with the torque on ( mathbf p_1 ) about that same point. 4. 30 An electric dipole directed along the (y )-axis is placed midway between two large conducting plates that make equal small angles ( theta ) with the (x )-axis and are held at potentials (+V ) and (-V ). A dipole ( mathbf p ) rides on the track and is constrained to remain tangent to the circle.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Use the dipole-force formula to show that the force is always forward along the track, and then explain why this does not create a perpetual-motion machine. 4. 32 Earnshaw's theorem forbids electrostatic trapping of a charged particle. Investigate whether a neutral but polarizable atom can be trapped instead. First show that ( mathbf F= frac 12 alpha nabla (E^2) ). Then show that (E^2 ) cannot have a local maximum in a charge-free region, so no stable electrostatic trap is possible. 4. 33 A dielectric cube of side (a ), centered at the origin, has frozen-in polarization ( mathbf P=k mathbf r ). Find all bound charges and verify that their total is zero. 4. 34 A parallel-plate capacitor is filled with dielectric whose dielectric constant varies linearly from (1 ) at the lower plate (x=0 ) to (2 ) at the upper plate (x=d ). The plates are connected to a battery of voltage (V ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Determine the complete bound-charge distribution and check that the net bound charge vanishes. 4. 35 A point charge (q ) is embedded at the center of a linear dielectric sphere of radius (R ) and susceptibility ( chi _e ). Find the electric field, the polarization, the bound volume charge density, and the bound surface charge density. Also determine the total bound charge on the surface and identify where the compensating negative bound charge is located. 4. 36 At the boundary between two linear dielectrics with no free surface charge, show that the field-line angles satisfy ( tan theta _2/ tan theta _1= epsilon _2/ epsilon _1 ). Then discuss whether a convex dielectric lens would tend to focus or defocus the electric field. 4. 37 A point dipole ( mathbf p ) is embedded at the center of a dielectric sphere of radius (R ) and dielectric constant ( epsilon _r ). Find the electrostatic potential both inside and outside the sphere. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 38 Prove the uniqueness theorem for a region containing a specified free-charge distribution together with linear dielectrics of known susceptibilities. Show that if the potential is specified on the boundary, then the potential everywhere inside is unique. Use the suggested integral involving ( nabla cdot (V_3 mathbf D_3) ). 4. 39 A conducting sphere at potential (V_0 ) is half buried in linear dielectric occupying the half-space (z<0 ). Test the claim that the potential is exactly the same as if the dielectric were absent. Do this by writing the candidate potential, finding the resulting field, polarization, bound charge, and free surface charge, showing that these reproduce the same potential, and then invoking the uniqueness theorem. Finally discuss whether the same idea works for the two modified geometries shown in Fig. ~4. 36. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 40 Use the force formula ( mathbf F= int ( mathbf P cdot nabla ) mathbf E_ ext ,d tau ) to find the force on a small dielectric sphere of radius (R ) and susceptibility ( chi _e ) located a distance (s ) from a thin wire carrying uniform line charge density ( lambda ). 4. 41 Relate microscopic polarizability ( alpha ) to macroscopic susceptibility ( chi _e ). Starting from the naive estimate (P=N alpha E ), account for the difference between the macroscopic field (E ) and the local field acting on a given atom, (E_ else ). Assuming each atom occupies a spherical cell of radius (R ), derive the Clausius-Mossotti formula connecting ( alpha ), (N ), and ( epsilon _r ). 4. 42 Check the Clausius-Mossotti relation numerically for the gases listed in Table 4. 1, using dielectric constants from Table 4. 2. Comment on why the correction term is hard to distinguish from the naive low-density approximation in gases. 4.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
43 Derive the Langevin theory for a polar dielectric. First, using the Boltzmann distribution for dipole orientations in an external field, obtain the polarization law (P=Np[ cosh (pE/kT)/ sinh (pE/kT)-kT/(pE)] ) and sketch (P/(Np) ) as a function of (pE/kT ). Then take the weak-field, high-temperature limit to extract the susceptibility, and compare the prediction with data for liquid water at (20^ circ C ) and for water vapor at (100^ circ C ) and (1 , atm ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 5. 1 A charged particle enters a region where the magnetic field is uniform and directed into the page. From the observed upward deflection shown in Fig. ~5. 8, determine the sign of the charge. Then express the particle's momentum in terms of (a ), (d ), (B ), and (q ). 5. 2 For the crossed-field setup of Example 5. 2, find and sketch the particle trajectory when the initial velocity is (a) ((E/B) mathbf y ), (b) ((E/2B) mathbf y ), and (c) ((E/B)( mathbf y + mathbf z ) ). 5. 3 Recreate J. ~J. ~Thomson's charge-to-mass measurement for cathode rays. First use crossed electric and magnetic fields adjusted to give zero deflection to determine the particle speed in terms of (E ) and (B ). Then switch off the electric field, measure the radius of curvature (R ) in the magnetic field alone, and use it to obtain (q/m ). 5.

Then show that the polygon result approaches the circular-loop field as (n to infty ). 5. 9 Compute the magnetic field at point (P ) for each of the steady-current geometries shown in Fig. ~5. 23. 5. 10 Find the net force on (a) the square current loop near an infinite straight wire and (b) the triangular loop shown in Fig. ~5. 24, assuming all currents have magnitude (I ). 5. 11 Using the axial field of a circular loop from Example 5. 6, find the magnetic field on the axis of a tightly wound solenoid of radius (a ) with (n ) turns per unit length carrying current (I ).

chunks.text: Follow the construction suggested in the text: set (A_x=0 ), solve for (A_y ) and (A_z ), verify by direct differentiation that ( nabla times mathbf A= mathbf F ), comment on whether your ( mathbf A ) is divergenceless, and test the construction on ( mathbf F = y , mathbf x +z , mathbf y +x , mathbf z ). 5. 32 Check the magnetic boundary conditions by verifying Eq. ~5. 76 for the setup in Example 5. 9 and Eqs. ~5. 77--5. 78 for the setup in Example 5. 11. 5. 33 Prove Eq. ~5. 78 from Eqs. ~5. 63, 5. 76, and 5. 77, using a local Cartesian coordinate system whose (z )-axis is normal to the surface and whose (x )-axis runs parallel to the surface current. 5. 34 Show that the magnetic field of a dipole can be written in coordinate-free form as ( mathbf B_ dip ( mathbf r)= mu _0 4 pi r^3 [3( mathbf m cdot mathbf r ) mathbf r - mathbf m] ). 5.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 35 A circular loop of radius (R ) lies in the (xy )-plane and carries current (I ) counterclockwise as viewed from (+z ). Find its magnetic dipole moment, the approximate far-field magnetic field, and show that the far-field expression agrees with the exact axial field when (z gg R ). 5. 36 Find the exact magnetic field a height (z ) above the center of a square loop of side (w ) carrying current (I ). Then show that the result reduces to the dipole field in the limit (z gg w ). 5. 37 (a) A circular record of radius (R ) carries uniform surface charge density ( sigma ) and rotates with angular speed ( omega ). Find its magnetic dipole moment. (b) Find the magnetic dipole moment of a spinning spherical shell and show that the exterior vector potential is exactly that of a perfect dipole. 5. 38 For a volume current distribution ( mathbf J ), write the multipole expansion of the vector potential analogous to the line-current expansion.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Show that the monopole term vanishes, and derive the magnetic dipole moment formula ( mathbf m = frac 12 int ( mathbf r times mathbf J) ,d tau ). 5. 39 Analyze the motion of a particle of charge (q ) and mass (m ) in the magnetic field of a long straight wire carrying current (I ). Determine whether the kinetic energy is conserved, write the force in cylindrical coordinates, derive the equations of motion, and describe the motion in the case ( dot z= ) constant. 5. 40 Parallel currents attract, so why does the current in a single wire not collapse onto the axis? Model a wire whose positive charges are fixed and whose negative charges move with speed (v ), with both charge densities independent of radius. Show that ( rho _-=- rho _+ gamma ^2 ), where ( gamma = 1/ 1-(v/c)^2 ), and determine where the compensating charge must reside if the wire as a whole is neutral. 5.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 41 A current (I ) flows through a rectangular conducting bar in a uniform magnetic field pointing out of the page. Analyze the Hall effect: determine the direction in which positive carriers are deflected, find the resulting Hall voltage in terms of (B ), the carrier speed (v ), and the bar dimensions, and explain how the analysis changes if the moving charge carriers are negative. 5. 42 A plane wire loop of arbitrary shape lies partly inside a region of uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the plane. Show that the net magnetic force is (F=IBw ), where (w ) is the subtended chord. Generalize the result to an irregular field region and determine the force direction. 5. 43 A circularly symmetric magnetic field, perpendicular to the page, occupies the shaded region in Fig. ~5. 58 and has zero net flux.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Show that a charged particle launched from the center emerges radially if it escapes, and that a particle sent inward from outside will reach the center if it has enough energy. Use angular momentum. 5. 44 Compute the magnetic force pulling together the northern and southern hemispheres of a spinning charged spherical shell. 5. 45 For a charged particle moving in the field of a hypothetical stationary magnetic monopole at the origin, derive the equation of motion, show that the speed is constant, prove that the quantity ( mathbf Q = m( mathbf r times mathbf v)-( mu _0 q_e q_m/4 pi ) mathbf r ) is conserved, show that the trajectory lies on a cone, determine the azimuthal evolution, obtain the differential equation for (r( phi ) ), and solve for the trajectory. 5.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 46 Use the Biot-Savart law, preferably in its surface-current form, to find the magnetic field both inside and outside an infinitely long solenoid of radius (R ), turn density (n ), and current (I ). 5. 47 Two identical circular loops can be arranged as a Helmholtz pair to produce a more uniform field than a single loop. Find the field (B(z) ) along the common axis for loops separated by distance (d ), show that the first derivative vanishes at the midpoint, choose (d ) so that the second derivative also vanishes there, and then find the field at the center. 5. 48 Use the axial loop field to derive the magnetic field on the axis of the rotating charged disk from Problem 5. 37(a), and show that the dipole approximation from the disk's magnetic moment is valid when (z gg R ). 5. 49 For a circular current loop, set up the Biot-Savart integrals for (B_x ), (B_y ), and (B_z ) at an off-axis point ((0,y,z) ), and evaluate (B_x ) explicitly.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 5. 50 Starting from the Biot-Savart law and the Lorentz force law for line currents, show that the force between two current loops can be rewritten in a form that makes Newton's third law manifest. 5. 51 For a plane current loop described in polar form by (r( theta ) ), show that the magnetic field at the origin is (B= mu _0 I 4 pi int d theta /r ). Check the result for a circular loop, use it for the lituus spiral closed by a straight segment, and then show that the field at the focus of a conic-section loop depends only on its semilatus rectum, not on its eccentricity. 5. 52 Use the analogy between ( mathbf A ) and ( mathbf B ) to derive a formula expressing ( mathbf A ) directly in terms of ( mathbf B ). Then derive the corresponding electrostatic formula expressing the scalar potential (V ) in terms of the electric field ( mathbf E ). 5.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 53 Test the naive proposal ( mathbf A( mathbf r)= int ( mathbf B times d mathbf l) ) as a relation between vector potential and magnetic field. Show its failure for uniform ( mathbf B ) and by evaluating it around a rectangular loop. Then use the alternative formulas given in the text to find ( mathbf A ) for uniform ( mathbf B ) and for an infinite straight wire, and comment on whether the result automatically satisfies ( nabla cdot mathbf A=0 ). 5. 54 Construct a magnetic scalar potential for (a) a pure magnetic dipole and (b) the spinning spherical shell of Example 5. 11. Then attempt the same construction for the interior field of a uniformly spinning solid sphere and explain what goes wrong. 5. 55 If ( nabla cdot mathbf A=0 ), one may write ( mathbf A= nabla times mathbf W ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Derive a general integral formula for ( mathbf W ) in terms of ( mathbf B ), then use it for a uniform magnetic field and for the interior and exterior of an infinite solenoid. 5. 56 Prove the magnetostatic uniqueness theorem: if ( mathbf J ) is specified throughout a volume and either ( mathbf A ) or ( mathbf B ) is specified on the boundary, then the magnetic field inside is uniquely determined. Follow the vector-identity hint provided in the text. 5. 57 A magnetic dipole ( mathbf m=-m_0 mathbf z ) sits at the origin in a uniform external field ( mathbf B=B_0 mathbf z ). Show that there is a spherical surface through which no field lines pass, determine its radius, and sketch the field lines inside and outside that sphere. 5. 58 A thin uniformly charged torus rotates about its symmetry axis. Find the gyromagnetic ratio, then use the same idea to get the gyromagnetic ratio of a uniformly spinning sphere.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Finally, using the spin angular momentum ( hbar /2 ), estimate the magnetic dipole moment of the electron. 5. 59 Prove that the average magnetic field over a sphere of radius (R ), due to steady currents inside the sphere, is ( mathbf B_ ave = ( mu _0/4 pi )(2 mathbf m/R^3) ), where ( mathbf m ) is the total dipole moment of the enclosed current. Then show that the average field due to currents outside the sphere equals the field they produce at the center. 5. 60 A uniformly charged solid sphere of radius (R ) and total charge (Q ) spins with angular velocity ( omega ). Find its magnetic dipole moment, the average magnetic field inside, the far-field vector potential, the exact exterior vector potential, and the exact interior magnetic field. Check that the limiting results are consistent. 5. 61 Average the dipole magnetic field over a sphere centered on the dipole and compare the result with the general averaging theorem.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
Explain the mismatch and determine the delta-function correction needed in the exact dipole field at the origin. 5. 62 A thin glass rod of radius (R ) and length (L ) carries uniform surface charge density ( sigma ) and spins about its axis with angular speed ( omega ). Using a stack-of-dipoles picture, find the magnetic field at points in the (xy )-plane a distance (s gg R ) from the axis.

This chapter contains 29 indexed problems.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 6. 1 A square current loop and a circular current loop are arranged as in Fig. ~6. 6, with their separation (r ) much larger than the loop dimensions (a ) and (b ). Find the torque on the square loop due to the circular loop, and determine the stable equilibrium orientation if the square loop can rotate freely. 6. 2 Starting from the Lorentz force law in its line-current form, show that the torque on any steady current distribution placed in a uniform magnetic field is ( mathbf N = mathbf m times mathbf B ). 6. 3 For the two magnetic dipoles arranged as in Fig. ~6. 7, separated by distance (r ), find their force of attraction in two ways: first from Eq. ~6. 2 and then from Eq. ~6. 3. 6. 4 Derive Eq. ~6. 3 for the force on a magnetic dipole in a nonuniform field. Follow the suggested route of treating the dipole as a tiny square loop, expanding ( mathbf B ) in a Taylor series, and summing the forces on the four sides. 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 5 A slab occupying (-a le x le a ) carries uniform current density ( mathbf J = J_0 mathbf z ). A magnetic dipole ( mathbf m = m_0 mathbf x ) is placed at the origin. Find the force on the dipole using Eq. ~6. 3, repeat the calculation for ( mathbf m = m_0 mathbf y ), and compare the expressions ( nabla ( mathbf p cdot mathbf E) ) and (( mathbf p cdot nabla ) mathbf E ) in electrostatics with their magnetic analogs by evaluating (( mathbf m cdot nabla ) mathbf B ) for the two cases. 6. 6 Classify the following materials as likely paramagnetic or diamagnetic: aluminum, copper, copper chloride, carbon, lead, nitrogen, salt, sodium, sulfur, and water. 6. 7 An infinitely long circular cylinder is uniformly magnetized parallel to its axis. Find the magnetic field produced by that magnetization inside and outside the cylinder. 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 8 A long cylinder of radius (R ) carries magnetization ( mathbf M = ks^2 phi ), where (k ) is constant and (s ) is the cylindrical radius. Find the magnetic field due to ( mathbf M ) for points inside and outside the cylinder. 6. 9 A short circular cylinder of radius (a ) and length (L ) carries a frozen uniform magnetization parallel to its axis. Find the bound currents and sketch the magnetic field for the cases (L gg a ), (L ll a ), and (L sim a ). Compare the result with the bar electret problem from Chapter 4. 6. 10 An iron rod with square cross section, uniformly magnetized along its length, is bent into an almost closed ring with a narrow gap of width (w ). Assuming (w ll a ll L ), find the magnetic field at the center of the gap. 6. 11 The derivation of bound-current formulas starts from perfect dipoles, whereas real matter contains physical dipoles. Show, by the method used in Section 4. 2.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 3, that the bound-current treatment nevertheless gives the correct macroscopic magnetic field. 6. 12 An infinitely long cylinder of radius (R ) has frozen magnetization ( mathbf M = ks , mathbf z ), with no free current anywhere. Find the magnetic field inside and outside by two methods: first by locating the bound currents explicitly, and second by finding ( mathbf H ) from Ampere's law and then recovering ( mathbf B ). 6. 13 In a large magnetized medium the field is ( mathbf B_0 ), with ( mathbf H_0 = mathbf B_0/ mu _0 - mathbf M ), where ( mathbf M ) is frozen in. A small cavity is carved out. Find the field and ( mathbf H ) at the center of (a) a spherical cavity, (b) a long needle-shaped cavity parallel to ( mathbf M ), and (c) a thin wafer-shaped cavity perpendicular to ( mathbf M ). Assume all background quantities are essentially uniform over the cavity. 6. 14 For the bar magnet of Problem 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 9 with (L approx 2a ), make careful sketches of ( mathbf M ), ( mathbf B ), and ( mathbf H ). 6. 15 When there is no free current, one may introduce a magnetic scalar potential (W ) through ( mathbf H = - nabla W ). Use this approach and separation of variables to rederive the field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere. 6. 16 A coaxial cable contains linear magnetic insulating material of susceptibility ( chi _m ) between two long cylindrical conductors. Current (I ) flows down the inner conductor and returns along the outer conductor, uniformly distributed over the surfaces. Find the magnetic field in the insulating region. Then compute the magnetization and bound currents and verify that, together with the free currents, they reproduce the same field. 6. 17 A long straight wire of radius (a ), made of linear magnetic material with susceptibility ( chi _m ), carries a uniformly distributed current (I ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Find the magnetic field as a function of distance (s ) from the axis, determine all bound currents, and compute the net bound current flowing along the wire. 6. 18 A sphere of linear magnetic material is placed in an otherwise uniform field ( mathbf B_0 ). Find the magnetic field inside the sphere. 6. 19 Using the simple classical model of Section 6. 1. 3, estimate the magnetic susceptibility of a diamagnetic metal such as copper. Compare the estimate with the measured value and comment on the discrepancy. 6. 20 Describe a practical procedure for demagnetizing a permanent magnet and returning it to a state with zero magnetization when the applied current is zero. 6. 21 Show that the energy of a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field is (U=- mathbf m cdot mathbf B ).

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Then derive the interaction energy of two magnetic dipoles, analyze the stable relative orientation of two freely rotating dipoles held a fixed distance apart, and predict the alignment of a row of compass needles mounted at equal intervals along a straight line. 6. 22 Re-derive the force on a magnetic dipole more elegantly than in Problem 6. 4 by Taylor-expanding ( mathbf B( mathbf r) ) about the dipole center and evaluating the resulting loop integral with the Levi-Civita symbol and the area identity from vector analysis. 6. 23 Toy ring magnets can float on a vertical rod. Treat the magnets as dipoles of mass (m_d ) and moment (m ). Find the equilibrium height of the upper magnet above a lower one placed back-to-back, and then determine the ratio of the two heights that occurs when a third magnet is added in the orientation shown. 6.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: 24 Two charged magnetic dipoles, each with electric charge (q ) and magnetic dipole moment (m ), are constrained to move on the (z )-axis. Electrical forces repel but magnetic dipole forces attract. Find the equilibrium separation, evaluate it for two electrons, and decide whether this implies a stable two-electron bound state. 6. 25 Use the dictionary ( mathbf D to mathbf B ), ( mathbf E to mathbf H ), ( mathbf P to mu _0 mathbf M ), and ( epsilon _0 to mu _0 ) to translate known electrostatic results into magnetostatics. Apply it to rederive (a) the field inside a uniformly magnetized sphere, (b) the field inside a sphere of linear magnetic material placed in a uniform field, and (c) the average magnetic field over a sphere due to steady internal currents. 6. 26 Notice that uniformly charged, uniformly polarized, and uniformly magnetized bodies involve the same basic geometric integral.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Use this observation to derive the scalar potential of a uniformly polarized sphere and the vector potential of a uniformly magnetized sphere from the corresponding uniformly charged-sphere result. 6. 27 At the boundary between two linear magnetic materials, the magnetic field lines bend. Show that, in the absence of free surface current, the angles satisfy ( tan theta _2/ tan theta _1 = mu _2/ mu _1 ). 6. 28 A magnetic dipole ( mathbf m ) is embedded at the center of a sphere of radius (R ) and permeability ( mu ). Show that the magnetic field inside the sphere has the form stated in the text, including the added uniform term, and then determine the field outside the sphere. 6. 29 You are asked to referee a proposal aimed at deciding whether the magnetization of iron is better modeled by Ampere dipoles or Gilbert dipoles.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text:
The plan is to probe the interior field of a uniformly magnetized iron cylinder by carving a small cavity and placing a tiny compass needle inside. Decide whether such an experiment should be funded, and if so what cavity shape would be appropriate; if not, explain the flaw in the proposal.

This chapter contains 64 indexed problems.

7. 1 Two concentric conducting spherical shells of radii (a ) and (b ) are separated by weakly conducting material of conductivity ( sigma ). Find the current for an applied potential difference (V ), determine the resistance between the shells, and explain why the answer becomes independent of (b ) when (b gg a ). Then use that observation to find the current between two widely separated metal spheres of radius (a ) immersed in seawater and held at potential difference (V ). 7. 2 A capacitor (C ), initially charged to voltage (V_0 ), is connected at (t=0 ) to a resistor (R ). Find (Q(t) ) and (I(t) ), and verify that the heat dissipated in the resistor equals the loss of electrostatic energy.

chunks.title: griffiths problem index aa
chunks.text: Then analyze the reverse process in which the capacitor and resistor are connected to a battery of emf (V_0 ): determine (Q(t) ), (I(t) ), the total battery work, the heat produced, the final stored energy, and the fraction of the battery's work that remains in the capacitor. 7. 3 Two conductors are embedded in a weakly conducting medium of conductivity ( sigma ). Show that the resistance (R ) between them and the capacitance (C ) of the same geometry satisfy (R= epsilon _0/( sigma C) ). Then suppose the conductors are first charged to a voltage (V_0 ) and disconnected from the battery; show that the voltage decays exponentially, and find the time constant in terms of ( epsilon _0 ) and ( sigma ). 7. 4 In the cylindrical resistor problem of Example 7. 2, replace the constant conductivity by ( sigma (s)=k/s ).

Find the emf around the loop and, if its total resistance is (R ), the current. Explain why the result does not lead to any perpetual-motion device. 7. 7 A metal bar of mass (m ) slides without friction on parallel conducting rails separated by distance (l ), with a resistor (R ) closing the circuit and a uniform magnetic field (B ) directed into the page.

Find the magnetic flux through the loop. Then determine the induced emf and current direction if the loop is pulled directly away from the wire at speed (v ), and repeat for the case in which the loop is translated parallel to the wire. 7. 9 Explain why the magnetic flux through a wire loop is well defined even though infinitely many different surfaces can share the same boundary curve. 7. 10 A square loop of side (a ) rotates with angular speed ( omega ) about a vertical shaft in a uniform magnetic field (B ).

Determine the terminal speed, the full velocity (v(t) ), and the time needed to reach (90 % ) of terminal speed. Then explain what changes if a narrow slit is cut in the loop so the circuit is broken. 7. 12 A long solenoid of radius (a ) produces a sinusoidal interior field (B(t)=B_0 cos ( omega t) , mathbf z ). A coaxial circular wire loop of radius (a/2 ) and resistance (R ) is placed inside the solenoid. In that region the magnetic field is ( mathbf B(y,t)=ky^3 t^2 , mathbf z ).

In the quasistatic approximation, find the induced electric field both inside and outside the solenoid, including its direction. 7. 16 An alternating current (I(t)=I_0 cos ( omega t) ) flows down a long straight wire and returns along a coaxial conducting tube of radius (a ). The magnetic field is then turned off. In what direction does the charge begin to move? 7. 22 A small circular loop of radius (a ) is held a distance (z ) above the center of a larger circular loop of radius (b ), with parallel planes and a common axis.

(1. 21) if necessary] to prove the following: (a) Any excess charge placed on a conductor must lie entirely on its surface.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: (A conductor by definition contains charges capable of moving freely under the action of applied electric fields. ) (b) A closed, hollow conductor shields its interior from fields due to charges outside, but does not shield its exterior from the fields due to charges placed inside it. (c) The electric field at the surface of a conductor is normal to the surface and has a magnitude σ/ε0, where σ is the charge density per unit area on the surface. 1. 2 Solution Physical principles A conductor contains free charges. If an electric field existed inside the conducting material, these charges would feel a force F= qE, and they would move. But in electrostatic equilibrium no macroscopic motion of charge occurs. Therefore the electric field inside the conductor must vanish: E = 0 inside the conducting material. This immediately implies that the potential is constant throughout the conductor.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Indeed, using V(b)−V(a) =− b a E·dl, if E = 0 everywhere inside the conductor, then V(b)−V(a) = 0. Hence V = constant throughout the conductor. E·da = 0. Qenc (a) Excess charge resides on the surface Take any closed Gaussian surface S lying entirely within the conducting material. Since the conductor is in electro- static equilibrium, E = 0 everywhere on S. Therefore the flux through S is S By Gauss’s law, S E·da = , ε0 so Qenc = 0 ⇒ Qenc = 0. ε0 Thus any closed surface taken entirely inside the conductor encloses zero net charge. Suppose, for contradiction, that some excess charge were located somewhere in the interior of the conductor. Then one could draw a Gaussian surface entirely inside the conductor that encloses that excess charge. But Gauss’s law would then require Qenc ̸= 0, contradicting the result above. Therefore no excess charge can remain in the bulk of the conductor.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Hence Any excess charge placed on a conductor lies entirely on its surface. 4 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (b) Shielding properties of a closed hollow conductor First consider a closed hollow conductor with an empty cavity and charges outside the conductor. Since E = 0 in the conducting material, the conductor is at constant potential V0. In particular, the inner surface of the cavity is also at potential V0. Inside the empty cavity, ρ= 0, so the potential satisfies Laplace’s equation, ∇2V = 0. The boundary condition on the cavity wall is V= V0 = constant. A constant function V(r) = V0 satisfies Laplace’s equation and the boundary condition, and by uniqueness this is the only solution. Therefore E= −∇V = 0 inside the cavity. So the cavity is shielded from outside fields: E = 0 inside the empty cavity. Now place a charge q inside the cavity.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Draw a closed Gaussian surface lying entirely in the conducting material and surrounding the cavity. Since it lies in the conductor, E = 0, so Gauss’s law gives Qenc = 0. The enclosed charge consists of the point charge q and the induced charge Qinner on the inner surface, so q+ Qinner = 0, which implies Qinner =−q. If the conductor as a whole is initially neutral, then the outer surface must carry charge Qouter = +q. This nonzero outer charge produces an electric field outside the conductor. Therefore the exterior is not shielded from charges inside the cavity. (c) Field at the surface of a conductor Suppose there were a tangential component of the electric field at the conductor surface: E∦= 0. Then a charge on the surface would feel a tangential force F∥= qE∥, so free charges would move along the surface. But in electrostatic equilibrium this cannot happen. Hence E∥= 0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Thus the electric field at the surface must be normal to the surface. To find its magnitude, take a very small cylindrical pillbox Gaussian surface straddling the conductor surface, with top face area A just outside and bottom face area A just inside. Since the field inside the conductor is zero, Φbottom = 0. The side contribution vanishes in the infinitesimal limit, and the flux through the top face is Φtop = Eout ⊥ A. 5 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Hence The enclosed charge is the surface charge in the pillbox: E·da = Eout ⊥ A. The absolute errors relative to the exact continuum values are Jacobi: (0. 004285, 0. 004680, 0. 006770), Gauss–Seidel: (0. 005051, 0. 006594, 0. 007306). Therefore Gauss–Seidel converges much faster to the discrete improved-grid solution, reaching near-stationarity after about five or six sweeps, whereas Jacobi converges more slowly. The limiting discrete solution is not exactly equal to the quoted exact continuum solution because the finite mesh still introduces discretization error. 7 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Problem 1. 2 3. 1 Problem Statement The Dirac delta function in three dimensions can be taken as the improper limit as α→0 of the Gaussian function D(α; x,y,z) = (2π)−3/2α−3 exp− 1 2α2 (x2 + y2 + z2).

chunks.text: Consider a general orthogonal coordinate system specified by the surfaces u = constant, v = constant, w = constant, with length elements du/U, dv/V, dw/W in the three perpendicular directions. Show that δ(x−x′) = δ(u−u′) δ(v−v′) δ(w−w′) UVW by considering the limit of the Gaussian above. Note that as α →0 only the infinitesimal length element need be used for the distance between the points in the exponent. 3. 2 Solution We begin from the standard Gaussian representation of the three-dimensional delta function: δ(x−x′) = lim α→0(2π)−3/2α−3 exp− |x−x′|2 2α2. We now express this in a general orthogonal curvilinear coordinate system (u,v,w). In such a system the infinitesimal line element is ds2 = du U 2 + dv V 2 + dw W 2 Hence, for two nearby points (u,v,w) and (u′,v′,w′), we may write . |x−x′|2 ≈(u−u′)2 U2 + (v−v′)2 V2 + (w−w′)2 W2 , where U,V,W are evaluated at the point x′(or equivalently at x in the limit α→0).

Problem 1. 3 5. 1 Problem Statement Using Dirac delta functions in the appropriate coordinates, express the following charge distributions as three- dimensional charge densities ρ(x). (a) In spherical coordinates, a charge Q uniformly distributed over a spherical shell of radius R. (b) In cylindrical coordinates, a charge λper unit length uniformly distributed over a cylindrical surface of radius b. (c) In cylindrical coordinates, a charge Q spread uniformly over a flat circular disc of negligible thickness and radius R. (d) The same as part (c), but using spherical coordinates. 5.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 2 Solution The general idea is that a singular charge distribution concentrated on a surface or line can be represented in three dimensions by inserting Dirac delta functions that localize the charge in the appropriate coordinate, and then choosing the coefficient so that the integral of ρ over all space gives the correct total charge. We will use the volume elements and dτ= r2 sin θdrdθdϕ (spherical), dτ= sdsdϕdz (cylindrical), where I use s for the cylindrical radial coordinate. ∞ We also use the basic property of the delta function: δ(x−a)f(x) dx= f(a). −∞ 13 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (a) Spherical shell of radius R carrying total charge Q The charge is uniformly distributed over the spherical surface r= R. So the density must vanish everywhere except at r= R, which means it must contain a factor δ(r−R). ρ(x) = Aδ(r−R), Let where A is to be determined. Since the total charge must be Q, Q= ρ(x) dτ.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Substitute the trial form: Q= Aδ(r−R) r2 sin θdrdθdϕ. Separate the integrals: Q= A ∞ 0 δ(r−R)r2 dr π 0 sin θdθ 2π 0 Now, ∞ 0 δ(r−R)r2 dr= R2 π 0 , 0 sin θdθ= 2, 2π dϕ= 2π. Therefore Q= A(R2)(2)(2π) = 4πAR2 . Hence A= Q 4πR2. dϕ . So the required charge density is ρ(x) = Q 4πR2 δ(r−R). This is exactly what we expect physically: the factor Q/(4πR2) is the uniform surface charge density on the sphere, and the delta function localizes it to the surface. (b) Cylindrical surface of radius b carrying charge λ per unit length Now the charge is uniformly distributed on the cylindrical surface s= b, with charge per unit length equal to λ. Because the distribution extends indefinitely along z, the total charge is infinite, so the correct normalization condition is that the charge in a length L of the cylinder must be QL = λL. Let ρ(x) = Aδ(s−b). Then the charge contained in a segment of length L is QL = L 0 2π 0 ∞ 0 Aδ(s−b) sdsdϕdz.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Separate the integrals: QL = A ∞ 0 δ(s−b)sds 2π 0 dϕ L 0 dz . 14 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions λ 2πb. These give ∞ 0 δ(s−b)sds= b, 2π 0 dϕ= 2π, L 0 dz= L. Hence QL = A(b)(2π)(L) = 2πbAL. But we require QL = λL. Therefore 2πbAL= λL ⇒ A= Thus ρ(x) = λ 2πbδ(s−b). The coefficient λ/(2πb) is precisely the uniform surface charge density on the cylindrical surface. (c) Flat circular disc of radius R in cylindrical coordinates The disc lies in the plane z= 0, and occupies the region 0 ≤s≤R. The charge is spread uniformly over the disc, so the surface charge density is σ= Q πR2. Since the disc has negligible thickness, the three-dimensional density must be concentrated at z = 0, so it must contain a factor δ(z). Also, the charge should exist only for s ≤R, so we multiply by a step-function restriction. Using the Heaviside function Θ(R−s), we write ρ(x) = σδ(z) Θ(R−s). Substituting σ, ρ(x) = Q πR2 δ(z) Θ(R−s).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Let us verify the normalization: ρ(x) dτ= ∞ −∞ 2π 0 ∞ 0 Q πR2 δ(z) Θ(R−s) sdsdϕdz. Because of Θ(R−s), the radial integral runs only from 0 to R: = Q πR2 ∞ −∞ δ(z) dz 2π 0 dϕ R 0 sds . Now ∞ −∞ δ(z) dz= 1, 2π 0 dϕ= 2π, R 0 R2 sds= Therefore ρ(x) dτ= Q πR2 (1)(2π) R2 2 = Q. So the normalization is correct. Hence the charge density is 2. ρ(x) = Q πR2 δ(z) Θ(R−s). 15 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (d) The same disc, but written in spherical coordinates Now we describe the same flat disc using spherical coordinates. The disc still lies in the plane z= 0. In spherical coordinates, z= rcos θ. So localization to the plane requires δ(z) = δ(rcos θ). Also, the condition that the point lie inside the radius-R disc is s≤R. But in spherical coordinates, s= rsin θ. Hence the restriction becomes rsin θ≤R, which can be written with a step function as Θ(R−rsin θ).

chunks.text: Therefore a natural spherical-coordinate representation is ρ(x) = Q πR2 δ(rcos θ) Θ(R−rsin θ). So one acceptable answer is ρ(x) = Q πR2 δ(rcos θ) Θ(R−rsin θ). We can also rewrite this in a more explicit form using the identity δ(f(θ)) = δ(θ−θi) |f′(θi)|. i For f(θ) = rcos θ, the zero in the interval 0 ≤θ≤π is at θ= π 2 , and d dθ(rcos θ) θ=π/2 =−rsin π =−r. 2 Hence δ(rcos θ) = 1 r δ θ− π 2. Problem 1. 22 6. 1 Problem Statement Two-dimensional relaxation calculations commonly use sites on a square lattice with spacing ∆x = ∆y= h, and label the sites by (i,j), where i,j are integers and xi = ih+ x0, yj= jh+ y0. The value of the potential at (i,j) can be approximated by the average of the values at neighboring sites. [Recall the relevant theorem about harmonic functions. ] But what average?

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: (a) If F(x,y) is a well-behaved function in the neighborhood of the origin, but not necessarily harmonic, by explicit Taylor series expansions, show that the “cross” sum Sc = F(h,0) + F(0,h) + F(−h,0) + F(0,−h) can be expressed as Sc = 4F(0,0) + h2∇2F + h4 12 (Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + O(h6). (b) Similarly, show that the “square” sum, Ss = F(h,h) + F(−h,h) + F(−h,−h) + F(h,−h) can be expressed as Ss = 4F(0,0) + 2h2∇2F− h4 3 (Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + h4Fxxyy + O(h6), or equivalently, Ss = 4F(0,0) + 2h2∇2F− h4 3 (Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + h4 2 ∇2(∇2F) + O(h6). 17 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Here Fxxxx is the fourth partial derivative of F with respect to x, evaluated at x= 0, y= 0, etc. If ∇2F = 0, the averages Sc/4 and Ss/4 each give the value of F(0,0), correct to order h3 inclusive. Note that an improvement can be obtained by forming the “improved” average, ⟨F(0,0)⟩= 1 5 Sc + 1 4Ss , where ⟨F(0,0)⟩= F(0,0) + 3 10 h4 h2∇2F + 40∇2(∇2F) + O(h6).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: If ∇2F = 0, then ⟨F(0,0)⟩gives F(0,0), correct to order h5 inclusive. For Poisson’s equation, the charge density and its lowest order Laplacian can be inserted for the same accuracy. 6. 2 Solution We expand F(x,y) in a Taylor series about the origin. Since all evaluations are made at points whose coordinates are ±h or 0, it is convenient to write the full two-variable Taylor expansion up to fourth order: 1 F(x,y) = F0 + xFx + yFy + 2 x2Fxx + 2xyFxy + y2Fyy + 1 6 x3Fxxx + 3x2yFxxy + 3xy2Fxyy + y3Fyyy 1 + 24 x4Fxxxx + 4x3yFxxxy + 6x2y2Fxxyy + 4xy3Fxyyy + y4Fyyyy + O(h5), where all derivatives are evaluated at (0,0), and we write F0 ≡F(0,0). Because the sums in the problem involve symmetrically placed points, all odd powers will cancel. That is the key reason these averaging schemes work well.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: (a) The cross sum Sc We first expand the four terms: F(h,0) = F0 + hFx + h2 2 Fxx + h3 6 Fxxx + h4 24 Fxxxx + O(h5), F(−h,0) = F0−hFx + h2 2 Fxx− h3 6 Fxxx + h4 24 Fxxxx + O(h5), F(0,h) = F0 + hFy + h2 2 Fyy + h3 6 Fyyy + h4 24 Fyyyy + O(h5), F(0,−h) = F0−hFy + h2 2 Fyy− h3 6 Fyyy + h4 24 Fyyyy + O(h5). Sc = 4F0 + h2(Fxx + Fyy) + h4 12(Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + O(h6). ∇2F= Fxx + Fyy, 6 So we are left with Since we obtain Sc = 4F(0,0) + h2∇2F + h4 12(Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + O(h6). That proves part (a). 18 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (b) The square sum Ss Now consider the four corner points: We expand each term to fourth order. For (h,h): For (−h,h): For (−h,−h): For (h,−h): Ss = F(h,h) + F(−h,h) + F(−h,−h) + F(h,−h).

First order: All linear terms cancel by symmetry. Second order: The Fxy terms cancel, while the Fxx and Fyy terms add: 4· h2 2 Fxx = 2h2Fxx, 4· h2 2 Fyy = 2h2Fyy. Thus the second-order contribution is 2h2(Fxx + Fyy) = 2h2∇2F. Third order: All cubic terms cancel by symmetry.
