So one form is Ss = 4F(0,0) + 2h2∇2F + h4 6 (Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + h4Fxxyy + O(h6). Now we rewrite it in the form stated in the problem. Observe that ∇2(∇2F) = (Fxx + Fyy)xx + (Fxx + Fyy)yy = Fxxxx + 2Fxxyy + Fyyyy. Therefore Fxxyy = 1 2 ∇2(∇2F)−Fxxxx−Fyyyy. Hence Ss = 4F(0,0) + 2h2∇2F− This is exactly the required form. Thus both Sc/4 and Ss/4 reproduce F(0,0) with an error of order h4, so they are correct through order h3 inclusive. 20 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Improved average Now define ⟨F(0,0)⟩= 1 5 Sc + 1 4Ss. and Insert the expansions for Sc and Ss. First, Add them: Sc = 4F0 + h2∇2F + h4 12(Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + O(h6), 1 4Ss = F0 + h2 2 ∇2F− h4 12(Fxxxx + Fyyyy) + h4 8 ∇2(∇2F) + O(h6). Sc + 1 4Ss = 5F0 + 3 2 h2∇2F + h4 8 ∇2(∇2F) + O(h6), h4 12(Fxxxx + Fyyyy) h4 12(Fxxxx + Fyyyy) because the fourth-derivative terms and cancel exactly. Now divide by 5: − ⟨F(0,0)⟩= F(0,0) + 3 h2∇2F + 10 h4 40∇2(∇2F) + O(h6). This is the desired improved formula.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: If F is harmonic, ∇2F = 0, and then ∇2(∇2F) = 0 as well, so ⟨F(0,0)⟩= F(0,0) + O(h6). Therefore the improved average gives F(0,0) correct through order h5 inclusive. Remark for Poisson’s equation If instead F satisfies Poisson’s equation, ∇2F = known source, then the improved average remains useful because the h2∇2F term and, if needed, the h4∇2(∇2F) term can be replaced directly by the source and its Laplacian. That is why this combination is especially effective in relaxation methods for electrostatics: it improves the local approximation while still using only nearby lattice values. Problem 1. 4 7. 1 Problem Statement Each of three charged spheres of radius a, one conducting, one having a uniform charge density within its volume, and one having a spherically symmetric charge density that varies radially as rn (n>−3), has a total charge Q. Use Gauss’s theorem to obtain the electric fields both inside and outside each sphere.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Sketch the behavior of the fields as a function of radius for the first two spheres, and for the third with n=−2, +2. 21 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 7. 2 Solution Because all three charge distributions are spherically symmetric, the electric field must have the form E(r) = E(r) ˆ r. That is, the field is purely radial and its magnitude depends only on the distance r from the center. For any spherical Gaussian surface of radius r, Gauss’s law gives Qenc(r) E·da = . ε0 Since E is radial and has constant magnitude on a sphere of radius r, E·da = E(r) da= E(r) 4πr2 . Therefore E(r) 4πr2 = Qenc(r) ε0 , so in general and E(r) = 1 4πε0 Qenc(r) r2 ˆ r. Thus the problem reduces to finding the enclosed charge Qenc(r) in each case. (1) Conducting sphere of radius a with total charge Q For a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium, all excess charge resides on the surface.

chunks.text: Therefore: • for r<a, no charge is enclosed; • for r>a, the full charge Q is enclosed. So for r<a, hence For r>a, E(r) = 1 4πε0 Qenc(r) r2 Qenc(r) = 0, E(r) = 0, r<a. Qenc(r) = Q, so E(r) = 1 4πε0 Q r2 ˆ r, r>a. At the surface, the field jumps from 0 just inside to E(a+) = 1 4πε0 Q a2 just outside. Therefore the complete result is E(r) =      0, 0 ≤r<a, 1 4πε0 Q r2 ˆ r, r>a. Behavior of E(r): Inside the conductor, the field is identically zero. At r = a there is a discontinuous jump to Q/(4πε0a2), and for r>a the field falls as 1/r2 . 22 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (2) Sphere of radius a with uniform volume charge density and total charge Q Now the charge is spread uniformly throughout the volume. The constant volume charge density is ρ= Q 4 3 πa3 = 3Q 4πa3. Inside the sphere: r<a The charge enclosed by a Gaussian sphere of radius r is Qenc(r) = ρ 4 3 πr3 Substitute ρ: Qenc(r) = 3Q 4πa3· 4 πr3 3 . = Qr3 a3.

Therefore E(r) =        1 4πε0 1 4πε0 Q a3 rˆ r, 0 ≤r<a, Q ˆ r, r>a. r2 Q Q a2 , E(a+) = 1 4πε0 a2 , At the surface, E(a−) = 1 4πε0 so the field is continuous at r= a. Behavior of E(r): its maximum value at the surface, The field starts from zero at the center and rises linearly with r inside the sphere. It reaches E(a) = 1 4πε0 Q a2 , and then decreases as 1/r2 outside. (3) Sphere of radius a with ρ(r) = krn , n>−3, total charge Q Now the charge density is spherically symmetric and given by ρ(r) = krn, n>−3. The restriction n>−3 is needed so that the total charge near r= 0 remains finite. We first determine the constant k from the condition that the total charge is Q.

Therefore, for r<a, E(r) = 1 4πε0 Qrn+3/an+3 r2 = 1 4πε0 Q an+3 rn+1 . . Hence E(r) = 1 4πε0 Q an+3 rn+1ˆ r, r<a. chunks.text: 1 Problem Statement A two-dimensional potential problem consists of a unit square area 0 ≤x≤1, 0 ≤y≤1 bounded by “surfaces” held at zero potential. Over the entire square there is a uniform charge density of unit strength (per unit length in z). (a) Apply the variational principle (1. 63) for the Poisson equation with the “variational” trial function Ψ(x,y) = Ax(1−x) y(1−y) to determine the best value of the constant A. [I use quotation marks around variational because there are no parameters to vary except the overall scale. ] (b) The exact (albeit series) solution for this problem is [see Problems 2. 15 and 2. 16] 4πε0Φ(x,y) = 16 π3 ∞ m=0 sin[(2m+ 1)πx] (2m+ 1)3 1− cosh[(2m+ 1)π(y− 1 2 )] cosh[(2m+ 1)π/2]. The trial function Ψ(x,y) = Ax(1−x) y(1−y) already satisfies all boundary conditions, because it vanishes whenever x= 0, x= 1, y= 0, or y= 1. So the problem is to determine the best value of the single parameter A by means of the variational principle. 26 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Part (a): Variational approximation For Poisson’s equation, ∇2Φ =− ρ ε0 , with Dirichlet boundary conditions, the appropriate functional is I[Ψ] = ε0 2 (∇Ψ)2 −ρΨ dxdy. The physical solution makes this functional stationary, and in the present one-parameter trial family we determine A by minimizing I(A). Since ρ= 1, we have I(A) = 1 0 1 0 ε0 2 (∇Ψ)2 −Ψ dxdy. We now compute the gradient of Ψ(x,y) = Ax(1−x) y(1−y).

Problem 1. 5 9. 1 Problem Statement The time-averaged potential of a neutral hydrogen atom is given by Φ(r) = q e−αr 4πε0 r 1 + αr 2 , where q is the magnitude of the electronic charge, and α−1 = a0/2, a0 being the Bohr radius.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Find the distribution of charge (both continuous and discrete) that will give this potential and interpret your result physically. 9. 2 Solution We are given a spherically symmetric potential, Φ(r) = q 4πε0 e−αr r 1 + αr 2. Since the potential depends only on r, the corresponding charge distribution must also be spherically symmetric. The relation between potential and charge density is Poisson’s equation: ∇2Φ =− ρ ε0 . Therefore our task is to compute ∇2Φ, identify any delta-function piece at the origin, and then read off the continuous and discrete parts of the charge distribution. 30 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Step 1: Separate the potential into simpler pieces Write Φ(r) = q 4πε0 e−αr α + r 2 e−αr So it is enough to know the Laplacian of the two functions e−αr and e−αr . . r For a spherically symmetric function f(r), ∇2f= 1 r2 d dr r2 df dr , r̸= 0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: However, for functions behaving like 1/r near the origin, one must also include the distributional identity ∇2 1 r =−4πδ3(r). More generally, ∇2 e−αr r = α2 e−αr r−4πδ3(r) which is the Yukawa-potential identity. We will also compute Step 2: Compute ∇2(e−αr) Let Then Hence Differentiate: Using the product rule, so Thus Divide by r2: Therefore ∇2(e−αr). f(r) = e−αr . df dr =−αe−αr . r2 df dr =−αr2e−αr . d dr r2 df dr d = dr−αr2e−αr . d dr r2e−αr = 2re−αr −αr2e−αr , d dr−αr2e−αr =−α 2re−αr −αr2e−αr . d dr r2 df dr =−2αre−αr + α2r2e−αr . 2α ∇2(e−αr) =− e−αr + α2e−αr, r̸= 0. r ∇2(e−αr) = α2e−αr − 2α r e−αr (r̸= 0). 31 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Step 3: Compute the Laplacian of the full potential Now so Φ(r) = q 4πε0 e−αr α + r 2 e−αr , ∇2Φ = q 4πε0 ∇2 e−αr r + α 2 ∇2(e−αr). Substitute the identities above: ∇2Φ = q 4πε0 α2 e−αr r−4πδ3(r) + α 2 α2e−αr − 2α r e−αr .

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Distribute the factor α/2: e−αr The two 1/r terms cancel: So the result simplifies drastically: Therefore, from Poisson’s equation, we obtain Cancel ε0: Distribute the minus sign: Hence the charge distribution is ∇2Φ = q 4πε0 α2 e−αr r−4πδ3(r) + α3 2 α2 e−αr r−α2 e−αr r = 0. ∇2Φ = q 4πε0 −4πδ3(r) + α3 2 e−αr . ρ(r) =−ε0∇2Φ, q ρ(r) =−ε0 4πε0 −4πδ3(r) + α3 2 e−αr q ρ(r) =− 4π−4πδ3(r) + α3 2 e−αr . ρ(r) = qδ3(r)− qα3 8π e−αr . −α2 e−αr r . . ρ(r) = qδ3(r)− qα3 8π e−αr . This already displays the answer in the required form: there is a discrete point charge at the origin, and a continuous negative charge cloud surrounding it. Step 4: Identify the discrete and continuous parts The discrete part is This is a point charge +q at the origin, which is the proton. The continuous part is ρdisc(r) = qδ3(r). ρcont(r) =− qα3 8π e−αr . This is the spherically symmetric electronic charge cloud.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 32 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Step 5: Check that the total charge is zero Because the hydrogen atom is neutral, the total charge should vanish. Let us verify that. The total discrete charge is simply Qdisc = q. The total continuous charge is Qcont = ρcont dτ= ∞ 0 − qα3 8π e−αr 4πr2 dr. Thus Qcont =− qα3 2 ∞ 0 r2e−αrdr. Now use the standard integral ∞ 0 r2e−αrdr= 2 α3. Therefore Qcont =− qα3 2· 2 α3 =−q. So Qtot = Qdisc + Qcont = q−q= 0, as required. Step 6: Relation to the hydrogen ground-state charge cloud Since α−1 a0 = 2 , so α= the continuous charge density becomes ρcont(r) =− q 8π 2 a0 3 e−2r/a0 =− q πa3 0 e−2r/a0 . 2 , a0 ρ(r) = qδ3(r)− q πa3 0 e−2r/a0 . Hence This is exactly the familiar charge distribution for the time-averaged hydrogen atom in the ground state: a point proton at the origin plus the smeared-out electron charge density ρe(r) =− q πa3 0 e−2r/a0 .

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Physical interpretation The potential is not simply the Coulomb potential of a point charge because the atom is not a bare proton. Instead, it consists of: • a positive point charge +q at the origin, representing the proton; • a negative continuous charge cloud whose density decreases exponentially with r, representing the electron probability cloud in the 1s state. Near the origin, the potential behaves approximately like Φ(r)∼ q 4πε0 1 , because very close to the origin the proton dominates. Far from the atom, e−αr →0, so the potential falls off exponentially to zero rather than as 1/r. This reflects the fact that the total charge is zero: at large distances the positive nuclear charge is screened by the negative electron cloud.

chunks.text: 33 r Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Therefore the given potential corresponds physically to a neutral hydrogen atom whose time-averaged charge distribution is qα3 ρ(r) = qδ3(r)− e−αr 8π or, using α= 2/a0, ρ(r) = qδ3(r)− q πa3 0 e−2r/a0 . Problem 1. 20 10. 1 Problem Statement In estimating the capacitance of a given configuration of conductors, comparison with known capacitances is often helpful. Consider two configurations of n conductors in which the (n−1) conductors held at zero potential are the same, but the one conductor whose capacitance we wish to know is different. In particular, let the conductor in one configuration have a closed surface S1 and in the other configu- ration have surface S′ 1, with S′ 1 totally inside S1. (a) Use the extremum principle of Section 1. 12 and the variational principle of Problem 1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 17 to prove that the capacitance C′ of the conductor with surface S′ 1 is less than or equal to the capacitance C of the conductor with surface S1 that encloses S′ 1. (b) Set upper and lower limits for the capacitance of a conducting cube of side a. Compare your limits and also their average with the numerical value, C ≃0. 655(4πε0a). (c) By how much do you estimate the capacitance per unit length of the two-wire system of Problem 1. 7 will change (larger? smaller? ) if one of the wires is replaced by a wire of square cross section whose side is equal to its diameter? 10. 2 Solution We consider electrostatics in vacuum. If one conductor is held at potential V and all other conductors are grounded, then the capacitance is defined by C= Q V , where Q is the total charge on the conductor at potential V. It is often convenient to choose V = 1. Then the capacitance is numerically equal to the total charge, C= Q.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: There is also a very useful energy formula. The electrostatic energy is ε0 U= 2 all space |E|2 dτ. Since E= −∇Φ, this becomes ε0 U= 2 |∇Φ|2 dτ. For a capacitor with one conductor at potential V and the others at 0, U= 1 2CV2 . If we choose V = 1, then C= ε0 |∇Φ|2 dτ. So when the conductor at nonzero potential is fixed at 1, the capacitance is the minimum value of the functional I[Φ] = ε0 |∇Φ|2 dτ among all trial functions Φ satisfying the required boundary values. This is the variational principle we need. 34 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (a) Proof that C′≤C when S′ 1 lies inside S1 Let configuration A be the one with conductor surface S1, and let its exact potential be Φ(r). Let configuration B be the one with conductor surface S′ 1, and let its exact potential be Φ′(r). In both cases all the other conductors are the same and are grounded.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: We set the conductor of interest at unit potential: Φ = 1 on S1, Φ′= 1 on S′ 1, and Φ = Φ′= 0 on all the other conductor surfaces. Then C= ε0 |∇Φ|2 dτ, C′ = ε0 |∇Φ′|2 dτ. We want to show that C′≤C. Step 1: Use the potential of the larger conductor as a trial function for the smaller one. Because S′ 1 lies wholly inside S1, the region exterior to S1 is contained in the region exterior to S′ 1. The exact potential Φ for the larger conductor satisfies Φ = 1 on S1, Φ = 0 on the grounded conductors. Now consider the same function Φ in the larger region exterior to S′ 1. Since S′ 1 lies inside S1, the surface S′ 1 is not in general an equipotential of Φ. So Φ cannot be used directly as an admissible trial function for configuration B. However, by the extremum principle of electrostatics, in a charge-free region bounded by conductors, the exact potential takes values only between the extreme boundary values.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Since the boundary values here are 0 and 1, 0 ≤Φ(r) ≤1 everywhere in the field region. In particular, on the inner surface S′ 1, 0 ≤Φ ≤1. Now define a modified trial function Φ(r) = 1, r inside S1, Φ(r), r outside S1. Since S′ 1 lies entirely inside S1, this trial function satisfies Φ = 1 on S′ 1, and still Φ = 0 on all the grounded conductors. Therefore Φ is an admissible trial function for configuration B. Step 2: Compare the corresponding energies. Inside S1, the trial function Φ is constant, so ∇Φ = 0. Outside S1, it agrees with Φ, so ∇Φ = ∇Φ. Hence |∇Φ|2 dτ= |∇Φ|2 dτ. outside S1 But for the original configuration with surface S1, the electric field exists only outside S1, so this is exactly |∇Φ|2 dτ= |∇Φ|2 dτ. 35 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Therefore the trial function Φ gives the value ε0 |∇Φ|2 dτ= C.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Now use the variational principle for configuration B: the exact potential Φ′gives the minimum of the functional among all admissible trial functions, so C′ = ε0 |∇Φ′|2 dτ ≤ε0 |∇Φ|2 dτ= C. C′≤C. Thus This proves that enlarging the conductor while leaving all the grounded conductors fixed can only increase the capacitance. Physical meaning. A larger conductor held at the same potential can store more charge because its electric field can spread more effectively through the surrounding region. Equivalently, when the shape expands outward toward the grounded conductors, the same potential difference can be maintained with a larger total charge. (b) Bounds for the capacitance of a conducting cube of side a Let Ccube be the capacitance of an isolated conducting cube of side a. We compare the cube with spheres: • the largest sphere completely inside the cube; • the smallest sphere completely enclosing the cube.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Since for an isolated sphere of radius R, Csphere = 4πε0R, these comparisons immediately give bounds. Lower bound: inscribed sphere. The largest sphere inside the cube has radius a Rin = 2. Ccube ≥Cin. Ccube ≥4πε0 a 2 = 2πε0a. Ccube ≥2πε0a. Because the cube encloses this sphere, part (a) implies Therefore So Upper bound: circumscribed sphere. cube’s corners. Its radius is half the body diagonal: The smallest sphere containing the cube is the one passing through the Rout = 2. This sphere encloses the cube, so by part (a), Ccube ≤Cout. Hence Ccube ≤4πε0 √3 a 2 √3 a = 2√3 πε0a. So Ccube ≤2√3 πε0a. Therefore the bounds are 2πε0a≤Ccube ≤2√3 πε0a. If we factor out 4πε0a, this becomes 1 2 ≤ Ccube 4πε0a ≤√3 2. 36 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Comparison with the numerical value. The quoted numerical value is Cnum ≃0. 655(4πε0a). Our bounds correspond to So the numerical value indeed lies between them: Average of the bounds.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Factoring out 4πε0a, Numerically, 0. 500(4πε0a) ≤Ccube ≤0. 866(4πε0a). 0. 500 <0. 655 <0. 866. The arithmetic average of the lower and upper bounds is Cavg = 1 2 2πε0a+ 2√3 πε0a = πε0a(1 + √3). Cavg = 1 + √3 4 (4πε0a). 1 + √3 1 + 1. 732 ≈ ≈0. 683. 4 4 Thus Cavg ≈0. 683(4πε0a). Compared with the quoted value 0. 655(4πε0a), the average is somewhat high, but still reasonably close: 0. 683−0. 655 = 0. 028. (c) Effect on the capacitance per unit length of the two-wire system Now consider the two-wire system of Problem 1. 7. One wire is replaced by a wire of square cross section whose side equals the diameter of the original circular wire. Let the original wire have radius b. Then its diameter is 2b. The new square has side 2b. A circle of radius bis exactly inscribed in a square of side 2b, so the new square cross section completely encloses the original circular cross section.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Therefore, with the other wire unchanged, the new conductor surface encloses the old one. By part (a), the capacitance per unit length must increase: C′>C. So the change is: the capacitance per unit length becomes larger. Physical explanation. Replacing the circular wire by the square wire makes that conductor effectively larger. Its surface extends farther outward, especially near the corners, so at the same potential difference it can hold more charge. Equivalently, the electric field is concentrated more strongly near the corners, which increases the total charge stored per unit potential difference. Hence the capacitance increases. 37 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Final answers C′≤C if S′ 1 lies entirely inside S1. 2πε0a≤Ccube ≤2√3 πε0a or equivalently 0. 500(4πε0a) ≤Ccube ≤0. 866(4πε0a). Cavg = 1 + √3 4 (4πε0a) ≈0. 683(4πε0a).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Replacing one round wire by a square wire of side equal to its diameter makes the capacitance per unit length larger. Problem 1. 6 11. 1 Problem Statement A simple capacitor is a device formed by two insulated conductors adjacent to each other. If equal and opposite charges are placed on the conductors, there will be a certain difference of potential between them. The ratio of the magnitude of the charge on one conductor to the magnitude of the potential difference is called the capacitance (in SI units it is measured in farads). Using Gauss’s law, calculate the capacitance of (a) two large, flat, conducting sheets of area A, separated by a small distance d; (b) two concentric conducting spheres with radii a, b (b>a); (c) two concentric conducting cylinders of length L, large compared to their radii a, b (b>a).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: (d) What is the inner diameter of the outer conductor in an air-filled coaxial cable whose center conductor is a cylindrical wire of diameter 1 mm and whose capacitance is 3 ×10−11 F/m? 3 ×10−12 F/m? 11. 2 Solution The capacitance is defined by Q C= V , where Q is the magnitude of the charge on either conductor and V is the potential difference between them. In each case we first determine the electric field using Gauss’s law, then compute the potential difference from V= E·dl, and finally form the ratio Q/V. (a) Two large parallel conducting sheets Consider two large flat conducting sheets of area A, carrying charges +Q and−Q, separated by a small distance d. Because the sheets are large compared with the separation, edge effects are neglected, so the field is essentially uniform between the sheets. Let the surface charge density be σ= Q A.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: A single infinite sheet with surface charge density σ produces an electric field of magnitude σ Esheet = 2ε0 on each side. For two oppositely charged sheets, the fields between the plates add, while outside they cancel. Therefore the field between the sheets is σ E= = ε0 Q ε0A. 38 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Since the field is uniform, the potential difference is Hence the capacitance is V= Ed= C= Q V= Q Q ε0Ad. = ε0A Qd/(ε0A) d . Therefore C= ε0A d . Physical interpretation. A larger plate area allows more charge to be stored for the same potential difference, so C increases with A. Increasing the separation weakens the electric interaction between the plates and increases the required voltage for a given charge, so C decreases with d. (b) Two concentric conducting spheres Now consider two concentric conducting spheres of radii a and b, with b>a. Let the inner sphere carry charge +Q and the outer sphere carry charge−Q.

Variational principle for the capacitance For a capacitor with one conductor held at unit potential and the other at zero potential, the exact electrostatic energy is U= 1 2C. chunks.text: The exact potential for a coaxial cylindrical capacitor is Φexact(ρ) = ln(b/ρ) ln(b/a). 1. 01366 1. 03972 1. 09861 This is a logarithmic function of ρ. The trial function used here is Ψ1(ρ) = b−ρ b−a , which is linear in ρ. dependence. So the reason for the trend is clear: the trial function has the correct boundary values, but not the correct radial When b/a is close to 1: If the gap b−a is small, then the region between the cylinders is thin, and over a thin interval a logarithmic function is very nearly linear. Therefore the trial function is a very good approximation to the exact potential, and the variational upper bound is close to the exact capacitance. When b/a becomes larger: As the annular region becomes wider, the exact logarithmic dependence differs more and more from a straight line. The trial function then becomes less accurate, so the variational functional gives a looser upper bound.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: That is why the percentage error increases as b a increases. 9. Physical interpretation The electrostatic energy density is proportional to |E|2 = |∇Φ|2 . The exact field in a coaxial capacitor is Eexact(ρ) = const. , ρ because the exact potential is logarithmic. The trial function instead gives a constant radial derivative: dΨ1 dρ =− 1 b−a , so it predicts a field of constant magnitude throughout the annulus. That is physically incorrect. In the real capacitor the field is stronger near the inner cylinder and weaker near the outer cylinder. By forcing the field to be constant, the trial function distorts the energy distribution and produces a value of the energy that is too large. Since the variational principle gives C ≤ε0 |∇Ψ|2 dτ, this overestimate of the energy leads to an overestimate of the capacitance.

Problem 1. 7 13. 1 Problem Statement Two long, cylindrical conductors of radii a1 and a2 are parallel and separated by a distance d, which is large compared with either radius. Because the wires are very long, the problem is effectively two-dimensional in the plane perpendicular to the wires. The idea is to approximate each cylindrical conductor by a line charge placed along its axis. This is valid because the separation d is assumed large compared with the radii a1, a2. So each wire is small compared with the distance to the other wire, and the field produced by one wire over the cross section of the other changes only slightly across that cross section. 1. chunks.text: 1 Problem Statement Consider the configuration of conductors of Problem 1. 17, with all conductors except S1 held at zero potential. (a) Show that the potential Φ(x) anywhere in the volume V and on any of the surfaces Si can be written Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S1 σ1(x′) G(x,x′) da′ , where σ1(x′) is the surface charge density on S1 and G(x,x′) is the Green function potential for a point charge in the presence of all the surfaces that are held at zero potential (but with S1 absent). Show also that the electrostatic energy is 1 W= 8πε0 S1 where the integrals are only over the surface S1. da S1 da′ σ1(x) G(x,x′) σ1(x′), (b) Show that the variational expression C−1[σ] = S1 da S1 da′ σ(x) G(x,x′) σ(x′) 4πε0 S1 σ(x) da 2 with an arbitrary integrable function σ(x) defined on S1, is stationary for small variations of σ away from σ1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Use Thomson’s theorem to prove that the reciprocal of C−1[σ] gives a lower bound to the true capacitance of the conductor S1. 14. 2 Solution We consider a set of conductors with surfaces S1,S2,. ,Sn. All conductors except S1 are grounded: Φ = 0 on S2,. ,Sn. The conductor S1 is held at some constant potential V1 and carries surface charge density σ1(x). The central idea is that, once the grounded conductors are fixed, the electrostatic field produced by the charge on S1 can be described through the Green function of the region with all grounded surfaces present and S1 removed. 51 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Part (a): Representation of the potential Step 1: Meaning of the Green function. Let G(x,x′) be the Green function for the region when all grounded conductors are present but S1 is absent. By definition, for fixed source point x′it satisfies ∇2 xG(x,x′) =−4πδ(x−x′), and G(x,x′) = 0 on all grounded surfaces S2,. ,Sn.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Physically, G(x,x′) is the potential produced at x by a unit point charge placed at x′ in the presence of those grounded conductors. Therefore, if an infinitesimal surface element da′on S1 carries charge dq= σ1(x′) da′ , its contribution to the potential at x is dΦ(x) = 1 G(x,x′) dq= 4πε0 1 4πε0 G(x,x′) σ1(x′) da′ . By superposition, summing the contributions of all surface elements on S1 gives Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S1 σ1(x′) G(x,x′) da′ . Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S1 σ1(x′) G(x,x′) da′ . Hence surfaces. Step 2: Energy in terms of the surface charge density. The electrostatic energy can be written as W= 1 2 S1 σ1(x) Φ(x) da, because the only conductor at nonzero potential is S1. Now substitute the representation for Φ(x): 1 W= 2 S1 σ1(x) 1 4πε0 S1 σ1(x′) G(x,x′) da′ da. Pull out the constant: W= 1 8πε0 S1 da S1 da′ σ1(x) G(x,x′) σ1(x′).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: This holds everywhere in the field region V, and by continuity of the potential it also holds on the conductor Therefore W= 1 8πε0 S1 da S1 da′ σ1(x) G(x,x′) σ1(x′). That proves part (a). Part (b): Stationarity of C−1[σ] We now introduce the trial functional C−1[σ] = N[σ] 4πε0 Q[σ]2 , where N[σ] = S1 da S1 da′ σ(x) G(x,x′) σ(x′), and Q[σ] = S1 σ(x) da. We must show two things: 1. C−1[σ] is stationary when σ= σ1 is the true charge density; 2. its reciprocal gives a lower bound to the true capacitance. 52 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 1 = Q1 C, Step 1: Evaluate the functional at the true charge density. For the true surface charge density σ1, from part (a) we have Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S1 G(x,x′) σ1(x′) da′ . But on the conductor S1 itself, the potential is constant: Φ(x) = V1 for x ∈S1. Hence S1 G(x,x′) σ1(x′) da′= 4πε0V1 (x ∈S1). Now compute N[σ1] = S1 daσ1(x) S1 da′G(x,x′) σ1(x′).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Using the previous relation, N[σ1] = S1 σ1(x) (4πε0V1) da= 4πε0V1Q1, where Q1 = S1 σ1(x) da is the total charge on the conductor. Therefore C−1[σ1] = 4πε0V1Q1 4πε0Q2 1 V1 = since C= Q1 V1 . So the functional indeed reproduces the true inverse capacitance when evaluated at the true charge density: C−1[σ1] = 1 C. Step 2: Show stationarity. Let σ= σ1 + δσ, where δσ is an arbitrary small variation. We compute the first variation of F[σ] ≡C−1[σ] = N[σ] 4πε0Q[σ]2. First vary Q[σ]: δQ= S1 δσ(x) da. Next vary N[σ]. Since G(x,x′) is symmetric, G(x,x′) = G(x′ ,x), we have δN = 2 S1 daδσ(x) S1 da′G(x,x′) σ1(x′). But for the true charge density, S1 da′G(x,x′) σ1(x′) = 4πε0V1. Hence δN = 2 S1 δσ(x) (4πε0V1) da= 8πε0V1 δQ. Now vary the quotient: δF= δN 4πε0Q2 1 − N[σ1] 4πε0 2δQ Q3 1 . 53 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions δN = 8πε0V1 δQ, δF= 8πε0V1 δQ 4πε0Q2 1 δF= 2V1 δQ Q2 1 − − at σ= σ1, Substitute N[σ1] = 4πε0V1Q1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Then 4πε0V1Q1 4πε0 2δQ Q3 1 . Simplify each term: 2V1 δQ = 0. Q2 1 Therefore δC−1[σ] = 0 so the functional is stationary for small variations away from the true surface charge density. Lower bound from Thomson’s theorem We now prove that the reciprocal of this functional gives a lower bound to the true capacitance. Step 1: Thomson’s theorem. Thomson’s theorem states that among all charge distributions on the conductors with the same total charge on each conductor, the true electrostatic distribution minimizes the electrostatic energy. In the present problem only the charge on S1 is nonzero. So among all trial surface densities σ(x) on S1 with fixed total charge Q= S1 σ(x) da, the true distribution σ1 minimizes the energy. From part (a), the energy corresponding to a trial density σ is W[σ] = 1 8πε0 S1 da S1 da′ σ(x) G(x,x′) σ(x′). That is, W[σ] = 1 8πε0 N[σ]. For the true distribution, Wtrue = Q2 2C.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Thomson’s theorem therefore implies W[σ] ≥Wtrue for fixed Q. So 1 8πε0 N[σ] ≥Q2 2C. Multiply both sides by 2: 1 4πε0 N[σ] ≥Q2 C . N[σ] 4πε0Q2 ≥ 1 C. Now divide by Q2: But the left-hand side is exactly C−1[σ]. Hence 1 C−1[σ] ≥ C. Taking reciprocals, 1 C−1[σ] ≤C. Thus the reciprocal of the variational expression gives a lower bound to the true capacitance: Ctrial ≡ 1 C−1[σ] ≤C. 54 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Physical interpretation The functional C−1[σ] is constructed from the Green-function interaction energy of a trial surface charge distribution on S1. If the trial charge is not the true one, then for the same total charge it stores too much electrostatic energy, because the true charge distribution is the one that arranges itself to minimize the energy. Since for fixed charge W= Q2 2C, a larger energy corresponds to a smaller capacitance.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: That is why the capacitance inferred from a trial charge distribution is always below the exact capacitance. Final results Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S1 σ1(x′) G(x,x′) da′ W= 1 8πε0 S1 da S1 da′ σ1(x) G(x,x′) σ1(x′) C−1[σ] = S1 da S1 da′ σ(x) G(x,x′) σ(x′) 4πε0 S1 σ(x) da 2 is stationary at the true charge density σ1, and C−1[σ] ≥ 1 C= ⇒ 1 C−1[σ] ≤C. So the reciprocal of the variational expression gives a lower bound to the true capacitance. Problem 1. 8 15. 1 Problem Statement (a) For the three capacitor geometries in Problem 1. 6 calculate the total electro- static energy and express it alternatively in terms of the equal and opposite charges Qand−Qplaced on the conductors and the potential difference between them. (b) Sketch the energy density of the electrostatic field in each case as a function of the appropriate linear coordinate. 15.

chunks.text: 2 Solution For any capacitor, the total electrostatic energy can be written in either of the equivalent forms W= 1 2CV2 = Q2 2C= 1 2QV, where Q= magnitude of the charge on either conductor, V = potential difference. It can also be written as a field energy: W= ε0 2 E2 dτ. The corresponding energy density is u= ε0 2 E2 . We now apply this to the three geometries of Problem 1. 6. 55 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (a) Parallel-plate capacitor Two large conducting plates of area A are separated by distance d, with charges +Q and−Q. From Problem 1. 6, C= ε0A d , E= σ = ε0 Q ε0A, V= Ed= Qd ε0A. Energy from C and V. Using W= 1 2CV2 , we get W= 1 2 ε0A d V2 . Hence W= 1 2 ε0A d V2 . Energy from Q and C. we obtain Thus Using Q2 Q2d W= 2ε0A. Energy from the field integral. The field occupies the volume Therefore in agreement with the previous result. So for the parallel-plate capacitor W= 2C, Q2 2(ε0A/d) = W= Q2d 2ε0A.

chunks.text: Since the field is essentially uniform between the plates and negligible outside, ε0 u= E2 ε0 = 2 Q ε0A 2 = Q2 2ε0A2. Ad. Final answers Parallel plates with Spherical capacitor with Cylindrical capacitor with Problem 1. 17 16. 1 Problem Statement A volume V in vacuum is bounded by a surface Sconsisting of several separate conducting surfaces Si. One conductor is held at unit potential and all the other conductors at zero potential. (a) Show that the capacitance of the one conductor is given by C= ε0 V |∇Φ|2 d3x where Φ(x) is the solution for the potential. (b) Show that the true capacitance C is always less than or equal to the quantity C[Ψ] = ε0 V |∇Ψ|2 d3x where Ψ is any trial function satisfying the boundary conditions on the conductors. This is a variational principle for the capacitance that yields an upper bound. 16.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 2 Solution We consider a region V of vacuum bounded by conducting surfaces S= S1 ∪S2 ∪···, with boundary conditions Φ = 1 on one conductor, say S1, Φ = 0 on all other conductors. Since there is no free charge in the vacuum region between the conductors, the exact potential satisfies Laplace’s equation: ∇2Φ = 0 in V. 60 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions The capacitance is defined by C= Q V0 . But here the conductor S1 is held at unit potential and the others at zero, so the potential difference is V0 = 1. Therefore C= Q, where Q is the total charge on the conductor at unit Problem 1. 9 17. 1 Problem Statement Calculate the attractive force between conductors in the parallel plate capacitor (Problem 1. 6a) and the parallel cylinder capacitor (Problem 1. 7) for (a) fixed charges on each conductor; (b) fixed potential difference between conductors. 17. 2 Solution The force can be obtained from the electrostatic energy.

chunks.text: If the geometry depends on a single separation parameter d, then the mechanical force in the direction of increasing d is dW Fd =− If this derivative is negative, the force tends to decrease d, which means the force is attractive. (1) Parallel-plate capacitor For two large parallel plates of area A separated by distance d, the capacitance is C= ε0A d .

Problem 1. 16 18. 1 Problem Statement Prove the following theorem: If a number of conducting surfaces are fixed in position with a given total charge on each, the introduction of an uncharged, insulated conductor into the region bounded by the surfaces lowers the electrostatic energy. 18. 2 Solution We are asked to prove an energy theorem in electrostatics. The situation is this: • A set of conductors is held fixed in space. • Each conductor carries a specified total charge. • The conductors produce some electrostatic field E in the surrounding region. • Then we insert an additional conductor into the field region. • This inserted conductor is uncharged and insulated, so its net charge remains zero.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: We must show that after the conductor is inserted and electrostatic equilibrium is re-established, the total electrostatic energy is smaller than before. The physical idea is that when the new conductor is inserted, charges are induced on its surface. These induced charges rearrange the field so that the field vanishes inside the new conductor. That removes some field-filled volume and therefore lowers the total field energy. We now prove this carefully. 1. Electrostatic energy in terms of the field In vacuum, the electrostatic energy is W= ε0 2 all space E2 dτ. Equivalently, if Φ is the electrostatic potential, E= −∇Φ, so W= ε0 2 |∇Φ|2 dτ. We will compare two electrostatic configurations: • the original configuration, before the new conductor is inserted; • the final configuration, after insertion and equilibration.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Let Φ0, E0 = −∇Φ0, W0 denote the potential, field, and energy before insertion, and let Φ, E= −∇Φ, W denote the corresponding quantities after insertion. We must prove W ≤W0. 65 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 2. The appropriate theorem to use The key theorem is Thomson’s theorem: Among all charge distributions on a set of conductors having specified total charges on each conductor, the electrostatic equilibrium distribution is the one of minimum electrostatic energy. So if we can construct any admissible trial charge distribution after the new conductor is inserted, and that trial distribution has the same total charge on each conductor as the original problem, then the true equilibrium energy after insertion must be less than or equal to the energy of that trial distribution. That is exactly the strategy. 3.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Construct a trial distribution after the conductor is inserted Suppose the new conductor occupies a volume v, bounded by a surface s. Before insertion, the original field E0 fills this region just as it fills the rest of space. Now imagine placing the new conductor into that region. We now define a trial charge distribution for the new configuration as follows: 1. Keep all the original conductors with exactly the same total charges they had before. 2. Put zero net charge on the new conductor. 3. Arrange charges on the surface s of the new conductor in such a way that the potential inside the conductor becomes constant, so that the electric field inside it vanishes. This is an admissible electrostatic configuration because: • the original conductors still have the same given total charges, • the inserted conductor is insulated and uncharged, so its total charge must be zero, • the field inside a conductor in equilibrium must vanish.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: By Thomson’s theorem, the actual equilibrium after insertion has energy less than or equal to the energy of this trial configuration. So it is enough to show that this trial configuration already has energy not larger than W0. 4. A convenient way to compare the energies A clean proof comes from Green’s first identity. For any sufficiently smooth scalar function Φ, V |∇Φ|2 dτ= Φ ∂Φ ∂V ∂n da− Φ ∇2Φ dτ. V In electrostatics in vacuum, away from the conductor surfaces, ∇2Φ = 0. Φ ∂Φ ∂n da. So in the field region, V |∇Φ|2 dτ= ∂V But for a conductor surface, σ=−ε0 ∂Φ ∂n, where ∂/∂n is the derivative along the outward normal from the field region. Therefore ε0 Φ ∂Φ ∂n da=− Φ σda. Accounting consistently for normal directions gives the standard energy formula W= 1 2 i QiVi, where Qi is the total charge on conductor i, and Vi is its potential. This formula is extremely useful here. 66 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 5.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Energy before insertion Before insertion, the conductors have charges Q1,Q2,. ,QN, fixed by the problem, and potentials V(0) 1 ,V(0) 2 ,. ,V(0) N. Therefore 1 W0 = N i=1 QiV(0) i. 2 6. Energy after insertion After inserting the new conductor, the original conductors still have the same total charges Q1,. ,QN. The new conductor has total charge QN+1 = 0. Let the final conductor potentials be V1,V2,. ,VN, and let the inserted conductor be at potential VN+1 (some constant, not specified in advance). Then the electrostatic energy of the final equilibrium is 1 W= N+1 i=1 QiVi. 2 Since the new conductor is uncharged, 2 QN+1 = 0, so 1 W= N QiVi. i=1 Thus the only way the energy can change is through the changes in the potentials of the original conductors. 7. Why the energy must decrease Now we invoke Thomson’s theorem directly.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Take the original equilibrium field E0 and imagine removing from space the volume vnow occupied by the inserted conductor. Inside that conductor the electrostatic field in the new equilibrium must be zero. So the field energy that originally occupied v, ε0 2 v E2 0 dτ, is eliminated. Of course, inserting the conductor also changes the field everywhere outside v, because induced surface charges appear on the new conductor and slightly redistribute the fields in the rest of space. But the important point is this: • the original field configuration E0 restricted to the exterior region is not the equilibrium configuration for the new set of conductors; • the true equilibrium for the new geometry is the one that minimizes the total energy subject to the fixed conductor charges.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Therefore the new equilibrium energy cannot exceed the energy of any admissible post-insertion configuration, and in particular it cannot exceed what one would get by simply removing the old field energy from the conductor volume and then adjusting the surface charges consistently. Hence the final energy must satisfy W ≤W0. To see the inequality in a more explicitly variational way, let the inserted conductor occupy volume v. Then the final equilibrium field is the minimum-energy field among all fields consistent with: • the same total charges on the original conductors, • zero net charge on the inserted conductor, • zero electric field inside the inserted conductor. 67 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Because the condition E = 0 in v removes a region where the original field had nonnegative energy density ε0 u= E2 ≥0, the minimizing field in the new class must have energy no greater than the old equilibrium field. So 2 W ≤W0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: If the original field in the region where the conductor is inserted is not identically zero, then a strictly positive amount of field energy is removed from that region, and in that generic case W <W0. Equality can occur only in the trivial case that the inserted conductor is placed entirely in a region where the original electric field already vanishes. 8. Alternative differential argument using polarization work There is also a very intuitive physical derivation. When the neutral insulated conductor is inserted, the external field induces surface charges on it: negative charge appears on the side facing the direction from which field lines enter, and positive charge appears on the opposite side. The conductor becomes polarized, though its net charge remains zero. Because opposite induced charges are pulled in opposite directions by the electric field, the conductor is drawn into regions of stronger field.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: If we insert it quasistatically, the field does positive work on the conductor. By energy conservation, that work must come from the electrostatic field energy. Therefore the field energy decreases. So physically: the introduction of a neutral insulated conductor lowers the electrostatic energy. 9. Final statement We conclude that if a system of conductors has fixed total charge on each conductor, then after inserting an additional uncharged insulated conductor and allowing the system to reach electrostatic equilibrium, the new energy satisfies W ≤W0. In general the inequality is strict: W <W0 unless the conductor is inserted only in a region where the original electric field was zero. Therefore the theorem is proved: The introduction of an uncharged, insulated conductor lowers the electrostatic energy. Problem 1. 10 19.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 1 Problem Statement Prove the mean value theorem: For charge-free space the value of the electrostatic potential at any point is equal to the average of the potential over the surface of any sphere centered on that point. 19. 2 Solution We must prove that if the electrostatic potential Φ(r) is harmonic in a region containing a sphere of radius Rcentered at a point r0, that is, ∇2Φ = 0 inside and on the sphere, then Φ(r0) = 1 4πR2 |r−r0 |=R Φ(r) da. This is the mean value theorem for harmonic functions in electrostatics. 68 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 1. Physical setting In charge-free space, Poisson’s equation reduces to Laplace’s equation: ∇2Φ = 0. So the electrostatic potential in any region containing no charge is a harmonic function. The theorem states that at the center of any sphere lying entirely in such a charge-free region, the potential equals the average of the potential over the spherical surface.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: This is a very characteristic property of harmonic functions. Physically it means that, in empty space, the potential at a point cannot be a strict local maximum or minimum produced by sources inside that region; it is determined in a balanced way by its surroundings. 2. Set up spherical coordinates about the point of interest Take the point of interest as the origin of a local spherical coordinate system. That is, without loss of generality, let r0 = 0. Then we want to prove Φ(0) = 1 4πR2 r=R Φ da. Since on a sphere of radius R the surface element is da= R2 dΩ, dΩ = sin θdθdϕ where is the solid-angle element, the statement is equivalently Φ(0) = 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ. So it is enough to show that the angular average of Φ on the sphere r= R equals the value at the center. 3.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: General harmonic solution inside a sphere Because Φ satisfies Laplace’s equation in a region containing the sphere and its interior, the general solution regular at the origin can be expanded in spherical harmonics: Φ(r,θ,ϕ) = ∞ ℓ ℓ=0 m=−ℓ AℓmrℓYℓm(θ,ϕ). We do not include the singular terms proportional to r−(ℓ+1), because the potential is assumed finite at the center and harmonic throughout the sphere. Now evaluate this at the center r= 0. Every term with ℓ≥1 contains a factor rℓ, so those terms vanish at r= 0. Therefore Φ(0) = A00Y00. Thus the value at the center is exactly the ℓ= 0 part of the harmonic expansion. 4. Average the potential over the sphere Now average Φ(R,θ,ϕ) over the sphere: 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ = 1 4π ∞ ℓ ℓ=0 m=−ℓ AℓmRℓYℓm(θ,ϕ) dΩ. Interchange sum and integral: 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ = 1 4π ∞ ℓ=0 ℓ m=−ℓ 69 AℓmRℓ Yℓm(θ,ϕ) dΩ.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Now use the fact that all spherical harmonics except the constant one have zero average over the sphere: YℓmdΩ = 0 for ℓ≥1. So only the ℓ= 0, m= 0 term survives. Hence 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ = 1 4π A00Y00 dΩ. Since dΩ = 4π, we get 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ = A00Y00. But from above, A00Y00 = Φ(0). Therefore Φ(0) = 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ. Rewriting this in surface-integral form, Φ(0) = 1 4πR2 r=R Φ da. Since we chose the center point to be the origin only for convenience, the general result is Φ(r0) = 1 4πR2 |r−r0 |=R Φ(r) da. This proves the mean value theorem. 5. Alternative derivation using the spherically averaged potential It is useful to give a second derivation that is very physical. Define the spherical average of the potential about the point r0 = 0 by Φ(R) = 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ. We will show directly that Φ(R) is independent of R in charge-free space.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: If that is true, then Φ(R) = lim R→0 Φ(R) = Φ(0), which is exactly the theorem. Differentiate with respect to R: R2 dΦ dR= Multiply by R2: Since this becomes dΦ dR= 1 4π ∂Φ ∂RdΩ. 1 4π R2 ∂Φ ∂RdΩ. da= R2 dΩ, R2 dΦ dR= 1 4π r=R Now use Gauss’s theorem: r=R ∂Φ ∂r da= r<R 70 ∂Φ ∂r da. ∇2Φ dτ. Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions But in charge-free space, ∇2Φ = 0. ∂Φ ∂r da= 0. Therefore r=R Hence so dΦ dR = 0. R2 dΦ dR = 0, Thus the average Φ(R) is independent of R. Take the limit as R→0. Since Φ is continuous, lim R→0 Φ(R) = Φ(0). Therefore for every R, that is, Φ(R) = Φ(0), Φ(0) = 1 4π Φ(R,θ,ϕ) dΩ or Φ(0) = 1 4πR2 r=R Φ da. This is the same result, obtained in a very direct way from Gauss’s theorem and Laplace’s equation. 6. Physical interpretation The theorem says that in empty space the potential at a point is completely balanced by the values of the potential on any surrounding sphere.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: This is one reason harmonic functions cannot have isolated maxima or minima in charge-free regions: if the value at the center were larger than all surrounding values, it could not equal their average. In electrostatics, this reflects the absence of sources inside the sphere. If there is no charge inside, then the field lines neither begin nor end there, and the potential is determined in a smooth, source-free way by the boundary values around the point. Final result For any sphere lying entirely in a charge-free region, ∇2Φ = 0 = ⇒ Φ(r0) = 1 4πR2 |r−r0 |=R Φ(r) da. That is, the electrostatic potential at the center equals the average of the potential over the spherical surface. Problem 1. 15 20.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 1 Problem Statement Prove Thomson’s theorem: If a number of surfaces are fixed in position and a given total charge is placed on each surface, then the electrostatic energy in the region bounded by the surfaces is an absolute minimum when the charges are placed so that every surface is an equipotential, as happens when they are conductors. 20. 2 Solution We are given a set of fixed closed surfaces S1,S2,. ,SN, and on each surface Si the total charge Qi is prescribed. We must show that, among all possible ways of distributing those charges over the surfaces, the electrostatic energy is minimized by the special distribution for which each surface is an equipotential. This is Thomson’s theorem. 71 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Strategy of the proof We compare two charge distributions on the same set of surfaces: • the equilibrium distribution, for which each surface is an equipotential; • an arbitrary other distribution with the same total charge Qi on each surface. Let Φ(x), E(x) = −∇Φ denote the potential and field for the equilibrium distribution, and let Φ(x), E(x) = −∇Φ denote the potential and field for any other admissible charge distribution with the same total charges on the surfaces. We will prove that the corresponding energies satisfy W ≥W. 2. Electrostatic energy The electrostatic energy in vacuum is W= ε0 2 V E2 dτ, where V is the field region outside the charged surfaces and dτ is the volume element. So for the two configurations we have W= ε0 2 V ε0 2 V |E|2 dτ. |E|2 dτ, W= Define the difference field Ed = E−E. Then E= E + Ed. Substitute into W: W= ε0 2 V |E + Ed|2 dτ. Expand the square: W= ε0 2 V E2 + 2E·Ed + E2 d dτ.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Thus W= W + ε0 V ε0 E·Eddτ + 2 V So the entire proof reduces to showing that the cross term vanishes: E·Eddτ = 0. V E2 ddτ. Then we would obtain W= W + ε0 2 V E2 ddτ ≥W, which is exactly the theorem. So now we prove that the cross term is zero. 72 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 3. The cross term Since E= −∇Φ, Ed =−(∇Φ −∇Φ) = −∇χ, where we define χ≡Φ−Φ, we have E·Ed = (−∇Φ)·(−∇χ) = ∇Φ ·∇χ. Therefore V Now use Green’s first identity: V E·Eddτ= V ∇Φ ·∇χdτ. ∇Φ ·∇χdτ= S χ ∂Φ ∂n da− V χ∇2Φ dτ, where S=region. iSi is the union of all the surfaces, and ∂/∂nis the derivative along the outward normal from the field In the field region there is no volume charge, so the equilibrium potential satisfies Laplace’s equation: ∇2Φ = 0. Hence the volume term vanishes, and we get V E·Eddτ= S χ ∂Φ ∂n da= N i=1 Si χ ∂Φ ∂n da.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Now comes the key point: in the equilibrium configuration, each surface Si is an equipotential, so Φ = Vi on Si, where Vi is a constant on that surface. Although χ need not be constant on Si, it is more convenient to integrate by parts the other way: V ∇Φ ·∇χdτ= Φ ∂χ S ∂nda− Φ ∇2χdτ. V Now both Φ and Φ are harmonic in the field region, so ∇2Φ = 0, ∇2Φ = 0, ∇2χ= 0. N i=1 Si Φ ∂χ ∂nda= N i=1 χ= Φ−Φ, ∂χ ∂nda= Si ∂Φ ∂n da− Si Qi =−ε0 Si ∂Φ ∂n da, and similarly for the alternative distribution, Qi =−ε0 Si ∂Φ ∂n da. 73 and therefore Thus V E·Eddτ= Now so Si But the total charge on surface Si is Vi Si ∂χ ∂nda. ∂Φ ∂n da. Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions By hypothesis, the total charge on each surface is the same in the two configurations: Qi = Qi. Therefore ∂Φ Si ∂n da= Si and hence ∂χ ∂nda= 0. Si So every term in the sum vanishes: Vi Si ∂χ ∂nda= 0. ∂Φ ∂n da, Therefore V E·Eddτ = 0. 4.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Conclude the proof Substituting this result into the energy difference, W= W + ε0 2 V Since E2 d ≥0 E2 ddτ. everywhere, ε0 2 V Hence E2 ddτ ≥0. W ≥W. Thus the equilibrium distribution, the one for which each surface is an equipotential, gives the absolute minimum electrostatic energy among all charge distributions with the same total charge on each surface. This proves Thomson’s theorem. 5. When does equality occur? Equality requires V Since the integrand is nonnegative, this can happen only if E2 ddτ = 0. Ed = 0 everywhere in V. That means E= E everywhere, so the two electrostatic configurations are actually the same. Therefore the minimum is strict unless the alternative distribution coincides with the equilibrium distribution. 6.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Physical interpretation The theorem says that if conductors are allowed to redistribute charge while keeping the total charge on each conductor fixed, they do so in exactly the way that minimizes field energy. Why? Because if some surface were not an equipotential, charges could still move along it. That motion would lower the energy until no tangential electric field remained, which is precisely the condition that the conductor be an equipotential. So the electrostatic equilibrium configuration is the one in which the charges have arranged themselves so efficiently that any other arrangement with the same conductor charges would produce more field energy. 74 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Final result If the total charge on each fixed surface is prescribed, then the electrostatic energy is minimized when each surface is an equipotential. Quantitatively, for any other admissible charge distribution, ε0 W−W= 2 V |E−E|2 dτ ≥0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Hence W is an absolute minimum at electrostatic equilibrium. 1 1 R2 Problem 1. 11 21. 1 Problem Statement Use Gauss’s theorem to prove that at the surface of a curved charged conductor, the normal derivative of the electric field is given by 1 E ∂E ∂n=− + R1 where R1 and R2 are the principal radii of curvature of the surface. , 21. 2 Solution We want to determine how the magnitude of the electric field changes as one moves normally away from the surface of a conductor. At a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium: • the electric field inside the conductor is zero; • just outside the conductor, the electric field is perpendicular to the surface. Therefore, near the surface, the electric field lines are normal to the conductor, and the nearby equipotential surfaces are obtained by moving a small distance along the outward normal direction.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: The key idea is to take a very small tube of field lines bounded by two nearby equipotential surfaces and use Gauss’s law in the empty space between them. 1. Geometry near the surface Choose a point on the conductor surface and let the outward normal direction be denoted by n. Let the two principal radii of curvature at that point be R1 and R2. Take an infinitesimal surface patch on the conductor. Choose its sides along the principal directions of curvature, and let the side lengths of the patch at the conductor surface be ∆s1, ∆s2. So its area is da= ∆s1 ∆s2. Now move a small distance dnoutward along the normal. This generates a nearby surface patch on the neighboring equipotential surface. Because the original patch is curved, its side lengths change as we move outward. Along the first principal direction, the radius of curvature is R1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: A small element of length ∆s1 on the original surface becomes, at distance dn, ∆s′ 1 = ∆s1 1 + dn R1 , to first order in dn. Similarly, along the second principal direction, ∆s′ 2 = ∆s2 1 + dn R2 . 75 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Thus Therefore the area of the corresponding patch on the displaced surface is da′= ∆s′ 1∆s′ 2 = ∆s1∆s2 1 + dn R1 1 + dn R2 . Expanding to first order in dn, da′ da′ = da 1 + dn R1 1 + dn R2 = da 1 + dn 1 R1 + 1 R2 . . So da′ = da 1 + dn 1 R1 + 1 R2 . 2. Construct a Gaussian tube Now consider the infinitesimal volume bounded by • the original surface patch da on the conductor, • the displaced patch da′on the neighboring equipotential surface at distance dn, • the side surface generated by the field lines through the boundary of the patch. This is a thin “flux tube. ” Since the region just outside the conductor contains no charge, Gauss’s law gives E·da = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Now examine the flux through each part of the tube. Flux through the side walls. The side walls are generated by field lines. Since the electric field is tangent to those walls, E·da = 0 there. So the side flux vanishes. Flux through the two end caps. The field is normal to the equipotential surfaces, so on each end cap the field is parallel to the outward normal of that cap. Let the field magnitude at the conductor surface be E, and at the displaced surface be E+ ∂E ∂n dn. The flux through the inner cap and outer cap must cancel, because the total flux is zero and the side flux is zero. Therefore the flux magnitude through the two caps is the same: Eda= E+ ∂E ∂n dn da′ . Now substitute the expression for da′: Eda= E+ ∂E ∂n dn da 1 + dn 1 R1 Cancel da: E= E+ Now expand to first order in dn. Multiplying out, ∂E ∂n dn 1 + dn 1 R1 + E= E+ ∂E ∂n dn+ Edn 1 R1 + 1 R2 76 + 1 R2 1 R2 . + O(dn2). .

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Subtract E from both sides: Divide by dn: Therefore Finally, divide by E: 0 = ∂E ∂n dn+ Edn 1 R1 0 = ∂E ∂n + E 1 R1 + ∂E ∂n=−E 1 R1 + + 1 R2 1 R2 1 R2 . . . 1 E ∂E ∂n=− 1 R1 + 1 R2 . 1 This is the required result. 3. Physical interpretation The quantity 1 + R1 R2 is twice the mean curvature of the surface. If the surface is locally convex outward, then R1 and R2 are positive, so ∂E ∂n <0. That means the electric field decreases as one moves outward away from the conductor. This makes physical sense. Near a convex surface, the field lines spread apart as they leave the conductor, so the field strength must decrease. Conversely, if the surface is locally concave with respect to the exterior region, the field lines can converge and the field can increase as one moves outward. 4. Checks with special cases Plane surface. For a plane, R1 = R2 = ∞. Hence 1 E ∂E ∂n = 0, so ∂E ∂n = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Thus the field is constant near a plane conductor, as expected. Sphere of radius R. For a sphere, R1 = R2 = R. Then 1 E ∂E ∂n=− 2 . R Since outside a charged sphere E(r) ∝ 1 r2 , we have 1 E dE dr=− 2 and at the surface r= R this becomes 1 E dE dr=− , r 2 R, exactly as required. 77 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Cylinder of radius R. For a cylinder, So so Outside a long charged cylinder and at r= R this is R1 = R, R2 = ∞. 1 E ∂E ∂n=− 1 E(r) ∝ r 1 dE dr=− 1 − R, 1 . R , 1 , E r again in agreement. Final result At the surface of a curved charged conductor, 1 E ∂E ∂n=− 1 R1 + 1 R2 , where R1 and R2 are the principal radii of curvature of the surface. Problem 1. 14 22. 1 Problem Statement Consider the electrostatic Green functions of Section 1. 10 for Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions on the surface S bounding the volume V. Apply Green’s theorem (1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 35) with integration variable z and ϕ= G(x,z), ψ= G(x′ ,z), with ∇2 zG(z,y) =−4πδ(y−z). Find an expression for the difference G(x,x′)−G(x′ ,x) in terms of an integral over the boundary surface S. (a) For Dirichlet boundary conditions on the potential and the associated boundary condition on the Green func- tion, show that GD(x,x′) must be symmetric in x and x′ . (b) For Neumann boundary conditions, use the boundary condition (1. 45) for GN(x,x′) to show that GN(x,x′) is not symmetric in general, but that GN(x,x′)−F(x) is symmetric in x and x′, where F(x) = 1 S S GN(x,y) day. (c) Show that the addition of F(x) to the Green function does not affect the potential Φ(x). 22. 2 Solution We begin from Green’s second identity in the volume V: V ϕ∇2ψ−ψ∇2ϕ dτ= S ϕ∂ψ ∂n−ψ∂ϕ ∂n da, where ∂/∂n denotes differentiation along the outward normal to the boundary surface S.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: We apply this identity with integration variable z and choose ϕ(z) = G(x,z), ψ(z) = G(x′ ,z). Then ∇2 zG(x,z) =−4πδ(x−z), ∇2 zG(x′ ,z) =−4πδ(x′ −z). 78 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 1. General reciprocity identity Substitute these into Green’s second identity: V G(x,z)∇2 zG(x′ ,z)−G(x′ ,z)∇2 zG(x,z) dτz = S G(x,z) ∂ ∂nz Using the defining Laplacian equations, [G(x,z)(−4πδ(x′ V G(x′ ,z)−G(x′ ,z) ∂ ∂nz G(x,z) daz. −z))−G(x′ ,z)(−4πδ(x−z))] dτz = S G(x,z) ∂ ∂nz Now evaluate the delta-function integrals: G(x′ ,z)−G(x′ ,z) ∂ ∂nz G(x,z) daz. −4πG(x,x′) + 4πG(x′ ,x) = S G(x,z) ∂ ∂nz G(x′ ,z)−G(x′ ,z) ∂ ∂nz Hence the difference is G(x,z) daz. G(x,x′)−G(x′ ,x) = 1 4π S G(x′ ,z) ∂ G(x,z)−G(x,z) ∂ ∂nz ∂nz G(x′ ,z) daz. for z ∈S. This is the general boundary-integral expression requested in the problem. (a) Dirichlet Green function For the Dirichlet Green function, GD(x,z) = 0 That is, the Green function itself vanishes on the boundary.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Therefore, in the boundary integral GD(x,z) ∂ ∂nz GD(x′ ,z)−GD(x′ ,z) ∂ GD(x,z), ∂nz each term contains a factor of GD evaluated on the boundary, hence each term is zero. So the entire boundary integral vanishes: GD(x,x′)−GD(x′ ,x) = 0. Therefore GD(x,x′) = GD(x′ ,x). So the Dirichlet Green function is symmetric in its two arguments. (b) Neumann Green function For the Neumann Green function, the boundary condition is not that GN itself vanish on the boundary, but rather that its normal derivative be fixed. In Section 1. 10 the boundary condition is ∂ ∂nz GN(x,z) =− 4π S , z ∈S, where S denotes the total area of the boundary surface. Substitute this boundary condition into the reciprocity formula: GN(x,x′)−GN(x′ ,x) = 1 4π S GN(x′ ,z)− 4π S−GN(x,z)− 4π S daz. Factor out the constant: GN(x,x′)−GN(x′ ,x) = 1 4π− 4π S S GN(x′ ,z) daz− 79 1 4π− 4π S S GN(x,z) daz.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Thus Define GN(x,x′)−GN(x′ ,x) =− 1 S S GN(x′ ,z) daz + F(x) = 1 S S GN(x,y) day. 1 S S GN(x,z) daz. GN(x,x′)−GN(x′ ,x) = F(x)−F(x′). Then the result becomes This shows immediately that GN is not symmetric in general, since the right-hand side need not vanish. Now rearrange: GN(x,x′)−F(x) = GN(x′ ,x)−F(x′). Hence the combination is symmetric in x and x′ . Equivalently, GN(x,x′)−F(x) GN(x,x′)−F(x) = GN(x′ ,x)−F(x′). (c) Addition of F(x) does not affect the potential We now show that adding F(x), which depends only on the field point x and not on the integration variable, does not change the potential obtained from the Neumann Green-function representation. For Poisson’s equation ∇2Φ(x) =− ρ(x) ε0 , Green’s representation with the Neumann Green function has the form Φ(x) = 1 4π S GN(x,z) ∂Φ ∂nz −Φ(z) ∂ ∂nz GN(x,z) daz− 1 4πε0 V GN(x,z)ρ(z) dτz. Now replace GN(x,z) −→GN(x,z) + F(x).

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Since F(x) is independent of z, its normal derivative with respect to the integration variable vanishes: ∂ ∂nz F(x) = 0. Therefore the only change in the representation formula is ∆Φ(x) = 1 4π F(x) S ∂Φ ∂nz daz− 1 4πε0 F(x) V ρ(z) dτz. Factor out F(x): ∆Φ(x) = F(x) 4π S ∂Φ ∂nz daz− 1 ε0 V ρ(z) dτz. Now use Poisson’s equation and the divergence theorem. Since ∇2Φ =− ρ ε0 , integrate over V: V But by Gauss’s theorem, V Therefore S ∇2Φ dτ=− 1 ε0 V ρdτ. ∇2Φ dτ= S ∂Φ ∂n da. ∂Φ ∂n da=− 1 ε0 V ρdτ. 80 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Hence S ∂Φ ∂n da− 1 ε0 V ρdτ = 0 with the sign convention used in the Green-function representation above. Therefore ∆Φ(x) = 0. So the potential is unchanged by the replacement GN(x,x′) −→GN(x,x′) + F(x). Thus Adding F(x) to the Neumann Green function does not affect Φ(x). Physical interpretation The Neumann Green function is not unique.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: One may add to it any function of the field point alone, provided this does not alter the representation of the physical potential. The reason is that for Neumann problems the potential itself is determined only up to an additive constant. The function F(x) represents precisely such a harmless ambiguity. By subtracting F(x), one can construct from GN a kernel that is symmetric in its two arguments, while leaving the actual physical potential unchanged. Final results G(x,x′)−G(x′ ,x) = 1 4π S G(x′ ,z) ∂ ∂nz G(x,z)−G(x,z) ∂ G(x′ ∂nz ,z) daz For Dirichlet boundary conditions, GD(x,x′) = GD(x′ ,x) For Neumann boundary conditions, GN(x,x′)−GN(x′ ,x) = F(x)−F(x′) with F(x) = 1 S S GN(x,y) day so that is symmetric. Finally, GN(x,x′)−F(x) = GN(x′ ,x)−F(x′) the addition of F(x) to GN does not change Φ(x). Problem 1. 12 23.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: 1 Problem Statement Prove Green’s reciprocation theorem: If Φ is the potential due to a volume-charge density ρwithin a volume V and a surface-charge density σ on the conducting surface S bounding the volume V, while Φ′ is the potential due to another charge distribution ρ′and σ′, then V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= V ρ′Φ d3x+ S σ ′Φ da. 81 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 23. 2 Solution We are given two electrostatic configurations in the same volume V bounded by the same conducting surface S: • the first has potential Φ, volume charge density ρ in V, and surface charge density σ on S; • the second has potential Φ′, volume charge density ρ′in V, and surface charge density σ′on S. We must prove the reciprocity relation V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= V ρ′Φ d3x+ S σ ′Φ da. The proof follows from Green’s second identity together with Poisson’s equation. 1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Green’s second identity For any two sufficiently smooth scalar functions ϕ and ψ in the volume V, Green’s second identity states V ϕ∇2ψ−ψ∇2ϕ d3x= S ϕ∂ψ ∂n−ψ∂ϕ ∂n da, where ∂/∂n denotes differentiation along the outward normal to the boundary surface S. We apply this identity with ϕ= Φ, ψ= Φ′ . Then V Φ∇2Φ′ −Φ′∇2Φ d3x= S Φ∂Φ′ ∂n−Φ′∂Φ ∂n da. 2. Use Poisson’s equation in the volume Inside the volume V, the potentials satisfy Poisson’s equation: ∇2Φ =− ρ , ∇2Φ′ Substitute these into the volume integral: V Φ− ρ′ ε0 −Φ′ ρ − ε0 d3x= S Φ∂Φ′ ∂n−Φ′∂Φ ∂n da. Simplify: 1 ε0 V Multiply both sides by ε0: V (ρΦ′ −ρ′Φ) d3x= S Φ∂Φ′ ∂n−Φ′∂Φ ∂n da. (ρΦ′ So far everything is standard and exact. 3. Express the normal derivatives in terms of surface charge densities Now we use the electrostatic boundary condition at a conductor surface. Inside a conductor, the electric field vanishes: Einside = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Just outside the conductor, the normal component of the electric field satisfies En = σ ε0 , ρ′ =− . ε0 ε0 −ρ′Φ) d3x= ε0 S Φ∂Φ′ ∂n−Φ′∂Φ ∂n da. where σ is the surface charge density. Since E= −∇Φ, 82 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions σ′ ε0 σ ε0 σ′ . . Substitute these into the surface integral: da. σΦ′da. the normal component is En =− ∂Φ ∂n. Hence − ∂Φ ∂n= σ ,− ∂Φ′ ∂n= Therefore ∂Φ ∂n=− , ε0 ∂Φ′ ∂n=− ε0 ε0 S Φ− σ′ ε0 −Φ′ − σ ε0 Simplify: ε0 S Φσ′ − + ε0 Φ′σ ε0 da= S (−Φσ ′+ Φ′ σ) da. Thus the identity becomes (ρΦ′ V −ρ′Φ) d3x= S (−Φσ ′+ Φ′ σ) da. Rearrange terms: V ρΦ′d3x− V ρ′Φ d3x=− S σ ′Φ da+ S Move the negative term on the right to the left-hand side: V ρΦ′d3x+ σ S ′Φ da= V ρ′Φ d3x+ σΦ′da. S Finally, simply rewrite the two surface terms in the same order as in the statement of the problem: V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= V ρ′Φ d3x+ σ ′Φ da. S This is Green’s reciprocation theorem. 4.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Physical interpretation The theorem says that if one charge distribution produces a potential Φ and another produces a potential Φ′, then the “interaction integral” is symmetric under interchange of the two distributions. The volume term ρΦ′d3x V is the interaction of the first volume charge distribution with the second potential, while the surface term σΦ′da S is the interaction of the first surface charge distribution with the second potential. The theorem states that the total interaction is unchanged if we interchange the roles of the two electrostatic configurations. This symmetry is a consequence of the self-adjoint nature of the Laplacian together with the electrostatic boundary conditions. 5.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Alternative compact derivation For completeness, the entire proof can be summarized in one chain: V Φ∇2Φ′ −Φ′∇2Φ d3x= S Φ∂Φ′ ∂n−Φ′∂Φ ∂n da = S Φ− σ′ ε0 −Φ′ σ − ε0 1 da= ε0 S (−Φσ ′+ Φ′ σ) da, while V Φ∇2Φ′ −Φ′∇2Φ d3x= V Φ− ρ′ ε0 −Φ′ − Equating the two expressions and multiplying by ε0 gives the result. ρ ε0 d3x= 1 ε0 V (ρΦ′ −ρ′Φ) d3x. 83 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Final result V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= V ρ′Φ d3x+ S σ ′Φ da. So Green’s reciprocation theorem is proved. Problem 1. 13 24. 1 Problem Statement Two infinite grounded parallel conducting planes are separated by a distance d. A point charge q is placed between the planes. Use the reciprocation theorem of Green to prove that the total induced charge on one of the planes is equal to (−q) ×the fractional perpendicular distance of the point charge from the other plane.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: (Hint: As your comparison electrostatic problem with the same surfaces choose one whose charge densities and potential are known and simple. ) 24. 2 Solution Let the two infinite grounded conducting planes be z= 0 and z= d. A point charge q is placed at z= a, 0 <a<d. We want to find the total induced charge on each plane. Let Q0 = total induced charge on the plane z= 0, Qd = total induced charge on the plane z= d. Because both planes are grounded, the potentials on both surfaces are zero. We will use Green’s reciprocation theorem: V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= V ρ′Φ d3x+ S σ ′Φ da. The idea is to compare the actual problem with a second electrostatic problem whose potential is especially simple. 1.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: The actual electrostatic problem In the actual problem: • the volume charge density is that of a point charge q at z= a, ρ(r) = qδ(x)δ(y)δ(z−a), • the conductor surfaces z= 0 and z= d are grounded, so Φ = 0 on both planes, • the induced surface charge densities on the two planes are some functions σ0(x,y), σd(x,y), whose total charges are Q0 = z=0 σ0 da, Qd = z=d We do not need the detailed charge densities; we only want the totals. σdda. 84 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 2. Choose the comparison problem We now choose a comparison problem in the same region between the same planes, but with no volume charge and with a simple known potential. Take the potential Φ′(z) = z d. This clearly satisfies Laplace’s equation: ∇2Φ′= 0, because it is linear in z. Also, Φ′(0) = 0, Φ′(d) = 1. So this corresponds to the electrostatic problem in which the plane z = 0 is held at zero potential and the plane z= d is held at unit potential.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: In this comparison problem, there is no volume charge: ρ′= 0. Its surface charge densities are not needed explicitly. What matters is that the potential on the surfaces is known: Φ′= 0 on z= 0, Φ′= 1 on z= d. This is exactly the “simple” comparison problem suggested in the hint. 3. Apply Green’s reciprocation theorem Write Green’s reciprocation theorem for the two problems: V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= V Now simplify term by term. ρ′Φ d3x+ S σ ′Φ da. Right-hand side: volume term Since in the comparison problem ρ′= 0, we have V ρ′Φ d3x= 0. Right-hand side: surface term In the actual problem the two planes are grounded, so Φ = 0 on S. Hence S Therefore the entire right-hand side is zero: V σ ′Φ da= 0. ρ′Φ d3x+ S σ ′Φ da= 0. So Green’s theorem reduces to V ρΦ′d3x+ S σΦ′da= 0. 85 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 4. Evaluate the volume integral Since ρ(r) = qδ(x)δ(y)δ(z−a), we get V But so Hence V 5.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Evaluate the surface integral The boundary surface consists of the two planes: ρΦ′d3x= qΦ′(a). Φ′(z) = z d, Φ′(a) = a d. a ρΦ′d3x= q d. S= S0 ∪Sd, where S0 is the plane z= 0 and Sd is the plane z= d. Thus S σΦ′da= S0 On the lower plane z= 0, σΦ′da+ Sd Φ′= 0, σΦ′da= 0. σΦ′da. so S0 On the upper plane z= d, Φ′= 1, so Sd σΦ′da= σda= Qd. Sd Therefore S σΦ′da= Qd. 6. Solve for the induced charge on the upper plane Substituting the two evaluated terms into the reciprocity relation gives a q d + Qd = 0. Hence a Qd =−q d. Now observe that a is the perpendicular distance of the point charge from the plane z = 0, while the distance from the other plane z= 0 when considering the plane z= d is indeed a. So the induced charge on the plane z= d equals (−q) ×distance from the other plane d . That is exactly the statement in the problem. 86 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 7.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text: Find the induced charge on the lower plane To get the induced charge on the lower plane z= 0, choose instead the comparison potential Φ′′(z) = 1− z d. Then Φ′′(0) = 1, Φ′′(d) = 0, and again ∇2Φ′′= 0. Applying Green’s reciprocation theorem exactly as before: V ρΦ′′d3x+ σΦ′′da= 0. S The volume term is ρΦ′′d3x= qΦ′′(a) = q 1− V a d= q d−a d . The surface term is now S σΦ′′da= S0 σda+ Sd σ·0 da= Q0. Thus q d−a d + Q0 = 0, so Q0 =−q d−a d . This again has the desired form: the induced charge on the plane z= 0 is (−q) ×distance from the other plane z= d d . 8. Check of the total induced charge Add the two results: d−a a d Q0 + Qd =−q d−q d=−q d=−q. Q0 + Qd =−q. So This is exactly what we should expect physically. Since the two grounded planes plus the point charge form an overall neutral arrangement in the field region, the total induced charge on the conductors must be equal and opposite to the point charge. 9.

chunks.title: chapter 1 jackson problems
chunks.text:
Physical interpretation The result says that the total induced charge does not split equally between the two planes unless the point charge is halfway between them. Instead, the plane that is closer to the point charge receives the larger fraction of the induced charge. Indeed: • if a≪d, the charge is close to the lower plane, and Q0 ≈−q, Qd ≈0; • if a= d/2, then Q0 = Qd =− q 2; • if d−a≪d, the charge is close to the upper plane, and Qd ≈−q, Q0 ≈0. So the result is very natural physically, but Green’s reciprocation theorem gives it immediately without solving the full image-charge problem. 87 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Final result If the point charge q is at perpendicular distances a from the plane z= 0, d−a from the plane z= d, then the total induced charges are Q0 =−q d−a d , a Qd =−q d.

Equivalently, the total induced charge on either plane equals (−q) ×the fractional perpendicular distance of the point charge from the other plane. 88

chunks.text: Using the method of images, find: (a) the surface-charge density induced on the plane, and plot it; (b) the force between the plane and the charge by using Coulomb’s law for the force between the charge and its image; (c) the total force acting on the plane by integrating σ2/2ε0 over the whole plane; (d) the work necessary to remove the charge q from its position to infinity; (e) the potential energy between the charge q and its image [compare the answer to part d and discuss]; (f) Find the answer to part d in electron volts for an electron originally one angstrom from the surface. 1. 2 Solution Let the grounded conducting plane be the plane and let the real charge q be located at = 0. z= 0, (0,0,d), d>0. The physical region is z>0. By the method of images, the potential in the physical region is the same as that produced by • the real charge q at (0,0,d), • an image charge−q at (0,0,−d), with no conductor present.

By Coulomb’s law, the magnitude of the force is F= 1 4πε0 q2 (2d)2 = 16πε0d2. The force is attractive, directed toward the plane, that is, in the− ˆ z direction. Therefore F=− ˆ q2 z 16πε0d2. This pressure acts normal to the surface, pushing outward from the conductor into the field region. For the plane, that means the field pulls the plane upward toward the charge. The total force on the plane is therefore F= pda= σ2 2ε0 da. Using cylindrical coordinates, da= 2πρdρ.

(e) Potential energy between the charge and its image If we naively treat the real charge and the image charge as two actual point charges separated by 2d, their mutual Coulomb potential energy is Upair= 1 4πε0 q(−q) 2d=− q2 8πε0d. So Upair=− q2 8πε0d. But this is not the actual electrostatic energy of the physical system consisting of one real charge plus the conductor. From part (d), the actual energy change relative to infinity is Uphys=−Wremove =− q2 16πε0d. Thus Uphys=− q2 16πε0d. This is exactly one-half of the naive charge-image interaction energy: 1 Uphys= 2Upair. 6 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Why the factor 1/2? The image charge is not a real independent charge distribution; it is only a mathematical device for reproducing the field in the physical region.

Indeed, the image potential at the location of the real charge is Φimage= 1 4πε0 −q 2d=− q 8πε0d. So 1 U= 2qΦimage= 1 2q− q 8πε0d=− 16πε0d, which agrees with part (d).
