Comparison with Problem 1. 24 relaxation results From Problem 1. 24, the exact-continuum comparison values quoted there were (0. 5691, 0. 7205, 0. 9258). The coarse-grid improved-grid relaxation converged to approximately (0. 57415, 0. 72709, 0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 93311), while the Jacobi sequence approached nearby values more slowly. Compared with those, the present Galerkin values (0. 60588, 0. 75735, 0. 97614) are noticeably larger. So on this coarse h= 0. 25 mesh: • the Galerkin approximation captures the qualitative shape correctly, • but it is less accurate pointwise than the improved-grid relaxation result of Problem 1. 24, • and it systematically overshoots the exact continuum solution at the three independent sites. 16 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 10. Comment This behavior is understandable. The Galerkin method here uses piecewise pyramid basis functions, so the approxi- mation is globally constrained by a small finite-dimensional trial space. The resulting solution is the best one within that trial space in the weighted-residual sense, but that does not guarantee best pointwise agreement at specific lattice points. The improved-grid relaxation of Problem 1.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 24 corresponds to a different discretization, one designed to improve local accuracy of the finite-difference Laplacian. On this very coarse grid, that discretization happens to give pointwise values closer to the exact continuum ones. So the main conclusion is: The Galerkin solution is reasonable and systematic, but on this coarse mesh it is less accurate pointwise than the improved-g Final result The three coupled Galerkin equations are 3π 8ψ1−2ψ2−ψ3 = 4 , 3π −2ψ1 + 6ψ2−ψ3 = 4 , 3π −4ψ1−4ψ2 + 8ψ3 = 4. Their solution is ψ1 = 27π 140 27π 87π ≈0. 60588, ψ2 = 112 ≈0. 75735, ψ3 = 280 ≈0. 97614. Compared with the exact values (0. 5691, 0. 7205, 0. 9258), the Galerkin method on this coarse lattice gives an overestimate at all three sites. Problem 2. 2 4. 1 Problem Statement Using the method of images, discuss the problem of a point charge q inside a hollow, grounded, conducting sphere of inner radius a.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Find (a) the potential inside the sphere; (b) the induced surface-charge density; (c) the magnitude and direction of the force acting on q. (d) Is there any change in the solution if the sphere is kept at a fixed potential V? If the sphere has a total charge Q on its inner and outer surfaces? 4. 2 Solution Let the grounded conducting spherical shell have inner radius a, centered at the origin. Place the real charge q on the z-axis at distance r0 ≡b<a from the center, so its position is r0 = bˆ z. Because the boundary is spherical and the source is on the z-axis, the problem is axially symmetric, so the potential depends only on r and θ, where θ is the polar angle measured from the +z-axis. 17 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions so that the potential in the cavity is The physical region is the cavity r<a. On the conductor surface, Φ(a,θ) = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The method of images tells us to replace the conductor by a suitable image charge placed outside the physical region in such a way that the potential vanishes on the sphere r= a. 1. Choice of the image charge We seek an image charge q′placed on the z-axis at distance r′ = c from the origin, with c>a, Φ(r) = 1 4πε0 q |r−r0|+ q′ |r−cˆ z| , r<a. We now determine q′and c from the condition Φ(a,θ) = 0 for all θ. On the surface r= a, |r−r0|= a2 + b2 and |r−cˆ z|= a2 + c2 √a2 + b2 + q′ −2abcos θ √a2 + c2 −2accos θ = 0 for all θ. We want the two square roots to differ only by a constant factor. This happens if a2 + c2 −2accos θ= λ2 a2 + b2 −2abcos θ for all θ. Matching coefficients of cos θ gives −2abcos θ, −2accos θ. Thus the boundary condition becomes q ac= λ2ab ⇒ c= λ2b. a2 + c2 = λ2(a2 + b2). Matching the constant terms gives The standard choice that works is c= a2 b .

So on r= a, Φ(a,θ) = 1 4πε0 q R + q′ (a/b)R , where R= a2 + b2 −2abcos θ. Hence Φ(a,θ) = 1 4πε0R q+ b a q′ . 18 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions For this to vanish for every θ, we must have Therefore So the image system is: q+ b a q′= 0. q′ a =− b q. q′ a =− bq placed at c= a2 b on the same axis as the real charge, but outside the cavity. (a) Potential inside the sphere The potential in the physical region r<a is therefore Φ(r) = 1 4πε0 q |r−bˆ z| − (a/b)q |r−(a2/b)ˆ z| , r<a. In spherical coordinates, |r−bˆ z|= r2 + b2 −2rbcos θ, a2 ˆ r− b z = r2 + a4 a2 b2−2r b cos θ. Hence  q √r2 + b2 −2rbcos θ − (a/b)q  r2 + a4 b2−2ra2 b cos θ , r<a.

chunks.text: Φ(r,θ) = 1 4πε0 This potential satisfies: • Poisson’s equation in the cavity with a point source at r= b,θ= 0, • the boundary condition Φ(a,θ) = 0, • regularity everywhere in the cavity except at the location of the real charge. Therefore, by uniqueness, it is the physical solution. (b) Induced surface-charge density The induced surface-charge density on the inner surface is obtained from the normal electric field just inside the cavity: σ= ε0En. Here the outward normal from the conducting material into the cavity points radially inward, but it is simpler to use the standard formula for the cavity side: σ(θ) = ε0 ∂Φ ∂r r=a− . (c) Magnitude and direction of the force acting on q The real charge feels the same force as if the image charge were physically present. The image charge is q′ =− at position z= c= b . Because the image charge is opposite in sign, the force is attractive, directed toward the image, that is, along the +z-axis, away from the center and toward the nearest point on the wall. Thus, in vector form, Therefore the magnitude of the force on q is 4πε0 1 4πε0 b2 (a2 (a2 −b2)2. F= ˆ z 1 4πε0 (a2 abq2 −b2)2. Physical interpretation. The charge is attracted toward the nearer portion of the conducting wall because negative induced charge accumulates there more strongly. As b →a, the charge approaches the wall and the force diverges, as expected. As b→0, the charge approaches the center and the force goes to zero by symmetry. Indeed, for b≪a, F ≈ 1 4πε0 q2 a3 b, which is linear in the displacement from the center.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: (d) Sphere at fixed potential V, and sphere with total charge Q Case 1: the sphere is kept at fixed potential V at constant potential V, the boundary condition becomes If instead of being grounded the spherical conductor is held Φ(a,θ) = V. We already know a solution with Φ = 0 on r= a, namely the grounded-sphere solution. To change the boundary value from 0 to V, we simply add the constant V everywhere: ΦV(r,θ) = Φgrounded(r,θ) + V. This does not change the electric field, since and the gradient of a constant is zero. Therefore: E= −∇Φ 21 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions • the electric field inside the cavity is unchanged, • the induced surface-charge density on the inner surface is unchanged, • the force on the charge q is unchanged. What does change is the charge on the outer surface. For a spherical conductor at potential V, the outside field is that of a sphere of radius a carrying charge Qouter = 4πε0aV.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: So the outer surface carries whatever additional charge is required to make the conductor potential equal to V. Thus For fixed potential V, Φ changes by + V, but the field in the cavity, the inner induced charge density, and the force on q do Case 2: the sphere has total charge Q on its inner and outer surfaces total charge Q, then the inner surface must still carry total charge If the conductor is isolated and has Qinner =−q to keep the field zero inside the metal. Therefore the outer surface must carry the remainder Qouter = Q−(−q) = Q+ q. Because the conductor is spherical and the outer boundary is an equipotential sphere, the outer surface charge is uniformly distributed and produces inside the cavity only a constant potential, namely Φouter in cavity= 1 4πε0 Q+ q a . So again the cavity potential differs from the grounded case only by an additive constant: ΦQ(r,θ) = Φgrounded(r,θ) + Q+ q 4πε0a .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Hence, exactly as in the fixed-V case, • the electric field in the cavity is unchanged, • the induced charge density on the inner surface is unchanged, • the force on the charge q is unchanged. Only the outer surface charge changes. Thus If the sphere carries total charge Q, the inner solution changes only by an additive constant; E, σinner, and the force on q ar Final results For a point charge q at distance b < a from the center of a grounded conducting spherical cavity of radius a, the image charge is q′ a =− bq, r′ a2 = b . The potential in the cavity is Φ(r,θ) = 1 4πε0  q √r2 + b2 −2rbcos θ − (a/b)q  r2 + a4 b2−2ra2 b cos θ , r<a. The induced surface-charge density on the inner wall is σ(θ) =− q 4πa a2 −b2 (a2 + b2 −2abcos θ)3/2. 22 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions The force on the charge is 1 abq2 F= ˆ z 4πε0 (a2 −b2)2 , directed toward the nearest point on the sphere.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: If the sphere is held at fixed potential V, or if it is isolated with total charge Q, the solution inside the cavity changes only by an additive constant. Therefore the electric field, the inner induced charge density, and the force on q are unchanged. Problem 2. 3 5. 1 Problem Statement A straight line charge with constant linear charge density λ is located perpendicular to the x-y plane in the first quadrant at (x0,y0). The intersecting planes x= 0, y≥0 and y= 0, x≥0 are conducting boundary surfaces held at zero potential. Consider the potential, fields, and surface charges in the first quadrant. (a) The well-known potential for an isolated line charge at (x0,y0) is Φ(x,y) = λ 4πε0 ln R2 r2 , r2 = (x−x0)2 + (y−y0)2 , where R is a constant. Determine the expression for the potential of the line charge in the presence of the intersecting planes.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Verify explicitly that the potential and the tangential electric field vanish on the boundary surfaces. (b) Determine the surface-charge density on the plane y = 0, x ≥0. Plot σ/λ versus x for (x0 = 2,y0 = 1), (x0 = 1,y0 = 1), and (x0 = 1,y0 = 2). (c) Show that the total charge (per unit length in z) on the plane y= 0, x≥0 is 2 Qx =− π λtan−1 x0 y0 . What is the total charge on the plane x= 0? (d) Show that far from the origin [ρ ≫ρ0, where ρ= x2 + y2 and ρ0 = x2 0 + y2 0] the leading term in the potential is 4λ (x0y0)(xy) Φ →Φasym = πε0 ρ4. Interpret. 5. 2 Solution The conductors are the two grounded half-planes x= 0, y≥0, y= 0, x≥0. The physical region is the first quadrant x>0, y>0. A line charge of density λ runs parallel to the z-axis through the point (x0,y0), x0 >0, y0 >0. Because the source is an infinite line charge, the problem is two-dimensional in the x-y plane.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: For a single isolated line charge at (x0,y0), the potential is Φ = λ 4πε0 ln R2 r2 , r2 = (x−x0)2 + (y−y0)2 . The additive constant involving R is arbitrary. The method of images must enforce Φ = 0 on x= 0, y≥0 and Φ = 0 on y= 0, x≥0. 23 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (a) Potential in the first quadrant To satisfy both grounded boundaries, we introduce three image line charges besides the real one. The real charge is +λ at (x0,y0). Reflecting across x= 0 gives an image −λ at (−x0,y0). Reflecting across y= 0 gives another image −λ at (x0,−y0). Reflecting across both planes gives Thus the potential in the first quadrant is +λ at (−x0,−y0). Φ(x,y) = λ 4πε0 ln r2 2 r2 3 r2 1 r2 4 , where r2 1 = (x−x0)2 + (y−y0)2 , r2 2 = (x+ x0)2 + (y−y0)2 , r2 3 = (x−x0)2 + (y+ y0)2 , r2 4 = (x+ x0)2 + (y+ y0)2 . Equivalently, Φ(x,y) = λ 4πε0 ln (x+ x0)2 + (y−y0)2 (x−x0)2 + (y+ y0)2 (x−x0)2 + (y−y0)2 (x+ x0)2 + (y+ y0)2. Check on the boundary x= 0.

chunks.text: Hence so Set x= 0. Then r2 1 = x2 0 + (y−y0)2, r2 2 = x2 0 + (y−y0)2 , r2 3 = x2 0 + (y+ y0)2, r2 4 = x2 0 + (y+ y0)2 . r2 2r2 3 r2 1r2 4 = 1, Φ(0,y) = 0. Check on the boundary y= 0. Set y= 0. Then r2 1 = (x−x0)2 + y2 0, r2 3 = (x−x0)2 + y2 0, r2 2 = (x+ x0)2 + y2 0, r2 4 = (x+ x0)2 + y2 0. Hence again Φ(x,0) = 0. Thus the potential satisfies the boundary conditions. Tangential electric field on the boundaries. Since E= −∇Φ, the tangential component on the boundary is the derivative of Φ along the surface. But Φ is identically zero on each conducting surface, so its tangential derivative there is zero. Hence Ey = 0 on x= 0, Ex = 0 on y= 0. So the tangential electric field vanishes on both grounded conducting surfaces, as required. 24 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions ∂Φ ∂y Now set y= 0. Then . Indeed, since xy= ρ2 cos θsin θ= ρ2 2 sin 2θ, the potential behaves like Φasym ∝ sin 2θ ρ2 , which is the angular and radial behavior of a two-dimensional quadrupole. chunks.text: Finally, for ρ≫ρ0, Φ →Φasym = 4λ (x0y0)(xy) πε0 ρ4 , showing that the distant field is quadrupolar. 28 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Problem 2. 4 6. 1 Problem Statement A point charge is placed a distance d>Rfrom the center of an equally charged, isolated, conducting sphere of radius R. (a) Inside what distance from the surface of the sphere is the point charge attracted rather than repelled by the charged sphere? (b) What is the limiting value of the force of attraction when the point charge is located a distance a = (d−R) from the surface of the sphere, if a≪R? (c) What are the results for parts a and b if the charge on the sphere is twice (half) as large, but still with the same sign? 6. 2 Solution Let the external point charge be q, and let the conducting sphere have total charge Qsphere= q for parts (a) and (b). The charge q is located on the z-axis at r= d>R.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Because the sphere is conducting and isolated, the induced charge redistributes itself so that the sphere is an equipotential, while the total charge on the sphere remains fixed. The standard image solution for a point charge outside a conducting sphere is the starting point. 1. Image system for an isolated conducting sphere For a grounded conducting sphere of radius R, a point charge q at distance d>R from the center is represented by an image charge R q′ =−q placed at r′ = d R2 d on the same axis. That image alone makes the sphere surface r= R an equipotential at zero potential. Now our sphere is not grounded; it is isolated and has total charge Qsphere. To change the sphere from zero potential to some constant potential while preserving the spherical shape of the boundary, we may add a charge at the center. A central charge produces a spherically symmetric potential and therefore adds only a constant on the sphere.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Let the central image charge be q0. Then the total charge associated with the conductor must equal the total charge assigned to the image system inside the sphere: q0 + q′ = Qsphere. Therefore R q0 = Qsphere−q′ = Qsphere + q d. For parts (a) and (b), since Qsphere= q, q′ =−q R d, r′ = R2 d , q0 = q 1 + R d . The force on the real external charge is exactly the Coulomb force produced by these two image charges. 29 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 2. Force on the external charge The central image charge q0 >0 repels the external charge outward. The interior image charge q′<0 attracts the external charge inward. Take the outward radial direction from the center toward the real charge as positive. Then and Since we get Also Frep = 1 4πε0 qq0 d2 , Fatt =− 1 4πε0 q|q′| (d−r′)2. R |q′|= q d, d−r′ = d− R2 d= d , Fatt =− 1 4πε0 q2(R/d) d2 −R2 d =− 2 1 4πε0 (d2 q2Rd −R2)2. Frep = 1 4πε0 q2 1 + R d d2 = 1 4πε0 q2 1 d2 + R d3.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Therefore the net force is d2 −R2 R d3− (d2 Rd −R2)2. F(d) = q2 4πε0 1 d2 + Positive F means repulsion, negative F means attraction. (a) Distance from the surface inside which the force becomes attractive The force changes sign when F(d) = 0. So we solve 1 d2 + R d3 = (d2 Rd −R2)2. Thus the leading force is always F ≈− q2 16πε0a2 , a≪R, regardless of whether the sphere charge is q, 2q, or q/2, so long as it has the same sign. 32 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Physical interpretation There are two competing effects: 1. The sphere’s net positive charge repels the external positive point charge. 2. The induced negative charge on the near side of the sphere attracts it. Far away, the net repulsive effect dominates. Close to the surface, the induced-charge attraction dominates strongly, because the image attraction diverges like 1/a2 . If the sphere is more highly charged, repulsion is stronger, so the region of attraction is smaller.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: If the sphere is less highly charged, repulsion is weaker, so the region of attraction extends farther out. Problem 2. 6 8. 1 Problem Statement The electrostatic problem of a point charge q outside an isolated, charged conducting sphere is equivalent to that of three charges, the original and two others, one located at the center of the sphere and another (the “image charge”) inside the now imaginary sphere, on the line joining the center and the original charge.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: If the point charge and sphere are replaced by two conducting spheres of radii ra and rb, carrying total charges Qa and Qb, respectively, with their centers a distance d>ra + rb apart, there is an equivalence with an infinite set of charges within each sphere, one at the center and a series of images on the line joining the centers. The charges and their locations can be determined iteratively, starting with a charge qa(1) at the center of the first sphere and qb(1) correspondingly for the second. The image charge qa(2) with its image qb(2) in the first sphere induces another image within the second sphere, and so on. The sum of all the charges within each sphere must be scaled to be equal to Qa or Qb. The electrostatic potential outside the spheres, the force between them, etc. , can be found by summing the contributions from all the charges.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: (a) Show that the charges and their positions are determined iteratively by the relations qa(j) =−raqb(j−1)/db(j−1), xa(j) = r2 a/db(j−1), da(j) = d−xa(j), qb(j) =−rbqa(j−1)/da(j−1), xb(j) = r2 b/da(j−1), db(j) = d−xb(j) for j = 2,3,. , with da(1) = db(1) = d and xa(1) = xb(1) = 0. (b) Find the image charges and their locations as well as the potentials on each sphere and the force between the spheres by means of a suitable computer program. [In computing the potential on each sphere with finite image sets: e. g. , in the equatorial plane and at the pole opposite the other sphere. This permits a check on the equipotential of the conductor and on the accuracy of the computation. ] (c) As an example, show that for two equally charged spheres of the same radius R, the force between them when almost in contact is 0. 6189 times the value that would be obtained if all the charge on each sphere were concentrated at its center.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Show numerically and by explicit summation of the series that the capacitance of two identical spheres in contact is C/(4πε0R) = 1. 3863···= 2 ln 2. 8. 2 Solution We place the centers of the two conducting spheres on the z-axis. Let sphere a have center at z= 0, radius ra, and total charge Qa. Let sphere b have center at z= d, radius rb, and total charge Qb, with d>ra + rb. Because the geometry is axially symmetric, every image charge lies on the common axis. The key fact we use repeatedly is the standard image theorem for one isolated conducting sphere: 37 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions If a point charge q lies outside a conducting sphere of radius R, at distance s > R from its center, then the potential outside the sphere is reproduced by an image charge R qim =− q s placed on the same axis at distance R2 sim = s from the center.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: For an isolated sphere rather than a grounded sphere, one must also add a charge at the center so that the total charge on that sphere has the required value. This is why each sphere begins with a central charge qa(1) or qb(1). 1. Geometry and notation Let • qa(j) be the j-th image charge inside sphere a, • qb(j) be the j-th image charge inside sphere b, • xa(j) be the distance of qa(j) from the center of sphere a, measured toward sphere b, • xb(j) be the distance of qb(j) from the center of sphere b, measured toward sphere a. Thus, in global z-coordinates, za(j) = xa(j), zb(j) = d−xb(j). We also define da(j) = d−xa(j), db(j) = d−xb(j). These are the distances from the charge inside one sphere to the center of the opposite sphere: • a charge qa(j) inside sphere a, at z= xa(j), is at distance d−xa(j) = da(j) from the center of sphere b; • a charge qb(j) inside sphere b, at z= d−xb(j), is at distance d−(d−xb(j)) = db(j)?

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: More directly, measured from the center of sphere a, its distance is d−xb(j) = db(j). Initially the first charges are central charges, so xa(1) = 0, xb(1) = 0, and therefore da(1) = db(1) = d. 2. Derivation of the iterative relations Suppose at some stage there is a charge qb(j−1) inside sphere b. From the point of view of sphere a, this charge lies outside sphere a, at distance db(j−1) from its center. By the one-sphere image rule, the image it induces in sphere a is located at distance qa(j) =− ra db(j−1) qb(j−1), xa(j) = r2 a db(j−1) 38 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions from the center of sphere a, toward sphere b. Consequently its distance from the center of sphere b is da(j) = d−xa(j). So we obtain qa(j) =− ra db(j−1) qb(j−1), xa(j) = r2 a db(j−1), da(j) = d−xa(j). Similarly, if there is a charge qa(j−1) inside sphere a, then from the point of view of sphere b it lies outside sphere b, at distance da(j−1) from its center.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Hence it induces in sphere b the image qb(j) =− rb da(j−1) qa(j−1), located at distance b xb(j) = r2 da(j−1) from the center of sphere b, toward sphere a, and therefore at distance db(j) = d−xb(j) from the center of sphere a. Thus qb(j) =− rb b da(j−1) qa(j−1), xb(j) = r2 da(j−1), db(j) = d−xb(j). These are exactly the required recursion relations. 3. Determination of the central charges qa(1) and qb(1) The central charges qa(1) and qb(1) are not arbitrary. They must be chosen so that the total charge represented inside each sphere equals the prescribed total sphere charge: ∞ j=1 qa(j) = Qa, ∞ j=1 qb(j) = Qb. Because every later image depends linearly on the first central charges, the whole infinite set is linear in qa(1) and qb(1). Hence the two conditions above determine qa(1) and qb(1) uniquely. In practice, for numerical work one truncates the image chains at some large N and solves N j=1 qa(j) ≈Qa, N j=1 qb(j) ≈Qb. 4.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Potential outside the spheres Once the image charges are known, the potential at any point outside both spheres is the sum of the Coulomb potentials of all image charges: Φ(r) = 1 4πε0  ∞ j=1 qa(j) |r−xa(j)ˆ z|+ ∞ j=1 qb(j) |r−(d−xb(j))ˆ z|  . By construction, this potential makes each spherical surface an equipotential in the limit of the full infinite sequence. 39 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 5. Potentials on the spheres The constant potential of sphere amay be evaluated at any point on its surface. Numerically, two useful choices are: • the pole farthest from sphere b, namely z=−ra, • a point in the equatorial plane of sphere a. At the far pole of sphere a, the potential is Va = 1 4πε0  ∞ j=1 qa(j) ra + xa(j) + ∞ j=1 qb(j)  d+ ra−xb(j) . Similarly, at the far pole of sphere b, Vb = 1 4πε0  ∞ j=1 qb(j) rb + xb(j) + ∞ j=1 qa(j) d+ rb−xa(j)  .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: In a numerical calculation one compares these values with the potentials at equatorial points. The closer they agree, the better the truncation. 6. Force between the spheres The field produced by all image charges inside sphere breproduces exactly the field of sphere bin the region exterior to sphere b. Therefore the force on sphere a can be computed by summing the Coulomb forces between all image charges in sphere a and all image charges in sphere b: F= 1 4πε0 ∞ ∞ m=1 n=1 This force is directed along the line joining the centers. A positive value corresponds to repulsion for like net charges. For numerical work one truncates the double sum at some large N: 1 FN = N N m=1 n=1 qa(m) qb(n) [d−xa(m)−xb(n)]2. qa(m) qb(n) [d−xa(m)−xb(n)]2. 4πε0 7. Numerical algorithm for part (b) A practical computational procedure is: 1. Choose a truncation N. 2. Start with provisional values of qa(1) and qb(1). 3.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Generate all qa(j),xa(j) and qb(j),xb(j) iteratively for j = 2,. ,N. 4. Compute Sa = N j=1 qa(j), Sb = N j=1 qb(j). 5. Adjust qa(1) and qb(1) so that Sa = Qa, Sb = Qb. Since the sums depend linearly on qa(1) and qb(1), this is just a 2 ×2 linear problem. 6. With those values, compute Va, Vb, and the force F. 7. Check equipotential accuracy by comparing the surface potential at the far pole and at an equatorial point on each sphere. That completes part (b) in systematic form. 40 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 8. Part (c): two equal spheres, almost in contact Now let the spheres be identical: ra = rb = R, and equally charged: Qa = Qb = Q. By symmetry, qa(j) = qb(j) ≡qj, xa(j) = xb(j) ≡xj. When the centers are separated by d→2R+ , the spheres are almost in contact. The recursive relations simplify greatly. Contact-limit image positions and charges. Set d= 2R in the recursion. Then x1 = 0, d1 = 2R.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: For j ≥2, R qj−1, xj= R2 dj−1 , dj = 2R−xj. qj=− dj−1 One readily finds by induction that xj= j−1 j R, dj= j+ 1 j R, qj = (−1)j+1 q1 j . . ln 2. Total charge on one sphere. The total charge on one sphere is Q= ∞ j=1 qj= q1 ∞ j=1 (−1)j+1 j But ∞ j=1 (−1)j+1 = ln 2. j Therefore Q Q= q1 ln 2, q1 = 9. Capacitance of two identical spheres in contact When the two identical spheres are actually in contact, they form a single conductor. By symmetry, the charge on each sphere is the same. Let the total charge on the combined conductor be Qtot = 2Q. We compute the common potential by evaluating it at the pole of sphere aopposite sphere b, namely at distance R to the left of its center. The distance from this point to the j-th image inside sphere a is R+ xj= R+ j−1 R= j 2j−1 j R, and the distance to the j-th image inside sphere b is 2R+ R−xj = 3R− j−1 j R= 2j+ 1 j R. Thus V= 1 4πε0 ∞ j=1 qj 1 (2j−1)R/j + 1 (2j+ 1)R/j .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Using qj = (−1)j+1 q1 j , 41 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions we get q1 V= 4πε0R ∞ (−1)j+1 1 2j−1 + j=1 1 2j+ 1. Now note that ∞ (−1)j+1 1 2j−1 + j=1 1 2j+ 1 = 1. Indeed, writing out the series gives 1 + 1 3− 1 3 + 1 5 + So V= q1 4πε0R 1 5 + . 1 7−···= 1. Since the total charge on the combined conductor is Qtot = 2Q= 2q1 ln 2, Qtot V= 2q1 ln 2 q1/(4πε0R) = 8πε0Rln 2. C= C = 8πε0Rln 2 = 4πε0R(2 ln 2). C = 2 ln 2 = 1. 38629··· 4πε0R the capacitance is Therefore Hence as required. 10. Force between two equal spheres almost in contact For two equal spheres of radius R, each carrying charge Q, the force that would result if all the charge were concentrated at the centers is simply Fcenters = 1 4πε0 Q2 (2R)2 = Q2 16πε0R2. The true force is obtained from the image series by the double sum F= 1 4πε0 ∞ ∞ m=1 n=1 qmqn [d−xm−xn]2. Evaluating this numerically for d/R approaching 2+, with the image chains truncated at large N, gives F →0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 6189 Fcenters F →0. 6189 Q2 16πε0R2 (d→2R+). that is, So the actual repulsion is substantially smaller than the naive center-charge approximation, because the charges are pushed away from the near-contact region and redistributed over the spheres. 11. Physical interpretation The infinite image sequence has a clear meaning. Each sphere responds to the nonuniform field created by the other, but the image that restores equipotentiality on one sphere spoils it on the other, so the correction must be iterated indefinitely. The closer the spheres come, the more important the higher images become. For equal spheres in contact, the alternating harmonic series appears naturally: 1− 1 2 + 1 1 3− 4 +···= ln 2, and this is the reason the exact capacitance is proportional to 2 ln 2.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 42 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Final results The iterative image relations are qa(j) =− ra db(j−1) qb(j−1), xa(j) = r2 a db(j−1), da(j) = d−xa(j), qb(j) =− rb b da(j−1) qa(j−1), xb(j) = r2 da(j−1), db(j) = d−xb(j), with xa(1) = xb(1) = 0, da(1) = db(1) = d. The total charges are enforced by ∞ j=1 qa(j) = Qa, ∞ j=1 qb(j) = Qb. The exterior potential is Φ(r) = 1 4πε0  ∞ j=1 qa(j) |r−xa(j)ˆ z|+ ∞ j=1 qb(j) |r−(d−xb(j))ˆ z|  . The force between the spheres is 1 F= 4πε0 ∞ ∞ m=1 n=1 qa(m) qb(n) [d−xa(m)−xb(n)]2. For two equal spheres of radius R that are almost in contact and carry equal charges, F →0. 6189 Q2 16πε0R2. For two identical spheres in contact, the capacitance is C = 8πε0Rln 2, C = 2 ln 2 = 1. 38629··· 4πε0R which is the stated result. Problem 2. 7 9. 1 Problem Statement Consider a potential problem in the half-space defined by z ≥0, with Dirichlet boundary conditions on the plane z= 0 (and at infinity).

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: (a) Write down the appropriate Green function G(x,x′). (b) If the potential on the plane z = 0 is specified to be Φ = V inside a circle of radius a centered at the origin, and Φ = 0 outside the circle, find an integral expression for the potential at the point P specified in terms of cylindrical coordinates (ρ,ϕ,z). (c) Show that, along the axis of the circle (ρ= 0), the potential is given by Φ = V 1− z √a2 + z2. (d) Show that at large distances (ρ2 + z2 ≫a2) the potential can be expanded in a power series in (ρ2 + z2)−1 , and that the leading terms are Φ = Va2 2 z (ρ2 + z2)3/2 1− 3a2 4(ρ2 + z2) + 15a2ρ2 8(ρ2 + z2)2 +··· . Verify that the results of parts c and d are consistent with each other in their common range of validity. 43 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 9. 2 Solution We work in the half-space z≥0, with boundary plane z= 0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The potential satisfies Laplace’s equation in the open region z>0: ∇2Φ = 0, together with Dirichlet boundary conditions on the plane and the requirement that Φ →0 as r→∞. The boundary values are Φ(ρ,ϕ,0) = V, ρ<a, 0, ρ>a. Because the boundary condition is axially symmetric, the final potential will be independent of ϕ, although it is useful to keep the full Green-function derivation at first. (a) Green function for the half-space For Dirichlet boundary conditions on the plane z= 0, the appropriate Green function is obtained by the method of images. Let the field point be x = (x,y,z), z>0, and the source point be x′= (x′,y′,z′), z′>0. Reflect the source point across the plane: x′∗= (x′,y′ ,−z′). Then the Green function is G(x,x′) = 1 |x−x′| 1 − |x−x′∗|. Why this is correct. First, ∇2 x 1 |x−x′|=−4πδ(x−x′), while ∇2 x 1 |x−x′∗| = 0 throughout the physical region z>0, since the image point lies outside that region.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Therefore ∇2 xG(x,x′) =−4πδ(x−x′). Second, on the boundary z= 0, the distances to the source and to its image are equal: |x−x′|= |x−x′∗|. Hence G(x,x′) = 0 for z= 0. So G satisfies the required Dirichlet boundary condition. Thus this is the correct Green function. 44 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions , (b) Integral expression for the potential Since there is no charge in the half-space, Green’s representation for a Dirichlet problem gives 1 Φ(x) =− 4π S Φ(x′)∂G(x,x′) ∂n′ da′ , because G= 0 on the boundary and the volume term vanishes. Here the boundary S consists of the plane z′ = 0 plus the hemisphere at infinity. The contribution at infinity vanishes because Φ →0. So only the plane contributes. The outward normal from the half-space z>0 is in the negative z-direction: ˆ n′ ˆ =− z. Therefore ∂ ∂n′ =− ∂ ∂z′. Now compute ∂G/∂z′on the plane z′= 0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Let R1 = |x−x′|= (x−x′)2 + (y−y′)2 + (z−z′)2 R2 = |x−x′∗|= (x−x′)2 + (y−y′)2 + (z+ z′)2 . Then G= 1 R1 − 1 R2 Differentiate with respect to z′: ∂G ∂z′ = z−z′ R3 1 + . z+ z′ R3 2 . At z′= 0, we have R1 = R2 ≡R, where R2 = (x−x′)2 + (y−y′)2 + z2 Hence ∂G ∂z′ = z R3 + z R3 2z = z′=0 Therefore ∂G ∂n′ =− 2z R3. z′=0 Substitute into Green’s formula: Φ(x) =− 1 4π plane Φ(x′,y′ ,0)− 2z R3 da′ . So Φ(x) = z 2π plane Now Φ = V only on the disk ρ′<a, and zero elsewhere. Hence Φ(x′,y′ ,0) R3 da′ . Φ(x) = Vz 2π ρ′<a da′ R3. In cylindrical coordinates on the boundary plane, x′ = ρ′cos ϕ′, y′ = ρ′sin ϕ′, da′ = ρ′dρ′dϕ′ . Also, if the field point is (ρ,ϕ,z), then R2 = ρ2 + ρ′2 −2ρρ′cos(ϕ−ϕ′) + z2 . Therefore the integral expression is . R3. dϕ′ Φ(ρ,ϕ,z) = Vz 2π a 0 ρ′dρ′ 0 2π [ρ2 + ρ′2 −2ρρ′cos(ϕ−ϕ′) + z2]3/2. Because the boundary data are axially symmetric, the answer cannot depend on ϕ.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: So, equivalently, one may set ϕ= 0 and write Φ(ρ,z) = Vz 2π a 0 ρ′dρ′ 0 2π dφ [ρ2 + ρ′2 −2ρρ′cos φ+ z2]3/2. 45 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (c) Potential along the axis ρ= 0 On the axis, Then the denominator becomes independent of ϕ′: ρ= 0. R2 = ρ′2 + z2 . So the integral simplifies immediately: Φ(0,z) = Vz 2π a 0 ρ′dρ′ 0 2π dϕ′ (ρ′2 + z2)3/2. The ϕ′-integral gives 2π, hence Φ(0,z) = Vz a 0 ρ′dρ′ (ρ′2 + z2)3/2. Now let u= ρ′2 + z2, du= 2ρ′dρ′, ρ′dρ′ Then Φ(0,z) = Vz· a2 +z2 1 2 z2 u−3/2 du. Since u−3/2 du=−2u−1/2 , we obtain Φ(0,z) = Vz· 1 2−2u−1/2 a2 +z2 z2. Therefore Φ(0,z) = Vz 1 z− 1 √a2 + z2. So du = 2. Φ(0,z) = V 1− z √a2 + z2. This is exactly the required result. (d) Far-field expansion We now want the asymptotic form for r≡ ρ2 + z2 ≫a. Because the boundary condition is axially symmetric, the solution is axially symmetric.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: In the charge-free region, an axisymmetric decaying harmonic function has the multipole form Φ(r,θ) = ∞ ℓ=0 Aℓ rℓ+1 Pℓ(cos θ), where z cos θ= . r Since the boundary plane z= 0 is held at zero potential, we must have Φ(r,π/2) = 0. Because Pℓ(0) = 0 for odd ℓ, and nonzero for even ℓ, it follows that only odd ℓ can appear. Thus Φ(r,θ) = A1 r2 P1(cos θ) + A3 r4 P3(cos θ) + A5 r6 P5(cos θ) +··· . 46 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions z To determine the coefficients, use the exact axial result from part (c): Φ(0,z) = V 1− z √a2 + z2. For large z, = √a2 + z2 1 1 + a2/z2 = 1− a2 2z2 + 3a4 8z4− 5a6 16z6 +··· Therefore Φ(0,z) = V a2 2z2− 3a4 8z4 + 5a6 16z6 +··· . But on the axis θ= 0, so Pℓ(1) = 1. Hence Φ(0,z) = A1 z2 + A3 z4 + A5 z6 +··· .

Va2 P1(cos θ) 2 r2 = Va2 2 z r3. 3Va4 P3(cos θ) r4 =− 3Va4 16 5z3 −3zr2 r7. r2 = ρ2 + z2 , 5z3 = z(5z2 −3ρ2 −3z2) = z(2z2 −3ρ2). 5z3 −3zr2 r7 =− 3Va4z(2z2 −3ρ2) 16r7. Va2 2 z r3. Φ = Va2 2 z r3 1− 3a2 8r4 (2z2 −3ρ2)· 2r3 a2z Va2z 2r3 +··· Φ = Va2 2 z r3 1− 3a2 4r2 + 15a2ρ2 8r4 +··· . . 16. P5(cos θ) r6 +··· . The first term is For the next term, Since we have So Now factor out the leading term Then which simplifies to Since r2 = ρ2 + z2, this becomes − 8 −3zr2 3Va4 − 16 Φ = Va2 2 z (ρ2 + z2)3/2 1− 3a2 4(ρ2 + z2) + 15a2ρ2 8(ρ2 + z2)2 +··· . This is the desired far-field expansion.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 47 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Consistency of parts (c) and (d) Along the axis ρ= 0, the far-field expansion becomes Φ(0,z) = Va2 2z2 1− 3a2 4z2 +··· . Expanding, Φ(0,z) = V a2 2z2− Now compare with the exact axial result from part (c): 3a4 8z4 +··· . Φ(0,z) = V 1− z √a2 + z2. Its large-z expansion is Φ(0,z) = V a2 2z2− 3a4 8z4 + 5a6 16z6 +··· So the two results agree term by term in the common range of validity Physical interpretation The far-field potential behaves like Φ∼ Va2 2 z r3 = . z≫a. 2 r2. This is the potential of an electric dipole oriented along the z-axis. Thus the circular patch held at potential V behaves at large distances like an effective dipole source on the conducting plane. This is natural: the total “monopole” contribution vanishes because the plane is grounded and the potential falls to zero at infinity, so the leading nonzero term is dipolar.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Final results The Dirichlet Green function for the half-space is Va2 cos θ 1 G(x,x′) = 1 |x−x′| − |x−x′∗|. The potential due to a circular patch of radius a on the plane z= 0 held at potential V is Φ(ρ,ϕ,z) = Vz 2π a 0 ρ′dρ′ 0 2π dϕ′ [ρ2 + ρ′2 −2ρρ′cos(ϕ−ϕ′) + z2]3/2. Along the axis, Φ(0,z) = V 1− z √a2 + z2. For large distances, Φ = Va2 2 z (ρ2 + z2)3/2 1− 3a2 4(ρ2 + z2) + 15a2ρ2 8(ρ2 + z2)2 +··· . Problem 2. 8 10. 1 Problem Statement A two-dimensional potential problem is defined by two straight parallel line charges separated by a distance R with equal and opposite linear charge densities λ and−λ. (a) Show by direct construction that the surface of constant potential V is a circular cylinder (circle in the transverse dimensions) and find the coordinates of the axis of the cylinder and its radius in terms of R, λ, and V.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 48 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (b) Use the result of part a to show that the capacitance per unit length C of two right-circular cylindrical conductors, with radii a and b, separated by a distance d>a+ b, is C= 2πε0 cosh−1 d2 −a2 −b2 2ab . (c) Verify that the result for C agrees with the answer in Problem 1. 7 in the appropriate limit and determine the next nonvanishing order correction in powers of a/d and b/d. (d) Repeat the calculation of the capacitance per unit length for two cylinders inside each other (d < |b−a|). Check the result for concentric cylinders (d= 0). 10. 2 Solution This is a two-dimensional electrostatics problem, so it is natural to work in the transverse (x,y)-plane. Let the two line charges lie parallel to the z-axis and let their transverse positions be x= ±R 2 , y= 0. Take the line charge +λ at + R 2 ,0 and the line charge−λ at R − 2 ,0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: For a single infinite line charge, the potential in two dimensions is logarithmic. Hence, for the pair +λ and−λ, the potential is Φ(x,y) = λ 2πε0 ln r− r+ , where r2 + = x− R 2 2 + y2, r2 − = x+ R 2 2 + y2 . Equivalently, Φ(x,y) = λ 2πε0 ln r− r+ . This is the starting point for all parts. (a) Equipotential surfaces are circles Set the potential equal to a constant value V: r− V= λ 2πε0 ln r− r+ . Exponentiating, r+ = k, k≡exp 2πε0V λ . Thus the equipotentials are the loci of points for which the ratio of distances to two fixed points is constant. Such curves are Apollonius circles. We now derive the equation explicitly. Square the relation: r2 − = k2r2 +. Substitute x+ R 2 2 + y2 = k2 x− R 2 2 + y2 . Expand both sides: x2 + Rx+ R2 4 + y2 = k2 x2 −Rx+ R2 4 + y2 . 49 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Bring everything to one side: R2 4. , (1−k2)(x2 + y2) + R(1 + k2)x+ R2 4 (1−k2) = 0. Assume k̸= 1 for the moment.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Divide by 1−k2: x2 + y2 + R1 + k2 1−k2 x+ R2 4 = 0. Complete the square in x: x+ R 2 Compute the right-hand side: R 2 1 + k2 2 1−k2 + y2 = R 2 1 + k2 2 1−k2 − 2 (1 + k2)2 −(1−k2)2 (1−k2)2. Now (1 + k2)2 −(1−k2)2 = 4k2 , so R 2 Therefore the equipotential is the circle 2 4k2 (1−k2)2 = R2k2 (1−k2)2. (x−xc)2 + y2 = ρ2 with center R xc =− 2 1 + k2 1−k2 , and radius Rk ρ= |1−k2|. It is much cleaner to rewrite these in hyperbolic form. Define 2πε0V u≡ λ , k= eu . Then 1 + k2 1−k2 1 + e2u 1−e2u =−coth u, and k |1−k2|= eu |1−e2u|= 1 2|sinh u|. Thus the center is at R 2 coth u= R 2 coth 2πε0V λ , and the radius is R ρ= 2|sinh u|= R 2 sinh 2πε0 V λ . So the surfaces of constant potential are circles whose centers lie on the x-axis. Useful relations. From the above formulas, x2 c−ρ2 = R 2 2 coth2 u−csch2 u = R 2 2 , because coth2 u−csch2 u= 1. Hence every equipotential circle of this family satisfies x2 c−ρ2 = xc = = R 2 2 .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: This relation will be crucial in part (b). 50 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (b) Capacitance per unit length of two separated cylinders Now consider two conducting cylinders of radii a and b, with axes separated by a distance d>a+ b. We want the capacitance per unit length. The idea is to identify these conducting cylinders with two equipotential circles of the line-charge problem of part (a). Let the two conductors correspond to the two circles V = +V0, V=−V0. Then the actual potential difference between the conductors is ∆V = 2V0. Let the line charges be at ±R/2, as before. Then the circle V = +V0 has center xa = R 2 coth u0, u0 ≡ 2πε0V0 λ , and radius a= R 2 sinh u0 . Similarly, the circle V=−V0 has center xb =− R 2 coth u0 and radius R b= 2 sinh u0 only in the equal-radius case. For unequal radii we need the more general pair of equipotentials u= u1, u= u2 in bipolar coordinates. A more direct derivation is cleaner.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Let the two conductors be represented by two equipotential circles from the same family, with centers x1,x2 and radii a,b. For each circle, x2 1−a2 = R 2 2 , x2 2−b2 = R 2 2 . Hence x2 1−a2 = x2 2−b2 . Subtract: x2 1−x2 2 = a2 −b2 . Factor: (x1−x2)(x1 + x2) = a2 Now the distance between the cylinder axes is d= x1−x2. Therefore a2 x1 + x2 = −b2 d . Solve for x1 and x2: x1 = x2 = 1 2 d+ 1 2−d+ a2 a2 −b2 d= −b2 d= Now use x2 1−a2 = R 2 d2 + a2 a2 2 . Then R2 = x2 1−a2 = d2 + a2 −b2 2d 51 −b2 . −b2 2d , −b2 −d2 2d . 2 −a2 . 4 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions A simpler symmetric form is obtained by using d2 −a2 −b2 2ab . Indeed, from the geometry of bipolar coordinates one finds d2 cosh η0 = −a2 −b2 2ab , where 2η0 is the potential parameter difference between the two cylinders. Let us derive the capacitance directly from the line-charge potential. For the pair ±λ, the potential is Φ = λ 2πε0 ln r− r+ .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: In bipolar coordinates, equipotentials correspond to constant η, and the potential difference between two cylinders at η= η1 and η= η2 is λ ∆V= (η1−η2). πε0 For the standard two-cylinder geometry, choosing the conductors as the two symmetric equipotentials η= ±η0, we get ∆V= 2λη0 πε0 Hence the capacitance per unit length is C= λ ∆V= . πε0 2η0 . But Jackson defines C for the two-conductor system using charge ±λ and full potential difference, so equivalently C= 2πε0 2η0 = πε0 η0 . Now the geometric relation is Therefore So cosh(2η0) = d2 2η0 = cosh−1 d2 −a2 −b2 2ab . −a2 −b2 2ab . 2πε0 C= cosh−1 d2 −a2 −b2 2ab . This is the required result. (c) Agreement with Problem 1. 7 and next correction Problem 1. 7 gave, for two widely separated wires, C ≈πε0 ln d ag −1 We now recover this from the exact formula when d≫a, d≫b. d2 −a2 −b2 X= , ag = √ab. Let Then for large d, Use the large-X expansion 2ab . X ≫1. cosh−1 X = ln(2X)− 1 4X2 + O(X−4).

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 52 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Now d2 −a2 2X= −b2 ab= d2 ab 1− a2 + b2 d2. Hence ln(2X) = ln d2 ab + ln 1− a2 + b2 d2. For small (a2 + b2)/d2 , ln 1− a2 + b2 d2 a2 + b2 =− d2 + O(d−4). Also, So ln d2 ab = 2 ln d √ab . cosh−1 X = 2 ln d − √ab a2 + b2 d2− where the 1/(4X2) term contributes first at order a2b2/d4 . Therefore C= 2πε0 2 ln d/√ab−(a2 + b2)/d2 + O(d−4) . Factor out 2: C= ln d/√ab− πε0 a2 +b2 2d2 + O(d−4) Thus the leading term is a2b2 d4 + O(d−4), . C∼πε0 ln d √ab −1 , which agrees with Problem 1. 7. The next nonvanishing correction is therefore πε0 C ≈ ln d/√ab− a2 + b2 . 2d2 Expanding this as a series, C ≈ πε0 L 1 + a2 + b2 2d2 L + O(d−4) , L= ln d √ab . (d) Cylinders one inside the other Now suppose one cylinder lies inside the other, with d<|b−a|. The same geometric construction applies, but now the relevant bipolar coordinate surfaces correspond to nested circles rather than separated circles.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The formula remains 2πε0 C= cosh−1 d2 −a2 −b2 2ab up to an absolute value issue, because for interior geometry the argument is less than−1. Using cosh−1(−x) is not standard for real values, so we rewrite the denominator in positive form. Since a2 + b2 −d2 2ab >1 for d<|b−a|, the correct real expression is C= 2πε0 cosh−1 a2 +b2 −d2 2ab , d<|b−a|. 53 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions b a Check for concentric cylinders. If d= 0, then a2 + b2 −d2 2ab= a2 + b2 2ab= 1 2 a b + . But cosh(ln(b/a)) = 1 2 b + a a b . Hence cosh−1 a2 + b2 2ab = ln b a . Therefore C= 2πε0 ln(b/a), which is exactly the standard capacitance per unit length of concentric cylinders. So as required. Final results The equipotentials of two opposite line charges are circles. If u= C= 2πε0 ln(b/a) (d= 0), 2πε0V λ , then the circle of potential V has center and radius xc = R 2 coth u R ρ= 2|sinh u|.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: For two separated cylinders of radii a and b, center separation d>a+ b, the capacitance per unit length is C= 2πε0 cosh−1 d2 −a2 −b2 2ab . For d≫a,b, C∼πε0 ln d √ab −1 , with next correction πε0 C ≈ ln d/√ab− a2 + b2 . 2d2 For one cylinder inside the other (d<|b−a|), 2πε0 C= cosh−1 a2 +b2 −d2 2ab . For concentric cylinders (d= 0), 2πε0 C= ln(b/a). 54 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Problem 2. 26 11. 1 Problem Statement The two-dimensional potential Φ(ρ,ϕ) satisfies Laplace’s equation in the region a<ρ<∞, 0 ≤ϕ≤β, bounded by conducting surfaces at ϕ= 0, ρ= a, ϕ= β held at zero potential, as indicated in the sketch. At large ρ the potential is determined by some configuration of charges and/or conductors at fixed potentials. (a) Write down a solution for the potential Φ(ρ,ϕ) that satisfies the boundary conditions for finite ρ.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: (b) Keeping only the first two lowest nonvanishing terms, calculate the electric field components Eρ and Eϕ and also the surface-charge densities σ(ρ,0), σ(ρ,β), and σ(a,ϕ) on the three boundary surfaces. (c) Consider β= π (a plane conductor with a half-cylinder of radius aon it). Show that far from the half-cylinder the lowest order terms from part b give a uniform electric field normal to the plane. Sketch the charge density on and in the neighborhood of the half-cylinder. For fixed electric field strength E far from the plane, show that the total charge on the half-cylinder (actually charge per unit length in the z direction) is twice as large as area would indicate for a strip of width 2ain its absence. Show that the extra portion is drawn from regions of the plane nearby, so that the total charge in a strip of width large compared to a is the same whether the half-cylinder is there or not. 11.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 2 Solution We solve Laplace’s equation in polar coordinates: ∇2Φ = 1 ρ ∂ ∂ρ ρ ∂Φ ∂ρ + 1 ρ2 ∂2Φ ∂ϕ2 = 0, in the wedge region a<ρ<∞, 0 <ϕ<β, with boundary conditions Φ(ρ,0) = 0, Φ(ρ,β) = 0, Φ(a,ϕ) = 0. The field at large ρ is produced by remote sources, so the solution must be chosen to match some asymptotic behavior as ρ→∞, but for finite ρ it must vanish on all three conducting boundaries. 1. Separation of variables We seek separated solutions of the form Φ(ρ,ϕ) = R(ρ)Θ(ϕ). Substituting into Laplace’s equation gives 1 ρ R d dρ ρ dR dρ + Θ d2Θ dϕ2 = 0. Since the first term depends only on ρ and the second only on ϕ, each must be a constant. Write so that and 1 Θ d2Θ dϕ2 =−ν2 , d2Θ dϕ2 + ν2Θ = 0, ρ2R′′+ ρR′ −ν2R= 0. 55 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Angular equation.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The angular equation has general solution Because we need which implies Then The second boundary condition, requires so Hence and therefore Θ(ϕ) = Asin(νϕ) + Bcos(νϕ). Φ(ρ,0) = 0, Θ(0) = 0, B = 0. Θ(ϕ) = Asin(νϕ). Φ(ρ,β) = 0, Θ(β) = 0, sin(νβ) = 0. νβ= nπ, n= 1,2,3,. νn = nπ β . So the allowed angular factors are Radial equation. sin nπϕ β . For each n, the radial equation is ρ2R′′+ ρR′ −ν2 nR= 0. R(ρ) = Cρνn + Dρ−νn . This is an Euler equation, with solutions Therefore the general separated solution is Φn(ρ,ϕ) = Cnρνn + Dnρ−νn sin nπϕ β . 2. Impose the boundary condition on ρ= a We need Φ(a,ϕ) = 0 for all ϕ. Thus for each n, Cnaνn + Dna−νn = 0, so Dn =−Cna2νn . Hence Rn(ρ) = Cn ρνn −a2νn ρ−νn Thus the general solution satisfying all three finite-ρ boundary conditions is Φ(ρ,ϕ) = ∞ n=1 An ρ a nπ/β − a ρ nπ/β sin nπϕ β , where the coefficients An are determined by the remote sources through the large-ρ behavior. This answers part (a). .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 56 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 3. Keep the first two lowest nonvanishing terms For large ρ, the dominant terms are those with the two smallest values of n whose coefficients do not vanish. Generically these are n= 1 and n= 2. Define ν ≡ π β. Then the first two terms are Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≈A1 ρ a ν − a ρ ν sin(νϕ) + A2 ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν sin(2νϕ). So 4. Electric field components In polar coordinates, We differentiate term by term. First term. For we have It is cleaner to factor 1/ρ: Also Second term. For we get and Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≈A1 ρ/aν − a/ρν sin(νϕ) + A2 ρ/a 2ν − a/ρ 2ν sin(2νϕ). 1 Eρ =− ∂Φ ∂ρ, Eϕ=− ρ ∂Φ ∂ϕ. ∂Φ1 ∂ρ ∂ρ ∂Φ1 Φ1 = A1 ρ a ν − a ρ ν sin(νϕ), = A1 sin(νϕ) ν a ρ a ν−1 + νaνρ−ν−1 ∂Φ1 = νA1 ρ ρ a ν + a ρ ν sin(νϕ). ∂ϕ = νA1 ρ a ν − a ρ ν cos(νϕ). Φ2 = A2 ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν sin(2νϕ), ∂Φ2 ∂ρ = 2νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν + a ρ 2ν sin(2νϕ), ∂Φ2 ∂ϕ = 2νA2 ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν cos(2νϕ). .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Therefore Eρ =− νA1 ρ ρ a ν + a ρ ν sin(νϕ)− 2νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν + a ρ 2ν sin(2νϕ), and Eϕ=− νA1 ρ ρ a ν − a ρ ν cos(νϕ)− 2νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν cos(2νϕ). These are the first two lowest nonvanishing terms in the field. 57 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 5. Surface-charge densities on the three conductors For a conductor, the surface charge density is σ= ε0En, where En is the outward normal component of the electric field from the conductor into the vacuum region. We now compute this on each boundary. Boundary ϕ= 0. ˆ in the + ϕ direction. Therefore At ϕ= 0, the conductor lies along the lower radial boundary. The inward-to-region normal is Eϕ(ρ,0) σ(ρ,0) = ε0 ρ? ˆ More directly: since the unit normal into the region is + ϕ, the normal field is simply En = Eϕ(ρ,0). So σ(ρ,0) = ε0Eϕ(ρ,0). Now cos(0) = 1, so Eϕ(ρ,0) =− νA1 ρ ρ a ν − a ρ ν − 2νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν . Hence ε0νA1 σ(ρ,0) =− ρ ρ a ν − a ρ ν − 2ε0νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν .

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: Boundary ϕ= β. Now So Therefore ˆ Here the normal into the region points in the− ϕ direction, so σ(ρ,β) =−ε0Eϕ(ρ,β). cos(νβ) = cos π=−1, cos(2νβ) = cos 2π= 1. Eϕ(ρ,β) = +νA1 ρ ρ a ν − a ρ ν − 2νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν . ε0νA1 σ(ρ,β) =− ρ ρ a ν − a ρ ν + 2ε0νA2 ρ ρ a 2ν − a ρ 2ν . Boundary ρ= a. Here the conductor is the circular arc ρ= a. The outward normal from the conductor into the vacuum region points in the +ˆ ρ direction, so σ(a,ϕ) = ε0Eρ(a,ϕ). At ρ= a, ρ a ν = a ρ ν = 1, ρ a 2ν = a ρ 2ν = 1. Therefore Eρ(a,ϕ) =− 2νA1 a sin(νϕ)− 4νA2 a sin(2νϕ). Thus 2ε0νA1 σ(a,ϕ) =− a sin(νϕ)− 4ε0νA2 a sin(2νϕ). This completes part (b). 58 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 6. Special case β= π Now set Then 2 a ρ β= π. π ν = β = 1. The geometry becomes a grounded plane with a grounded half-cylinder of radius a sitting on it. The potential to the first two orders becomes Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≈A1 ρ a− a ρ sin ϕ+ A2 ρ a − 2 sin 2ϕ. Far from the half-cylinder.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: For large ρ, the dominant term is Φ∼A1 ρ a sin ϕ. Since in polar coordinates y= ρsin ϕ, this is Φ∼ A1 a y. A potential of the form Φ∼Ey corresponds to a uniform electric field E=−Eˆ y, that is, uniform and normal to the grounded plane y= 0. On a flat grounded plane in uniform normal field E, the surface charge density is σflat =−ε0E. So a strip of width 2a would carry charge per unit length Qstrip = (−ε0E)(2a) =−2ε0Ea. Hence Qhalf-cyl = 2Qstrip. So the half-cylinder carries twice as much charge as area alone would suggest. 9.

This corresponds to a semi-infinite thin sheet of conductor on the positive x-axis from x = a to infinity with a conducting cylinder of radius a fastened to its edge. (a) Sketch the surface-charge densities on the cylinder and on the top and bottom of the sheet, using the lowest order solution.

chunks.text: (b) Calculate the total charge on the cylinder and compare with the total deficiency of charge on the sheet near the cylinder, that is, the total difference in charge for finite a compared with a= 0, assuming that the charge density far from the cylinder is the same. 12. 2 Solution This problem is a special case of Problem 2. 26. We therefore start from the general lowest-order separated solution for the wedge region a<ρ<∞, 0 <ϕ<β, with grounded boundaries at ϕ= 0, ϕ= β, ρ= a. From Problem 2. 26, the general solution satisfying the finite-ρ boundary conditions is Φ(ρ,ϕ) = ∞ n=1 An ρ a nπ/β − a ρ nπ/β sin nπϕ β . For the present problem, β = 2π, so ν ≡ π β = 1 2. The lowest-order nonvanishing term is therefore the n= 1 term: Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≈A1 ρ a 1/2 − 1/2 a ρ sin ϕ 2. Thus we shall use Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≈A1 ρ a 1/2 − a ρ 1/2 sin ϕ 2. This already captures the leading behavior near the conductor edge and at large ρ. 1.

chunks.text: Geometry of the configuration When β = 2π, the wedge fills the full plane except for a slit along the positive x-axis together with the circular boundary ρ= a. Geometrically, this corresponds to • a conducting cylinder of radius a centered at the origin, • attached to a semi-infinite conducting sheet along the positive x-axis from x= a to +∞, • with two physical sheet surfaces: the upper side ϕ= 0 and the lower side ϕ= 2π. Thus the three conducting boundaries are ϕ= 0 (top of sheet), ϕ= 2π (bottom of sheet), ρ= a (cylinder). 62 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 2. Thus the charge density vanishes where the sheet meets the cylinder, then becomes negative and gradually approaches the same edge-singular profile that an isolated semi-infinite sheet would have. Sketch summary. • Cylinder: zero at both contact points, most negative on the far side. • Top and bottom sheet: zero at ρ= a, then negative, approaching a 1/√ρ decay far away; top and bottom are identical. chunks.text: Therefore Comparing with the cylinder charge, we obtain So the total charge on the cylinder is exactly equal to the total deficiency of charge on the sheet near the cylinder. Physical interpretation The cylinder does not create new total charge; it redistributes charge that otherwise would have occupied the neighborhood of the edge of the semi-infinite sheet. The presence of the curved conductor draws additional negative charge onto itself, especially onto its far side, while depleting charge from the nearby portions of both the top and bottom faces of the sheet. Far from the cylinder, the sheet recovers the same 1/√ρ edge profile as in the a= 0 case. Final results Using only the lowest-order term for β = 2π, the potential is Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≈A1 ρ a 1/2 − a ρ 1/2 sin ϕ 2. The charge densities are ε0A1 σ(ρ,0) = σ(ρ,2π) =− 2ρ ρ a 1/2 − a ρ 1/2 , ρ>a, and σ(a,ϕ) =− ε0A1 a sin ϕ 2 , 0 <ϕ<2π.

Problem 2. 9 13. 1 Problem Statement An insulated, spherical, conducting shell of radius a is in a uniform electric field E0. If the sphere is cut into two hemispheres by a plane perpendicular to the field, find the force required to prevent the hemispheres from separating (a) if the shell is uncharged; (b) if the total charge on the shell is Q. 67 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 13. 2 Solution Take the uniform field to be along the +z-direction: E0 = E0 ˆ z. ˆ z) da.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The sphere is cut by the equatorial plane z= 0, so we want the net force tending to pull the upper hemisphere away from the lower one, that is, the total +z-directed force on the upper hemisphere. The cleanest way to do this is to use the electrostatic pressure on a conductor surface. For a conductor in vacuum, since the field just inside is zero and just outside is normal to the surface, the outward force per unit area is p= ε0 2 E2 = σ2 2ε0 , where E is the field just outside the conductor. Thus the total separating force on the upper hemisphere is F= upper hemisphere p(ˆ n· Here ˆ n is the outward normal, so on a sphere ˆ n· ˆ z = cos θ, with θ the usual polar angle from the +z-axis. Also da= a2 sin θdθdϕ. Therefore F= 2π 0 π/2 p(θ) cos θa2 sin θdθdϕ. 0 So the main task is to find the electric field just outside the sphere. 1. The radial field is Er =− ∂Φ ∂r. Differentiate: ∂Φ ∂r=−E0 cos θ−2E0 a3 r3 cos θ. Hence Er = E0 cos θ+ 2E0 a3 r3 cos θ. At the surface r= a, Er(a,θ) = 3E0 cos θ. Also 1 ∂Φ Eθ =− r ∂θ. At r= a, the surface is an equipotential, so Eθ(a,θ) = 0, as it must be for a conductor. Thus the field just outside is purely normal and has magnitude E(a,θ) = 3E0 cos θ on the upper hemisphere (0 ≤θ≤π/2). 68 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Charged conducting sphere in a uniform field. potential: If the sphere carries total charge Q, we superpose the monopole ΦQ(r) = 1 4πε0 Q r . Physical interpretation For the neutral sphere, the field polarizes the conductor, producing positive charge on the upper hemisphere and negative charge on the lower hemisphere.

chunks.text: The Maxwell pressure is larger where the field is stronger, and the net effect is to pull the upper hemisphere upward and the lower hemisphere downward, tending to separate them. When the sphere also carries total charge Q, there is an additional uniform self-repulsion contribution Q2 32πε0a2 , QE0 plus an interference term 2 , between the monopole field and the induced dipole field from the external uniform field. 70 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions Final results Funcharged= 9π 4 ε0a2E2 0 and FQ= Q2 32πε0a2 + QE0 2 + 9π 4 ε0a2E2 0. These are the forces tending to separate the two hemispheres. The force required to prevent separation is equal and opposite. Problem 2. 10 14. 1 Problem Statement A large parallel plate capacitor is made up of two plane conducting sheets with separation D, one of which has a small hemispherical boss of radius a on its inner surface (D ≫a).

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The conductor with the boss is kept at zero potential, and the other conductor is at a potential such that far from the boss the electric field between the plates is E0. (a) Calculate the surface-charge densities at an arbitrary point on the plane and on the boss, and sketch their behavior as a function of distance (or angle). (b) Show that the total charge on the boss has the magnitude 3πε0E0a2 . (c) If, instead of the other conducting sheet at a different potential, a point charge q is placed directly above the hemispherical boss at a distance d from its center, show that the charge induced on the boss is q′ =−q 1− d2 −a2 d√d2 + a2. 14. 2 Solution Because the boss is small compared with the plate separation, D≫a, the field in the neighborhood of the boss is essentially the same as the field near a hemisphere sitting on an infinite grounded plane in a uniform electric field E0 normal to the plane.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: The standard way to solve this is to reflect the hemisphere through the plane. Then the problem in the upper half-space becomes equivalent to the problem of a complete grounded conducting sphere of radius acentered on the plane, placed in a uniform field. We choose the z-axis perpendicular to the plane, with the grounded plane at z= 0, the hemispherical boss occupying x2 + y2 + z2 = a2, z≥0, and the uniform field far away taken to be E∞=−E0 ˆ z. Then the corresponding far-field potential is Φ∞= E0z. 71 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 1. Equivalent full-sphere problem For a grounded conducting sphere of radius a in a uniform field−E0 ˆ z, the potential outside the sphere is Φ(r,θ) = E0 r− a3 r2 cos θ, r≥a. This satisfies Φ(a,θ) = 0 and Φ∼E0rcos θ= E0z (r→∞). Because on the equatorial plane z = 0, i. e.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: θ= π/2, the potential is also zero, the restriction of this solution to the upper half-space gives exactly the physical solution for the hemisphere-on-plane geometry. 2. Surface charge density on the boss For a conducting surface, σ= ε0En, where En is the outward normal field just outside the conductor. On the sphere, the field is purely radial at the surface because the tangential component vanishes there. So we first compute Starting from we have Therefore At the surface r= a, Er =− ∂Φ ∂r. Φ(r,θ) = E0 r− a3 r2 cos θ, ∂Φ ∂r= E0 1 + 2a3 r3 cos θ. Er =−E0 1 + 2a3 r3 cos θ. Er(a,θ) =−3E0 cos θ. Thus the surface charge density on the boss is π σboss(θ) = ε0Er(a,θ) =−3ε0E0 cos θ, 0 ≤θ≤ 2. Behavior. On the boss: • at the top (θ= 0), which is the most negative value; • at the rim (θ= π/2), σboss(0) =−3ε0E0, σboss π 2 = 0. So the charge density decreases smoothly from a maximum magnitude at the top of the boss to zero at the rim.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: 72 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions 3. Surface charge density on the plane To find the induced charge density on the plane outside the boss, use the same potential, written in cylindrical coordinates. Since z= rcos θ, r= ρ2 + z2 , the potential is Φ(ρ,z) = E0z 1− On the plane z= 0, the normal field is the z-component: a3 (ρ2 + z2)3/2. Ez =− ∂Φ ∂z. Differentiate: a3 (ρ2 + z2)3/2 + E0z ∂Φ ∂z z=0 = E0 1− a3 ρ3. Ez(ρ,0+) =−E0 1− a3 ρ3. Therefore the surface charge density on the plane is ∂Φ ∂z= E0 1− 3a3z (ρ2 + z2)5/2. At z= 0, the second term vanishes, so Hence σplane(ρ) = ε0Ez(ρ,0+) =−ε0E0 1− a3 ρ3 , ρ≥a. Behavior. On the plane: • far from the boss (ρ≫a), σplane(ρ) →−ε0E0, which is exactly the uniform surface charge density of a flat grounded plate in a uniform field E0; • at the rim (ρ= a), σplane(a) = 0. So the plane charge density starts at zero at the edge of the boss and approaches the uniform value−ε0E0 as ρ→∞.

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: (a) Summary and sketch We have found and π σboss(θ) =−3ε0E0 cos θ, 0 ≤θ≤ 2 , σplane(ρ) =−ε0E0 1− a3 ρ3 , ρ≥a. Thus: • the boss has the largest magnitude of negative charge at its top and zero at its rim; • the plane has zero charge density at the rim and tends monotonically to−ε0E0 far away. 73 Electrostatics Jackson-style Solutions (b) Total charge on the boss The total charge on the boss is found by integrating the surface charge density over the hemispherical surface. The area element on the hemisphere is da= a2 sin θdθdϕ. So Qboss = 2π 0 π/2 0 σboss(θ) a2 sin θdθdϕ. Substitute σboss(θ) =−3ε0E0 cos θ: Qboss =−3ε0E0a2 2π 0 dϕ π/2 0 Now 2π dϕ= 2π, 0 and π/2 1 0 Therefore cos θsin θdθ. Qboss =−3ε0E0a2(2π) 1 2 Hence the total charge on the boss is negative, with magnitude cos θsin θdθ= 2. =−3πε0E0a2 . |Qboss|= 3πε0E0a2 . This proves part (b).

chunks.title: chapter 2 jackson problems
chunks.text: (c) Point charge above the boss Now replace the remote plate by a point charge q located on the symmetry axis of the boss, at distance d from the center of the hemisphere. The same reflection argument applies: reflect the entire configuration in the plane z = 0. Then the problem of the hemispherical boss on a grounded plane becomes the problem of a complete grounded sphere of radius acentered at the origin, with two external point charges: • a real charge q at z= +d, • an image charge−q at z=−d. The induced charge on the hemispherical boss is therefore equal to the total induced charge on the upper hemi- sphere of the grounded sphere due to these two external charges.
