$$ \phi_{\mathrm{out}}=-E_0 r\cos\theta+\frac{B\cos\theta}{r^2}. $$

Applying continuity of $\phi$ at $r=R$ and continuity of the normal component of $\mathbf{D}$ in the absence of free surface charge,

$$ \varepsilon \left.\frac{\partial \phi_{\mathrm{in}}}{\partial r}\right _{R} =\varepsilon_0 \left.\frac{\partial \phi_{\mathrm{out}}}{\partial r}\right _{R}, $$

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ A=\frac{3\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}E_0, $$

$$ B=E_0 R^3\frac{\varepsilon-\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}. $$

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{in}}= -\frac{3\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}E_0 r\cos\theta, $$

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{out}}= -E_0 r\cos\theta +E_0 R^3\frac{\varepsilon-\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}\frac{\cos\theta}{r^2}. $$

The second term outside is a dipole potential, so the induced dipole moment is

$$ \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{ind}} =4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^3 \frac{\varepsilon-\varepsilon_0}{\varepsilon+2\varepsilon_0}\mathbf{E}_0. $$

For weak susceptibility, $\varepsilon=\varepsilon_0(1+\chi_e)$ with $ \chi_e \ll 1$, the dipole moment is proportional to $\chi_e$. In the conductor limit $\varepsilon/\varepsilon_0\to\infty$, one obtains

$$ \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{ind}}\to 4\pi\varepsilon_0 R^3 \mathbf{E}_0, $$

and the internal field tends to zero, as expected for a conducting sphere in electrostatic equilibrium.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text: The macroscopic polarization field is a coarse-grained description. At the microscopic level matter contains discrete nuclei and electrons; the microscopic electric field fluctuates violently over atomic distances. Macroscopic electrodynamics smooths these variations by averaging over volumes large compared with microscopic scales but small compared with the spatial scale on which the external fields vary appreciably.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
This averaging justifies the use of continuous fields such as $\mathbf{P}$, $\mathbf{E}$, and $\mathbf{D}$. The relation between $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{E}$ can be motivated from molecular polarizability: an individual molecule may acquire an induced dipole moment $\mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{mol}}=\alpha \mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{loc}}$. After averaging, one obtains a polarization density. However, the local field acting on a molecule is not generally identical to the macroscopic field, which is why microscopic derivations of constitutive relations require care. For present purposes the important point is that macroscopic dielectric theory is an averaged description, not a literal statement about pointwise microscopic charge motion.

Computational Demonstration: Bound Charge on a Polarized Sphere

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
For a uniformly polarized sphere with $\mathbf{P}=P_0\hat{\mathbf{z}}$, the surface bound charge density is $\sigma_b(\theta)=P_0\cos\theta$. The following code plots this distribution on the sphere and as a one-dimensional function of polar angle. It also compares the exact internal field with the theoretical value $-P_0/(3\varepsilon_0)\,\hat{\mathbf{z}}$ in normalized units.

The plot makes the angular structure of the bound surface charge explicit. Positive charge accumulates on the hemisphere toward which $\mathbf{P}$ points, negative charge on the opposite hemisphere, and the equator carries zero bound surface charge. The example is a concrete illustration that a uniform polarization field need not generate any bound volume charge, yet can still produce a nontrivial electric field through its surface charge.

Computational Demonstration: Dielectric Sphere in an External Field

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The dielectric sphere in a uniform external field is a classical boundary value problem. The code below evaluates the exact potential inside and outside the sphere, visualizes equipotentials and field lines in a meridional cross section, and extracts the induced dipole moment. We use normalized units with $\varepsilon_0=1$ and choose a moderate dielectric contrast so that the interior field reduction is visually clear.

The interior equipotentials are nearly parallel, reflecting the fact that the exact internal field is uniform. Outside the sphere, the field is the superposition of the applied uniform field and the induced dipole field. As the dielectric contrast increases, the field lines bulge more strongly around the sphere and the induced dipole moment approaches the conductor limit.

Computational Demonstration: Piecewise-Constant Permittivity in One Dimension

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(\varepsilon(x)\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)=-\rho_f(x) $$

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
is especially transparent in one dimension. The code below solves a simple finite-difference problem with piecewise constant permittivity and localized free charge. It then plots the potential, electric field, and displacement field, allowing one to see directly how the dielectric interface alters the slope of the potential while preserving the expected interface conditions.

The potential remains continuous, as expected in electrostatics. The electric field generally changes across the interface because the permittivity changes, while the displacement field is much closer to continuity away from the localized free-charge region. This is the one-dimensional manifestation of the boundary condition $\hat{\mathbf{n}}\cdot(\mathbf{D}_2-\mathbf{D}_1)=\sigma_f$.

Bound charge is not an independent new species of charge. It is ordinary electric charge appearing in a reorganized form once the microscopic constituents of matter respond to an applied field. The split

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text:
is therefore a bookkeeping distinction within macroscopic theory, not a statement that nature contains two kinds of electric charge.

The constitutive relation $\mathbf{P}\propto \mathbf{E}$ breaks down when the response becomes nonlinear, anisotropic, hysteretic, frequency dependent, or nonlocal. Ferroelectrics, strongly inhomogeneous composites, and many optical materials lie beyond the scope of the elementary linear dielectric model.

Nevertheless, the simple theory developed here is foundational. It provides the conceptual bridge from vacuum electrostatics to electromagnetic waves in matter, refractive index, polarization currents, and the broader continuum theory of media.

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text: Key Takeaways Polarization $\mathbf{P}$ is electric dipole moment per unit volume. Bound charge arises from polarization according to $\rho_b=-\nabla\cdot\mathbf{P}$ and $\sigma_b=\mathbf{P}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{n}}$. The displacement field $\mathbf{D}=\varepsilon_0\mathbf{E}+\mathbf{P}$ satisfies $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{D}=\rho_f$. In a linear isotropic dielectric, $\mathbf{D}=\varepsilon\mathbf{E}$ and the field equations are modified accordingly. Dielectric interfaces are governed by continuity of tangential $\mathbf{E}$ and the free-charge jump condition for normal $\mathbf{D}$. Dielectrics alter electrostatic energy storage and naturally enter capacitor and boundary value problems. Common Pitfalls Confusing free charge with total charge or treating bound charge as a separate fundamental charge species. Assuming $\mathbf{D}$ is more physically fundamental than $\mathbf{E}$ rather than a constitutive convenience. Forgetting that a uniform polarization can produce zero bound volume charge but nonzero bound surface charge. Applying $\mathbf{P}=\varepsilon_0\chi_e\mathbf{E}$ outside its regime of validity. Ignoring interface conditions when the permittivity changes spatially. Connection to Later Topics

chunks.title: Dielectrics in electrostatics
chunks.text: The theory of dielectrics leads directly to boundary value problems in matter, capacitance and energy methods, forces in polarizable media, multipole descriptions of induced charge, and eventually electromagnetic waves in continuous media. It also provides the static foundation for later discussions of dispersion, susceptibility tensors, and more general constitutive response.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
This notebook develops the Dirac delta function at the level used in graduate electrodynamics, with the style and rigor associated with Jackson. The goal is twofold:

1. To understand the delta function as a mathematical distribution rather than an ordinary function. 2. To see why it is indispensable for compact descriptions of localized charge distributions in electrodynamics.

Throughout, we balance formal definitions with computational intuition using Gaussian approximations that become increasingly localized while preserving total weight.

In electrodynamics, one repeatedly encounters idealized sources that are sharply localized in space: a point charge, a thin charged wire, or an infinitesimally thin charged sheet. Physically, these are limiting descriptions of very concentrated charge distributions. Mathematically, ordinary functions are often inadequate for expressing such idealizations in a way that preserves the correct total charge.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
The Dirac delta function solves this problem. It is written suggestively as though it were a function, but its real meaning is operational: it extracts the value of a test function at a point. This makes it the natural language for describing localized sources in Maxwell's equations and in Poisson's equation.

A useful physical picture is the following: a delta distribution represents a quantity concentrated into an infinitesimal region while keeping a finite integral. That is precisely what is needed when the total charge is finite but its spatial extent is idealized to zero thickness.

Mathematical Definition of the Dirac Delta Function

The symbol $\delta(x-a)$ is not an ordinary function in the classical sense. If one tried to interpret it naively as being zero everywhere except at $x=a$, where it is somehow infinite, one would immediately leave the category of usual functions. The correct framework is that of **distributions** (or generalized functions).

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
A distribution is defined by how it acts under integration against sufficiently smooth test functions. The Dirac delta centered at $a$ is characterized by the **sifting property**

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,\delta(x-a)\,dx = f(a), $$

for suitable test functions $f$. This equation is the definition that matters. It says that $\delta(x-a)$ extracts the value of a function at the point $x=a$.

$$ \delta(x-a)=0 \quad \text{for } x\neq a, $$

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(x-a)\,dx = 1, $$

but these statements are shorthand for the distributional meaning above. The delta function is therefore best thought of not as a usual graphable object, but as a linear functional whose effect is seen only inside an integral.

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\delta(x-a)\,dx = f(a). $$

This is the statement that makes the delta distribution useful. Inside an integral, it localizes the integrand at a single point. 2. Translation Property

The shift from $\delta(x)$ to $\delta(x-a)$ simply relocates the point at which the sampling occurs:

$$ \delta(x-a) \text{ samples at } x=a. $$

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\delta(x-a)\,dx = f(a), $$

so the translation moves the support of the distribution from the origin to $a$. 3. Scaling Property

$$ \delta(ax)=\frac{1}{ a }\delta(x), \qquad a\neq 0. $$

This follows from a change of variables in an integral. The factor $1/ a $ ensures that the total weight remains unity:

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\delta(ax)\,dx = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\frac{1}{ a }\delta(x)\,dx = \frac{1}{ a }f(0). $$

More generally, if $g(x)$ has isolated simple zeros $x_i$, then

$$ \delta(g(x)) = \sum_i \frac{\delta(x-x_i)}{ g'(x_i) }. $$

This identity appears frequently in coordinate transformations and in electrodynamics problems with symmetry. 4. Gaussian Representation

A standard approximation to the delta distribution is a sequence of normalized Gaussians:

$$ \delta_\varepsilon(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}\,\varepsilon}e^{-x^2/\varepsilon^2}. $$

Each $\delta_\varepsilon(x)$ is an ordinary function with unit area:

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\delta_\varepsilon(x)\,dx = 1. $$

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
As $\varepsilon \to 0$, the Gaussian becomes narrower and taller, and in the distributional sense

$$ \delta_\varepsilon(x) \to \delta(x). $$

This limit is not pointwise in the ordinary function sense; it is meaningful only under integration against test functions.

Delta Function in 1D, 2D, and 3D One Dimension

In one dimension, the delta distribution $\delta(x-a)$ localizes at a single point on the line. Its defining action is

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\delta(x-a)\,dx = f(a). $$ Two Dimensions

In two dimensions, the delta distribution is written

$$ \delta^{(2)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)=\delta(x-x_0)\delta(y-y_0). $$

$$ \int\!\!\int f(x,y)\,\delta(x-x_0)\delta(y-y_0)\,dx\,dy = f(x_0,y_0). $$

This describes localization at a point in a plane. Three Dimensions

$$ \delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)=\delta(x-x_0)\delta(y-y_0)\delta(z-z_0), $$

$$ \iiint f(\mathbf{r})\,\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)\,d^3r = f(\mathbf{r}_0). $$

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
It has dimensions of inverse volume. This dimensional point is important in electrodynamics: if $\rho(\mathbf{r})$ is a volume charge density, then $\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)$ must scale as $1/\text{volume}$ so that the total charge comes out with the correct units.

In curvilinear coordinates one must also include the Jacobian. For example, in spherical coordinates,

$$ \delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0) = \frac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\delta(r-r_0)\delta(\theta-\theta_0)\delta(\phi-\phi_0), $$

with the understanding that the volume element is $d^3r=r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$.

The delta distribution appears naturally whenever charge is idealized as localized on sets of lower dimension.

For a point charge $q$ located at $\mathbf{r}_0$, the volume charge density is

$$ \rho(\mathbf{r}) = q\,\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0). $$

This formula is exact in the distributional sense because

$$ \iiint \rho(\mathbf{r})\,d^3r = q\iiint \delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)\,d^3r = q. $$

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
Similarly, if charge is concentrated along a line, the density can still be represented as a three-dimensional volume density by using delta functions in the transverse coordinates. If charge is concentrated on a surface, one uses a delta function normal to the surface.

This viewpoint is especially powerful because Maxwell's equations and Poisson's equation are written in terms of volume densities. Delta functions let lower-dimensional charge distributions be embedded into that common framework without changing the field equations.

Examples with Point, Line, and Surface Charge Densities 1. Point Charge in 3D

A point charge $q$ at position $\mathbf{r}_0$ is described by

$$ \rho(\mathbf{r}) = q\,\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0). $$

This is the unique compact expression that places all the charge at one point while preserving the total charge under volume integration.

In electrostatics, this charge density appears in Poisson's equation,

$$ \nabla^2 \Phi(\mathbf{r}) = -\frac{\rho(\mathbf{r})}{\varepsilon_0} = -\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0). $$

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
The solution is the familiar Coulomb potential,

$$ \Phi(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{q}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0 }. $$ 2. Infinite Line Charge

Consider a line charge of linear density $\lambda$ running along the $z$-axis. Although the charge occupies a one-dimensional set, one can express it as a three-dimensional density by confining it to $x=0$ and $y=0$:

$$ \rho(x,y,z)=\lambda\,\delta(x)\delta(y). $$

To check the meaning, integrate over a cylinder of finite length $L$ around the axis:

$$ Q = \iiint \rho\,d^3r = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\!dz\int dx\int dy\,\lambda\delta(x)\delta(y)=\lambda L. $$

Thus the volume density correctly reproduces the charge per unit length. 3. Infinite Plane Charge

For a plane charge of surface density $\sigma$ lying in the plane $z=0$, the corresponding volume density is

Integrating through a slab of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness containing $z=0$ gives

$$ Q = \iiint \rho\,d^3r = \iint_A dx\,dy \int dz\,\sigma\delta(z)=\sigma A, $$

as required for a sheet of charge. Why This Simplifies Electrodynamics

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
Delta functions let us represent point, line, and surface sources using a single formal language of volume densities. That means Gauss's law, Poisson's equation, Green-function methods, and Fourier methods can all be applied without rewriting the theory separately for each geometric type of source.

The next sections build intuition for the delta distribution using normalized Gaussians. The key idea is that the delta function is approached by a family of ordinary functions whose width shrinks while their total area remains fixed at unity.

Each code cell is preceded by the relevant mathematics and followed by a short discussion of the output.

Narrow Gaussian Approximations Approaching the Delta Function

$$ \delta_\varepsilon(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}\,\varepsilon}e^{-x^2/\varepsilon^2}. $$

As $\varepsilon$ decreases, the profile becomes narrower and its peak grows, while the area remains equal to $1$. This does not converge to an ordinary function; it converges only in the distributional sense.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
The curves become sharply localized near $x=0$ as $\varepsilon$ decreases. The important point is not the increasing height by itself, but the simultaneous narrowing that preserves total area. This is why the Gaussian family is a good approximation to the delta distribution under integration.

Area Preservation Under the Gaussian Approximation

A defining requirement for any delta-sequence is preservation of total weight:

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta_\varepsilon(x)\,dx = 1. $$

We now verify this numerically for several widths by direct quadrature using the trapezoidal rule.

The numerical areas stay extremely close to $1$, confirming the central property of the approximation. This is the mechanism by which a sequence of increasingly concentrated functions can still represent a finite amount of charge or probability weight.

Numerical Illustration of the Sifting Property

To test the sifting property, choose a smooth function such as

and compare the exact value $f(a)$ with the integral

$$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\delta_\varepsilon(x-a)\,dx. $$

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
As $\varepsilon \to 0$, the integral should converge to $f(a)$.

The numerical integral approaches $f(a)$ as the Gaussian narrows. This is the computational version of the sifting property. It makes clear that the delta distribution should always be interpreted through what it does inside an integral, not as an ordinary plotted spike.

Visualization of a Localized 2D Delta Approximation

In two dimensions, the normalized Gaussian approximation is

$$ \delta_\varepsilon^{(2)}(x,y)=\frac{1}{\pi\varepsilon^2} \exp\left[-\frac{x^2+y^2}{\varepsilon^2}\right]. $$

This has unit integral over the plane and becomes increasingly localized near the origin as $\varepsilon$ decreases. A heatmap is a convenient way to visualize the concentration in the plane.

The heatmap shows strong localization around the origin, with the color scale encoding the large peak near $(0,0)$. The printed integral remains close to $1$, confirming that this sharply peaked object is properly normalized as a two-dimensional delta-sequence.

3D Surface Visualization of a Sharply Peaked Approximation

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
Although the true delta distribution is not an ordinary surface, a 3D rendering of the 2D Gaussian approximation helps visualize the way a localized source becomes simultaneously narrower and taller. This is especially useful when thinking about point-like sources embedded in higher-dimensional space.

The surface rises very steeply near the origin and falls off rapidly away from it. This conveys the geometric content of a delta-sequence: localization in position space together with normalization of the total integral.

The Dirac delta function is best understood as a distribution defined by its action on test functions. It is not an ordinary function, but it behaves in a controlled and highly useful way inside integrals. Its main properties, especially sifting, translation, and scaling, make it an essential tool in graduate electrodynamics.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text:
From the physical side, delta functions provide a compact and rigorous way to encode idealized charge distributions. Point charges, line charges, and surface charges can all be represented within the same volume-density framework used in Maxwell's equations and Poisson's equation. From the computational side, narrow Gaussian sequences provide intuition for how localization and normalization coexist.

This interplay between rigorous distributional language and concrete approximating sequences is exactly what makes the Dirac delta so powerful in theoretical physics.

chunks.title: Dirac delta functions
chunks.text: Key Takeaways The Dirac delta function is a distribution, not an ordinary function. Its defining property is the sifting relation $\int f(x)\delta(x-a)\,dx=f(a)$. The scaling law $\delta(ax)=\delta(x)/ a $ preserves normalization under changes of variables. In multiple dimensions, the delta distribution factorizes into products such as $\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}_0)=\delta(x-x_0)\delta(y-y_0)\delta(z-z_0)$. In electrodynamics, point, line, and surface charges are described compactly by delta-supported volume densities. Gaussian delta-sequences give computational intuition: they become narrower and taller while maintaining unit integral. Delta functions unify the treatment of localized sources in Maxwell's equations, Poisson's equation, and Green-function methods.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
Electromagnetic radiation is one of the decisive themes of classical electrodynamics because it marks the transition from static field theory to genuinely dynamical propagation. In electrostatics and magnetostatics, the fields are tied instantaneously to time-independent sources or steady currents. By contrast, radiation arises from time-dependent charge and current distributions, propagates causally at finite speed, and transports energy and momentum away from the source region. The theory therefore combines Maxwell's equations, wave propagation, retarded solutions, and asymptotic analysis into a single framework.

A static charge distribution produces an electrostatic field determined by Coulomb's law, and a steady current distribution produces a magnetostatic field determined by the Biot-Savart law or Ampere's law. Neither case involves the continual transport of energy to infinity. Radiation appears only when the sources vary in time in such a way that the electromagnetic field develops a propagating component.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text: The physical statement that an accelerated charge radiates is more precise than a slogan. What matters is that the field contains an asymptotic contribution which remains finite when multiplied by the distance from the source and which therefore gives a nonvanishing outward energy flux through spheres of arbitrarily large radius. If the Poynting flux through a sphere of radius $r$ scales as $r^{-2}$, then the total power crossing the sphere remains finite as $r \to \infty$; this is the characteristic signature of radiation.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
It is useful to distinguish three spatial regimes for localized, time-dependent sources: **Near fields** are dominated by terms falling as $1/r^3$. They retain a quasi-static character and are strongly tied to the instantaneous source configuration. **Induction fields** fall as $1/r^2$. They represent intermediate behavior associated with time-varying source moments but do not by themselves carry finite power to infinity. **Radiation fields** fall as $1/r$. These are transverse fields whose Poynting flux decays as $1/r^2$, thereby transporting finite energy outward.

These distinctions make clear why the full time-dependent Maxwell equations are essential. Once the sources vary in time, one must abandon the static picture and replace it with a causal propagation theory in which field values at $(\mathbf r,t)$ depend on the sources at earlier times.

In vacuum, Maxwell's equations in SI units are

$$ \nabla \cdot \mathbf E = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}, \qquad \nabla \cdot \mathbf B = 0, $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
$$ \nabla \times \mathbf E = -\frac{\partial \mathbf B}{\partial t}, \qquad \nabla \times \mathbf B = \mu_0 \mathbf J + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf E}{\partial t}. $$

The homogeneous equations suggest the introduction of scalar and vector potentials,

$$ \mathbf B = \nabla \times \mathbf A, \qquad \mathbf E = -\nabla \phi - \frac{\partial \mathbf A}{\partial t}. $$

The potentials are not unique. Under the gauge transformation

$$ \mathbf A \mapsto \mathbf A + \nabla \chi, \qquad \phi \mapsto \phi - \frac{\partial \chi}{\partial t}, $$

the physical fields remain unchanged. For radiation theory the most natural choice is the **Lorenz gauge**,

$$ \nabla \cdot \mathbf A + \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = 0, \qquad c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}. $$

Substituting the potentials into Maxwell's equations and imposing the Lorenz gauge yields decoupled inhomogeneous wave equations:

$$ \nabla^2 \phi - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}, $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
$$ \nabla^2 \mathbf A - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf A}{\partial t^2} = -\mu_0 \mathbf J. $$

The formal resemblance to the scalar wave equation is essential. The electromagnetic potentials are not determined by instantaneous source values but by solutions of hyperbolic equations, so propagation occurs at the finite speed $c$. This causal structure is the mathematical foundation of radiation theory.

The Green's function of the wave operator in three spatial dimensions selects the retarded solution consistent with causality. For localized sources one obtains

$$ \phi(\mathbf r,t)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\int \frac{\rho(\mathbf r',t_r)}{ \mathbf r-\mathbf r' }\,d^3r', $$

$$ \mathbf A(\mathbf r,t)=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\int \frac{\mathbf J(\mathbf r',t_r)}{ \mathbf r-\mathbf r' }\,d^3r', $$

$$ t_r=t-\frac{ \mathbf r-\mathbf r' }{c}. $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
These formulas state that the field observed at $(\mathbf r,t)$ depends on the source at the earlier time required for a signal to propagate from $\mathbf r'$ to $\mathbf r$ at speed $c$. This is not a calculational refinement but a structural necessity: the theory is causal. The source cannot influence the field at spacelike separated events.

The appearance of $t_r$ also shows why time-dependent electrodynamics is intrinsically nonlocal in time. One cannot simply insert the instantaneous charge and current into static formulas. Radiation theory begins precisely where this static intuition fails.

The electric and magnetic fields follow from

$$ \mathbf E = -\nabla \phi - \frac{\partial \mathbf A}{\partial t}, \qquad \mathbf B = \nabla \times \mathbf A. $$

Because the sources appear at retarded time, differentiating the potentials requires care: both the explicit factor $1/ \mathbf r-\mathbf r' $ and the implicit dependence through $t_r$ contribute. The exact results may be organized into Jefimenko-type expressions, for example

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
$$ \mathbf E(\mathbf r,t)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\int \left[\frac{\rho(\mathbf r',t_r)}{R^2}\hat{\mathbf R}+\frac{1}{cR}\frac{\partial \rho(\mathbf r',t_r)}{\partial t}\hat{\mathbf R}-\frac{1}{c^2R}\frac{\partial \mathbf J(\mathbf r',t_r)}{\partial t}\right]d^3r', $$

$$ \mathbf B(\mathbf r,t)=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\int \left[\frac{\mathbf J(\mathbf r',t_r)}{R^2}+\frac{1}{cR}\frac{\partial \mathbf J(\mathbf r',t_r)}{\partial t}\right]\times \hat{\mathbf R}\,d^3r', $$

with $\mathbf R = \mathbf r-\mathbf r'$, $R= \mathbf R $, and $\hat{\mathbf R}=\mathbf R/R$.

These formulas exhibit the radial hierarchy directly. Terms proportional to $R^{-2}$ and stronger are associated with near and induction behavior, while the terms proportional to $R^{-1}$ involve time derivatives of the sources and become dominant in the far zone. The complexity of the exact fields is therefore not arbitrary; it reflects the coexistence of several physically distinct regimes within a single exact solution.

Radiation Zone and Far-Field Approximation

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
Let the source be confined to a region of characteristic size $a$. The **radiation zone** or **far zone** is the regime in which

and, for monochromatic sources of angular frequency $\omega$, usually also $r \gg \lambda = 2\pi c/\omega$ when one wants clean asymptotic separation of terms. In this regime the observation point is so far from the source that all source points are viewed under nearly the same direction.

$$ R = \mathbf r-\mathbf r' \approx r-\hat{\mathbf r}\cdot \mathbf r', \qquad \frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{1}{r}, $$

while retaining the source-position dependence inside the retarded time,

$$ t_r \approx t-\frac{r}{c}+\frac{\hat{\mathbf r}\cdot \mathbf r'}{c}. $$

This distinction is central. The amplitude factor may be approximated by $1/r$, but phase variations across the source can still matter. For sufficiently localized sources, the leading far-zone fields become transverse:

$$ \mathbf E_{\mathrm{rad}} \perp \hat{\mathbf r}, \qquad \mathbf B_{\mathrm{rad}} = \frac{1}{c}\hat{\mathbf r}\times \mathbf E_{\mathrm{rad}}. $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The hierarchy of radial decay is then: near-field terms: $1/r^3$, induction terms: $1/r^2$, radiation terms: $1/r$.

Because the outward energy flux is quadratic in the fields, only the $1/r$ contribution yields a finite integrated power at large radius. This is the operational meaning of radiation.

For a localized source whose dimensions are small compared with the observation distance and, in the simplest long-wavelength limit, small compared with the wavelength, the leading radiative term is usually the electric dipole contribution. Define the electric dipole moment by

$$ \mathbf p(t)=\int \mathbf r'\,\rho(\mathbf r',t)\,d^3r'. $$

In the far zone, the radiation field of a time-dependent dipole can be written in compact vector form as

$$ \mathbf E_{\mathrm{rad}}(\mathbf r,t)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 c^2}\frac{\hat{\mathbf r}\times\left[\hat{\mathbf r}\times \ddot{\mathbf p}(t-r/c)\right]}{r}, $$

$$ \mathbf B_{\mathrm{rad}}(\mathbf r,t)=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{\hat{\mathbf r}\times \ddot{\mathbf p}(t-r/c)}{r} = \frac{1}{c}\hat{\mathbf r}\times \mathbf E_{\mathrm{rad}}. $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
Hence radiation is controlled by the second time derivative of the dipole moment, not by the dipole moment itself. For a monochromatic dipole oriented along $\hat{\mathbf z}$,

$$ \mathbf p(t)=p_0\cos \omega t\,\hat{\mathbf z}, $$

the far-zone fields are purely transverse and have the angular form

$$ E_\theta(r,\theta,t)=\frac{\mu_0 \omega^2 p_0}{4\pi}\frac{\sin\theta}{r}\cos\!\left[\omega\left(t-\frac{r}{c}\right)\right], $$

$$ B_\phi(r,\theta,t)=\frac{1}{c}E_\theta(r,\theta,t). $$

The field vanishes on the dipole axis and is maximal in the equatorial plane. The radiation is linearly polarized, with the electric field in the local $\hat{\boldsymbol\theta}$ direction and the magnetic field in the $\hat{\boldsymbol\phi}$ direction. The transversality of the far field is exact at leading order in $1/r$.

The electromagnetic energy flux is described by the Poynting vector,

$$ \mathbf S = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\mathbf E\times \mathbf B. $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
In the far zone of dipole radiation, $\mathbf E$, $\mathbf B$, and $\hat{\mathbf r}$ form a mutually orthogonal triad, so the Poynting vector points radially outward:

For the oscillating electric dipole $\mathbf p(t)=p_0\cos \omega t\,\hat{\mathbf z}$, the instantaneous angular distribution is

$$ \frac{dP}{d\Omega} = r^2 S_r = \frac{\mu_0 \omega^4 p_0^2}{16\pi^2 c}\sin^2\theta\,\cos^2\!\left[\omega\left(t-\frac{r}{c}\right)\right]. $$

$$ \left\langle \frac{dP}{d\Omega} \right\rangle = \frac{\mu_0 \omega^4 p_0^2}{32\pi^2 c}\sin^2\theta. $$

$$ \int \sin^2\theta\,d\Omega = \frac{8\pi}{3}, $$

one finds the average total dipole power,

$$ \langle P \rangle = \frac{\mu_0 \omega^4 p_0^2}{12\pi c}. $$

For a nonrelativistic point charge $q$ with acceleration $\mathbf a$, the dipole moment of the source is $\mathbf p = q\mathbf x$, so $\ddot{\mathbf p}=q\mathbf a$. One thereby obtains the nonrelativistic Larmor formula,

$$ P=\frac{\mu_0 q^2 a^2}{6\pi c} = \frac{q^2 a^2}{6\pi\varepsilon_0 c^3}. $$

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
Its meaning is direct and profound: a charge radiates in proportion to the square of its acceleration. Velocity alone does not suffice.

Hertzian Dipole: Canonical Elementary Source

The Hertzian dipole, or short oscillating dipole, is the standard elementary radiation source in classical electrodynamics and antenna theory. One imagines a thin current element of length $\ell$ carrying a time-harmonic current

with $\ell \ll \lambda$. In this limit the current is effectively uniform along the element and the source behaves as an electric dipole with moment amplitude

$$ E_\theta = \frac{\eta_0 k I_0 \ell}{4\pi r}\sin\theta\cos\!\left[\omega\left(t-\frac{r}{c}\right)\right], \qquad B_\phi = \frac{1}{c}E_\theta, $$

where $k=\omega/c$ and $\eta_0=\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}=\mu_0 c$ is the impedance of free space. The angular dependence is the familiar toroidal pattern proportional to $\sin^2\theta$ in intensity.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
This source is canonical because any sufficiently small localized radiator reduces, to leading order, to some combination of low multipoles. The short dipole is the simplest realization of electric dipole radiation and serves as the asymptotic model for a broad range of physical systems.

Electric dipole radiation is usually the leading contribution because it is the lowest multipole moment capable of time-dependent radiation for a localized neutral system. However, symmetry may force the electric dipole moment to vanish. Examples include sources with inversion symmetry or transitions between states for which the electric dipole matrix element is zero.

When electric dipole radiation vanishes, the next contributions are typically: **magnetic dipole radiation**, associated with time-dependent current loops or angular momentum structure, **electric quadrupole radiation**, associated with time-dependent second-rank charge moments.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The multipole expansion of radiation theory is therefore not merely classificatory; it is an asymptotic ordering principle. Higher multipoles are typically suppressed by powers of $ka$, where $a$ is the source size, but they become indispensable whenever lower moments vanish by symmetry or conservation laws.

Radiation Reaction and Conceptual Remarks

A radiating charge loses energy and momentum through the fields it emits. Energy conservation therefore demands that work be done on the charge, or on the source system more generally, to sustain radiation. At a macroscopic level this is encoded by Poynting's theorem: the decrease of mechanical plus field energy inside a volume equals the outward flux of electromagnetic energy through the boundary.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
At a more delicate level one encounters **radiation reaction**, namely the effect of the emitted field back on the motion of the source itself. The full treatment leads to subtleties such as self-force, runaway solutions, and pre-acceleration in the Abraham-Lorentz theory. Those issues lie beyond the present notebook, but the central conceptual point should be retained: radiation is not an externally appended phenomenon. It is part of the self-consistent energy accounting of Maxwell's theory.

Canonical Worked Examples 1. Oscillating Electric Dipole

Let $\mathbf p(t)=p_0\cos \omega t\,\hat{\mathbf z}$. Then

$$ \ddot{\mathbf p}(t)=-\omega^2 p_0\cos \omega t\,\hat{\mathbf z}. $$

Substituting into the dipole radiation formula gives a far-zone electric field proportional to $\omega^2 p_0 \sin\theta/r$. The two key structural facts are immediate: radiation strengthens with increasing temporal variation, and the angular dependence suppresses emission along the dipole axis. 2. Angular Radiation Pattern

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The time-averaged intensity of electric dipole radiation is proportional to $\sin^2\theta$. Hence the emission is maximal in the plane perpendicular to the dipole axis and vanishes at $\theta=0$ and $\theta=\pi$. The resulting pattern is a torus in three dimensions and a figure-eight in a polar cross-section. 3. Power Radiated by a Nonrelativistic Accelerating Charge

For a single point charge with acceleration $\mathbf a$, one identifies $\ddot{\mathbf p}=q\mathbf a$ and obtains the Larmor formula

$$ P=\frac{q^2 a^2}{6\pi\varepsilon_0 c^3}. $$

Thus a uniformly accelerated magnitude of acceleration yields constant radiated power in the nonrelativistic approximation. The result is quadratic in $q$ and in $a$. 4. Comparison of Near-Field and Far-Field Scaling

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
For a localized oscillating dipole, the full field contains terms scaling as $1/r^3$, $1/r^2$, and $1/r$. At small distance, the quasi-static $1/r^3$ contribution dominates. At intermediate distance, induction effects can be significant. At sufficiently large distance, only the $1/r$ term survives in the sense relevant for energy transport to infinity. 5. Symmetry and Allowed or Forbidden Dipole Radiation

If the source has a time-dependent charge distribution but its dipole moment remains identically zero by symmetry, then electric dipole radiation is absent. Radiation may still occur through magnetic dipole or electric quadrupole terms. This is one of the main practical uses of multipole analysis: it tells us not only what is present, but also what symmetry forbids.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The following computations are not substitutes for the analysis above; they are visual complements to it. Each code cell is preceded by a short mathematical explanation and followed by a brief interpretation. The numerical work focuses on asymptotic structure rather than on a fully resolved solution of Maxwell's equations for an extended source.

We adopt a consistent plotting style and collect the physical constants needed for SI-unit illustrations.

Angular Radiation Pattern of an Oscillating Dipole

For a monochromatic electric dipole aligned with $\hat{\mathbf z}$, the time-averaged radiated power per unit solid angle is proportional to $\sin^2\theta$. The first plot shows this function directly, while the second gives the corresponding polar representation. This is the canonical dipole pattern.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The vanishing intensity at $\theta=0$ and $\theta=\pi$ is a geometric consequence of transversality: along the dipole axis the acceleration projected perpendicular to the line of sight vanishes. The polar plot emphasizes the toroidal three-dimensional structure encoded in the two-dimensional cross-section.

Near-, Induction-, and Radiation-Zone Scaling

A localized time-dependent dipole field contains terms with radial dependence $1/r^3$, $1/r^2$, and $1/r$. The precise coefficients depend on the source and on phase, but the hierarchy of powers is universal in the asymptotic expansion. The next computation compares these magnitudes on the same axes.

On logarithmic axes the three slopes are distinct and easy to compare. The graph should be read asymptotically: as $r$ increases, the $1/r$ term eventually dominates all faster-decaying contributions. This is why only the far-zone field is relevant for the total radiated power measured at large distance.

Far-Zone Electric and Magnetic Field Amplitudes

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
For an oscillating dipole $\mathbf p(t)=p_0\cos \omega t\,\hat{\mathbf z}$, the peak far-zone amplitudes are

$$ E_{\theta,0}(r,\theta)=\frac{\mu_0 \omega^2 p_0}{4\pi r}\sin\theta, \qquad B_{\phi,0}(r,\theta)=\frac{E_{\theta,0}}{c}. $$

The next code cell evaluates these amplitudes at fixed radius and then computes the corresponding cycle-averaged Poynting flux.

The electric and magnetic amplitudes share the same angular dependence because the far-zone fields are locked into the transverse radiation relation $\mathbf B = \hat{\mathbf r}\times \mathbf E/c$. Since the Poynting flux is quadratic in the field amplitude, its angular profile is proportional to $\sin^2\theta$, in agreement with the analytic derivation.

Total Radiated Power as a Function of Frequency and Dipole Strength

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
For a monochromatic electric dipole, the average radiated power scales as $\langle P \rangle \propto \omega^4 p_0^2$. The quartic dependence on frequency is particularly important: even modest changes in frequency can greatly alter the radiated power. The following plots illustrate these parameter dependences in a simplified model.

The slopes on the log-log plots encode the analytic scaling laws directly. The first graph has slope $4$ because dipole radiation is controlled by the second time derivative of the dipole moment, and the second graph has slope $2$ because the radiated power is quadratic in field amplitude.

Two-Dimensional Cross-Section of the Far-Zone Intensity

Although radiation is a three-dimensional phenomenon, it is often useful to examine a planar cross-section. For a dipole aligned with the vertical axis, the far-zone intensity in a meridional plane is proportional to $\sin^2\theta/r^2$. The next plot visualizes this quantity away from the origin, where the far-zone approximation is not intended to be trusted.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The dark lobes along the horizontal direction correspond to maximal radiation in the plane orthogonal to the dipole axis, while the nulls along the vertical axis are the familiar dipole zeros. The excluded central region is important conceptually: the far-zone formula is asymptotic and should not be interpreted as an exact near-source field.

Simple Animation of an Oscillating Dipole Moment and Its Pattern

The final computation animates an oscillating dipole moment together with the instantaneous angular weighting $\sin^2\theta\,\cos^2\omega t$. This is not a full Maxwell solver; rather, it is a pedagogical visualization of how a time-dependent dipole moment drives an oscillatory radiation pattern whose spatial shape is fixed while the instantaneous intensity pulsates.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
The animation makes two points transparent. First, reversing the sign of the dipole moment does not change the radiated intensity because the power is quadratic in the field. Second, the angular structure remains dipolar throughout the oscillation: the source strength varies in time, but the symmetry of the radiation pattern is fixed by the source geometry.

A uniformly moving charge does not radiate in the same way as an accelerated charge because its field configuration, although time-dependent in a given frame, does not contain the same asymptotic transverse $1/r$ component associated with irreversible energy transport to infinity. Acceleration changes the field in a way that cannot be accounted for by mere translation of a static configuration; it generates genuine disturbances that detach from the source and propagate outward.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
Retarded time is the mathematical expression of causality in this context. The field at a distant point is determined by the source history on the past light cone, not by the source at the same coordinate time. This is why the wave equation and the retarded Green's function are so central: they encode both the propagation speed and the correct causal support of the solution.

The far field carries energy to infinity precisely because its amplitude scales as $1/r$. The corresponding Poynting vector therefore scales as $1/r^2$, so the flux through a sphere of area $4\pi r^2$ tends to a finite limit. Faster-decaying fields do not survive this large-radius integration and therefore cannot represent outgoing radiation in the strict asymptotic sense.

This notebook is the natural gateway to more advanced subjects: systematic multipole radiation theory, radiation from relativistic charges, antenna analysis, scattering theory, and the use of Green's functions in both classical and quantum field contexts.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text: Key Takeaways Radiation is the part of the electromagnetic field that propagates causally and carries finite energy to infinity. The Lorenz gauge converts the potential equations into inhomogeneous wave equations with retarded solutions. Retarded potentials make causality explicit: fields depend on earlier source values located on the past light cone. In the far zone, the fields are transverse, scale as $1/r$, and satisfy $\mathbf B = \hat{\mathbf r}\times \mathbf E / c$. Electric dipole radiation is the leading radiative contribution for many localized sources and has angular intensity proportional to $\sin^2\theta$. The average dipole power scales as $\omega^4 p_0^2$, and for a point charge reduces to the Larmor formula. Common Pitfalls Confusing time-dependent near fields with radiation fields. Not every time-varying field transports power to infinity. Replacing retarded source values by instantaneous ones.

chunks.title: Electromagnetic Radiation
chunks.text:
This destroys the causal structure of the theory. Forgetting that the far-zone approximation applies asymptotically and should not be used near the source. Treating dipole radiation as universal without checking whether the electric dipole moment vanishes by symmetry. Ignoring the distinction between field amplitude scaling and power scaling. Connection to Later Topics

The present development leads directly to retarded Green's functions, systematic multipole radiation expansions, relativistic radiation from moving charges, antenna theory, scattering, and synchrotron or bremsstrahlung-type processes. In each of these subjects the same structural ideas reappear: wave equations, causality, asymptotic fields, source moments, and energy flux.

chunks.title: FFT Poisson solver
chunks.text:
This notebook solves Poisson's equation,

$$ \nabla^2 \phi(x,y) = -\frac{\rho(x,y)}{\varepsilon_0}, $$

using the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** on a **periodic 2D domain**.

The point of the notebook is to show all of the ideas in one place:

1. the physics of Poisson's equation, 2. the mathematics of the Fourier transform, 3. and the numerical implementation that turns the PDE into an algebraic solve.

1. a periodic manufactured-solution test, 2. and a physical dipole-like charge distribution built from two Gaussian lumps.

In real space, Poisson's equation couples every grid point to its neighbors. A finite-difference method therefore leads to a large linear system or an iterative relaxation method.

In Fourier space, the Laplacian becomes multiplication:

$$ \mathcal{F}[\nabla^2 \phi] = -k^2 \hat{\phi}, $$

$$ -k^2 \hat{\phi}(k_x,k_y) = -\frac{\hat{\rho}(k_x,k_y)}{\varepsilon_0}, $$

$$ \hat{\phi}(k_x,k_y) = \frac{\hat{\rho}(k_x,k_y)}{\varepsilon_0\,k^2} \qquad (k \neq 0). $$

chunks.title: FFT Poisson solver
chunks.text:
That is the whole idea: in the Fourier basis, the Laplacian is diagonal, so the PDE becomes a simple division mode by mode.

Important Physics Assumption: Periodic Boundaries

An FFT Poisson solver is most natural on a **periodic box**. That means the left and right edges match, and the bottom and top edges match.

This has an important consequence: the total charge in the periodic box should be zero. Integrating Poisson's equation over the whole domain gives

$$ \int \nabla^2\phi\, dV = -\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\int \rho\, dV. $$

For a periodic domain, the boundary contributions cancel, so the left-hand side is zero. Therefore the periodic problem is solvable only if

Numerically, this means the **zero-frequency mode** of the charge density must vanish. In practice, we often subtract the mean charge density before solving.

Also, the potential is determined only up to an additive constant. Setting the k=0 mode of $\hat{\phi}$ to zero is equivalent to choosing the mean potential to be zero.

Example 1: Manufactured Periodic Solution

chunks.title: FFT Poisson solver
chunks.text:
To verify the method, choose a periodic exact potential:

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{exact}}(x,y) = \cos\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L_x}\right)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi y}{L_y}\right). $$

$$ \nabla^2 \phi_{\mathrm{exact}} = -\left[\left(\frac{2\pi}{L_x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{2\pi}{L_y}\right)^2\right]\phi_{\mathrm{exact}}. $$

Since Poisson's equation is $\nabla^2\phi = -\rho/\varepsilon_0$, the corresponding charge density is

$$ \rho(x,y) = \varepsilon_0\left[\left(\frac{2\pi}{L_x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{2\pi}{L_y}\right)^2\right]\phi_{\mathrm{exact}}(x,y). $$

This example is periodic and has zero mean, so it is ideal for an FFT-based benchmark.

Example 2: Dipole-Like Charge Density in a Periodic Box

Now build a more physical source from two Gaussian charge clouds, one positive and one negative. This gives a neutral configuration with a clear electrostatic structure.

chunks.title: FFT Poisson solver
chunks.text:
This is a good example because it shows: how the FFT solver handles smooth localized sources, how the potential responds to positive and negative charge, and how the electric field lines run from the positive lump toward the negative lump.

Because the domain is periodic, the source interacts with its periodic images. That is not a bug: it is the physical meaning of solving Poisson's equation on a torus rather than in an isolated grounded box.

The FFT Poisson solver is excellent when: the domain is periodic, the source is smooth, and you want a direct, non-iterative solve.

It is often much shorter and faster than relaxation-based solvers. What It Does Not Replace

It does **not** replace finite-difference methods for grounded boxes or arbitrary Dirichlet boundaries. Those problems are not naturally periodic, so the FFT method is not the right tool there.

So the numerical picture is: **finite difference / SOR** for grounded boxes and fixed boundaries, **FFT / spectral methods** for periodic domains.

chunks.title: FFT Poisson solver
chunks.text: That is exactly why this notebook complements the finite-difference Poisson notebook rather than duplicating it.

chunks.title: Finite difference Poisson solver
chunks.text:
Finite-Difference Poisson Solver in Electrostatics

This notebook builds a simple **2D finite-difference solver** for Poisson's equation,

$$ \nabla^2 \phi(x,y) = -\frac{\rho(x,y)}{\varepsilon_0}, $$

on a square domain with **Dirichlet boundary conditions**. The goal is to make the numerical method explicit rather than hidden inside a black-box package.

1. A manufactured-solution test where the numerical answer is compared against a known analytic potential. 2. A grounded-box example with a localized charge distribution, showing how the same solver handles a more physical electrostatic configuration.

On a uniform grid with spacing $h$, the five-point finite-difference approximation to the Laplacian is

$$ \frac{\phi_{i+1,j} + \phi_{i-1,j} + \phi_{i,j+1} + \phi_{i,j-1} - 4\phi_{i,j}}{h^2} = -\frac{\rho_{i,j}}{\varepsilon_0}. $$

Solving this for the interior node gives the update rule

$$ \phi_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\phi_{i+1,j} + \phi_{i-1,j} + \phi_{i,j+1} + \phi_{i,j-1} + h^2\frac{\rho_{i,j}}{\varepsilon_0}\right). $$

chunks.title: Finite difference Poisson solver
chunks.text:
Below, the update is accelerated with **successive over-relaxation (SOR)**.

To verify the solver, choose a potential that is known analytically:

$$ \phi_{\mathrm{exact}}(x,y) = \sin(\pi x)\sin(\pi y). $$

$$ \nabla^2 \phi_{\mathrm{exact}} = -2\pi^2 \sin(\pi x)\sin(\pi y), $$

so with $\varepsilon_0 = 1$ the corresponding charge density is

$$ \rho(x,y) = 2\pi^2\sin(\pi x)\sin(\pi y). $$

Because the exact potential vanishes on the boundary of the unit square, this is an ideal benchmark for the grounded-box solver.

Example 2: Localized Charge Distribution in a Grounded Box

Now keep the boundary grounded but use a source that looks more like an electrostatic charge configuration than a manufactured test. The charge density below is a superposition of a positive and a negative Gaussian lump.

This example is useful because it shows the solver responding to: nontrivial source structure, grounded boundary conditions, and the resulting electric field geometry.

chunks.title: Finite difference Poisson solver
chunks.text:
This notebook is intentionally simple, but the same framework can be extended in several useful directions: compare Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, and SOR iteration counts, allow nonzero Dirichlet boundary data, move from constant $\varepsilon$ to spatially varying dielectric media, switch from a uniform square grid to rectangular or adaptive meshes, or replace the iterative solver by a sparse linear algebra approach.

In other words, this finite-difference Poisson solver is a good numerical bridge between the theoretical Poisson notebook and more advanced computational electrodynamics.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
Green's functions provide one of the most systematic ways to solve Poisson and Laplace boundary value problems in electrostatics. They convert a differential equation into an integral representation in which the geometry and the boundary conditions are built directly into the kernel. In Jackson-style electrodynamics, this is not merely a formal trick: it is a unifying framework that clarifies the role of the delta function, the meaning of the Coulomb potential, the logic of the method of images, and the structure of electrostatic boundary value theory.

Electrostatic potentials satisfy Poisson's equation,

$$ \nabla^2 \phi(\mathbf{r}) = -\frac{\rho(\mathbf{r})}{\varepsilon_0}. $$

In free space, this equation admits the familiar Coulomb integral solution. In the presence of boundaries, however, the problem becomes substantially more subtle. One may know the potential on a conducting surface, the normal electric field on a boundary, or some mixed set of conditions. Direct solution of the differential equation then depends strongly on the geometry.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
Green's functions provide a systematic response kernel adapted to the operator and to the boundary conditions. Roughly speaking, the Green's function is the potential generated at the field point $\mathbf{r}$ by a unit point source placed at the source point $\mathbf{r}'$, subject to the same boundary conditions as the physical problem.

This viewpoint turns the electrostatic problem into a superposition principle of the most refined kind: every source distribution is represented as a continuous superposition of point sources, and the boundary geometry is incorporated from the beginning rather than appended afterward.

Throughout this notebook we adopt the convention

$$ \nabla^2 G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = -4\pi\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}'). $$

This is the conventional electrostatic normalization used in Jackson. The SI factor $1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0)$ then appears in the potential representation formula rather than inside the definition of the Green's function itself.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The differential operator acts on the field point $\mathbf{r}$, while the source point $\mathbf{r}'$ is held fixed. This distinction is essential: $\mathbf{r}'$ locates the point source. $\mathbf{r}$ is the point at which the potential is evaluated.

Thus, for fixed $\mathbf{r}'$, the function $G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')$ is harmonic everywhere in $\mathbf{r}$ except at $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{r}'$, where it has the singularity required to reproduce a delta distribution.

Under suitable conditions, particularly for self-adjoint boundary value problems, one has the reciprocity relation

$$ G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = G(\mathbf{r}',\mathbf{r}). $$

This symmetry is not automatic for every conceivable formulation, but it holds in the standard electrostatic Dirichlet and suitably normalized Neumann settings.

In unbounded free space, the Green's function is determined by translational and rotational invariance. It can depend only on the separation $ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' $, and the appropriate solution is

$$ G_0(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }. $$

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \nabla^2 \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' } = 0, \qquad \mathbf{r}\neq \mathbf{r}'. $$

Globally, however, the correct distributional identity is

$$ \nabla^2 \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' } = -4\pi\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}'). $$

This is the mathematical statement that the Coulomb kernel is the fundamental solution of the Laplacian in three dimensions.

The Coulomb potential for a point charge $q$ at $\mathbf{r}'$ follows immediately:

$$ \phi(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} G_0(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{q}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }. $$

For a general charge density, superposition yields the free-space solution of Poisson's equation,

$$ \phi(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\int \frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}')}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }\,d^3r'. $$

Thus the usual Coulomb integral is nothing more than the Green's-function solution with the free-space kernel.

Derivation of the Green's Function Solution

Let $\phi(\mathbf{r}')$ satisfy Poisson's equation in a region $V$,

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ \nabla'^2 \phi(\mathbf{r}') = -\frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}')}{\varepsilon_0}, $$

and let $G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')$ satisfy

$$ \nabla'^2 G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = -4\pi\delta^{(3)}(\mathbf{r}'-\mathbf{r}). $$

Applying Green's second identity in the source variable $\mathbf{r}'$,

$$ \int_V \left(\phi\nabla'^2 G - G\nabla'^2\phi\right)d^3r' = \oint_S \left(\phi\frac{\partial G}{\partial n'} - G\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n'}\right)dA', $$

and substituting the defining equations gives

$$ -4\pi \phi(\mathbf{r}) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\int_V G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')\rho(\mathbf{r}')\,d^3r' = \oint_S \left(\phi\frac{\partial G}{\partial n'} - G\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n'}\right)dA'. $$

$$ \phi(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\int_V \rho(\mathbf{r}')G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')\,d^3r' \frac{1}{4\pi}\oint_S \phi(\mathbf{r}')\frac{\partial G}{\partial n'}\,dA' \frac{1}{4\pi}\oint_S G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial n'}\,dA'. $$

This is the general Green's-function representation formula.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The first term is the volume contribution from the source distribution. The boundary terms encode the influence of the enclosing surfaces. Their presence is essential: in bounded electrostatic problems, the solution is determined not only by charges in the volume but also by the boundary data.

The power of the method lies in choosing a Green's function adapted to the specified boundary condition so that one of the surface terms vanishes or simplifies.

Dirichlet and Neumann Green's Functions Dirichlet Green's Function

A Dirichlet Green's function is chosen to satisfy

$$ G_D(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = 0 \qquad \text{for } \mathbf{r}' \in S. $$

$$ \phi(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\int_V \rho(\mathbf{r}')G_D(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')\,d^3r' \frac{1}{4\pi}\oint_S \phi(\mathbf{r}')\frac{\partial G_D}{\partial n'}\,dA'. $$

This is the natural choice when the boundary potential is specified. Physically, the Green's function is the point-source response in a region whose boundary is held at zero potential. Neumann Green's Function

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
A Neumann Green's function is adapted to prescribed normal derivative on the boundary. One chooses

$$ \frac{\partial G_N}{\partial n'} = \text{specified function on } S, $$

typically arranged so that the boundary term involving $\partial \phi/\partial n'$ becomes the directly usable one. In the pure Neumann problem, the potential is determined only up to an additive constant. Correspondingly, the Green's function is defined only up to the addition of functions consistent with that ambiguity, together with an appropriate compatibility condition on the total normal derivative.

The constant ambiguity is not a defect. It reflects the physical fact that only potential differences matter unless a reference level is fixed.

Suppose $G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')$ and $G(\mathbf{r}',\mathbf{r})$ satisfy the same self-adjoint boundary value problem. Applying Green's identity to the pair of functions

$$ u(\mathbf{x}) = G(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{r}), \qquad v(\mathbf{x}) = G(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{r}'), $$

one finds, after the boundary terms vanish appropriately,

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
$$ G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = G(\mathbf{r}',\mathbf{r}). $$

This reciprocity relation relies on the self-adjoint character of the Laplacian together with compatible boundary conditions. Mathematically, it expresses the symmetry of the inverse operator. Physically, it says that the response measured at $\mathbf{r}$ due to a source at $\mathbf{r}'$ is the same as the response measured at $\mathbf{r}'$ due to an identical source placed at $\mathbf{r}$, provided the surrounding boundary conditions are the same.

Canonical Examples 1. Free-Space Green's Function

The fundamental solution in unbounded space is

$$ G_0(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' }. $$

It is translationally invariant and depends only on the distance between source and field points. 2. Dirichlet Green's Function for a Grounded Plane

Consider the half-space $z>0$ with boundary plane $z=0$ held at zero potential. A suitable Dirichlet Green's function is

$$ G_D(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' } - \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}'_{\mathrm{image}} }, $$

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
where the image point is obtained by reflecting $\mathbf{r}'=(x',y',z')$ across the plane:

$$ \mathbf{r}'_{\mathrm{image}}=(x',y',-z'). $$

On the boundary $z=0$, the two distances are equal, so $G_D=0$. Thus the boundary condition is built directly into the kernel. 3. Green's Function Inside a Spherical Boundary

For a sphere of radius $a$ with grounded boundary, one can construct a Dirichlet Green's function by combining the free-space singularity with an image term placed at the Kelvin-inverted point. The result is more intricate than the planar case, but the logic is identical: the singular Coulomb kernel is corrected by a harmonic term chosen to enforce the boundary condition on the sphere.

These examples show the general strategy: the Green's function consists of the fundamental singular piece plus an additional harmonic contribution selected so that the required boundary condition is satisfied.

The method of images is best understood as a special technique for constructing Green's functions in geometries with enough symmetry.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
For the grounded conducting plane, the Green's function is

$$ G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' } - \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}'_{\text{image}} }. $$

The first term gives the singular response to the source itself. The second term is harmonic in the physical half-space and is chosen precisely so that the total Green's function vanishes on the plane. This construction is exactly what the method of images produces.

Thus the method of images is not a separate theory. It is a particularly elegant way of building a Green's function for certain special boundaries where a reflected or transformed source can enforce the boundary condition.

A Green's function encodes two pieces of information simultaneously: the inverse of the differential operator, and the boundary condition appropriate to the physical problem.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
This is what makes Green's functions so powerful in electrostatics. Once the correct kernel is found, the potential follows by an integral formula, and the uniqueness theorem ensures that the result is the physical solution. In effect, all the geometric difficulty of the boundary value problem is shifted into the construction of the kernel itself.

The computational examples below are designed to make the Green's-function viewpoint concrete. We will visualize the free-space kernel, compare it with the grounded-plane Green's function, and use the image construction to show how the same source produces very different potentials in the presence of a boundary.

For clarity, we work in dimensionless units and suppress the overall factor $1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0)$ in most plots. The geometry of the Green's function is the main focus.

Free-Space Green's Function as a Spatial Response Kernel

We first visualize the free-space Green's function on a two-dimensional slice through three-dimensional space. The kernel

$$ G_0(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}') = \frac{1}{ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' } $$

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
is singular at the source point and decays away from it. In the plot below, the source is placed at a fixed point above the origin in the $(x,z)$ plane.

The large peak marks the singularity at the source point. Away from that point, the kernel is smooth and decreases with distance. This is the universal free-space response of the Laplacian in three dimensions.

1D Slice Through the Singularity Structure

A line slice is useful for visualizing the singular behavior more transparently. We compare a horizontal slice through the source height with a slice well away from the source. The singularity appears only when the observation line passes through the source point.

The slice through the source exhibits the regularized spike associated with the Coulomb singularity, while the displaced slice remains finite everywhere. This highlights the distinction between the singular source point and the harmonic behavior away from it.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
For the half-space $z>0$ with a grounded plane at $z=0$, the Dirichlet Green's function is obtained by subtracting the image contribution. The resulting kernel vanishes on the boundary while preserving the correct point singularity in the physical region.

Compared with the free-space kernel, the response is suppressed near the boundary because the image term enforces zero potential at the plane. The source singularity remains, but the surrounding field is reshaped globally by the boundary condition.

Potential of a Point Charge Near a Grounded Plane

For a point charge above a grounded conducting plane, the physical potential in the half-space is proportional to the grounded-plane Green's function. Contour plots make the geometric effect of the image construction especially clear.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
The potential contours meet the grounded plane in a way consistent with zero potential there. Relative to free space, the field is compressed toward the boundary because the image charge contributes an equal and opposite response. This is the electrostatic content of the Dirichlet Green's function for the half-space.

Free Space Versus Grounded Boundary for the Same Source

The same physical source produces different potentials in different boundary environments. We compare the free-space kernel and the grounded-plane kernel for the same source point to make this dependence explicit.

The third panel isolates the boundary contribution. Mathematically, this is the added harmonic term that converts the fundamental solution into the Green's function appropriate to the half-space. Physically, it is the boundary-induced distortion of the response to the source.

Numerical Evaluation of a Green-Function Integral in a Simplified Setting

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
To illustrate the integral-superposition viewpoint directly, we compute a regularized one-dimensional analogue of a Green-function convolution for a smooth localized charge distribution. The goal is pedagogical rather than literal: it makes transparent how a source distribution is turned into a potential by integrating against a kernel.

Although this is a simplified analogue rather than the exact three-dimensional electrostatic integral, it captures the essential logic of the Green's-function method: the source is weighted by a response kernel and summed over all source points. The resulting potential is smoother and more spatially extended than the original charge distribution because the kernel spreads the influence of each source point throughout the domain.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text:
A Green's function is not unique until the boundary conditions are specified. The singular part is fixed locally by the requirement that the Laplacian reproduce the delta function, but one may always add a function of $\mathbf{r}$ that is harmonic in the domain away from the source point. Different choices correspond to different boundary value problems.

This leads to an important distinction: The **fundamental solution** is the free-space kernel $1/ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' $. The **Green's function for a bounded domain** is the fundamental singularity plus whatever additional harmonic correction is needed to enforce the prescribed boundary condition.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text: From the operator viewpoint, the Green's function is the inverse of the differential operator in a distributional sense. More precisely, for the Laplacian subject to specified boundary conditions, the Green's function is the integral kernel of the inverse map from source to solution. This language becomes central far beyond electrostatics: wave equations, Helmholtz problems, diffusion equations, quantum mechanics, and field theory all rely on Green-function ideas in one form or another.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text: Key Takeaways Green's functions convert Poisson and Laplace boundary value problems into integral representations. The Green's function is the response to a unit point source, with the boundary conditions of the physical problem built directly into the kernel. In the Jackson convention used here, the Green's function satisfies $\nabla^2 G = -4\pi\delta^{(3)}$. The free-space Green's function is $1/ \mathbf{r}-\mathbf{r}' $, which is the fundamental solution of the Laplacian in three dimensions. Boundary-adapted Green's functions differ from the free-space kernel by added harmonic terms chosen to enforce Dirichlet or Neumann conditions. The method of images is a special way of constructing Green's functions in highly symmetric geometries. Reciprocity and uniqueness make Green's-function methods both mathematically rigorous and physically reliable.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text: Common Pitfalls Confusing the free-space fundamental solution with the Green's function appropriate to a bounded domain. Forgetting which variable the Laplacian acts on in $G(\mathbf{r},\mathbf{r}')$. Neglecting the boundary terms in the representation formula when the Green's function has not been chosen to eliminate them. Treating Dirichlet and Neumann Green's functions as interchangeable even though they encode different physical data. Overlooking the additive constant ambiguity in Neumann problems. Using an image construction without verifying that the resulting kernel satisfies the correct boundary condition and singularity structure.

chunks.title: Green's functions in electrostatics
chunks.text: Green's functions sit at the center of later electrodynamics and mathematical physics: **Method of images:** special Green-function constructions for simple boundaries. **Multipole expansion:** asymptotic analysis of Green-function integrals for localized sources. **Wave equations:** time-dependent analogues lead to retarded and advanced Green's functions. **Radiation theory:** propagators organize fields produced by time-varying sources. **Mathematical physics more broadly:** operator inversion, spectral theory, and integral equations all rely on the same conceptual framework.

chunks.path: notebooks/Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder.ipynb
chunks.title: Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder
chunks.text:
Jackson 2.11: Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder

This notebook develops the solution of Jackson 2.11 for a line charge placed outside a conducting cylinder. The solution in the PDF from Jackson_2_11_Homework_Solution.pdf is reproduced here with a more explicit physical interpretation and a few numerical checks.

A line charge with linear density $\tau$ is located parallel to the $z$ axis at a distance $R>b$ from the axis of a conducting cylinder of radius $b$. The cylinder is held at a fixed potential and the potential is chosen to vanish at infinity.

1. the image charge magnitude and position, 2. the exact potential in polar coordinates, 3. the asymptotic form far from the cylinder, 4. the induced surface-charge density, 5. and the force per unit length on the physical line charge.

Take cylindrical-polar coordinates $(\rho,\phi,z)$ centered on the cylinder axis. The physical line charge sits on the positive $x$ axis at $\rho=R$ and $\phi=0$.

For a line charge in two dimensions, the potential has the logarithmic form

chunks.path: notebooks/Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder.ipynb
chunks.title: Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder
chunks.text:
$$ \Phi_{\rm line}(r)=\frac{\tau}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{B^2}{r^2}\right), $$

where $r$ is the distance to the line charge and $B$ is an arbitrary constant.

We try the same strategy as the point-charge/sphere method of images: place one image line charge inside the cylinder at radius $R'$ on the same axis. The total potential becomes

$$ \Phi(\rho,\phi)=\frac{\tau}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{B^2}{r_1^2}\right)+\frac{\tau'}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{B^2}{r_2^2}\right), $$

$$ r_1^2=\rho^2+R^2-2\rho R\cos\phi, \qquad r_2^2=\rho^2+R'^2-2\rho R'\cos\phi. $$

The condition $\Phi\to 0$ as $\rho\to\infty$ forces the total line charge to vanish, so

The remaining boundary condition is that the cylinder surface $\rho=b$ be an equipotential. The cleanest way to see the answer is to demand that the ratio $r_2^2/r_1^2$ be independent of $\phi$ when $\rho=b$. Choosing

$$ b^2+R'^2-2bR'\cos\phi =\frac{b^2}{R^2}\left(b^2+R^2-2bR\cos\phi\right), $$

so the ratio is constant on the boundary. That is the inversion property that makes the image method work for the cylinder.

chunks.path: notebooks/Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder.ipynb
chunks.title: Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder
chunks.text:
With $\tau'=-\tau$ and $R'=b^2/R$, the potential outside the cylinder is

$$ \Phi(\rho,\phi)=\frac{\tau}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \ln\left(\frac{\rho^2+R'^2-2\rho R'\cos\phi}{\rho^2+R^2-2\rho R\cos\phi}\right), \qquad R'=\frac{b^2}{R}. $$

On the cylinder surface $\rho=b$, this becomes a constant:

$$ \Phi(b,\phi)=\frac{\tau}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{b^2}{R^2}\right) =\frac{\tau}{2\pi\varepsilon_0}\ln\left(\frac{b}{R}\right). $$

So the cylinder is indeed an equipotential. Since $R>b$, the logarithm is negative, so the cylinder potential is negative relative to infinity.

Far from both the line charge and the cylinder, the leading asymptotic term is

$$ \Phi_{\rm asym}(\rho,\phi)=\frac{\tau}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \frac{R^2-b^2}{\rho R}\cos\phi. $$

This is the potential of a two-dimensional dipole. The real line charge plus the induced charge on the conductor form a neutral system, so the monopole term vanishes and the first surviving far-field term is dipolar.

A good sanity test is that the potential on the cylinder be constant and that its angular derivative vanish there. Since

chunks.path: notebooks/Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder.ipynb
chunks.title: Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder
chunks.text:
$$ E_\phi=-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial \phi}, $$

a constant boundary potential automatically means zero tangential field.

The normal-field discontinuity on a conductor gives

$$ \sigma=-\varepsilon_0\left.\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial \rho}\right _{\rho=b}. $$

$$ \sigma(\phi)=\frac{\tau}{2\pi b} \frac{1-(R/b)^2}{1+(R/b)^2-2(R/b)\cos\phi}. $$

The numerator is negative because $R>b$, so the induced charge density is everywhere negative, as expected for a positive external line charge.

Because the real line charge and the induced conductor charge sum to zero, the far field cannot be monopolar. The exact potential should approach the dipole-like asymptotic form

$$ \Phi_{\rm asym}(\rho,\phi)=\frac{\tau}{2\pi\varepsilon_0} \frac{R^2-b^2}{\rho R}\cos\phi. $$

The physical line charge feels only the field of the image line charge. Since the image carries charge $-\tau$ and sits at radius $R'=b^2/R$, the separation between the two lines is

$$ d=R-R'=R-\frac{b^2}{R}=\frac{R^2-b^2}{R}. $$

The field at the physical line charge due to the image is therefore

chunks.path: notebooks/Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder.ipynb
chunks.title: Jackson 2.11 Line Charge Outside a Conducting Cylinder
chunks.text:
$$ E=\frac{-\tau}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 d}\,\hat{\rho}, $$

$$ \frac{\mathbf{F}}{L}=-\frac{\tau^2 R}{2\pi\varepsilon_0(R^2-b^2)}\,\hat{\rho}. $$

The sign shows that the force is attractive: the line charge is pulled inward toward the cylinder.

The whole problem works because inversion in the circle maps the outside source at $R$ to the inside image at $b^2/R$. Together with the requirement that the potential vanish at infinity, that fixes the image charge to be $-\tau$. The conductor surface then becomes an exact equipotential, the induced charge density follows directly from the normal derivative, and the far field reduces to a dipole because the net charge of the full system is zero.
