chunks.title: chapter 1
chunks.text:
These notes are basically my way of slowing Jackson down a little bit and rewriting the chapter in a way that I can actually study from later without feeling lost after two pages.

I do not want this to read like a polished textbook. I want it to feel like the kind of notes I would keep next to me while working problems: direct, a little informal, and focused on what actually matters.

So the point here is: keep the main ideas clear write the derivations in a way that still makes sense when I come back later connect the math to the physical picture leave myself something more useful than just highlighting the book

This chapter is the starting point for electrostatics, so I want to use it to build the language correctly from the beginning instead of memorizing formulas without context.

I will keep the jackson notation as that was something I realized was a problem for me the change of notation in every book or paper.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Introduction to Electrostatics Chapter 1 Introduction to Electrostatics Diego Juarez. These notes are written in the notation used in Jackson: charge density ρ(x), electric field E(x), scalar potential Φ(x), outward unit normal n, and permittivity ε0 in SI units. Prepared from the material spanning pp. 24–48 of Jackson, Chapter 1. Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: 1 Contents 1 Coulomb’s Law 2 2 Electric Field 2 3 Gauss’s Law 3 4 Differential Form of Gauss’s Law 4 5 Another Equation of Electrostatics and the Scalar Potential 4 6 Surface Distributions of Charges and Dipoles; Discontinuities in Field and Potential 5 7 Poisson and Laplace Equations 6 8 Green’s Theorem 7 9 Uniqueness with Dirichlet or Neumann Boundary Conditions 7 10 Formal Solution with Green Function 8 11 Electrostatic Potential Energy, Energy Density, and Capacitance 9 12 Variational Approach to the Solution of Laplace and Poisson Equations 10 13 Relaxation Method for Two-Dimensional Electrostatic Problems 10 1 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 Global framework of electrostatics Electrostatics concerns time-independent charge distributions and the fields they generate. In Jackson’s presentation, the subject is built from three layers: 1. the empirical law of force between charges (Coulomb’s law), 2.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: the field description in terms of E(x) and Φ(x), 3. the differential and variational formulations of boundary-value problems. The real power of the subject lies in the transition from forces between individual charges to field equations and then to general methods for solving them under boundary conditions. 1. Coulomb’s Law Coulomb’s law is the experimental starting point of electrostatics. For two point charges q1 and q2 located at x1 and x2, the force on q1 due to q2 is 1 x1−x2 F= q1q2 4πε0 |x1−x2|3. Its content is richer than the formula alone suggests. It asserts: • the force is central, directed along the line joining the charges; • the magnitude scales as 1/r2; • attraction and repulsion are encoded by the algebraic sign of q1q2; • forces obey linear superposition. At a graduate level, one should distinguish the physical and mathematical roles of the law. Physically, it is an empirical statement valid for charges at rest.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Mathematically, it provides the kernel from which the entire integral theory of electrostatics follows. In particular, because the kernel is central and scales as r−2, the associated potential will scale as r−1, and this is precisely the function whose Laplacian generates a delta distribution. The superposition principle is indispensable. If there are many charges, the total force is the vector sum of pairwise Coulomb forces. Without superposition, one would not obtain linear field equations. It is this linearity that ultimately makes Green functions, variational methods, and relaxation schemes possible. 2. Electric Field The electric field is introduced to separate the source configuration from the response of a particular test charge. Jackson defines it operationally as force per unit charge in the limit of a sufficiently 2 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 small test charge: F= qE. This limiting process matters.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: A finite test charge would in general disturb the original charge configuration, so the field is not merely “the force divided by charge” in a naive sense; it is the force-to-charge ratio in the idealized limit where the test charge does not modify the sources. For a point charge q1 at x1, E(x) = 1 4πε0 x−x1 q1 |x−x1|3. For a collection of point charges, E(x) = 1 4πε0 i x−xi qi |x−xi|3 , and for a continuous distribution, E(x) = 1 4πε0 ρ(x′) x−x′ |x−x′|3 d3x ′ . This transition from discrete to continuous sources is conceptually important. The density ρ(x) is not merely a convenience; it is the macroscopic quantity appropriate when many microscopic charges are present. Jackson emphasizes that the mathematical idealizations of electrostatics are macroscopic constructs. The field is thus a coarse-grained description whose success depends on scale separation between microscopic structure and macroscopic measurement.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: At this stage the Dirac delta function naturally appears. A point charge at xi is represented by ρ(x) = qi δ(x−xi), or more generally, ρ(x) = qiδ(x−xi). i This is not just notation. It is the exact bridge between localized sources and field equations. The singularity structure of the field near point charges is encoded distributionally through δ(x−xi). 3. Gauss’s Law Gauss’s law gives an integral relation between the electric flux through a closed surface and the enclosed charge: 1 E·n da= S ε0 V ρ(x) d3x. For a point charge, the derivation is geometrical. The quantity E·n da can be written in terms of the solid angle element dΩ subtended by the oriented surface element at the charge. One finds q E·n da= 4πε0 dΩ. 3 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 Integrating over a closed surface yields 4π if the charge lies inside and 0 if it lies outside, giving the point-charge form of Gauss’s law.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Superposition then extends the result to arbitrary source distributions. The law is deeper than a computational trick for spheres, cylinders, or planes. It states that the total outward flux is determined only by the total enclosed charge, independent of how that charge is arranged. This is a global constraint on the field. It does not determine the full vector field by itself, but it fixes a crucial invariant of the field. One should also notice the logical ingredients behind Gauss’s law: the inverse-square dependence, central forces, and linear superposition. If any one of these failed, the surface integral would no longer collapse to an enclosed-source statement of this form. 4. Differential Form of Gauss’s Law By the divergence theorem, S A·n da= V ∇·A d3x, so Gauss’s law becomes V ρ ∇·E− ε0 d3x= 0. Because this holds for arbitrary volumes V, one obtains the local equation ρ ∇·E= ε0.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: This step marks the transition from a global statement to a local field equation. The divergence measures the local rate at which field lines “emerge” from a point, so the equation says that charge density acts as the source density of the electric field. In free space, where ρ = 0, the field is divergence-free. The conceptual shift is major: instead of integrating the effect of sources over all space every time one wants the field, one may instead solve a local partial differential equation. This is the beginning of the field-theoretic viewpoint that dominates the remainder of the book. 5. Another Equation of Electrostatics and the Scalar Potential The divergence equation alone is insufficient to determine a vector field. A second equation is needed. For electrostatics, the Coulomb integral implies ∇×E = 0. The reason is that x−x′ |x−x′|3 = −∇ 1 |x−x′|, 4 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: 1 so the electric field is the gradient of an integral over a scalar kernel. Since the curl of a gradient vanishes, electrostatic fields are irrotational. Hence there exists a scalar potential Φ(x) such that E= −∇Φ. The minus sign is physical: the electric field points in the direction of decreasing potential. The potential itself is Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 ρ(x′) |x−x′|d3x ′ . The potential is defined only up to an additive constant. This reflects the fact that only potential differences are physically meaningful in electrostatics. Work done in moving a test charge from Ato B is W=−q B E·dl= q ΦB−ΦA. A Thus qΦ is potential energy, and the line integral of E depends only on the endpoints. In particular, E·dl = 0. C This path independence is the defining signature of conservative fields. 6.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Surface Distributions of Charges and Dipoles; Discontinuities in Field and Potential σ A surface-charge density σ(x) concentrated on a surface S produces a jump in the normal component of the field. If E1 and E2 are the limiting fields on the two sides of the surface and n points from side 1 to side 2, then (E2−E1)·n = ε0. The tangential component remains continuous in electrostatics because ∇×E = 0 implies the vanishing of the circulation around an infinitesimal loop crossing the boundary. The scalar potential due to a surface charge is Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S σ(x′) |x−x′|da′ . For ordinary surface charge distributions, Φ is continuous even though E is not. This is a subtle but fundamental point: a derivative of a continuous function may jump, while the function itself remains continuous. A dipole layer is more singular. Jackson constructs it as the limit of two oppositely charged nearby 5 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: 1 sheets, with surface dipole-moment density D(x) held fixed in the limit. The potential becomes Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 S D(x′) n ·∇′ 1 |x−x′| da′ 1 =− 4πε0 S D(x′) dΩ. For a dipole layer, the potential itself is discontinuous across the surface: D Φ2−Φ1 = ε0. This is one of the most instructive examples in classical field theory. A surface charge creates a discontinuity in the normal derivative of the potential; a dipole layer creates a discontinuity in the potential itself. The hierarchy reflects the distributional order of the source. 7. Poisson and Laplace Equations Combining ρ ∇·E= , E= −∇Φ, ε0 one obtains ∇2Φ =− This is Poisson’s equation. In charge-free regions, ∇2Φ = 0, ρ ε0. which is Laplace’s equation. These equations are central because they replace the nonlocal Coulomb integral by a local partial differential equation.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Their mathematical character is elliptic, and that ellipticity is what gives electrostatics its smoothing, averaging, and uniqueness properties. Jackson also shows how Poisson’s equation is satisfied by the Coulomb integral. The key identity is the distributional Laplacian of the Coulomb kernel: ∇2 1 |x−x′|=−4πδ(x−x′). This identity should be regarded as the precise analytic statement behind the phrase “the potential of a point charge. ” Away from the source point, 1/R is harmonic; at the source, its singularity carries exactly the delta-function strength needed to represent a localized charge. 6 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 8. Green’s Theorem Green’s identities follow from the divergence theorem applied to fields of the form ϕ∇ψ. The first identity is V ϕ∇2ψ+ ∇ϕ·∇ψ d3x= ϕ∂ψ S ∂nda, and the second identity is V ϕ∇2ψ−ψ∇2ϕ d3x= S ϕ∂ψ ∂n−ψ∂ϕ ∂n da.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Choosing ϕ= Φ(x′) and ψ = 1/|x−x′|yields an integral representation of the potential. For x inside the volume, Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 V ρ(x′) |x−x′|d3x ′+ 1 4π S 1 R ∂Φ ∂n′−Φ ∂ ∂n′ 1 R da′ . This formula is profound. It states that the potential inside a region is determined by two types of data: the sources within the volume and boundary information on the enclosing surface. Electrostatics is therefore fundamentally a boundary-value theory, not merely a source-integration theory. The surface term contains both Φ and its normal derivative, revealing that one must choose which type of boundary data is physically prescribed. 9. Uniqueness with Dirichlet or Neumann Boundary Conditions Suppose two solutions Φ1 and Φ2 satisfy Poisson’s equation in the same volume with the same boundary conditions. Their difference U = Φ2−Φ1 satisfies Laplace’s equation: ∇2U = 0. Using Green’s first identity with ϕ= ψ= U gives V U∇2U + |∇U|2 d3x= S U∂U ∂n da.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Since ∇2U = 0, V |∇U|2 d3x= S U∂U ∂n da. For Dirichlet boundary conditions, U = 0 on S, so the right-hand side vanishes. For Neumann boundary conditions, ∂U/∂n= 0 on S, and again the right-hand side vanishes. Thus |∇U|2 d3x= 0, V 7 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 which implies ∇U = 0 everywhere. Therefore U is constant. Under Dirichlet conditions the constant must vanish, so the solution is unique. Under Neumann conditions the solution is unique up to an arbitrary additive constant. This theorem is not just a mathematical nicety. It guarantees that electrostatic problems are physically well-posed once the appropriate boundary data are given. It also shows why specifying both Φ and ∂Φ/∂n on a closed surface is in general an overdetermination: that is the Cauchy problem, which is not the natural electrostatic boundary-value problem. 10.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Formal Solution with Green Function A Green function G(x,x′) satisfies ∇2G(x,x′) =−4πδ(x−x′), so it differs from the free-space kernel 1/R by a harmonic function chosen to enforce the boundary conditions: G(x,x′) = 1 |x−x′|+ F(x,x′), ∇2F = 0 in V. Using Green’s theorem, Jackson obtains the general representation Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 V ρ(x′)G(x,x′) d3x ′+ 1 4π S G∂Φ ∂n′−Φ ∂G ∂n′ da′ . The freedom in choosing F is used to eliminate one of the two boundary terms. For Dirichlet problems one chooses GD such that GD(x,x′) = 0 for x′∈S, which gives Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 V 1 ρ(x′)GD(x,x′) d3x ′ − 4π S Φ(x′)∂GD ∂n′ da′ . For Neumann problems one imposes an appropriate condition on ∂GN /∂n′instead. The physical meaning is elegant. The harmonic correction F may be interpreted as the potential generated by charges external to the region or by induced charges on boundaries. In conductor problems, this is the viewpoint behind the method of images.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: The Green function is therefore not merely a formal device; it is a source-plus-boundary response function. 8 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 11. Electrostatic Potential Energy, Energy Density, and Capaci- tance |xi−xj |, Hence the electrostatic energy density is identified as If a charge qi is brought into a pre-existing potential Φ(xi), the work required is Wi = qiΦ(xi). Summing over the assembly of charges gives for discrete charges W= 1 8πε0 i j qiqj with self-terms omitted. For continuous distributions, W= 1 2 ρ(x)Φ(x) d3x. Using Poisson’s equation and integrating by parts gives the field-energy form W= ε0 2 |E|2 d3x. w= ε0 2 |E|2 . This expression is conceptually powerful because it relocates the energy from “the interaction of charges” to the field filling space. Field theory here becomes more than a calculational reformulation: it changes where one says the energy resides.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Jackson also explains the subtlety that w is positive definite, whereas the interaction energy of unlike charges is negative. The resolution is that the field-energy expression contains divergent self-energy contributions that are absent from the pairwise interaction energy once self-terms are removed. For conductors, one writes linear relations between total charges Qi and potentials Vi: Qi = j Cij Vj. The coefficients Cii are capacitances; the off-diagonal coefficients are coefficients of induction. The electrostatic energy becomes 1 2 i rac12 i j Cij ViVj. Capacitance is thus not merely a property of an isolated object but of a configuration together with its surrounding conductors and boundary conditions. W= QiVi = 9 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 12. Variational Approach to the Solution of Laplace and Poisson Equations Jackson introduces the functional I[ψ] = 1 2 V ∇ψ·∇ψd3x− V gψd3x.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: If one varies ψ→ψ+ δψ, then to first order δI= V ∇ψ·∇(δψ) d3x− V gδψd3x. Using Green’s first identity, δI= V −∇2ψ−g δψd3x+ S δψ∂ψ ∂nda. If δψ= 0 on the boundary (Dirichlet class of variations), stationarity requires ∇2ψ=−g. Thus Poisson’s equation appears as the Euler–Lagrange equation of the functional. For electrostatics one identifies g= ρ/ε0 and ψ= Φ. This viewpoint is valuable for several reasons: • it proves that the solution is an extremum (indeed a minimum) of an energy-like functional; • it provides systematic approximation schemes through trial functions; • it connects electrostatics to the general variational structure of mathematical physics. The method is not merely formal. One chooses a family of trial functions satisfying the boundary conditions, inserts them into I[ψ], and minimizes with respect to the free parameters.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: The quality of the approximation then depends on how well the trial family captures the true qualitative behavior of the solution. This is why symmetry, regularity at the origin, and correct boundary behavior matter so much in the choice of ansatz. For Neumann boundary conditions, the functional is modified by a boundary term so that arbitrary variations are compatible with prescribed normal derivative on the surface. 13. RelaxationMethodforTwo-DimensionalElectrostaticProblems The relaxation method is the numerical incarnation of the variational and elliptic character of Laplace’s equation. On a square lattice with spacing h, one approximates the potential at a grid point (i,j) by ψij . Minimization of the discrete analogue of the Dirichlet functional leads to the 10 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 1 update rule 1 ψ0 = 4(ψN + ψE + ψS + ψW ).

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: That is, at equilibrium each interior lattice value equals the average of its nearest neighbors. This is the discrete mean-value property of harmonic functions. Iteratively replacing old values by neighbor averages drives the discrete configuration toward the minimum of the discretized functional and therefore toward the numerical solution of Laplace’s equation. The method is conceptually simple because it mirrors the physics: in the absence of sources, the potential at a point is determined by the surrounding values. Jackson further notes that better weighted averages can be formed using both cross and diagonal points, improving accuracy by canceling lower-order discretization error. This is the numerical- analysis side of electrostatics: the same structure that yields uniqueness and smoothing in the continuum yields stable iterative schemes on a grid. Closing synthesis The sequence of topics in Jackson is tightly organized.

chunks.title: chapter 1 notes
chunks.text: Coulomb’s law introduces the source-field relation; Gauss’s law and ∇×E = 0 convert that relation into local equations; the potential reformulates the vector problem as a scalar one; Poisson and Laplace equations define the core elliptic PDE structure; Green’s identities and Green functions turn boundary data into integral representations; uniqueness theorems justify the physical meaning of the resulting solutions; energy and variational principles reveal deeper structure; and relaxation methods show how the same mathematics becomes a practical computational algorithm. In this sense, Chapter 1 is not merely a review of electrostatics. It is the template for much of classical field theory. 11

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Boundary-Value Problems in Electrostatics Chapter 2 Boundary-Value Problems in Electrostatics: I Diego Juarez. These notes follow the notation and viewpoint of Jackson on pp. 57–84. I keep the same symbols used in the text whenever possible: scalar potential Φ, charge density ρ, surface-charge density σ, Green function G(x,x′), and SI units with ε0. The emphasis here is on what each construction is really doing physically, not only on the final formulas. Prepared from Chapter 2 of Classical Electrodynamics. Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: 2 Contents 1 Method of Images 2 2 Point Charge in the Presence of a Grounded Conducting Sphere 3 3 Point Charge in the Presence of a Charged, Insulated Conducting Sphere 4 4 Point Charge Near a Conducting Sphere at Fixed Potential 4 5 Conducting Sphere in a Uniform Electric Field by Method of Images 5 6 Green Function for the Sphere; General Solution for the Potential 6 7 Conducting Sphere with Hemispheres at Different Potentials 6 8 Orthogonal Functions and Expansions 7 9 Separation of Variables; Laplace Equation in Rectangular Coordinates 8 10 A Two-Dimensional Potential Problem; Summation of a Fourier Series 8 11 Fields and Charge Densities in Two-Dimensional Corners and Along Edges 9 12 Introduction to Finite Element Analysis for Electrostatics 10 1 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 Guiding idea of the chapter Chapter 1 gave the formal solution of Poisson and Laplace problems through Green functions.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Chapter 2 is where that formalism starts becoming useful in practice. Jackson focuses on three strategies: 1. replacing conductors by fictitious charges through the method of images, 2. representing solutions in orthogonal bases adapted to the geometry, 3. introducing numerical ideas through finite elements. The common theme is simple: the field equation is local, but the boundary conditions encode the real geometry of the problem. Every method in this chapter is really a way of making the boundary conditions manageable. 1. Method of Images The method of images is one of the cleanest examples of how uniqueness theorems turn a clever guess into an exact solution. The electrostatic problem usually begins with a point charge in the presence of one or more conducting surfaces, grounded or held at fixed potential.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Instead of solving Laplace’s equation with those surfaces present explicitly, one enlarges the region and replaces the conductors by fictitious charges placed outside the physical region of interest. The logic is as follows. Inside the physical region, away from the real charges, the potential must satisfy Laplace’s equation. If I can invent an auxiliary configuration of charges outside that region whose potential satisfies the same differential equation inside and also reproduces the required boundary values on the conductor, then by uniqueness the auxiliary potential is the physical one. The method works only when the geometry is favorable. A plane, a sphere, and a few other highly symmetric boundaries allow the image charges to be guessed in finite number. In more complicated geometries the Green-function language remains exact, but the images are no longer easy to identify or may require infinite sets.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: The simplest example is a charge q in front of a grounded infinite plane. The physical problem is equivalent to replacing the plane by a mirror charge−q at the reflected point. The potential in the allowed half-space is then the superposition of the real charge and the image charge. The conductor disappears from the calculation, but its boundary condition remains built into the construction. What matters conceptually is that image charges are not physically present. They are bookkeeping devices that manufacture the correct harmonic correction to the potential. 2 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 2. Point Charge in the Presence of a Grounded Conducting Sphere Jackson’s first major spherical example is a point charge q located at distance y from the center of a grounded conducting sphere of radius a. The goal is to find the potential outside the sphere subject to Φ(|x|= a) = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: By symmetry, if a single image charge is enough, it must lie on the same ray from the center as the real charge. One therefore tries =− = Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 q |x−y|+ q′ |x−y′|. Imposing the vanishing of the potential on the sphere determines the image data uniquely: a a2 ′ q ′ q, y . y y So the image charge lies inside the sphere, with opposite sign and reduced magnitude. This result is elegant because the geometry of the sphere turns the boundary condition into an inversion relation. The image point is not merely a reflected point as in the plane case; it is the inverse point under the mapping yy′ = a2. This inversion structure reappears later when Jackson writes the Green function for the sphere. Once the potential is known, the induced surface-charge density follows from the normal derivative at the conductor surface.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Jackson obtains σ=−ε0 ∂Φ ∂x x=a q =− 4πa2 a y (1 + a2/y2 1−a2/y2 −2(a/y) cos γ)3/2 , where γ is the angle between the observation point on the sphere and the radius toward the charge. The qualitative behavior is very clear. The surface charge is strongly concentrated on the near side of the sphere. As the external charge approaches the surface, the induced density becomes more and more peaked. The total induced charge is equal to the magnitude of the image charge, which is exactly what Gauss’s law demands. The force on the real charge is easiest to obtain by treating the image charge as if it were physical. One gets the attractive force 1 |F|= 4πε0 q2 a2 a y 3 1− −2 a2 y2 . Far away the force falls as y−3, the signature of an induced dipole interaction. Very near the sphere it grows much more sharply because the local induced charge distribution becomes strongly nonuniform.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: An important remark in Jackson is that the same formulas can be translated to the case y ≤a, 3 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 when the charge lies inside the sphere, provided one reinterprets the outward normal correctly. The mathematics is the same; only the physical side of the boundary changes. 3. PointChargeinthePresenceofaCharged, InsulatedConducting Sphere Now the sphere is no longer grounded. Instead it carries a specified total charge Q. Jackson builds this problem by superposition. Start with the grounded-sphere solution. That already accounts for the induced redistribution needed to enforce an equipotential surface. Then add enough charge to the sphere to bring its total charge from q′to the desired value Q. Because the added charge lives on a conducting sphere, it distributes uniformly. Outside the sphere, that extra piece of the potential is the same as the potential of a point charge at the center.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Thus the total potential becomes Φ(x) = 1 4πε0  q |x−y| aq − + Q+ a yq  . The force on the point charge is then 1 F= y x− a2 y2 y |x| y 4πε0 q y2 Q− qa3(2y2 −a2) y(y2 −a2)2 . y This example is physically rich. Even if the sphere has the same sign as the point charge, the force can still be attractive when the point charge is close enough, because the induced surface polarization dominates the direct Coulomb repulsion from the net charge. Only far away does the force reduce to the naive Coulomb form for two small charged bodies. Near the surface, the conductor behaves less like a point object and more like a deformable charge distribution. This is one of the nicest illustrations of why conductors can hold excess charge despite mutual repulsion: removing a bit of surface charge from the conductor costs work because the image attraction tends to pull it back. 4.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Point Charge Near a Conducting Sphere at Fixed Potential Instead of specifying the total charge Q, one may prescribe the potential V of the sphere. The construction is almost the same as in the previous section. The grounded-sphere contribution is retained, but now the central correction is chosen so that the potential on |x|= a equals V. The result is − y x− a2 y2 y  + Φ(x) = 1 4πε0  q |x−y| aq 4 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 racVa|x|. The force on the external charge is 1 y (y2 F= q y2 Va− 4πε0 qay3 −a2)2 . y The structure is the same as before, but conceptually this problem is cleaner if one thinks in terms of boundary conditions rather than conserved total charge. The conductor is connected to a reservoir that can add or remove charge as needed so long as the surface remains at potential V.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: For corresponding values of V and Q, the force law resembles the previous one closely, but the approach to the far-field limit is somewhat different. Again there can be an unstable equilibrium point where attraction and repulsion balance. 5. Conducting Sphere in a Uniform Electric Field by Method of Images Jackson next treats a conducting sphere in a uniform applied field E0. A uniform field may be imagined as the field created near the origin by two distant charges ±Qat z= ∓R, with Q/R2 fixed as R→∞. After inserting a conducting sphere of radius aat the origin, one adds the corresponding images of those distant charges and finally takes the large-R limit. The result is the compact potential Φ(r,θ) =−E0 r− a3 r2 cos θ. The first term is simply the background uniform field. The second is the potential of an induced dipole.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: This is why a neutral conductor in a uniform field behaves, from far away, as if it had acquired dipole moment p = 4πε0a3E0ˆ z. The induced surface-charge density is obtained from the radial derivative at r= a: σ= 3ε0E0 cos θ. It integrates to zero, as it should for a neutral isolated sphere. In fact there is no difference between grounded and insulated here because the total induced charge vanishes automatically. This is one of the classic polarizability results. The sphere responds to a uniform field by creating precisely the dipole correction needed to enforce that its surface is an equipotential. 5 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 6. GreenFunctionfortheSphere; GeneralSolutionforthePotential The image solution for the sphere can be reinterpreted as a Green function.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: For Dirichlet boundary conditions on a sphere of radius a, the Green function is the potential of a unit source at x′together with its image chosen so that G= 0 on the sphere. Jackson writes it as G(x,x′) = 1 |x−x′| a − x′ x− a2 x′2 x′. In spherical form, G(x,x′) = 1 (x2 + x′2 −2xx′cos γ)1/2− rac1 x2x′2 a2 + a2 −2xx′cos γ 1/2 , where γ is the angle between x and x′ . This expression is symmetric in x and x′, as a Dirichlet Green function should be. More importantly, it converts the image construction from a special trick into a general integral formula. The normal derivative on the boundary enters directly into the representation of the potential outside the sphere: Φ(x) = 1 4π Φ(a,θ′,ϕ′) a(x2 −a2) (x2 + a2 −2axcos γ)3/2 dΩ′ . So the earlier image problem is now seen as one concrete realization of the formal Green-function method of Chapter 1. 7.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Conducting Sphere with Hemispheres at Different Potentials Jackson then applies the spherical Green function to a sphere split into two hemispheres held at potentials +V and−V. This is a particularly instructive example because it is simple enough to set up exactly, yet complicated enough that a closed form for the general point is not immediate. Using the boundary integral formula, the potential outside the sphere is written as an angular integral over the upper hemisphere minus the lower hemisphere. On the positive z-axis, where the geometry simplifies, the integral can be done explicitly and gives Φ(z) = V 1− z2 −a2 z√z2 + a2. Far from the sphere the field behaves like a dipole field, as symmetry demands. Since the net boundary potential is antisymmetric under z→−z, only odd angular structures survive.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Jackson then expands the full solution in powers of the dimensionless parameter ax α= a2 + x2 , which produces only odd powers of cos θ. The leading terms show explicitly that the potential is 6 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 dominated by P1(cos θ) and then receives corrections from P3(cos θ), P5(cos θ), and so on. This is a nice preview of why Legendre polynomials are the natural language for separated solutions in spherical geometry. 8. Orthogonal Functions and Expansions Jackson pauses here to review orthogonal-function expansions because boundary-value methods will depend on them repeatedly. If Un(ξ) is an orthonormal set on (a,b), then b U∗ n(ξ)Um(ξ) dξ= δnm. a Any square-integrable function can then be expanded as f(ξ) =∞ anUn(ξ), an = b n=1 a U∗ n(ξ)f(ξ) dξ. The expansion coefficients minimize the mean-square error.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: That is why orthogonal bases are not merely convenient; they are optimal in a precise metric sense. Completeness introduces the closure relation ∞ n=1 U∗ n(ξ′)Un(ξ) = δ(ξ′ −ξ). This is the continuous analogue of a basis decomposition in linear algebra. Jackson then reviews Fourier series, f(x) = 1 A0 + 2 ∞ m=1 Amcos 2πmx a + Bmsin 2πmx a , and the Fourier integral, f(x) = 1 √2π ∞ A(k)eikxdk, A(k) = 1 −∞ √2π ∞ −∞ e−ikxf(x) dx. The message is that the choice of basis is controlled by symmetry and domain. Once the geometry suggests the right eigenfunctions, the boundary conditions determine the coefficients. 7 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 9. Separation of Variables; Laplace Equation in Rectangular Coor- dinates For Laplace’s equation in Cartesian coordinates, ∂2Φ ∂x2 + ∂2Φ ∂y2 + ∂2Φ ∂z2 = 0, Jackson assumes a product form 1 1 Y Z Φ(x,y,z) = X(x)Y(y)Z(z).

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: After division by XYZ, each coordinate dependence must separately equal a constant. One writes X d2X dx2 =−α2 , d2Y dy2 1 =−β2 , d2Z dz2 = γ2, α2 + β2 = γ2 . This produces sines, cosines, exponentials, or hyperbolic functions depending on which boundaries are to be satisfied. Jackson’s representative problem is a rectangular box with five grounded faces and the sixth face at prescribed potential V(x,y). The solution is expanded as Φ(x,y,z) =∞ n,m=1 Anmsin nπx a sin mπy b sinh(γnmz), with m2 γnm = π n2 a2 + b2. Matching the boundary value at z= c yields the coefficients Anm = 4 absinh(γnmc) a 0 b 0 V(x,y) sin nπx a sin mπy b dxdy. This is the canonical separated solution: the geometry dictates the eigenfunctions, and the nonho- mogeneous boundary data appear only in the expansion coefficients. 10.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: A Two-Dimensional Potential Problem; Summation of a Fourier Series Jackson next turns to the two-dimensional strip-like region 0 ≤x≤a, y≥0, 8 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 with Φ = 0 on x= 0 and x= a, Φ = V on y = 0, and Φ →0 as y →∞. Separation of variables gives Φ(x,y) =∞ n=1 Ane−nπy/asin nπx a , and matching the constant boundary value at y= 0 leaves only odd terms, 4V An = , n odd. πn Hence 1 Φ(x,y) = 4V π nodd n e−nπy/asin nπx a . This is already useful, but Jackson goes further and sums the series exactly by introducing Z= e(iπ/a)(x+iy) . Then the odd series becomes a logarithm, and the potential can be written in closed form as Φ(x,y) = 2V π tan−1 sin(πx/a) sinh(πy/a). This is a beautiful example of how separation of variables, Fourier analysis, and complex-variable structure are all different faces of the same harmonic problem. 11.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Fields and Charge Densities in Two-Dimensional Corners and Along Edges 1 1 One of the most important local lessons of electrostatics is that sharp corners and edges generate singular fields. Jackson studies the idealized geometry of two conducting planes meeting at opening angle β, both held at potential V. In polar coordinates, Laplace’s equation is ∂ ρ ∂ρ ρ ∂Φ ∂ρ + ρ2 ∂2Φ ∂ϕ2 = 0. A separated form Φ(ρ,ϕ) = R(ρ)Ψ(ϕ) yields radial powers and angular sines/cosines. Enforcing the constant potential on the two conducting faces gives the near-corner expansion Φ(ρ,ϕ) ≃V + a1ρπ/βsin πϕ β . The electric-field components therefore behave as Eρ ∼ρ(π/β)−1, Eϕ∼ρ(π/β)−1 , 9 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 and the surface-charge density on the conducting faces has the same power-law behavior, σ(ρ)∼ρ(π/β)−1 . This single exponent explains a lot of physics.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: If β < π, the exponent is positive and the field actually dies away at the corner. For a flat surface, β= π, the field remains finite. If β > π, corresponding to an edge rather than an interior corner, the exponent is negative and the field diverges as the edge is approached. For the edge of a thin conducting sheet, β = 2π, so σ(ρ)∼ρ−1/2 . The singularity is still integrable, so the total charge near the edge remains finite, but the local field becomes very large. This is not a mathematical curiosity. Jackson explicitly connects it with the practical effectiveness of pointed conductors and lightning rods. Sharp edges intensify the field enough to trigger breakdown first in their neighborhood. 12. Introduction to Finite Element Analysis for Electrostatics The final section of the chapter introduces finite element analysis (FEA) through Galerkin’s method.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Jackson starts from the two-dimensional Poisson equation ∇2ψ=−g in a region R with Dirichlet boundary conditions. Multiplying by a test function ϕthat vanishes on the boundary and integrating by parts gives the weak form [∇ϕ·∇ψ−gϕ] dxdy= 0. R The key move is to approximate the unknown field by a finite expansion in localized basis functions. Jackson first discusses a square-lattice choice, ϕij(x,y) = 1− |x−xi| h 1− |y−yj| h , for |x−xi|≤h, |y−yj|≤h, and zero otherwise. The approximate field is ψ(x,y) ≈ Ψklϕkl(x,y). k,l Choosing the test function to be one basis function at a time leads to a sparse linear system. Only nearby nodes couple to each other, which is the basic numerical advantage of localized bases. Jackson then moves to the more flexible triangular element. On a triangle with vertices (x1,y1), 10 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 (x2,y2), (x3,y3), the field is approximated linearly: ψe(x,y) = A+ Bx+ Cy.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: It is more systematic to express this through shape functions N(e) i (x,y), one for each vertex, such that N(e) i = 1 at vertex i and 0 at the other two. Then within one element, ψ(x,y) ≈ 3 j=1 Ψ(e) j N(e) j (x,y). The element matrix takes the form k(e) ij= Se(bibj + cicj), where Se is the triangle area and the coefficients bi,ci come from the linear shape functions. For each element, the Galerkin equations become 3 j=1 k(e) ij Ψ(e) j= Se 3 ge, i= 1,2,3, with ge approximated at the centroid. Assembling all elements gives the global sparse matrix equation KΨ= G. The diagonal entries of K collect contributions from all triangles sharing a node, and the off-diagonal entries collect couplings between nodes connected by element edges. This section is brief, but it already contains the central philosophy of FEA: one does not solve the differential equation pointwise.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: One solves a weak integral statement in a finite-dimensional space built from local shape functions. The geometry can then be refined where needed, which is precisely why finite elements are so much more adaptable than a uniform square grid. Jackson ends by remarking that the three-dimensional generalization is immediate in spirit: one replaces triangular elements by tetrahedra and uses four shape functions instead of three. Closing synthesis Chapter 2 is where electrostatics stops looking like a collection of isolated textbook tricks and starts becoming a real theory of boundary-value problems. The method of images shows how symmetry can collapse a boundary into a few fictitious charges. Orthogonal expansions show how geometry selects a natural basis in which the boundary data can be decomposed.

chunks.title: chapter 2 notes
chunks.text: Separation of variables reveals that the admissible functional forms are not arbitrary but are eigenfunctions of the coordinate-separated operators. The corner analysis shows that local geometry controls singular structure. And the finite-element discussion shows how all of this survives numerically once exact analytic forms are no longer available. 11 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 2 To me, the most useful lesson in this chapter is that the field equation is usually the easy part. The hard part is always the boundary. 12

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Boundary-Value Problems in Electrostatics Chapter 3 Boundary-Value Problems in Electrostatics: II Diego Juarez. These notes follow the notation and organization of Jackson’s Chapter 3. I kept the emphasis on the structure of the differential equations, the role of orthogonal functions, and the way Green functions adapt to separable geometries. The aim here is not to reproduce every algebraic line from the book, but to keep the logic of the chapter visible enough that the method behind each result is hard to lose. Prepared from the uploaded Chapter 3 notes PDF. Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: 3 Contents 1 Laplace Equation in Spherical Coordinates 2 2 Legendre Equation and Legendre Polynomials 3 3 Boundary-Value Problems with Azimuthal Symmetry 3 4 Behavior of Fields in a Conical Hole or Near a Sharp Point 4 5 Associated Legendre Functions and the Spherical Harmonics Ylm(θ,ϕ) 5 6 Addition Theorem for Spherical Harmonics 6 7 Laplace Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates; Bessel Functions 6 8 Boundary-Value Problems in Cylindrical Coordinates 8 9 Expansion of Green Functions in Spherical Coordinates 8 10 Solution of Potential Problems with Spherical Green Function Expansion 9 11 Expansion of Green Functions in Cylindrical Coordinates 9 12 Eigenfunction Expansions for Green Functions 9 13 Mixed Boundary Conditions, Conducting Plane with a Circular Hole 10 1 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 How this chapter fits into the story Chapter 1 gave the general field equations and Green’s theorem.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Chapter 2 showed how boundary conditions can sometimes be handled almost geometrically, especially by images. Chapter 3 is where the orthogonal-function machinery really takes over. The central idea is simple: if the boundary matches a coordinate system in which Laplace’s equation separates, then the natural basis functions of that coordinate system become the language of the solution. In spherical problems this means Legendre functions and spherical harmonics; in cylindrical problems it means Bessel functions; in both cases the Green function can be expanded in the same basis. 1. Laplace Equation in Spherical Coordinates 1 r2 sin2 θ ∂2Φ ∂ϕ2 = 0. 1 In spherical coordinates (r,θ,ϕ) the Laplace equation is 1 r ∂2 ∂r2 (rΦ) + 1 r2 sin θ ∂ ∂θ sin θ∂Φ ∂θ + Jackson writes the separated form as Φ(r,θ,ϕ) = U(r) r P(θ)Q(ϕ). Substitution isolates the azimuthal part first, so one sets Q d2Q dϕ2 =−m2, Q= e±imϕ .

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Single-valuedness under ϕ→ϕ+ 2π forces m to be an integer. The remaining angular and radial equations are d dθ sin θdP dθ + l(l+ 1)− m2 sin2 θ P = 0, d2U l(l+ 1) dr2− r2 U = 0. The radial equation is already telling: it is scale-invariant, so its solutions are pure powers, U= Arl+1 + Br−l . Therefore the potential itself has the characteristic spherical form Φ∼Arl + Br−(l+1) . This is one of the structural facts of electrostatics worth memorizing. In spherical geometry, regular solutions near the origin are positive powers of r, while decaying solutions outside sources are inverse powers. 1 sin θ 2 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 2. Legendre Equation and Legendre Polynomials For m= 0 it is convenient to set x= cos θ. Then the angular equation becomes d dx (1−x2)dP dx + l(l+ 1)P = 0, which is the ordinary Legendre equation.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Jackson develops the solution by power series and shows that finiteness at x= ±1 forces the series to terminate. That restriction is what quantizes l to a nonnegative integer and turns the regular solutions into the Legendre polynomials Pl(x). The first few are P0(x) = 1, P1(x) = x, P2(x) = 1 2(3x2 −1), P3(x) = 1 2(5x3 −3x), P4(x) = 1 8(35x4 −30x2 + 3). A compact representation is Rodrigues’ formula, Pl(x) = 1 2ll! dl dxl(x2 −1)l . The orthogonality relation is 1 Pl(x)Pl′ (x) dx= −1 2 2l+ 1 δll′ . This is the statement that matters for boundary-value problems. Once the potential on a spherical boundary is known as a function of θ, the coefficients in the interior or exterior solution are determined by projection onto the Pl basis. In other words, separation of variables by itself gives a family of solutions; orthogonality is what turns that family into a calculational tool.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Jackson also writes a Legendre-series expansion for a generic function, f(x) =∞ l=0 AlPl(x), Al = 2l+ 1 2 1 −1 f(x)Pl(x) dx. That formula is the spherical analogue of a Fourier series, and it is used repeatedly in the rest of the chapter. 3. Boundary-Value Problems with Azimuthal Symmetry If a problem has azimuthal symmetry, then m= 0 and the general solution simplifies to Φ(r,θ) =∞ l=0 Alrl + Blr−(l+1) Pl(cos θ). This is the natural expansion for all axisymmetric spherical problems. The geometry determines whether the Al or Bl branch survives. For example: 3 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 • inside a sphere containing no source at the origin, one keeps only rl; • outside a localized source region, one keeps only r−(l+1) if the potential must vanish at infinity. If the potential is specified on a sphere r= a as V(θ), then for the interior problem V(θ) =∞ AlalPl(cos θ), l=0 so that 2l+ 1 Al = 2al π V(θ)Pl(cos θ) sin θdθ.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: 0 The exterior coefficients are obtained in the same way, with al replaced by a−(l+1) . A point that Jackson emphasizes and that is easy to overlook is uniqueness of representation. Once the proper regularity conditions are imposed, the coefficients are not arbitrary ornaments. They encode the full physics, and each term has a recognizable multipole meaning. The l = 0 term is monopolar, l= 1 dipolar, l= 2 quadrupolar, and so on. The same section gives a very useful expansion of the Coulomb kernel: 1 |x−x′|= ∞ l=0 rl < rl+1 > Pl(cos γ), where r< and r> are the smaller and larger of r and r′, and γ is the angle between x and x′ . This identity is one of the true workhorses of the subject. It is the bridge between local spherical harmonics and the nonlocal Green kernel. 4. Behavior of Fields in a Conical Hole or Near a Sharp Point This section is one of the most physical in the chapter.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: One considers an axisymmetric region bounded by a cone θ= β held at fixed potential. The angular equation is no longer required to be regular on the whole interval−1 ≤x≤1; only the restricted range cos β ≤x≤1 matters. The result is that the allowed separation parameter need not be an integer. Jackson writes it as ν, and the regular separated solutions become Φ(r,θ)∼ArνPν(cos θ), with the eigenvalue condition Pν(cos β) = 0. This changes the near-origin behavior dramatically. The dominant field components scale as Er ∼rν−1, Eθ ∼rν−1 . Hence the geometry decides whether the field is suppressed or singular near the tip: 4 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 • for a narrow conical hole (β <π/2), one has ν >1, so the field goes to zero near the vertex; • for a sharp conducting point (β >π/2), one has 0 <ν <1, so the field diverges as r→0. This is a clean mathematical explanation of field enhancement near sharp tips.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: The singularity is not mysterious; it is the inevitable consequence of the lowest allowed exponent in the separated solution. Jackson also gives useful approximations for ν in extreme limits. For small β, 2. 405 1 ν ≈ − while for π−β ≪1, β 2, ν ≈ 2 ln 2 π−β −1 . The second form shows just how slowly ν tends to zero as the point becomes very sharp. 5. Associated Legendre Functions and the Spherical Harmonics Ylm(θ,ϕ) When azimuthal symmetry is absent, m̸= 0, and the relevant angular functions are the associated Legendre functions. For positive m, Pm l (x) = (−1)m(1−x2)m/2 dm Pl(x). dxm A more symmetric formula valid in either sign convention is obtained from Rodrigues’ representation. The key relation for negative m is P−m l (x) = (−1)m(l−m)! (l+ m)! Pm l (x). These functions are orthogonal in l for fixed m, 1 Pm l (x)Pm l′ (x) dx= −1 2 2l+ 1 (l+ m)! (l−m)! δll′ .

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Combining the θ- and ϕ-dependence leads to the normalized spherical harmonics, Ylm(θ,ϕ) = 2l+ 1 4π (l−m)! (l+ m)! Pm l (cos θ)eimϕ . They satisfy 2π 0 dϕ 0 π This is the natural orthonormal basis on the sphere. In practice, every well-behaved angular function 5 sin θdθY∗ l′m′ Ylm = δll′ δmm′ . Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 can be expanded as g(θ,ϕ) =∞ l=0 l AlmYlm(θ,ϕ), Alm = dΩ Y∗ lm(θ,ϕ)g(θ,ϕ). m=−l Once this basis is available, the fully general solution of Laplace’s equation in spherical coordinates can be written as Φ(r,θ,ϕ) =∞ l=0 l m=−l Almrl + Blmr−(l+1) Ylm(θ,ϕ). That formula is the complete spherical counterpart of the Fourier expansion of a two-dimensional potential. 6. Addition Theorem for Spherical Harmonics Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ), The addition theorem is one of the central identities in the chapter: Pl(cos γ) = 4π 2l+ 1 l m=−l where γ is the angle between the two directions (θ,ϕ) and (θ′,ϕ′).

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: This theorem deserves to be understood, not only remembered. The left-hand side depends only on the relative angle between two directions; the right-hand side rewrites that rotationally invariant quantity as a bilinear combination of basis functions defined with respect to a fixed coordinate system. It is the precise statement that spherical harmonics provide the irreducible angular content of the Coulomb kernel. A direct consequence is the fully factorized form of the free-space Green kernel: 1 |x−x′|= 4π ∞ l=0 1 2l+ 1 Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ). This identity is what turns many charge-density integrals into manageable radial integrals plus angular projections. l m=−l rl < rl+1 > 7. Laplace Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates; Bessel Functions In cylindrical coordinates (ρ,ϕ,z), ∂2Φ ∂ρ2 + 1 ∂Φ ∂ρ + 1 ∂2Φ ∂ϕ2 + ∂2Φ ∂z2 = 0. With the product ansatz ρ ρ2 Φ(ρ,ϕ,z) = R(ρ)Q(ϕ)Z(z), 6 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: 3 one finds d2Z dz2−k2Z = 0, d2R dρ2 + 1 ρ dR dρ + k2 d2Q dϕ2 + ν2Q= 0, ν2 − ρ2 R= 0. After setting x= kρ, the radial equation becomes Bessel’s equation, d2R dx2 + 1 x dR dx + 1− ν2 x2 R= 0. Its regular power-series solution is the Bessel function of the first kind, Jν(x) = x 2 ν ∞ j=0 (−1)j j! Γ(j+ ν+ 1) x 2 2j . The second linearly independent solution is the Neumann function Nν(x), and the outgoing/incoming wave combinations are the Hankel functions, H(1) ν (x) = Jν(x) + iNν(x), H(2) ν (x) = Jν(x)−iNν(x). ρ a dρ= a2 2 [Jν+1(xνn)]2δnn′ . The limiting forms matter constantly in practice: Jν(x)∼ 1 Γ(ν+ 1) x 2 ν , x≪1, Jν(x)∼ 2 πx cos x− νπ 2− π 4 , x≫1. Jackson then proves the orthogonality relation for Bessel functions whose arguments are scaled by the zeros xνn of Jν: a 0 ρJν xνn ρ a Jν xνn′ This leads to the Fourier–Bessel expansion f(ρ) =∞ n=1 AνnJν xνn ρ a , with coefficients determined by projection.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: So again the pattern is the same as before: separation gives the basis, orthogonality makes the basis useful. Jackson also notes the modified Bessel equation obtained when the separation constant has the opposite sign. The corresponding solutions are Iν(x) and Kν(x). These become indispensable whenever exponential growth or decay in the radial direction is the physically relevant behavior. 7 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 8. Boundary-Value Problems in Cylindrical Coordinates For a finite cylinder of radius a and height L, with the side and bottom grounded and the top held at Φ = V(ρ,ϕ), the separated solution takes the form Φ(ρ,ϕ,z) =∞ ∞ m=0 n=1 Jm(kmnρ) sinh(kmnz) (Amnsin mϕ+ Bmncos mϕ), where a xmn kmn = , Jm(xmn) = 0. The coefficients are fixed by expanding the boundary data V(ρ,ϕ) in a Fourier series in ϕ and a Fourier–Bessel series in ρ.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: What matters conceptually here is that cylindrical boundary conditions select zeros of Bessel functions in exactly the same way that rectangular boxes select integer multiples of π and spherical boundaries select Legendre coefficients. The eigenvalues are the geometric fingerprints of the coordinate system. Jackson also discusses the infinite half-space z ≥0, where sums over discrete radial modes are replaced by integrals over continuous k. In that limit one arrives naturally at Hankel transforms. The discrete Fourier–Bessel series turns into an integral representation, just as ordinary Fourier series turn into Fourier transforms on unbounded intervals. 9. Expansion of Green Functions in Spherical Coordinates The same basis that solves Laplace’s equation also expands the Green function satisfying the appropriate spherical boundary conditions. In free space one has 1 |x−x′|= 4π l,m 1 2l+ 1 rl < Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ).

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: rl+1 > For a spherical boundary at r= a, the Green function is obtained by adding a homogeneous term chosen so that the correct boundary condition is enforced. For the exterior Dirichlet problem, Jackson writes a series of the schematic form G(x,x′) = 4π l,m 1 2l+ 1 rl < rl+1 > −(image-like correction) Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ). The exact correction is determined by demanding G= 0 at the boundary. The important lesson is that the image method and the orthogonal-function method are not competing viewpoints here; they are two faces of the same Green function. Once the series for G is known, Poisson problems in spherical geometry reduce to radial integrals plus angular harmonic projections. This is one of the cleanest demonstrations in the book of why the Green-function formalism becomes especially transparent in separable coordinates. 8 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 10.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Solution of Potential Problems with Spherical Green Function Expansion After the Green function has been expanded, the next step is to use it rather than admire it. The potential follows from Jackson’s general representation, Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 V ρ(x′)G(x,x′) d3x ′+ boundary term. If the boundary data live naturally on a sphere, one expands both the source and the boundary function in spherical harmonics. The angular integrations become diagonal because of orthogonality. What remains is usually a radial integral for each (l,m) channel. This way of organizing the calculation is extremely efficient. Instead of solving one three-dimensional problem all at once, one solves an infinite family of one-dimensional radial problems, each labeled by angular momentum indices. The decomposition is exact, not approximate, and the truncation error is completely under control when the source has simple angular structure. 11.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Expansion of Green Functions in Cylindrical Coordinates In cylindrical geometries the same story repeats with a different basis. The free-space Green function can be written as a sum or integral over mand k, with angular dependence eim(ϕ−ϕ′), axial dependence e−k|z−z′|or its hyperbolic variants, and radial dependence expressed through Bessel or modified Bessel functions. In schematic form one encounters representations like G(x,x′)∼ ∞ m=−∞ ∞ 0 dkRm(k; ρ,ρ ′)eim(ϕ−ϕ′)e−k|z−z′| , where Rm is built from the appropriate cylindrical solutions and then modified to satisfy the boundary conditions. The conceptual point is the same as in the spherical case, but the bookkeeping is different: cylindrical symmetry does not quantize the axial variable on an unbounded interval, so integrals over k appear naturally. One gets a mixed discrete–continuous spectral representation. 12.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: Eigenfunction Expansions for Green Functions This section makes the underlying principle explicit. A Green function is not just a special trick cooked up from an image or guessed from experience. It can be built systematically from the eigenfunctions of the self-adjoint differential operator associated with the boundary-value problem. 9 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 If {un}are orthonormal eigenfunctions satisfying Lun = λnun, then the Green function is represented schematically as un(x)u∗ n(x′) G(x,x′) = λn , n with the obvious modifications when part of the spectrum is continuous. For electrostatics the operator is essentially the Laplacian together with the imposed boundary conditions. This eigenfunction viewpoint is the abstract version of everything Jackson has been doing concretely with Legendre, Bessel, and Fourier bases.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: It is useful because it shows what all those expansions have in common and why they must work. 13. Mixed Boundary Conditions, Conducting Plane with a Circular Hole The final section addresses a problem that is geometrically simple but methodologically richer: a conducting plane with a circular aperture. One does not have a pure Dirichlet or pure Neumann condition everywhere on the boundary. Instead, different pieces of the boundary obey different conditions. This is the natural setting for mixed boundary conditions. The main lesson is that mixed problems are still linear, but the basis functions and the matching conditions become subtler. One cannot impose a single convenient boundary condition globally on the whole surface. Instead one constructs the solution piece by piece, usually with an integral transform or a specially adapted Green function, and then matches the conditions across the different boundary segments.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: For the circular hole, cylindrical coordinates are unavoidable, and the result is naturally expressed in terms of Bessel-function integrals. Physically the solution describes the fringing of electric field lines near the rim of the aperture. Mathematically it is a reminder that separability alone is not the whole story: one also needs to know how the imposed data sit on the boundary. Closing synthesis The intellectual center of Chapter 3 is not any one special function. It is the idea that geometry chooses the basis. Spherical boundaries lead to Legendre functions and spherical harmonics; cylindrical boundaries lead to Bessel functions; Green functions inherit the same eigenfunction structure. Once that is understood, many formulas that first look unrelated fall into place.

chunks.title: chapter 3
chunks.text: The chapter is really teaching one habit of mind: never attack a boundary-value problem before asking which coordinate system makes the boundary look natural and which orthogonal family belongs to that coordinate system. 10 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 That is the point where electrostatics starts to feel less like a list of solved problems and more like a coherent mathematical method. 11
