chunks.text: Combining the θ- and ϕ-dependence leads to the normalized spherical harmonics, Ylm(θ,ϕ) = 2l+ 1 4π (l−m)! (l+ m)! Pm l (cos θ)eimϕ . They satisfy 2π 0 dϕ 0 π This is the natural orthonormal basis on the sphere. In practice, every well-behaved angular function 5 sin θdθY∗ l′m′ Ylm = δll′ δmm′ . Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 can be expanded as g(θ,ϕ) =∞ l=0 l AlmYlm(θ,ϕ), Alm = dΩ Y∗ lm(θ,ϕ)g(θ,ϕ). m=−l Once this basis is available, the fully general solution of Laplace’s equation in spherical coordinates can be written as Φ(r,θ,ϕ) =∞ l=0 l m=−l Almrl + Blmr−(l+1) Ylm(θ,ϕ). That formula is the complete spherical counterpart of the Fourier expansion of a two-dimensional potential. 6. Addition Theorem for Spherical Harmonics Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ), The addition theorem is one of the central identities in the chapter: Pl(cos γ) = 4π 2l+ 1 l m=−l where γ is the angle between the two directions (θ,ϕ) and (θ′,ϕ′).

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: This theorem deserves to be understood, not only remembered. The left-hand side depends only on the relative angle between two directions; the right-hand side rewrites that rotationally invariant quantity as a bilinear combination of basis functions defined with respect to a fixed coordinate system. It is the precise statement that spherical harmonics provide the irreducible angular content of the Coulomb kernel. A direct consequence is the fully factorized form of the free-space Green kernel: 1 |x−x′|= 4π ∞ l=0 1 2l+ 1 Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ). This identity is what turns many charge-density integrals into manageable radial integrals plus angular projections. l m=−l rl < rl+1 > 7. Laplace Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates; Bessel Functions In cylindrical coordinates (ρ,ϕ,z), ∂2Φ ∂ρ2 + 1 ∂Φ ∂ρ + 1 ∂2Φ ∂ϕ2 + ∂2Φ ∂z2 = 0. With the product ansatz ρ ρ2 Φ(ρ,ϕ,z) = R(ρ)Q(ϕ)Z(z), 6 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: 3 one finds d2Z dz2−k2Z = 0, d2R dρ2 + 1 ρ dR dρ + k2 d2Q dϕ2 + ν2Q= 0, ν2 − ρ2 R= 0. After setting x= kρ, the radial equation becomes Bessel’s equation, d2R dx2 + 1 x dR dx + 1− ν2 x2 R= 0. Its regular power-series solution is the Bessel function of the first kind, Jν(x) = x 2 ν ∞ j=0 (−1)j j! Γ(j+ ν+ 1) x 2 2j . The second linearly independent solution is the Neumann function Nν(x), and the outgoing/incoming wave combinations are the Hankel functions, H(1) ν (x) = Jν(x) + iNν(x), H(2) ν (x) = Jν(x)−iNν(x). ρ a dρ= a2 2 [Jν+1(xνn)]2δnn′ . The limiting forms matter constantly in practice: Jν(x)∼ 1 Γ(ν+ 1) x 2 ν , x≪1, Jν(x)∼ 2 πx cos x− νπ 2− π 4 , x≫1. Jackson then proves the orthogonality relation for Bessel functions whose arguments are scaled by the zeros xνn of Jν: a 0 ρJν xνn ρ a Jν xνn′ This leads to the Fourier–Bessel expansion f(ρ) =∞ n=1 AνnJν xνn ρ a , with coefficients determined by projection.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: So again the pattern is the same as before: separation gives the basis, orthogonality makes the basis useful. Jackson also notes the modified Bessel equation obtained when the separation constant has the opposite sign. The corresponding solutions are Iν(x) and Kν(x). These become indispensable whenever exponential growth or decay in the radial direction is the physically relevant behavior. 7 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 8. Boundary-Value Problems in Cylindrical Coordinates For a finite cylinder of radius a and height L, with the side and bottom grounded and the top held at Φ = V(ρ,ϕ), the separated solution takes the form Φ(ρ,ϕ,z) =∞ ∞ m=0 n=1 Jm(kmnρ) sinh(kmnz) (Amnsin mϕ+ Bmncos mϕ), where a xmn kmn = , Jm(xmn) = 0. The coefficients are fixed by expanding the boundary data V(ρ,ϕ) in a Fourier series in ϕ and a Fourier–Bessel series in ρ.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: What matters conceptually here is that cylindrical boundary conditions select zeros of Bessel functions in exactly the same way that rectangular boxes select integer multiples of π and spherical boundaries select Legendre coefficients. The eigenvalues are the geometric fingerprints of the coordinate system. Jackson also discusses the infinite half-space z ≥0, where sums over discrete radial modes are replaced by integrals over continuous k. In that limit one arrives naturally at Hankel transforms. The discrete Fourier–Bessel series turns into an integral representation, just as ordinary Fourier series turn into Fourier transforms on unbounded intervals. 9. Expansion of Green Functions in Spherical Coordinates The same basis that solves Laplace’s equation also expands the Green function satisfying the appropriate spherical boundary conditions. In free space one has 1 |x−x′|= 4π l,m 1 2l+ 1 rl < Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ).

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: rl+1 > For a spherical boundary at r= a, the Green function is obtained by adding a homogeneous term chosen so that the correct boundary condition is enforced. For the exterior Dirichlet problem, Jackson writes a series of the schematic form G(x,x′) = 4π l,m 1 2l+ 1 rl < rl+1 > −(image-like correction) Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′)Ylm(θ,ϕ). The exact correction is determined by demanding G= 0 at the boundary. The important lesson is that the image method and the orthogonal-function method are not competing viewpoints here; they are two faces of the same Green function. Once the series for G is known, Poisson problems in spherical geometry reduce to radial integrals plus angular harmonic projections. This is one of the cleanest demonstrations in the book of why the Green-function formalism becomes especially transparent in separable coordinates. 8 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 10.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: Solution of Potential Problems with Spherical Green Function Expansion After the Green function has been expanded, the next step is to use it rather than admire it. The potential follows from Jackson’s general representation, Φ(x) = 1 4πε0 V ρ(x′)G(x,x′) d3x ′+ boundary term. If the boundary data live naturally on a sphere, one expands both the source and the boundary function in spherical harmonics. The angular integrations become diagonal because of orthogonality. What remains is usually a radial integral for each (l,m) channel. This way of organizing the calculation is extremely efficient. Instead of solving one three-dimensional problem all at once, one solves an infinite family of one-dimensional radial problems, each labeled by angular momentum indices. The decomposition is exact, not approximate, and the truncation error is completely under control when the source has simple angular structure. 11.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: Expansion of Green Functions in Cylindrical Coordinates In cylindrical geometries the same story repeats with a different basis. The free-space Green function can be written as a sum or integral over mand k, with angular dependence eim(ϕ−ϕ′), axial dependence e−k|z−z′|or its hyperbolic variants, and radial dependence expressed through Bessel or modified Bessel functions. In schematic form one encounters representations like G(x,x′)∼ ∞ m=−∞ ∞ 0 dkRm(k; ρ,ρ ′)eim(ϕ−ϕ′)e−k|z−z′| , where Rm is built from the appropriate cylindrical solutions and then modified to satisfy the boundary conditions. The conceptual point is the same as in the spherical case, but the bookkeeping is different: cylindrical symmetry does not quantize the axial variable on an unbounded interval, so integrals over k appear naturally. One gets a mixed discrete–continuous spectral representation. 12.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: Eigenfunction Expansions for Green Functions This section makes the underlying principle explicit. A Green function is not just a special trick cooked up from an image or guessed from experience. It can be built systematically from the eigenfunctions of the self-adjoint differential operator associated with the boundary-value problem. 9 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 If {un}are orthonormal eigenfunctions satisfying Lun = λnun, then the Green function is represented schematically as un(x)u∗ n(x′) G(x,x′) = λn , n with the obvious modifications when part of the spectrum is continuous. For electrostatics the operator is essentially the Laplacian together with the imposed boundary conditions. This eigenfunction viewpoint is the abstract version of everything Jackson has been doing concretely with Legendre, Bessel, and Fourier bases.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: It is useful because it shows what all those expansions have in common and why they must work. 13. Mixed Boundary Conditions, Conducting Plane with a Circular Hole The final section addresses a problem that is geometrically simple but methodologically richer: a conducting plane with a circular aperture. One does not have a pure Dirichlet or pure Neumann condition everywhere on the boundary. Instead, different pieces of the boundary obey different conditions. This is the natural setting for mixed boundary conditions. The main lesson is that mixed problems are still linear, but the basis functions and the matching conditions become subtler. One cannot impose a single convenient boundary condition globally on the whole surface. Instead one constructs the solution piece by piece, usually with an integral transform or a specially adapted Green function, and then matches the conditions across the different boundary segments.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: For the circular hole, cylindrical coordinates are unavoidable, and the result is naturally expressed in terms of Bessel-function integrals. Physically the solution describes the fringing of electric field lines near the rim of the aperture. Mathematically it is a reminder that separability alone is not the whole story: one also needs to know how the imposed data sit on the boundary. Closing synthesis The intellectual center of Chapter 3 is not any one special function. It is the idea that geometry chooses the basis. Spherical boundaries lead to Legendre functions and spherical harmonics; cylindrical boundaries lead to Bessel functions; Green functions inherit the same eigenfunction structure. Once that is understood, many formulas that first look unrelated fall into place.

chunks.title: chapter 3 notes
chunks.text: The chapter is really teaching one habit of mind: never attack a boundary-value problem before asking which coordinate system makes the boundary look natural and which orthogonal family belongs to that coordinate system. 10 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 3 That is the point where electrostatics starts to feel less like a list of solved problems and more like a coherent mathematical method. 11

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: Multipoles and Dielectrics Chapter 4 Multipoles, Electrostatics of Macroscopic Media, Dielectrics Diego Juarez. These notes follow Jackson’s notation and organization: localized charge density ρ(x′), electric dipole moment p, traceless quadrupole tensor Qij , polarization P, electric displacement D, susceptibility χe, and permittivity ϵ. I kept the discussion close to the text, but tried to make the logical connections more explicit and the physical meaning a bit easier to see while still keeping the graduate-level flavor. Prepared from the material spanning pp. 145–169 of Jackson, Chapter 4. Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: 4 Contents 1 Multipole Expansion 2 2 Multipole Expansion of the Energy of a Charge Distribution in an External Field 4 3 Elementary Treatment of Electrostatics with Ponderable Media 4 4 Boundary-Value Problems with Dielectrics 6 5 Molecular Polarizability and Electric Susceptibility 7 6 Models for the Molecular Polarizability 8 7 Electrostatic Energy in Dielectric Media 9 1 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 What this chapter is really doing Chapter 4 has two threads that at first look separate but are actually tightly connected. The first is the multipole expansion of localized charge distributions, which tells us how a finite source looks when observed from far away. The second is the macroscopic description of dielectrics, where many microscopic dipoles are averaged into continuum fields such as P and D.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: The bridge between the two viewpoints is the dipole moment: it is the first genuinely structural correction beyond the total charge, and in matter it becomes the natural macroscopic density of polarization. 1. Multipole Expansion Consider a localized charge density ρ(x′) that is nonzero only inside a finite region. For observation points outside that region, Jackson expands the potential in spherical harmonics as Φ(x) = 1 4πϵ0 ∞ l=0 l m=−l 4π 2l+ 1 qlm Ylm(θ,ϕ) rl+1. The coefficients are q √4π , qlm = Y∗ lm(θ′,ϕ′) r ′lρ(x′) d3x ′ . These are the spherical multipole moments. What the expansion says is simple: once one is far enough away, the detailed charge distribution no longer matters point by point. Instead, the source is encoded in a hierarchy of moments, each with a characteristic angular dependence and a characteristic falloff in r. The first few moments make the physical meaning transparent.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: The l= 0 term is the monopole, q00 = 1 √4π ρ(x′) d3x ′ = so the monopole coefficient is just the total charge. The l= 1 terms are the dipole moments and are linear combinations of the Cartesian dipole vector p= x′ ρ(x′) d3x ′ . The l= 2 terms are the quadrupole moments and can be expressed in terms of the traceless tensor Qij= (3x ′ ′ ix j−r ′2δij)ρ(x′) d3x ′ . Jackson’s Cartesian form of the expansion is Φ(x) = 1 4πϵ0  q r + p·x r3 + 1 2 i,j Qij xixj r5 +··· . 2 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 This makes the hierarchy especially clear: the monopole falls as 1/r, the dipole as 1/r2 in the potential, the quadrupole as 1/r3, and so on. A nice point Jackson emphasizes is that the spherical and Cartesian multipoles are not identical objects arranged in different notation; they reflect different transformation properties.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: The Cartesian moments for fixed l are generally more numerous than the 2l+ 1 spherical coefficients, and the spherical moments correspond to irreducible pieces under rotations. For l = 2, using a traceless quadrupole tensor is exactly what removes the redundant trace part and isolates the irreducible quadrupole content. The electric field of a definite multipole term follows by E= −∇Φ. In spherical coordinates Jackson writes the components explicitly; for a dipole p aligned with the z axis one recovers the standard form 2pcos θ 4πϵ0r3 , Eθ = psin θ 4πϵ0r3 , Eϕ = 0. Er = In vector form, for a point dipole at x0, E(x) = 3n(p·n)−p 4πϵ0|x−x0|3 , where n points from x0 to x. One subtle but important feature is origin dependence. A general charge distribution changes its higher multipole moments when the origin is shifted. Only the lowest nonvanishing moment is origin-independent.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: Thus, if the total charge is nonzero, the monopole is independent of origin but the dipole is not. If the total charge vanishes, then the dipole becomes origin-independent. This is why the dipole moment of a neutral system is especially meaningful. Jackson then includes a very useful theorem about the volume average of the electric field over a sphere. If a sphere of radius R contains all the charge, then E(x) d3x=− p r<R 3ϵ0. If instead all the charge lies outside the sphere, then the average field over the spherical volume equals the field at the center: E(x) d3x= r<R 4π 3 R3E(0). This result later becomes the key step in the Lorentz local-field argument for dielectrics. It is one of those places where what first looks like a technical detour turns out to be foundational for the rest of the chapter.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: Finally, to make the dipole field consistent with the spherical average relation, Jackson writes the point-dipole field as E(x) = 1 4πϵ0 3n(p·n)−p 4π |x−x0|3− 3 p δ(x−x0). The delta-function term does not affect the field away from the dipole, but it fixes the correct integral properties at the source point. This is one of the earliest places where Jackson insists that singular idealizations must be handled distributionally if one wants the global statements to remain 3 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 correct. 2. Multipole Expansion of the Energy of a Charge Distribution in an External Field ∂2Φ If a localized charge distribution is placed in an external potential Φ(x), the electrostatic energy is W= ρ(x)Φ(x) d3x. Assuming the external potential varies slowly across the size of the source, one expands it about a chosen origin: Φ(x) = Φ(0) + x ·∇Φ(0) + 1 2 i,j xixj (0) +··· ∂xi∂xj .

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: Since E= −∇Φ and the external field is source-free in the region of the distribution, Jackson rewrites the expansion so that the traceless quadrupole tensor appears naturally. The resulting energy expansion is 1 W= qΦ(0)−p·E(0)− 6 i,j Qij ∂Ej ∂xi (0) +··· . This formula is conceptually beautiful because it shows how the external field probes successively finer structure in the source: the monopole couples to the potential itself, the dipole to the field, the quadrupole to the field gradient, and higher moments to higher spatial derivatives. Jackson briefly remarks on nuclear physics here. The quadrupole moment of a nucleus measures departures from spherical symmetry, and because nuclear states can sit in electric field gradients, quadrupole couplings split otherwise degenerate levels. That remark is short in the text, but it helps make the abstract tensor formula feel concrete.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: A second classic result follows immediately from the dipole term: the interaction energy of two point dipoles p1 and p2 separated by x1−x2 is p1·p2−3(n·p1)(n·p2) W12 = 4πϵ0|x1−x2|3 , with n the unit vector along the line joining them. The sign depends strongly on orientation. Two parallel dipoles arranged head-to-tail attract; side-by-side they repel. So even before the chapter turns to media, one already sees how dipolar structure naturally produces anisotropic interactions. 3. Elementary Treatment of Electrostatics with Ponderable Media Up to this point, electrostatics was discussed mostly for charges and conductors. In matter one must distinguish microscopic fields from macroscopic averaged fields. Jackson keeps the discussion intentionally elementary here, but the key macroscopic ideas already appear. 4 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 First, averaging does not spoil the electrostatic condition ∇×E = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: So the macroscopic electric field still derives from a scalar potential. The genuinely new ingredient is polarization. Under an applied field, molecules distort or reorient, producing microscopic dipole moments. The dipole moment per unit volume is the polarization, P(x) = Ni⟨pi⟩. i If there is also free or excess charge, the macroscopic charge density is ρ(x) = Ni⟨ei⟩+ ρexcess. i Usually the average molecular charge vanishes, so ρ denotes the free charge. Jackson then constructs the potential of a polarized medium by treating each small volume element as carrying both charge density and dipole density. The exact macroscopic potential is Φ(x) = 1 4πϵ0 d3x ′ ρ(x′) |x−x′|+ P(x′) ·∇′ 1 |x−x′|. After integrating by parts, Φ(x) = 1 4πϵ0 Thus the effective source density is not just ρ but ρeff = ρ−∇·P. This naturally leads to the macroscopic Gauss law 1 ∇·E= (ρ−∇·P). ρ(x′) −∇′ ·P(x′) |x−x′| d3x ′ .

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: ϵ0 Introducing the electric displacement, D= ϵ0E + P, one gets the standard macroscopic equation ∇·D= ρ. So D is designed precisely so that only free charge appears on the right-hand side. For a linear, isotropic medium Jackson assumes P= ϵ0χeE, 5 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 where χe is the electric susceptibility. Then D= ϵE, ϵ= ϵ0(1 + χe). The ratio ϵ/ϵ0 = 1 + χe is the dielectric constant or relative permittivity. In a uniform medium, the field equation becomes ∇·E= ρ ϵ . So within a homogeneous dielectric all the vacuum formulas reappear after replacing ϵ0 by ϵ. The real new difficulty comes at interfaces. Jackson states the boundary conditions in the now- familiar form (D2−D1)·n21 = σ, (E2−E1) ×n21 = 0, where σ is the free surface charge. These are exactly the macroscopic boundary conditions one expects from Maxwell’s equations, but here they are deployed in electrostatic problems with matter. 4.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: Boundary-Value Problems with Dielectrics Jackson then works through two representative problems. The first is a point charge q in a dielectric ϵ1 at distance d from a planar interface with a second dielectric ϵ2. Using a method of images adapted to dielectrics, he writes for the region containing the charge Φ = 1 4πϵ1 q + q′ R2 , z>0, R1 and for the other region q′′ Φ = 1 4πϵ2 , z<0. R1 Enforcing the continuity of tangential E and normal D at the interface gives ′ q =− ϵ2−ϵ1 ϵ2 + ϵ1 ′′ q, q = 2ϵ2 ϵ2 + ϵ1 q. This is a very clean generalization of the conducting-plane image method. In the limit ϵ2 ≫ϵ1, the second medium behaves almost like a conductor and the image charge approaches the familiar conducting result. Jackson then interprets the interface physically in terms of polarization charge. Since the suscepti- bility jumps across the interface, there is a surface polarization charge σpol=−(P2−P1)·n21.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: For this geometry he obtains an explicit surface charge density proportional to d σpol ∝ (ρ2 + d2)3/2. 6 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 So the image construction is not just a mathematical trick; it is representing the actual induced polarization of the second medium. The second example is a dielectric sphere of radius a and permittivity ϵ placed in a uniform external field E0ˆ z. Because the problem is axially symmetric, the potential is expanded in Legendre polynomials inside and outside. Matching tangential E and normal D at r= aleaves only the l= 1 term. Jackson finds Φin =− 3 ϵ/ϵ0 + 2 Φout =−E0rcos θ+ ϵ/ϵ0−1 ϵ/ϵ0 + 2 E0rcos θ, E0 a3 r2 cos θ. Thus the internal field is uniform, 3 Ein = E0, ϵ/ϵ0 + 2 and the outside field is the applied field plus that of a dipole at the origin with moment p = 4πϵ0 ϵ/ϵ0−1 ϵ/ϵ0 + 2 a3E0ˆ z.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: The sphere therefore behaves like a polarizable object whose induced dipole is proportional to the applied field. Jackson immediately uses the same algebra to discuss a spherical cavity in a dielectric. Replacing ϵ/ϵ0 →ϵ0/ϵ gives a field inside the cavity 3ϵ Ein = E0, 2ϵ+ ϵ0 which is larger than E0 if ϵ > ϵ0. That contrast between the dielectric sphere and the cavity is physically instructive: matter inserted into a field tends to reduce the field inside itself, while a cavity carved inside a polarized medium experiences an enhanced local field. 5. Molecular Polarizability and Electric Susceptibility Now the chapter turns microscopic again. The constitutive law P= ϵ0χeE is macroscopic, but what determines χe? Jackson addresses this by introducing the molecular polarizability γmol. A key distinction is between the macroscopic field E and the actual field acting on a given molecule.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: The local field is written as Ei = Enear−EP, where the second term is the contribution of the nearby molecules replaced by a smooth polarization continuum. Using the spherical-average result from Section 4. 1, Jackson finds for a small spherical cavity in a uniformly polarized medium P EP =− 3ϵ0. 7 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 Hence 1 Ei = P + Enear. 3ϵ0 Lorentz showed that for a simple cubic lattice the near-field term vanishes, and Jackson treats Enear ≈0 as a good working approximation for many materials. Then the local field is Ei ≈E + P 3ϵ0. If the average molecular dipole is proportional to the local field, ⟨pmol⟩= ϵ0γmol(E + Ei), and since one arrives at Solving for χe yields P= N⟨pmol⟩, P= Nγmol ϵ0E + Nγmol χe = 1 3 P. 1− 1 3 Nγmol. Equivalently, 3 γmol = N ϵ/ϵ0−1 ϵ/ϵ0 + 2. This is the Clausius–Mossotti relation.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: It provides the bridge between the microscopic parameter γmol and the macroscopic dielectric constant. Jackson remarks that it works best for dilute systems such as gases and only approximately for dense liquids and solids. 6. Models for the Molecular Polarizability Jackson then sketches two classical models. The first is induced polarization. A charge e harmonically bound with natural frequency ω0 obeys F=−mω2 0 x, mω2 0 x= eE. Hence the induced dipole moment is e2 pmol = ex= E. Thus the molecular polarizability is γmol = mω2 0 e2 , mω2 0 ϵ0 8 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 and for many charges in a molecule, e2 j 1 γmol = ϵ0 j mjω2 j . This explains why molecular polarizability has the dimensions of volume and why its magnitude is usually comparable to molecular size. Jackson also checks the role of thermal motion.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: For a harmonically bound charge in a classical Boltzmann distribution, the average dipole moment still comes out exactly e2 ⟨pmol⟩= E, mω2 0 so the induced polarizability is independent of temperature within this model. The second mechanism is orientation polarization. Molecules with permanent dipole moment p0 tend to align with an applied field but are randomized by thermal agitation. Using the Boltzmann factor with Hamiltonian H= H0−p0·E, Jackson derives, for weak fields, ⟨pmol⟩≈1 3 p2 0 kTE. So orientation polarizability varies as 1/T. Combining both mechanisms, γmol ≈γi + 1 3ϵ0 p2 0 kT. This is the basic reason why polar substances show a temperature-dependent molecular polarizability while nonpolar substances do not. Jackson’s Figure 4. 10 illustrates exactly that: one obtains approximately a straight line when plotting γmol against 1/T for polar molecules. 7.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: Electrostatic Energy in Dielectric Media The last section revisits energy, now in matter. The vacuum formula W= 1 2 ρ(x)Φ(x) d3x cannot simply be taken over unchanged because work is required not only to assemble free charge but also to polarize the medium itself. Jackson starts from the differential work associated with a small change in free charge density, δW= δρ(x)Φ(x) d3x. 9 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 Since ∇·D= ρ, one has δρ= ∇·(δD), and after integration by parts, δW= E·δD d3x. Therefore the total electrostatic energy can be written formally as W= d3x D 0 E·δD. If the medium is linear, then 1 E·δD= δ(E·D), 2 so 1 W= 2 E·D d3x. Thus the familiar quadratic energy density survives only in linear media. Jackson is careful here: for nonlinear media the energy depends on the path in constitutive space, and one must use the integral form rather than the simple quadratic expression.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: He then considers the change in energy when a linear dielectric body is inserted into a field whose sources are fixed. Let E0,D0 be the field before insertion and E,D after. After a neat integration-by-parts argument using the fact that the free source charge is unchanged, Jackson finds W= 1 2 (E·D0−D·E0) d3x. Restricting to the volume V1 of the dielectric body and using D= ϵE there, this becomes 1 W=− 2 V1 (ϵ1−ϵ0)E·E0 d3x. If the surrounding medium is free space, Jackson rewrites it with the polarization as 1 W=− 2 V1 P·E0 d3x. So the energy density of a polarizable dielectric in an external field with fixed sources is 1 w=− P·E0. 2 The factor 1/2 is crucial. A permanent dipole in a fixed field has energy−p·E, but an induced dipole builds up gradually with the applied field, so the work accumulates with the extra factor of one half. Jackson then turns to forces.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: If the free charges are fixed, a dielectric with ϵ1 >ϵ0 is drawn into regions of stronger field. The force on a generalized displacement ξ is Fξ=− ∂W ∂ξ Q . 10 Graduate Notes on Electrostatics Jackson, Ch. 4 But if instead the electrodes are held at fixed potential by a battery, external work is supplied as the dielectric moves and charge flows. Repeating the bookkeeping leads to the opposite sign, Fξ = + ∂W ∂ξ V . Jackson summarizes the relation symbolically as δWV =−δWQ. This distinction between fixed-charge and fixed-potential conditions is one of the most important conceptual points in practical electrostatics. The same physical motion can be described by two different energy functions, depending on what the external circuit is holding fixed. Closing synthesis This chapter begins with the far-field expansion of a localized source and ends with the macroscopic energy of polarizable bodies.

chunks.title: chapter 4
chunks.text: The common thread is that dipoles sit at the center of both stories. In the first half, the dipole is the first structural correction beyond total charge and often the first origin-independent moment of a neutral system. In the second half, polarization is just dipole moment density, and the constitutive law of a dielectric is therefore a continuum version of the dipole response of molecules. The chapter also quietly assembles several of the standard formulas of electromagnetism into one chain: the spherical average of a dipole field, the Lorentz local field, the Clausius–Mossotti relation, the dielectric sphere in a uniform field, and the energy formulas for dielectric bodies. Once those are all in place, the later theory of macroscopic electrodynamics feels much less mysterious. 11

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Magnetostatics, Faraday’s Law, Quasi-Static Fields Chapter 5 Magnetostatics, Faraday’s Law, Quasi-Static Fields Diego Juarez. These notes follow Jackson’s SI notation. The magnetic-flux density is B, the magnetic field in matter is H, the vector potential is A, the current density is J, the magnetization is M, and the permeability of free space is µ0. The aim here is not to paraphrase the book mechanically, but to write the chapter in a way that makes the structure of the subject visible while keeping Jackson’s notation and logic. Prepared from the uploaded Chapter 5 material. Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: 5 Contents 1 Introduction and Definitions 2 2 Biot–Savart Law 3 3 Differential Equations of Magnetostatics and Ampère’s Law 3 4 Vector Potential 4 5 Circular Current Loop 5 6 Localized Current Distribution and Magnetic Moment 5 7 Force, Torque, and Energy of a Localized Current Distribution 6 8 Macroscopic Equations; B and H 7 9 Methods of Magnetostatic Boundary-Value Problems 8 10 Uniformly Magnetized Sphere 9 11 Faraday’s Law 9 12 Inductance and Flux Linkage 10 13 Magnetic Energy 10 14 Quasi-Static Fields 11 1 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 Global framework of Chapter 5 Electrostatics began from charges as sources of a conservative field. Magnetostatics begins from a different fact: there are no free magnetic charges. The sources are currents, and the field equations are correspondingly rotational rather than purely potential in character.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: The first half of the chapter develops the steady magnetic field of prescribed current distributions; the second half introduces macroscopic media, Faraday induction, and the quasi-static limit in which time dependence is slow enough that full wave propagation is not yet the central issue. 1. Introduction and Definitions The first conceptual break with electrostatics is immediate. In electrostatics one writes ρ ∇·E= , ε0 and this already announces what the sources are. In magnetostatics the corresponding fundamental statement is not a source equation but the absence of isolated magnetic charge, ∇·B = 0. Hence magnetic field lines do not begin or end; they form closed curves or extend to infinity in such a way that the net flux through any closed surface vanishes. Jackson introduces B operationally by the torque on a magnetic dipole µ, N= µ ×B.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: This is the magnetic analogue of defining E by force on a test charge, except that here the basic probe is not a monopole but a dipole. Historically this is not a cosmetic difference. Magnetism was first encountered through compasses and permanent magnets, so the dipole entered before the field equations were fully understood. A current density J(x) is the flow of charge per unit area per unit time. Charge conservation gives the continuity equation ∂ρ ∂t + ∇·J = 0. For steady currents, the charge density is time independent and therefore ∇·J = 0. This divergence-free property is one of the basic structural facts of magnetostatics. It is what allows the field equations to simplify so beautifully later. 2 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 2. Biot–Savart Law The experimental relation between steady currents and magnetic induction is the Biot–Savart law.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: For a filamentary current element Idl and an observation point displaced by x from the element, dB= µ0 4π Idl ×x |x|3. The 1/r2 character survives from Coulomb’s law, but the vector structure is entirely different: the source element contributes perpendicular both to the current direction and to the line joining source and field point. This already tells us that magnetic fields naturally circulate around currents. For an infinitely long straight wire carrying current I, direct integration gives |B|= µ0I 2πR, where R is the perpendicular distance to the wire. The field lines are circles concentric with the wire. This is one of the central pictures of magnetostatics and will later reappear in Ampère’s law as the simplest symmetry argument. The force law on a current element in an external field is dF= Idl ×B. Hence for a continuous current distribution, F= J(x) ×B(x) d3x, N= x ×[J(x) ×B(x)] d3x.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: These formulas are more than appendices to the field law. They show that B is the field quantity whose physical content is fixed by magnetic forces and torques. 3. Differential Equations of Magnetostatics and Ampère’s Law The integral Biot–Savart expression for a general current density is B(x) = µ0 4π J(x′) × x−x′ |x−x′|3 d3x ′ . Using x−x′ |x−x′|3 one may rewrite it as B(x) = µ0 4π = −∇ 1 |x−x′|, ∇× J(x′) |x−x′|d3x ′ . 3 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 From this form the divergence equation follows immediately: ∇·B = 0. To obtain the curl, one uses the vector identity ∇×(∇×A) = ∇(∇·A) −∇2A together with the Green-function relation ∇2 1 |x−x′|=−4πδ(x−x′). After an integration by parts, the term involving ∇·J vanishes in the steady state, yielding ∇×B= µ0J. Thus the basic differential equations of magnetostatics are ∇×B= µ0J, ∇·B = 0.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: They are the magnetic counterparts of the electrostatic equations ∇×E = 0 and ∇·E= ρ/ε0, but the roles of divergence and curl have been interchanged. Integrating ∇×B= µ0J over an open surface and using Stokes’ theorem gives the integral form, B·dl= µ0Irmenc, C Ampère’s law. In highly symmetric situations, this is the magnetic analogue of Gauss’s law. 4. Vector Potential Because ∇·B = 0, one may introduce a vector potential A such that B= ∇×A. This is not a trick but a consequence of the topology of divergence-free fields in simply connected regions. Substituting into Ampère’s law gives ∇×(∇×A) = µ0J, or ∇(∇·A) −∇2A= µ0J. The freedom A →A + ∇Ψ leaves B unchanged and is called a gauge transformation. Jackson chooses the Coulomb gauge, ∇·A = 0, 4 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 for which each Cartesian component satisfies a Poisson equation, ∇2A=−µ0J.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: The solution in unbounded space is therefore A(x) = µ0 4π J(x′) |x−x′|d3x ′ . This is the magnetic counterpart of the electrostatic scalar-potential integral. The difference is not merely scalar versus vector. In electrostatics the potential is already the directly useful quantity. In magnetostatics the vector potential is partly auxiliary, but it becomes indispensable once induction and flux are discussed. 5. Circular Current Loop A circular loop is the natural testing ground for the formalism because it is simple enough to solve but already rich enough to show the geometry of magnetic multipoles. For a loop of radius a in the x–y plane carrying current I, symmetry implies that only the azimuthal component of A survives: A= Aϕ(r,θ) ˆ ϕ. The exact result can be expressed in terms of complete elliptic integrals. Jackson then develops a spherical-harmonic expansion to expose the structure more transparently.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Far from the loop, Br = Iπa2 cos θ r3 , Bθ = µ0 4π Iπa2 sin θ r3. This is precisely the field of a dipole. It is therefore natural to define the magnetic moment of a planar loop by m = I(area) ˆ n, µ0 2π so that for a circle, m= Iπa2 . This result is basic enough that one should remember it geometrically rather than only algebraically: magnetic moment equals current times oriented area. 6. Localized Current Distribution and Magnetic Moment For a localized steady current, expand the vector potential for |x|≫|x′|: 1 |x−x′|= 1 |x|+ x·x′ |x|3 +··· . 5 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 The monopole term vanishes because the current is localized and divergenceless. The first nonvan- ishing contribution is dipolar, and Jackson shows that it can be written in terms of the magnetic moment Then m = 1 2 x′ ×J(x′) d3x ′ . A(x) = µ0 m ×x 4π |x|3 , and the magnetic induction is x r .

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: B(x) = µ0 4π 3n(n·m)−m r3 , n = So the asymptotic magnetic field of any localized steady current distribution is dipolar. For a closed plane circuit one can rewrite the moment as I 2 x ×dl, which reduces to current times area. If the current arises from orbiting charges with a common charge-to-mass ratio, then q L. m = m = 2M This classical relation between angular momentum and magnetic moment is the ancestor of the quantum-mechanical story, even though it is not the full story for intrinsic spin. 7. Force, Torque, and Energy of a Localized Current Distribution In a slowly varying external field one expands B around a convenient origin: Bk(x) = Bk(0) + x ·∇Bk(0) +··· . The leading nonvanishing term in the force is then F= ∇(m·B), using ∇·B = 0. The leading torque is N= m ×B. Hence the potential energy of a magnetic dipole in an external field is U=−m·B.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: This has exactly the same algebraic form as the electric dipole energy−·E, but one should not overdo the analogy. The magnetic dipole is produced by a current that must be maintained. The quantity above is the interaction energy relevant to the mechanical orientation of the dipole, not a 6 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 complete accounting of field energy for arbitrary time-dependent processes. Jackson also emphasizes a subtle point of singular structure. To make the spherical volume average of the dipole field come out correctly, the point-dipole field must contain a delta-function term: B(x) = µ0 4π 3n(n·m)−m r3 + 8π m δ(x). This term matters in hyperfine structure and contact interactions. It is not optional bookkeeping. 3 8. Macroscopic Equations; B and H In matter, microscopic currents from orbital motion and intrinsic magnetic moments fluctuate on atomic scales.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Averaging over a macroscopically small but microscopically large region introduces the magnetization M(x), the magnetic dipole moment per unit volume. Its contribution to the vector potential is analogous to the polarization term in electrostatics. Jackson shows that magnetization contributes an effective current density JM = ∇×M. Thus the macroscopic Ampèrian equation becomes ∇×B= µ0(J + ∇×M). This motivates the definition of the macroscopic field 1 H= B−M, µ0 for which the equations become ∇×H= J, ∇·B = 0. These are directly analogous to the electrostatic macroscopic equations ∇·= ρ, ∇×E = 0, with H playing the role of in the sense of a source-counting auxiliary field. In linear isotropic media, B= µH. Here µis the permeability. For diamagnets and paramagnets this relation is usually linear with µonly slightly different from µ0.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: For ferromagnets the constitutive law is nonlinear and history-dependent, B= F(H), and hysteresis becomes essential. 7 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 Boundary conditions Across an interface, the normal component of B is continuous and the tangential component of H jumps by any surface current density K: (B2−B1)·n = 0, For linear media without surface current this becomes B2n = B1n, = When µ1 ≫µ2, field lines of H meet the high-permeability material almost normally. This is the magnetic analogue of the electrostatic conductor limit. n ×(H2−H1) = K. B2t B1t µ2 µ1. 9. Methods of Magnetostatic Boundary-Value Problems Jackson organizes the practical methods into three broad classes. First, the generally applicable method is the vector potential. If the constitutive relation is explicit and simple enough, one solves for A from ∇×H[∇×A] = J.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: In linear media with constant µ in each region, Coulomb gauge reduces the problem to Poisson equations for A with matching conditions across boundaries. Second, in current-free regions one may introduce a magnetic scalar potential ΦM by H= −∇ΦM. In linear media this gives ∇·(µ∇ΦM) = 0, and in piecewise uniform media each region satisfies Laplace’s equation. This route makes the analogy with electrostatics very tight, especially near high-permeability boundaries. Third, for hard ferromagnets with specified magnetization M and no free current, one may work either with an effective magnetic charge density ρM = −∇·M, and surface density σM = n·M, or with the effective current picture JM = ∇×M, KM = M ×n. 8 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 The two descriptions are mathematically equivalent when handled consistently, but Jackson warns not to mix formulas carelessly across discontinuities. 10.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Uniformly Magnetized Sphere 1 3 2 3µ0M. A uniformly magnetized sphere is the classic example because it can be solved exactly and illustrates all three methods at once. Let M= M0ˆ z. Then the scalar-potential method shows that only the dipole term survives. Inside the sphere, Hin =− M, Bin = Outside, the potential is that of a dipole with moment 4πa3 3 M. This problem is worth remembering because it gives the demagnetizing field in its simplest exact form. The internal H opposes the magnetization, while B remains parallel to it. That distinction between B and H is often where intuition first begins to wobble, so the sphere is the right place to fix it. m = 11. Faraday’s Law The static equations cannot be the whole story because changing magnetic flux produces electric fields with nonzero circulation. The local law is ∂B ∇×E=− ∂t , and the integral form is d E·dl=− C dt S B·n da. This is Faraday’s law.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Its content is profound: time-dependent magnetic induction does not create charge, it creates vorticity in the electric field. Thus the field ceases to be globally representable by a single-valued scalar potential alone. The minus sign is Lenz’s law in differential form. The induced electromotive force opposes the change that produces it. At the level of field theory this sign is not an afterthought; it is what makes electromagnetic induction dynamically self-consistent and energetically stable. Once ∂B/∂t̸= 0, the electrostatic decomposition E= −∇Φ is incomplete. One must write ∂A E= −∇Φ− ∂t , 9 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 with the second term carrying the nonconservative part. 12. Inductance and Flux Linkage For a circuit carrying current I, the magnetic flux through a surface bounded by the circuit is ΦB = S B·n da.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: In linear media the flux is proportional to current, and one defines the self-inductance by ΦB = LI. For several coupled circuits, LijIj, dIj Lij Φi = j where the Lij are inductance coefficients. Faraday’s law then gives the induced emf, Ei =− . j The reciprocity Lij= Lji is one of the most useful general theorems of circuit theory and follows from the same symmetry that underlies Green-function reciprocity in electrostatics. dt 13. Magnetic Energy In a linear medium, the work required to build the current distribution can be written in field form as W= 1 2 J·A d3x= 1 2 B·H d3x. In vacuum this reduces to 1 W= 2µ0 B2 d3x. This is the magnetic counterpart of the electrostatic energy density 1 2 ε0E2, but the route to it is different because currents rather than charges are the direct sources. For a linear inductor one gets the familiar lumped expression W= 1 2 LI2 . This formula is easy to memorize and even easier to misuse.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Its validity depends on linearity and on using the quasistatic regime where radiation can be neglected. 10 Graduate Notes on Magnetostatics Jackson, Ch. 5 14. Quasi-Static Fields The quasi-static approximation is the intermediate regime between strict statics and full wave electrodynamics. The fields vary in time, but slowly enough that retardation across the system is negligible on the timescale of interest. One keeps induction effects, because ∂B/∂t matters, but discards wave propagation as a dominant phenomenon. Therearetwocomplementarylimits. Intheelectroquasistaticlimit, displacementeffectsareweakand the field is still mostly determined by instantaneous charge distributions. In the magnetoquasistatic limit, induction and conduction currents dominate, while the displacement current remains negligible.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: Dimensional comparison of characteristic size L, timescale T, and light-travel time L/c determines whether this approximation is self-consistent. In conducting media, quasi-static evolution often leads to diffusion-type equations rather than wave equations. With Ohm’s law J= σE and neglect of displacement current, Maxwell’s equations imply magnetic diffusion on a characteristic time scale of order τ ∼µσL2 . This is the time over which currents rearrange and fields penetrate into conductors. It is one of the cleanest examples of how field theory interpolates continuously between statics and dynamics. Closing synthesis Chapter 5 changes the tone of the subject. In electrostatics one could often pretend that the field was merely the gradient of a scalar and that the difficult part was the boundary conditions. In magnetostatics that innocence disappears.

chunks.title: chapter 5
chunks.text: The source is a flow, the field naturally curls around it, and the cleanest mathematical object is a vector potential carrying gauge freedom. Once matter is introduced, one must distinguish between the fundamental field B and the auxiliary field H; once time dependence is admitted, even gently, Faraday’s law forces a nonconservative electric field into the story. The chapter therefore does two jobs at once. It completes the static theory of magnetic fields produced by currents and magnetized matter, and it opens the door to electrodynamics proper by showing exactly where strict statics fails and quasi-statics begins. In that sense Chapter 5 is the hinge between electrostatics and the full Maxwell theory. 11

chunks.title: mini electrodynamics lab notes
chunks.text:
This little lab is meant to be a visual electrostatics playground, not a high-precision simulator. The idea is simple: place a few point charges in two dimensions, compute the potential and electric field they generate, then draw the results as a heatmap, vector arrows, contours, and a test-particle trajectory.

At a point (x,y) , the lab computes the scalar potential by summing the contribution from every charge:

phi (x,y) = sum _i q_i r_i , qquad r_i = (x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 .

Here (x_i,y_i) is the position of the i th charge and q_i is its strength. In the actual code, a small softening term is added to r_i^2 before taking the square root. That is just there to avoid a numerical blow-up exactly on top of a charge and to keep the visualization stable.

The potential is what drives the heatmap coloring. Positive regions and negative regions are colored differently so the field geometry is easier to read at a glance.

The electric field is related to the potential by

For point charges, the lab computes the field components directly:

chunks.title: mini electrodynamics lab notes
chunks.text:
E_x(x,y) = sum _i q_i(x-x_i) r_i^3 , qquad E_y(x,y) = sum _i q_i(y-y_i) r_i^3 .

The readout labeled |E| is just the field magnitude:

The arrow plot uses these field components. The arrow direction shows the local direction of the field, while the length changes with the field strength.

The contour lines are rough level sets of the potential. In other words, they try to mark curves where

These are useful because the electric field points perpendicular to equipotential lines. So when the contours are packed tightly together, the field is usually stronger.

When grounded-plane mode is turned on, the lab uses the method of images. For every real charge above the plane y=0 , an image charge is added with

(x_i,y_i,q_i) longrightarrow (x_i,-y_i,-q_i).

This mirrored charge distribution makes the potential vanish on the plane:

That is the correct boundary condition for a grounded conductor. So the lab is using the standard image-charge trick to imitate a conducting plane without explicitly solving the boundary-value problem numerically.

chunks.title: mini electrodynamics lab notes
chunks.text:
The moving particle is just a numerical intuition tool. The local field is sampled at the particle position, the velocity is updated, and then the position is stepped forward in time. This is enough to show how a test object tends to move through the field.

The speed slider in the interface is not meant to represent a physical unit like meters per second. It is only a simulation-control parameter that changes how fast the trajectory evolves on the screen.

The point of the lab is not to replace full derivations. The point is to build intuition:

how positive and negative charges reshape the potential landscape,

how field arrows line up with the charge configuration,

how grounded boundaries can be represented with images,

and how a test particle reacts to the geometry of the field.

So this should be thought of as a visual companion to the notes, not as a black-box solver.

chunks.title: Vector formulas
chunks.text: Derivation: We begin with (a ·∇)[nf(r)]. Use the product rule: (a ·∇)(fn) = [(a ·∇)f]n + f(a ·∇)n. So we compute these two terms separately. First term: [(a ·∇)f ]n Since we have Therefore, ∇f(r) = n df dr, (a ·∇)f= a ·∇f= a·n df dr. [(a ·∇)f]n = n(a·n)df dr. Second term: f (a ·∇)n We now compute (a ·∇)n. Since x n = , r in components, xi ni = . r Differentiate with respect to xj : ∂j ni = ∂j xi r . Using the product rule, ∂j = ∂j xi r + xi ∂j . Now ∂j xi = δij , and ∂j 1 r =− 1 r2 ∂j r. 7 xi r 1 r But from the preliminary result, xj r3. xixj r3. ∂j r= xj r , so ∂j 1 r =− 1 r2 xj r =− Thus ∂j ni = δij r− xixj r3. Now contract with aj : (a ·∇)ni = aj ∂j ni = aj δij r− Therefore (a ·∇)ni = ai r− xi(aj xj ) r3. Now aj xj= a·x, and since x = rn, we get a·x = a·(rn) = r(a·n). Also xi = rni. Substituting, (a ·∇)ni = ai r− (rni) r(a·n) r3 = ai r− . r So in vector form, (a ·∇)n = 1 r[a−n(a·n)]. Thus f(a ·∇)n = f(r) r [a−n(a·n)].

chunks.title: Vector formulas
chunks.text: Combine both terms Putting the two pieces together, (a ·∇)[nf(r)] = n(a·n)df dr + f(r) r [a−n(a·n)]. ni(a·n) Reordering, (a ·∇)[nf(r)] = f(r) r [a−n(a·n)] + n(a·n)df dr. 12. Angular Momentum Identity ∇(x·a) = a + x(∇·a) + i(L ×a), L= 1 i(x ×∇). 8 Derivation: We begin from the standard vector identity ∇(u·v) = (u ·∇)v + (v ·∇)u + u ×(∇×v) + v ×(∇×u). Set u = x, v = a. Then ∇(x·a) = (x ·∇)a + (a ·∇)x + x ×(∇×a) + a ×(∇×x). From Section 11(b), ∇×x = 0, so the last term vanishes: a ×(∇×x) = 0. Also, (a ·∇)x = a, because differentiating (x,y,z) in the direction of a gives exactly a. Hence ∇(x·a) = (x ·∇)a + a + x ×(∇×a). So ∇(x·a) = a + (x ·∇)a + x ×(∇×a). This is already a correct form of the identity. Now we relate the last two terms to the angular-momentum operator. Since L= 1 i(x ×∇), we have iL= x ×∇. Thus i(L ×a) = (x ×∇) ×a. To evaluate this carefully, use index notation: [i(L ×a)]i = ϵijk(iLj )ak = ϵijk(ϵjlmxl∂m)ak.

chunks.title: Vector formulas
chunks.text: Using we get Therefore Recognizing this becomes ϵijkϵjlm= δilδkm−δimδkl, [i(L ×a)]i = (δilδkm−δimδkl)xl∂mak. [i(L ×a)]i = xi∂kak−xl∂ial. ∂kak = ∇·a, [i(L ×a)]i = xi(∇·a)−xl∂ial. 9 Now compute the gradient of x·a directly: [∇(x·a)]i = ∂i(xlal) = (∂ixl)al + xl∂ial. Since ∂ixl = δil, we get [∇(x·a)]i = ai + xl∂ial. Thus Substitute this into the previous expression: xl∂ial = [∇(x·a)]i−ai. [i(L ×a)]i = xi(∇·a)− [∇(x·a)]i−ai. Therefore [i(L ×a)]i = ai + xi(∇·a)−[∇(x·a)]i. Rearranging, [∇(x·a)]i = ai + xi(∇·a)−[i(L ×a)]i. So in vector form, ∇(x·a) = a + x(∇·a)−i(L ×a). Remark on sign convention: some texts write With the definition Depending on the precise operator-order convention used for L, ∇(x·a) = a + x(∇·a) + i(L ×a). L= 1 i(x ×∇), the careful index derivation above gives ∇(x·a) = a + x(∇·a)−i(L ×a). 10

State clearly why the answer to each integral follows from the defining property of the delta distribution. 2. 2 Problem 2: Translation and Symmetry For a smooth test function ϕ(x), prove the identity ∞ ϕ(x) δ(x−a) dx= ϕ(a). −∞ Then use the same reasoning to show that δ(−x) = δ(x). 2. 3 Problem 3: Scaling Show that for a̸= 0, δ(ax) = 1 |a|δ(x). Use this result to evaluate I4 = ∞ −∞ I5 = ∞ −∞ δ(5x) g(x) dx, δ(3x−6) g(x) dx, where g(x) is an arbitrary smooth function. 3 Composite Arguments 3. 1 Problem 4: Delta of a Function Let a) Find all simple zeros of g(x). g(x) = x2 2 −9. chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Defining Green’s Function For electrostatics in a volume V, define a Green’s function G(r,r′) by the equation ∇2G(r,r′) =−4πδ(3)(r−r′). Explain the role of the source point r′ and field point r. 2. 2 Problem 2: Free-Space Green’s Function Show that in free space, G(r,r′) = 1 |r−r′| is the appropriate Green’s function. State carefully in what sense this solves the defining equation. 2. 3 Problem 3: Integral Representation Starting from Green’s second identity, derive the formal solution for the electrostatic potential in a bounded volume in terms of volume and boundary integrals involving G. 3 Boundary Conditions 3. 1 Problem 4: Dirichlet Green’s Function Define the Dirichlet Green’s function for a region V with boundary S. State the boundary condition it satisfies and explain why it is useful when the potential is prescribed on S. 3. 2 Problem 5: Neumann Green’s Function Define the Neumann Green’s function.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: Explain why an additional compatibility condition is required in Neumann problems and identify that condition. 3. 3 Problem 6: Reciprocity Show that under suitable conditions, G(r,r′) = G(r′ ,r). State the assumptions needed for this symmetry. 4 Canonical Examples 4. 1 Problem 7: Grounded Infinite Plane Construct the Dirichlet Green’s function for the half-space z > 0 with grounded plane at z = 0. Verify explicitly that the boundary condition is satisfied. 4. 2 Problem 8: Point Charge Near a Grounded Plane Use the Green’s function from the previous problem to write the potential due to a point charge q located at (0,0,a) with a>0. Compare the result with the method of images. 2 Green’s Functions Test Jackson-style Practice 4. 3 Problem 9: Spherical Boundary For a point charge inside a grounded conducting sphere of radius R, write the strategy for constructing the Green’s function.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: You do not need to derive the full formula, but state the image construction and the boundary condition it enforces. 5 Interpretation and Comparison 5. 1 Problem 10: Green’s Function as a Response Kernel Explain why a Green’s function may be viewed as the response of the system to a point source. Then explain how superposition turns this into the response to an arbitrary charge density. 5. 2 Problem 11: Connection to the Method of Images Explain why the method of images can be interpreted as constructing a Green’s function that already satisfies the boundary conditions. Give one example where this is possible and one where it is not straightforward. 5. 3 Problem 12: Boundary Value Problem View Discuss why the same differential equation can have different Green’s functions depending on the geometry and boundary conditions. 6 Computation-Oriented Problems 6.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: 1 Problem 13: Singularity Structure Take the free-space Green’s function G(r,r′) = 1 |r−r′|. Describe its singular behavior as r →r′ and explain why that singularity is physically necessary. 6. 2 Problem 14: Simplified Integral Evaluation Consider a one-dimensional model problem with kernel G(x,x′) =− 1 2|x−x′|. Verify directly that d2 dx2 G(x,x′) =−δ(x−x′) in the distributional sense. 7 Challenge Problems 7. 1 Problem 15: Uniqueness with Green’s Functions Show that if two Green’s functions satisfy the same defining equation and the same homogeneous boundary conditions, then their difference must be harmonic and vanish on the boundary. Conclude that the Green’s function is unique. 7. 2 Problem 16: Surface Integral Interpretation In the Green-function representation formula, explain the physical role of the boundary term. What information does it encode in Dirichlet and Neumann problems respectively? 3

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Polarization and Bound Charge Define the polarization field P(r). Show that the bound volume charge density is ρb = −∇·P. State also the expression for bound surface charge density. 2. 2 Problem 2: Displacement Field Define the electric displacement field D= ε0E + P. Starting from Gauss’s law, derive ∇·D= ρf , where ρf is the free charge density. 2. 3 Problem 3: Linear Dielectrics For a linear isotropic dielectric, write the constitutive relation between P and E. Then derive D= εE and identify ε in terms of susceptibility. 3 Canonical Configurations 3. 1 Problem 4: Uniformly Polarized Slab Consider a slab polarized uniformly in the +ˆ z direction. a) Find the bound volume charge density. b) Find the bound surface charge densities on the two faces. c) Sketch or describe the qualitative electric field produced by the slab. 3. 2 Problem 5: Uniformly Polarized Sphere A sphere of radius R has uniform polarization P= P0 ˆ z.

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: a) Find ρb and σb. b) Explain why the interior electric field is uniform. c) State the direction of the interior field relative to P. 3. 3 Problem 6: Dielectric in a Parallel-Plate Capacitor A dielectric slab completely fills the space between parallel conducting plates held at fixed potential difference V. a) Determine the electric field in the dielectric. b) Find the displacement field and free surface charge on the plates. c) Compare the capacitance with the vacuum case. 2 Dielectrics Test Jackson-style Practice 4 Interfaces and Boundary Conditions 4. 1 Problem 7: Interface Between Two Dielectrics Two linear dielectrics with permittivities ε1 and ε2 meet at a planar interface. a) State the boundary condition on the normal component of D. b) State the boundary condition on the tangential component of E. c) Explain how these conditions change if a free surface charge density is present. 4.

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: 2 Problem 8: Refraction of Field Lines Using the dielectric boundary conditions, derive the relation between the angles that the electric field makes with the normal on the two sides of a dielectric interface. 5 Potential Formulation 5. 1 Problem 9: Poisson Equation in Dielectrics Starting from ∇·(ε∇Φ) =−ρf , derive the form of the electrostatic equation for a) constant ε, b) piecewise constant ε, c) no free charge. 5. 2 Problem 10: Piecewise Constant Permittivity In one dimension, let ε(x) = ε1, x<0, ε2, x>0. Assume no free charge except possibly at the interface. State the continuity or jump conditions satisfied by Φ and εdΦ/dx at x= 0. 6 Dielectric Sphere in an External Field 6. 1 Problem 11: Polarizable Sphere A dielectric sphere of radius R and permittivity ε is placed in an initially uniform external field E0 = E0 ˆ z. a) Write the general form of the interior and exterior potentials. b) Impose the boundary conditions at r= R.

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: c) State the resulting interior field and induced dipole moment. 6. 2 Problem 12: Limiting Cases For the dielectric sphere in the previous problem, discuss the limits: a) ε= ε0, b) ε→∞, c) ε≫ε0 but finite. 3 Dielectrics Test Jackson-style Practice 7 Energy and Interpretation 7. 1 Problem 13: Electrostatic Energy in Matter Write the electrostatic energy density in a linear dielectric. Explain why the presence of dielectric material changes the energy stored at fixed free charge. 7. 2 Problem 14: Microscopic Versus Macroscopic Picture Explain the conceptual difference between microscopic charges and the macroscopic fields P and D. Why are bound charges useful even though they are not independent sources? 8 Challenge Problems 8. 1 Problem 15: Point Charge in a Uniform Dielectric A point charge q is embedded in a homogeneous linear dielectric of permittivity ε. a) Write the potential. b) Compare it with the vacuum result.

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: c) Explain the physical meaning of the reduction by ε. 8. 2 Problem 16: Common Pitfall A student says that if ρf = 0 then D = 0 everywhere. Explain why this is false and give a counterexample. 4

chunks.title: electro magnetic radiation
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Wave Equations from Maxwell’s Equations Starting from Maxwell’s equations in vacuum, derive the wave equations for E and B in source-free space. 2. 2 Problem 2: Retarded Potentials Write the scalar and vector retarded potentials in the Lorenz gauge for general charge and current densities. Explain the meaning of retarded time. 2. 3 Problem 3: Causality Why are retarded rather than advanced potentials used in classical electrodynamics? Give a physical explanation rather than a formal one only. 3 Radiation Zone 3. 1 Problem 4: Near, Induction, and Radiation Terms For a localized oscillating source, explain the qualitative difference between terms that scale as 1 1 1 r3 , r2 , . r Which term carries energy to infinity? 3. 2 Problem 5: Far-Field Approximation State the approximations that define the radiation zone. Explain why in that region the electric and magnetic fields are transverse. 3.

chunks.title: electro magnetic radiation
chunks.text: 3 Problem 6: Poynting Vector Starting from the far-zone relation between E and B, show that the Poynting vector points radially outward on average for outgoing radiation. 4 Dipole Radiation 4. 1 Problem 7: Oscillating Electric Dipole An electric dipole moment oscillates as p(t) = p0 cos(ωt) ˆ z. a) Write the far-zone angular dependence of the radiated electric field. b) Write the corresponding magnetic field. c) State the dependence on r, ω, and sin θ. 4. 2 Problem 8: Radiation Pattern For the oscillating dipole oriented along the z-axis: a) At which angles is the radiation maximal? b) At which angles does it vanish? c) Sketch the qualitative angular pattern. 2 Electromagnetic Radiation Test Jackson-style Practice 4. 3 Problem 9: Total Radiated Power Use the angular distribution of dipole radiation to derive the total time-averaged radiated power of a nonrelativistic oscillating dipole. 4.

chunks.title: electro magnetic radiation
chunks.text: 4 Problem 10: Hertzian Dipole Explain what is meant by a Hertzian dipole. Why is it a useful elementary source in radiation theory? 5 Physical Interpretation 5. 1 Problem 11: Accelerating Charge Explain why a uniformly moving charge does not radiate, while an accelerating charge can. Your answer should distinguish clearly between static, induction, and radiation behavior. 5. 2 Problem 12: Multipole Viewpoint Why is electric dipole radiation usually the leading radiation mechanism for a localized oscillating source? Under what symmetry conditions can dipole radiation vanish? 5. 3 Problem 13: Radiation Reaction Discussion What is meant by radiation reaction? Explain why it is conceptually subtle even before writing down a detailed formula. 6 Computation-Oriented Problems 6. 1 Problem 14: Frequency Dependence How does the total radiated power of an oscillating dipole scale with angular frequency ω and dipole amplitude p0?

chunks.title: electro magnetic radiation
chunks.text: What does this imply about high-frequency sources? 6. 2 Problem 15: Near-Field Versus Far-Field Suppose measurements are made at several distances from a source. Explain how one could distinguish experimentally between near-field and far-field behavior using radial scaling. 7 Challenge Problems 7. 1 Problem 16: Symmetry and Forbidden Dipole Radiation Give one example of a source configuration for which the electric dipole moment vanishes by symmetry. What multipole order would then typically dominate the radiation? 7. 2 Problem 17: Dimensional Analysis of Larmor-Type Power Use dimensional analysis and basic physical reasoning to motivate the dependence of radiated power on charge, acceleration, vacuum permittivity, and the speed of light for a nonrelativistic point charge. 3

chunks.title: method of images
chunks.text: . . . . . . 3 6 Worked-Result Style Problems 3 6. 1 Problem 11: Two Charges Near a Plane . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 6. 2 Problem 12: Neutral Conductor Question . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 7 Challenge Problems 3 7. 1 7. 2 Problem 13: Energy Interpretation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 Problem 14: Conducting Wedge Discussion . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 1 Method of Images Test Jackson-style Practice 1 Source Notebook This problem set follows the notebook . /notebooks/Methodofimagesinelectrostatics. ipynb and focuses on image constructions for planes and spheres. 2 Conceptual Foundations 2. 1 Problem 1: Why the Method Works Explain the logic of the method of images. Why is it sufficient to find any potential that satisfies both Poisson’s equation in the physical region and the correct boundary conditions on the conductor? 2.

chunks.title: method of images
chunks.text: 2 Problem 2: Uniqueness Principle State the uniqueness theorem that justifies the method of images. Explain carefully why the image charges are not physically present in the region of interest. 3 Grounded Infinite Plane 3. 1 Problem 3: Potential of a Point Charge Near a Grounded Plane A point charge q is located at (0,0,a) with a>0 above a grounded conducting plane at z= 0. a) Write the image configuration. b) Write the electrostatic potential in the region z>0. c) Verify that Φ = 0 on the plane. 3. 2 Problem 4: Electric Field and Force For the same geometry: a) Compute the force on the real charge. b) Explain why the force is attractive. c) Compare the result with Coulomb’s law between the real and image charges. 3. 3 Problem 5: Induced Surface Charge Determine the induced surface charge density σ(x,y) on the grounded plane for the point charge problem. Then integrate σ over the plane and show that the total induced charge equals−q.

chunks.title: method of images
chunks.text: 4 Grounded Conducting Sphere 4. 1 Problem 6: Image Construction for a Sphere A point charge q is placed outside a grounded conducting sphere of radius R at distance a>R from the center. a) State the magnitude and position of the image charge. b) Write the potential outside the sphere. c) Verify that the potential vanishes on the surface r= R. 4. 2 Problem 7: Force on the External Charge Using the image construction for the grounded sphere, derive the force on the external point charge. Discuss the limiting behavior as a→∞and as a→R+ . 2 Method of Images Test Jackson-style Practice 4. 3 Problem 8: Induced Charge on the Sphere Describe how one would compute the induced surface charge density on the sphere from the normal electric field at the surface. You do not need the final closed-form expression, but you must give the correct procedure. 5 Comparison and Interpretation 5.

chunks.title: method of images
chunks.text: 1 Problem 9: Plane Versus Sphere Compare the image solutions for a grounded plane and a grounded sphere. Which features are similar, and which arise from the curvature of the boundary? 5. 2 Problem 10: Relation to Green’s Functions Explain how the image solution for a grounded plane can be interpreted as a Dirichlet Green’s function for the half-space. 6 Worked-Result Style Problems 6. 1 Problem 11: Two Charges Near a Plane Two point charges q1 and q2 are located above a grounded conducting plane. Describe the image configuration and write the total potential in the physical half-space. 6. 2 Problem 12: Neutral Conductor Question Explain the difference between a grounded conductor and an isolated neutral conductor in the context of image methods. Why does grounding matter? 7 Challenge Problems 7.

chunks.title: method of images
chunks.text: 1 Problem 13: Energy Interpretation Show that the interaction energy of a charge with a grounded plane is not simply the full Coulomb energy between the charge and its image. Explain the factor of one-half that appears in electrostatic energy arguments. 7. 2 Problem 14: Conducting Wedge Discussion Under what special geometric conditions can image methods be generalized to wedges? Explain why generic geome- tries usually do not admit a finite image construction. 3

chunks.title: multipole expansion
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Coulomb Kernel Expansion Starting from Φ(r) = 1 4πε0 ρ(r′) |r−r′|d3r′ , derive the idea of expanding the kernel for r≫r′. State the small parameter controlling the approximation. 2. 2 Problem 2: Monopole Term Show that the leading far-field term is where Φmono(r) = 1 4πε0 r Q= ρ(r′) d3r′ . Q , Explain why this term vanishes for a neutral distribution. 2. 3 Problem 3: Dipole Term Show that the next term has the form with dipole moment Φdip(r) = 1 4πε0 ˆ p· r r2 , p= r′ρ(r′) d3r′ . 2. 4 Problem 4: Field of an Ideal Dipole Starting from Φ(r) = 1 4πε0 p·r r3 , compute the electric field of an ideal dipole. 3 Quadrupole and Symmetry 3. 1 Problem 5: Quadrupole Structure Define the quadrupole tensor Qij= ρ(r′) 3x′ ix′ j−r′2δij d3r′ . Explain why it is symmetric and traceless in the standard convention. 3. 2 Problem 6: Two Equal and Opposite Charges For charges +q at (0,0,a) and−q at (0,0,−a): a) Find the total charge.

chunks.title: multipole expansion
chunks.text: b) Find the dipole moment. c) Determine which multipole term is the leading nonzero contribution. 2 Multipole Expansion Test Jackson-style Practice 3. 3 Problem 7: Simple Quadrupole Arrangement Consider four point charges arranged so that the total charge and dipole moment vanish. Propose one explicit configuration and show that the leading far-field term is quadrupolar. 4 Symmetry Arguments 4. 1 Problem 8: Spherical Symmetry Show that a spherically symmetric charge distribution has only a monopole term in its exterior potential. 4. 2 Problem 9: Reflection and Inversion Symmetry Explain how reflection symmetry or inversion symmetry can force certain multipole moments to vanish. Give one concrete example for each case. 4. 3 Problem 10: Axial Symmetry Discuss how axial symmetry simplifies the angular dependence of the multipole expansion. Why are Legendre polynomials natural in this setting? 5 Canonical Examples 5.

chunks.title: multipole expansion

Write its leading far-field potential. 5. 3 Problem 13: Neutral Distribution with Vanishing Dipole Moment Construct a simple configuration with zero total charge and zero dipole moment but nonzero quadrupole moment. 6 Validity and Error 6. 1 Problem 14: Region of Validity Explain precisely when a multipole expansion is expected to converge well. What goes wrong when the observation point lies inside or too near the source distribution? 6. 2 Problem 15: Error Decay If the monopole and dipole terms are retained, estimate how the neglected terms scale with distance. Give the answer in powers of 1/r. 7 Challenge Problems 7.

chunks.title: multipole expansion
chunks.text: 1 Problem 16: Translating the Origin Show that the dipole moment depends on the choice of origin unless the total charge vanishes. Then explain why, for a neutral system, the dipole moment is origin independent. 7. 2 Problem 17: Angular Pattern For an ideal dipole oriented along the z-axis, write the angular dependence of the potential and field. Explain why the dipole field has strong directional structure unlike the monopole field. 3

chunks.title: Dirac Delta problems
chunks.text: Evaluate the following integrals: I1 = ∞ −∞ I2 = ∞ −∞ I3 = 5 0 f(x) δ(x−2) dx, (x2 −4x+ 1) δ(x+ 3) dx, sin(πx) δ(x−4) dx. State clearly why the answer to each integral follows from the defining property of the delta distribution. 2. 2 Problem 2: Translation and Symmetry For a smooth test function ϕ(x), prove the identity ∞ ϕ(x) δ(x−a) dx= ϕ(a). −∞ Then use the same reasoning to show that δ(−x) = δ(x). 2. 3 Problem 3: Scaling Show that for a̸= 0, δ(ax) = 1 |a|δ(x). Use this result to evaluate I4 = ∞ −∞ I5 = ∞ −∞ δ(5x) g(x) dx, δ(3x−6) g(x) dx, where g(x) is an arbitrary smooth function. 3 Composite Arguments 3. 1 Problem 4: Delta of a Function Let a) Find all simple zeros of g(x). g(x) = x2 2 −9. Dirac Delta Function Test Jackson-style Practice b) Use the identity δ(x−xi) δ(g(x)) = |g′(xi)| i to express δ(x2 −9) as a sum of ordinary delta distributions. c) Evaluate I6 = ∞ −∞ (2x+ 1) δ(x2 −9) dx. 3.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Defining Green’s Function For electrostatics in a volume V, define a Green’s function G(r,r′) by the equation ∇2G(r,r′) =−4πδ(3)(r−r′). Explain the role of the source point r′ and field point r. 2. 2 Problem 2: Free-Space Green’s Function Show that in free space, G(r,r′) = 1 |r−r′| is the appropriate Green’s function. State carefully in what sense this solves the defining equation. 2. 3 Problem 3: Integral Representation Starting from Green’s second identity, derive the formal solution for the electrostatic potential in a bounded volume in terms of volume and boundary integrals involving G. 3 Boundary Conditions 3. 1 Problem 4: Dirichlet Green’s Function Define the Dirichlet Green’s function for a region V with boundary S. State the boundary condition it satisfies and explain why it is useful when the potential is prescribed on S. 3. 2 Problem 5: Neumann Green’s Function Define the Neumann Green’s function.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: Explain why an additional compatibility condition is required in Neumann problems and identify that condition. 3. 3 Problem 6: Reciprocity Show that under suitable conditions, G(r,r′) = G(r′ ,r). State the assumptions needed for this symmetry. 4 Canonical Examples 4. 1 Problem 7: Grounded Infinite Plane Construct the Dirichlet Green’s function for the half-space z > 0 with grounded plane at z = 0. Verify explicitly that the boundary condition is satisfied. 4. 2 Problem 8: Point Charge Near a Grounded Plane Use the Green’s function from the previous problem to write the potential due to a point charge q located at (0,0,a) with a>0. Compare the result with the method of images. 2 Green’s Functions Test Jackson-style Practice 4. 3 Problem 9: Spherical Boundary For a point charge inside a grounded conducting sphere of radius R, write the strategy for constructing the Green’s function.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: You do not need to derive the full formula, but state the image construction and the boundary condition it enforces. 5 Interpretation and Comparison 5. 1 Problem 10: Green’s Function as a Response Kernel Explain why a Green’s function may be viewed as the response of the system to a point source. Then explain how superposition turns this into the response to an arbitrary charge density. 5. 2 Problem 11: Connection to the Method of Images Explain why the method of images can be interpreted as constructing a Green’s function that already satisfies the boundary conditions. Give one example where this is possible and one where it is not straightforward. 5. 3 Problem 12: Boundary Value Problem View Discuss why the same differential equation can have different Green’s functions depending on the geometry and boundary conditions. 6 Computation-Oriented Problems 6.

chunks.title: Green functions
chunks.text: 1 Problem 13: Singularity Structure Take the free-space Green’s function G(r,r′) = 1 |r−r′|. Describe its singular behavior as r →r′ and explain why that singularity is physically necessary. 6. 2 Problem 14: Simplified Integral Evaluation Consider a one-dimensional model problem with kernel G(x,x′) =− 1 2|x−x′|. Verify directly that d2 dx2 G(x,x′) =−δ(x−x′) in the distributional sense. 7 Challenge Problems 7. 1 Problem 15: Uniqueness with Green’s Functions Show that if two Green’s functions satisfy the same defining equation and the same homogeneous boundary conditions, then their difference must be harmonic and vanish on the boundary. Conclude that the Green’s function is unique. 7. 2 Problem 16: Surface Integral Interpretation In the Green-function representation formula, explain the physical role of the boundary term. What information does it encode in Dirichlet and Neumann problems respectively? 3
