chunks.title: Laplace equation
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Laplace Equation from Poisson Equation Starting from Poisson’s equation, explain why in a charge-free region the electrostatic potential satisfies ∇2Φ = 0. State the physical meaning of a source-free region. 2. 2 Problem 2: Mean Value Property State the mean value property for harmonic functions. Then explain why it immediately implies that a harmonic function cannot have an isolated interior maximum or minimum unless it is constant. 2. 3 Problem 3: Uniqueness Prove the Dirichlet uniqueness theorem for Laplace’s equation in electrostatics. Keep the proof at the level of an energy integral argument. 3 One- and Two-Dimensional Problems 3. 1 Problem 4: One-Dimensional Laplace Equation Solve d2Φ dx2 = 0 on the interval 0 <x<Lwith boundary conditions Φ(0) = V0 and Φ(L) = V1. Interpret the electric field physically. 3.

chunks.title: Laplace equation
chunks.text: 2 Problem 5: Parallel Plates Two infinite conducting plates are located at x= 0 and x= d, held at potentials 0 and V0 respectively. a) Find the potential between the plates. b) Find the electric field. c) Explain why fringing fields are absent in this idealization. 3. 3 Problem 6: Rectangular Region Solve Laplace’s equation in the rectangle 0 <x<a, 0 <y<b with boundary conditions Φ(0,y) = 0, Φ(a,y) = 0, Φ(x,0) = 0, Φ(x,b) = V0 sin πx a . Use separation of variables and determine the full solution. 3. 4 Problem 7: General Fourier-Series Boundary Data Repeat the previous problem when the boundary value at y= b is an arbitrary function f(x) expanded in a sine series. Write the solution as a series without evaluating the coefficients explicitly. 2 Laplace’s Equation Test Jackson-style Practice 4 Symmetry-Based Solutions 4. 1 Problem 8: Spherical Symmetry Assume a radially symmetric solution Φ(r) in a charge-free region.

chunks.title: Laplace equation
chunks.text: a) Reduce Laplace’s equation to an ordinary differential equation. b) Solve for Φ(r). c) Explain the physical meaning of each integration constant. 4. 2 Problem 9: Exterior of a Conducting Sphere A conducting sphere of radius R is held at potential V0. a) Solve for the exterior potential assuming Φ(∞) = 0. b) Find the electric field outside the sphere. c) Compute the surface charge density from the discontinuity of the normal electric field. 4. 3 Problem 10: Cylindrical Symmetry Assume Φ = Φ(s) depends only on cylindrical radius s. a) Derive the radial form of Laplace’s equation in cylindrical coordinates. b) Solve it. c) Explain why logarithms appear in two-dimensional electrostatics. 5 Properties of Harmonic Functions 5. 1 Problem 11: No Interior Extrema Suppose Φ is harmonic in a bounded domain.

chunks.title: Laplace equation
chunks.text: Give a proof, based on either the mean value property or the maximum principle, that Φ cannot attain a strict interior maximum unless it is constant. 5. 2 Problem 12: Consequence for Conductors Explain why a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium must have constant potential throughout its interior. Then connect this result to Laplace’s equation in the empty region outside the conductor. 6 Boundary Value Perspective 6. 1 Problem 13: Mixed Boundary Conditions Discuss the difference between Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions for Laplace’s equation. Give one elec- trostatic example of each. 6. 2 Problem 14: Changing Boundary Data Explain why changing the potential on only one boundary segment can alter the solution everywhere in a connected region. Relate your answer to harmonicity and nonlocality. 7 Challenge Problems 7.

Compare the result with the corresponding three-dimensional spherically symmetric problem. 4

Explain the physical meaning of this result. 2. 3 Problem 3: Units Check the SI units of each term in ∇2Φ =− ρ ε0 . Confirm that the equation is dimensionally consistent. 3 Canonical Examples 3. 1 Problem 4: Uniform Charge Density in a Slab An infinite slab occupies−a<z<a and has constant volume charge density ρ0. a) Derive the one-dimensional Poisson equation for Φ(z). b) Solve for the electric field inside and outside the slab using symmetry. c) Obtain the potential, taking Φ(0) = 0. d) Check continuity conditions at z= ±a. 3. 2 Problem 5: Point Charge in Three Dimensions Show that for a point charge q at the origin, ρ(r) = qδ(3)(r), Poisson’s equation becomes ∇2Φ(r) =− q ε0 δ(3)(r).

chunks.title: Poisson equation
chunks.text: Verify that Φ(r) = 1 4πε0 q r solves the equation away from the origin, and explain the role of the singularity at r= 0. 3. 3 Problem 6: Spherical Symmetry Assume a spherically symmetric potential Φ(r). a) Write Poisson’s equation in spherical symmetry. b) Solve it for a general radial charge density ρ(r) in integral form. c) State what simplification occurs in regions where ρ(r) = 0. 2 Poisson’s Equation Test Jackson-style Practice 3. 4 Problem 7: Uniformly Charged Solid Sphere A sphere of radius R has uniform charge density ρ0. a) Find the electric field inside and outside the sphere. b) Derive the potential everywhere, imposing Φ(∞) = 0. c) Check continuity of Φ and Er at r= R. 3. 5 Problem 8: Piecewise One-Dimensional Source Consider ρ(x) = ρ0, |x|<a, 0, |x|>a. Solve the one-dimensional Poisson equation for Φ(x) piecewise. Determine the integration constants from continuity and symmetry assumptions. 4 Boundary Value Viewpoint 4.

chunks.title: Poisson equation
chunks.text: 1 Problem 9: Uniqueness State and prove a uniqueness theorem for Poisson’s equation with Dirichlet boundary conditions. Your proof may use the energy method. 4. 2 Problem 10: Boundary Conditions At an interface between two regions with different charge densities, state the continuity properties required of Φ and of the normal derivative of Φ. Explain how this connects Poisson’s equation with Coulomb’s law. 6 Computational Interpretation 6.

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: 1 Problem 1: Polarization and Bound Charge Define the polarization field P(r). Show that the bound volume charge density is ρb = −∇·P. State also the expression for bound surface charge density. 2. 2 Problem 2: Displacement Field Define the electric displacement field D= ε0E + P. Starting from Gauss’s law, derive ∇·D= ρf , where ρf is the free charge density. 2. 3 Problem 3: Linear Dielectrics For a linear isotropic dielectric, write the constitutive relation between P and E. Then derive D= εE and identify ε in terms of susceptibility. 3 Canonical Configurations 3. 1 Problem 4: Uniformly Polarized Slab Consider a slab polarized uniformly in the +ˆ z direction. a) Find the bound volume charge density. b) Find the bound surface charge densities on the two faces. c) Sketch or describe the qualitative electric field produced by the slab. 3. 2 Problem 5: Uniformly Polarized Sphere A sphere of radius R has uniform polarization P= P0 ˆ z.

chunks.title: dielectrics problems
chunks.text: a) Find ρb and σb. b) Explain why the interior electric field is uniform. c) State the direction of the interior field relative to P. 3. 3 Problem 6: Dielectric in a Parallel-Plate Capacitor A dielectric slab completely fills the space between parallel conducting plates held at fixed potential difference V. a) Determine the electric field in the dielectric. b) Find the displacement field and free surface charge on the plates. c) Compare the capacitance with the vacuum case. 2 Dielectrics Test Jackson-style Practice 4 Interfaces and Boundary Conditions 4. 1 Problem 7: Interface Between Two Dielectrics Two linear dielectrics with permittivities ε1 and ε2 meet at a planar interface. a) State the boundary condition on the normal component of D. b) State the boundary condition on the tangential component of E. c) Explain how these conditions change if a free surface charge density is present. 4.

Assume no free charge except possibly at the interface. chunks.text:
Consider four point charges arranged so that the total charge and dipole moment vanish. Propose one explicit configuration and show that the leading far-field term is quadrupolar.

Show that a spherically symmetric charge distribution has only a monopole term in its exterior potential.

Problem 9: Reflection and Inversion Symmetry

Explain how reflection symmetry or inversion symmetry can force certain multipole moments to vanish. Give one concrete example for each case.

Discuss how axial symmetry simplifies the angular dependence of the multipole expansion. Why are Legendre polynomials natural in this setting?

Find the exterior multipole expansion of a uniformly charged sphere and compare it with the exact potential.

Problem 12: Neutral Distribution with Nonzero Dipole Moment

Construct a simple continuous or discrete neutral distribution with nonzero dipole moment. Write its leading far-field potential.

Problem 13: Neutral Distribution with Vanishing Dipole Moment

Construct a simple configuration with zero total charge and zero dipole moment but nonzero quadrupole moment.

chunks.title: multipole expansion test
chunks.text:
Explain precisely when a multipole expansion is expected to converge well. What goes wrong when the observation point lies inside or too near the source distribution?

If the monopole and dipole terms are retained, estimate how the neglected terms scale with distance. Give the answer in powers of 1/r .

Show that the dipole moment depends on the choice of origin unless the total charge vanishes. Then explain why, for a neutral system, the dipole moment is origin independent.

For an ideal dipole oriented along the z -axis, write the angular dependence of the potential and field. Explain why the dipole field has strong directional structure unlike the monopole field.

chunks.title: poisson equation test
chunks.text:
This problem set follows the notebook ../notebooks/Poisson's equation.ipynb and focuses on electrostatic source problems, boundary conditions, and symmetry reductions.

Starting from Gauss's law in differential form,

and using E =- nabla Phi , derive Poisson's equation. State clearly when it reduces to Laplace's equation.

nabla ^2 Phi _1=- rho _1 varepsilon _0 , qquad nabla ^2 Phi _2=- rho _2 varepsilon _0 .

Show that any linear combination a Phi _1+b Phi _2 satisfies a corresponding Poisson equation. Explain the physical meaning of this result.

Confirm that the equation is dimensionally consistent.

Problem 4: Uniform Charge Density in a Slab

An infinite slab occupies -a<z<a and has constant volume charge density rho _0 .

Derive the one-dimensional Poisson equation for Phi (z) .

Solve for the electric field inside and outside the slab using symmetry.

Obtain the potential, taking Phi (0)=0 .

Check continuity conditions at z= pm a .

Problem 5: Point Charge in Three Dimensions

Show that for a point charge q at the origin,

chunks.title: poisson equation test
chunks.text:
nabla ^2 Phi ( r )=- q varepsilon _0 delta ^ (3) ( r ).

solves the equation away from the origin, and explain the role of the singularity at r=0 .

Assume a spherically symmetric potential Phi (r) .

Write Poisson's equation in spherical symmetry.

Solve it for a general radial charge density rho (r) in integral form.

State what simplification occurs in regions where rho (r)=0 .

Problem 7: Uniformly Charged Solid Sphere

A sphere of radius R has uniform charge density rho _0 .

Find the electric field inside and outside the sphere.

Derive the potential everywhere, imposing Phi ( infty )=0 .

Check continuity of Phi and E_r at r=R .

Problem 8: Piecewise One-Dimensional Source

Solve the one-dimensional Poisson equation for Phi (x) piecewise. Determine the integration constants from continuity and symmetry assumptions.

State and prove a uniqueness theorem for Poisson's equation with Dirichlet boundary conditions. Your proof may use the energy method.

chunks.title: poisson equation test
chunks.text:
At an interface between two regions with different charge densities, state the continuity properties required of Phi and of the normal derivative of Phi . Explain how a surface charge modifies the normal derivative condition.

Suppose rho =0 in a region V , but rho neq 0 elsewhere. Explain why the potential inside V still depends on charges outside the region. Give a concrete electrostatic example.

Assuming free space and suitable decay at infinity, show formally that the solution of Poisson's equation can be written as

Phi ( r )= 1 4 pi varepsilon _0 int rho ( r ') | r - r '| ,d^3r'.

Explain how this connects Poisson's equation with Coulomb's law.

Explain the geometric relation between the sign of rho (x) and the curvature of Phi (x) . What does a localized positive charge do to the graph of the potential?

On a uniform one-dimensional grid with spacing h , derive the finite-difference approximation

frac Phi _ i+1 -2 Phi _i+ Phi _ i-1 h^2 =- rho _i varepsilon _0 .

State the order of the truncation error and explain how boundary conditions enter the discrete system.

chunks.title: repo mindmap
chunks.text:
This file gives a high-level map of how the repository is organized. Visual Map

Text Outline notebooks/ Theory notebooks, derivations, computational experiments, Jackson-focused worked studies, and visualization notebooks. problems/ Topic-centered problem sets in LaTeX and PDF. Jackson-problems/ Chapter-based Jackson problem material. Griffiths-problems/ Griffiths exercise index and solution-related material. Formula Sheet/ Compact formula sheets in both . tex and . pdf . notes/ and notes-diego/ Structured notes, chapter summaries, calculus support material, and personal study references. slides/ Presentation PDFs and lecture-style material. codes/ Python scripts for animations, games, numerical workflows, plotting, and exports. codes/cpp/ Standalone C++ solvers, especially yee_fdtd/ and fft_poisson/ . docs/ The main static website deployment target, including interactive pages and media assets. site/ A parallel static-site layer with overlapping pages and supporting assets.

chunks.title: repo mindmap
chunks.text: overleaf_uploads/ Exportable copies of formula-sheet and problem-set projects for external editing workflows. Root PDFs Primary textbook references and a few top-level artifacts used to support the study workflow. How To Read This Repo Theory -> problems -> notes -> code -> visualization is the main academic workflow. docs/ is the public-facing layer. codes/ and codes/cpp/ are the implementation layer. notebooks/ , problems/ , Formula Sheet/ , and notes-diego/ are the learning-content core.

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: Green functions Diego Juarez Department of Physics The University of Texas at El Paso April 15, 2026 Green functions 1/18 Presentation map 1 Green’s Functions Format This section gives a graduate-level introduction to Green’s functions in electrostatics, including the derivation of the integral representation, the free-space kernel, and boundary-condition examples. Green functions 2/18 Green’s Function for Poisson’s Equation: Motivation ρ(r) We want to solve Poisson’s equation in electrostatics, ∇2V(r) =− , ε0 either in free space or in a region with specified boundary conditions. The key idea is to first solve the response to a point source. Define the Green’s function G(r,r′) by ∇2G(r,r′) =−δ(3)(r−r′). Here: r is the observation point, r′is the source point, G(r,r′) is the potential at r due to a unit point source at r′ . Once G is known, the solution for an arbitrary charge density follows by superposition.

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: Main idea Green’s functions are inverse kernels of linear differential operators. Green functions 3/18 Green’s Function for Poisson’s Equation: First Step Start from Poisson’s equation written in the source coordinates r′: ρ(r′) ∇′2V(r′) =− . ε0 ∇′2G(r,r′) =−δ(3)(r−r′). G(r,r′) ρ(r′) Let the Green’s function satisfy Multiply Poisson’s equation by G(r,r′): G(r,r′) ∇′2V(r′) =− . ε0 Multiply the Green’s-function equation by V(r′): V(r′) ∇′2G(r,r′) =−V(r′) δ(3)(r−r′). Subtract the second equation from the first: Gρ G∇′2V−V∇′2G=− + V(r′)δ(3)(r−r′). ε0 Green functions 4/18 Green’s Function for Poisson’s Equation: Green’s Identity Use Green’s second identity: ∇′ ·(G∇′V−V∇′G) = G∇′2V−V∇′2G. Therefore, Gρ ·(G∇′V−V∇′G) =− ∇′ Now integrate over a volume V: 1 ∇′ ·(G∇′V−V∇′G) d3r′ =− V ε0 V Apply the divergence theorem to the left-hand side: ∂V G∂V ∂n′−V ∂G ∂n′ da′ 1 =− ε0 V + V(r′)δ(3)(r−r′). ε0 Gρd3r′+ V V(r′)δ(3)(r−r′) d3r′ .

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: Gρd3r′+ V V(r′)δ(3)(r−r′) d3r′ . Green functions 5/18 Green’s Function for Poisson’s Equation: General Representation Using the delta-function property, V(r′)δ(3)(r−r′) d3r′ V we obtain ∂V G∂V ∂n′−V ∂G ∂n′ da′ 1 =− ε0 V Hence, = V(r), G(r,r′)ρ(r′) d3r′+ V(r). V(r) = 1 ε0 V G(r,r′)ρ(r′) d3r′+ ∂V G∂V ∂n′−V ∂G ∂n′ da′ Interpretation the boundary data. The potential is determined by a volume contribution from the sources and a surface contribution from Green functions 6/18 Free-Space Green’s Function: Symmetry Argument In unbounded free space, the medium is translationally and rotationally invariant. Therefore the Green’s function can only depend on the scalar distance R= |r−r′|. So we write G(r,r′) = G(R). Away from the source point R̸= 0, the delta function vanishes, so ∇2G= 0. For a spherically symmetric function of R, ∇2G(R) = 1 R2 Thus the equation becomes d dR R2 dG dR . d R2 dR R2 dG dR = 0.

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: This is just Laplace’s equation in spherical symmetry about the source point. 1 Green functions 7/18 Free-Space Green’s Function: Solving the ODE Starting from 1 R2 multiply by R2: Integrate once: Then Integrate again: d dR R2 dG dR = 0, d dR R2 dG dR = 0. R2 dG dR= C1. dG dR= C1 G(R) = C1 R2 dR=− R2. C1 R + C2. Green functions 8/18 Free-Space Green’s Function: Fixing the constant Rename the constant: G(R) = A R + C2. To ensure the potential vanishes at infinity, we set C2 = 0. Therefore the free-space Green’s function must have the form G(R) = A . R Green functions 9/18 Free-Space Green’s Function: Fixing the Constant We now determine A from the defining equation ∇2G(r,r′) =−δ(3)(r−r′). Integrate over a small sphere Bη of radius η centered at r′: Bη ∇2Gd3r=− δ(3)(r−r′) d3r=−1. Bη Apply the divergence theorem: Sη ∇G·da =−1. Since G(R) = A R, ∇G= A R on the sphere R= η we have ∇G·da =− d dR A η2 da.

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: A ˆ R=− ˆ R, R2 Green functions 10/18 Free-Space Green’s Function: Therefore, Sη Hence, ∇G·da =− A η2 Sη da=− A η2 (4πη2) =−4πA. 1 −4πA=−1 = ⇒ A= . 4π Green functions 11/18 Free-Space Green’s Function: Final Result Substituting the normalization constant, we obtain G(r,r′) = 1 4π|r−r′| This is the free-space Green’s function of the Laplacian in three dimensions. It represents the potential at r due to a unit point source located at r′ . The dependence on |r−r′|−1 is exactly the Coulomb form, so the Coulomb kernel is the inverse kernel of the Laplace operator in free space. r′ Green functions response R= |r−r′| 12/18 r Electrostatic Potential in Free Space For free space, if the boundary term vanishes at infinity, the general representation reduces to V(r) = 1 ε0 G(r,r′)ρ(r′) d3r′ . Substitute G(r,r′) = 1 4π|r−r′|: V(r) = 1 4πε0 ρ(r′) |r−r′|d3r′ . Thus, V(r) = 1 4πε0 ρ(r′) |r−r′|d3r′ Meaning ρ(r′) d3r′ .

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: The total potential is the superposition of the contributions from all infinitesimal source elements Green functions 13/18 Example 1: Point Charge Let the charge density be a point source at r0: ρ(r′) = qδ(3)(r′ −r0). Insert this into the Coulomb integral: V(r) = 1 4πε0 qδ(3)(r′ −r0) |r−r′| d3r′ . Using the delta-function identity f(r′)δ(3)(r′ −r0) d3r′ = f(r0), Green functions we obtain V(r) = q 4πε0|r−r0|. Therefore, V(r) = q 4πε0|r−r0| equipotential surfaces q 14/18 Dirichlet Green’s Function Suppose the potential is specified on the boundary: V(r′) known on ∂V. To adapt the integral formula to this case, choose a Green’s function satisfying the Dirichlet condition G(r,r′) = 0 for r′∈∂V. Then the term involving G∂V/∂n′vanishes on the boundary, and the solution becomes V(r) = 1 ε0 V G(r,r′)ρ(r′) d3r′ − ∂V V(r′) ∂G ∂n′da′ Why this is useful The boundary condition is built directly into the Green’s function itself.

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: Green functions 15/18 Example 2: Grounded Conducting Plane For a grounded conducting plane, V = 0 on the plane. This is a Dirichlet boundary condition. The appropriate Green’s function can be constructed with the method of images. If the real source is at r′and its mirror point is r′′, define G(r,r′) = 1 4π|r−r′| 1 − 4π|r−r′′|| On the plane, the two distances are equal: |r−r′|= |r−r′′||, so the two terms cancel and therefore G= 0. Hence the boundary condition is satisfied exactly. Green functions 16/18 Method of Images: Geometric Picture The image source is not physical. It is a mathematical construction that modifies the kernel so that the potential vanishes on the conducting boundary. For a real point charge +q near a grounded plane: the real source is located above the plane, an image source−q is placed symmetrically below the plane, the sum of their contributions produces V = 0 on the plane.

chunks.title: Greens function
chunks.text: Interpretation +q−q grounded plane The image term changes the free-space kernel into a boundary-adapted Green’s function. Green functions 17/18 Why Green’s Functions Matter Green’s functions solve the response to a point source. By linearity and superposition, they generate the solution for arbitrary ρ(r). The operator determines the local form of the kernel. The geometry and boundary conditions determine the global form of the kernel. For electrostatics in free space, G(r,r′) = 1 4π|r−r′| and therefore V(r) = 1 4πε0 ρ(r′) |r−r′|d3r′ Graduate-level takeaway A Green’s function is the inverse kernel of a linear differential operator, and it encodes both source propagation and boundary geometry in a single object. Green functions 18/18

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: Griffiths Ch. 3 Laplace’s Equation, Uniqueness, and Images Diego Juarez Department of Physics The University of Texas at El Paso April 15, 2026 1/15 Presentation map 1 Laplace’s Equation 2 Uniqueness and Conductors 3 Method of Images 4 Takeaways Source These slides summarize the electrostatics logic developed in Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, 4th ed. , Chapter 3, especially Sections 3. 1 and 3. 2. Griffiths Ch. 3 2/15 Why Laplace’s Equation Matters Electrostatic potentials are governed by Poisson’s equation, ρ(r) ∇2V(r) =− . ε0 In any charge-free region, ρ= 0, and therefore ∇2V = 0. This is Laplace’s equation. It does not mean the potential is trivial; it means the local value of V is determined entirely by surrounding boundary data. Griffiths viewpoint Electrostatics is fundamentally a boundary-value theory: sources matter, but the physically realized solution is selected by the boundaries. Griffiths Ch.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: 3 3/15 One-Dimensional Warm-Up If the potential depends only on one coordinate, Laplace’s equation becomes d2V dx2 = 0. Integrating twice gives V(x) = C1x+ C2. So in one dimension, a charge-free potential is linear. The constants are not fixed by the differential equation itself; they come from boundary conditions such as V(0) = V0, V(a) = Va. Key lesson inseparable. Even the simplest harmonic solution shows that local source-free behavior and global boundary data are Griffiths Ch. 3 4/15 Harmonic Functions and the Averaging Property Solutions of Laplace’s equation are called harmonic functions. Griffiths emphasizes a remarkable property: V(r0) = average of V over any sphere centered at r0 provided the sphere lies entirely in a charge-free region. This means a harmonic function cannot be shaped by local spikes or dips. The value at a point is tied to the values all around it.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: It also explains why field-free regions can still have nontrivial potentials: outside charges and boundaries determine the average. boundary values r0 center value equals spherical average Griffiths Ch. 3 5/15 No Local Extrema in Empty Space The averaging property has an immediate consequence: A harmonic function cannot have a local maximum or minimum in the interior. If an interior point were a true maximum, the average over a small sphere around it would have to be smaller than the center value, contradicting the averaging theorem. The same reasoning rules out a local minimum. Physical interpretation In a charge-free region, the potential cannot “peak” or “bottom out” by itself. Any extremum must be imposed by the boundary. Griffiths Ch. 3 6/15 First Uniqueness Theorem Suppose the charge density ρ(r) is known throughout a volume and the potential is specified on the entire boundary. Then the electrostatic solution is unique.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: The practical consequence is stronger than it first appears: if you find any function satisfying Poisson’s equation in the volume, and it matches the boundary potential, then it is automatically the physical answer. Why this matters This theorem turns clever guesses into rigorous solutions. Griffiths Ch. 3 7/15 Conductors and the Second Uniqueness Theorem In electrostatic equilibrium, each conductor is an equipotential surface, but those constant values may not be known in advance. Griffiths reformulates the problem this way: If the total charge on each conductor is specified, together with the volume charge density ρ, then the electric field is uniquely determined. The surface charge distribution is not known beforehand. The constant potential on each conductor is not known beforehand. Yet the final field configuration is still unique once the total conductor charges are fixed.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: Use in practice This theorem is what lets conductor problems be solved by indirect constructions, not by brute-force guessing of surface charge densities. Griffiths Ch. 3 8/15 Boundary-Value Strategy in Electrostatics Griffiths’s Chapter 3 logic can be summarized as a workflow: 1 identify the region where you want the potential, 2 write the governing equation there, 3 encode the physical boundary conditions, 4 construct a candidate solution using symmetry or an auxiliary problem, 5 invoke uniqueness to certify it. Core idea The hard part is often not solving the PDE directly. The hard part is finding the right representation that already respects the boundaries. Griffiths Ch. 3 9/15 Classic Image Problem: Charge Above a Grounded Plane A point charge q sits at height d above an infinite grounded conducting plane. In the region z>0, the boundary conditions are V = 0 at z= 0, V →0 far away.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: Replace the conductor by an image charge−q at (0,0,−d). Then for z≥0, V(x,y,z) = 1 4πε0 q x2 + y2 + (z−d)2 − q x2 + y2 + (z+ d)2 Reason it works This auxiliary two-charge problem satisfies the correct differential equation in z>0 and forces V = 0 on the plane, so uniqueness says it must equal the conductor solution there. Griffiths Ch. 3 10/15 Image-Charge Geometry The image charge is not physically present. It is placed only to make the boundary condition automatic. For a grounded plane: the real charge is +q at z= d, the image charge is−q at z=−d, points on the plane are equidistant from both charges, so the two contributions cancel there. Griffiths Ch. 3 +q d grounded plane d −q 11/15 Induced Surface Charge on the Plane Once the potential is known, the induced surface charge follows from the normal derivative: ∂V ∂n surface . σ=−ε0 For the grounded plane, the normal direction isˆ z, so σ(x,y) =−ε0 ∂V ∂z z=0 =− qd 2π(x2 + y2 + d2)3/2.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: The total induced charge is Qind = σda=−q. Interpretation The conductor rearranges exactly enough negative charge to terminate the electric field on its surface while keeping the plane at zero potential. Griffiths Ch. 3 12/15 Force and Energy: A Useful Caution Near the real charge, the field in the physical problem matches the field in the auxiliary two-charge problem, so the force on q is 1 q2 F=− ˆ z. 4πε0 (2d)2 But the energy is not the same as the energy of two actual point charges in free space. For the grounded-plane problem, 1 q2 W=− 4πε0 4d . This is one-half of the two-point-charge value because only the upper half-space contains field energy in the conductor problem. Lesson The image construction reproduces the potential in the region of interest, but it is not a license to identify every global quantity with the auxiliary problem. Griffiths Ch.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: 3 13/15 Grounded Conducting Sphere For a point charge q outside a grounded conducting sphere of radius R, Griffiths gives another exact image construction. If the real charge is a distance a from the center (a>R), then place an image charge R q′ =− q at distance a R2 b= a from the center along the same axis, inside the sphere. The outside potential is then V(r) = 1 4πε0 q r + q′ r′ where r and r′ are distances from the real and image charges. Constraint Image charges must lie outside the region where you are solving Poisson’s equation; otherwise you would change the source term of the actual problem. Griffiths Ch. 3 14/15 Main Takeaways from Griffiths Chapter 3 Laplace’s equation describes source-free regions, but the solution is still globally shaped by the boundary. Harmonic functions obey an averaging property and therefore cannot have interior extrema.

chunks.title: Laplaces eq, Uniqe and Images
chunks.text: Uniqueness theorems justify indirect methods: once the equation and boundaries are matched, the answer is fixed. The method of images works by replacing a conductor problem with an auxiliary source configuration that reproduces the same potential in the region of interest. Image methods are elegant but geometry-dependent; they succeed only for specially structured boundaries. Reference Based on Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, 4th ed. , Chapter 3, Sections 3. 1–3. 2. Griffiths Ch. 3 15/15
